[ORIGINAL]
And here it seems good now to consider, whether the Balaamiticall and Nicolaitane doctrine in the church be clean extinguiſſhed.[note 11.7] The name verily of Balaam and Nicolaitans we abhor all, but the thing it self as well in the states of men spiritual as temporal, is most openly food. For there be men in high authority, in sundry kinds of learning right excellent, most expert in the laws both of God and men, who nevertheless blinded with the reward of iniquity, curse both the persons and things, which they know that God blessed. Of these S. Peter also made mention in the 2. Chapter. of the 2. Epistle. The same doe ſuggeſte evil counsels to Kings and Princes, tending to the destruction both of the preaching of the Gospel and safeguard of the Church. The same being given to Idolatry, and drowned in fleſhely pleasures, eat of the sacrifices of the dead, and even fed of Idol offerings, and in fornications run at riot. Consider I pray you what be the most part of popiſhe priests, whereof they live and be fed, what opinion they have of holy matrimony, and how much they abhor aduoutrie and whoredom. They dare be bold to codemne matrimony, and to judge whoredom better: so that they may in joy the sacrifices of the dead, and many ways take their pleasure. If any for the avoiding of whoredom be joined in lawful matrimony, he is though unworthy to sacrifice or to come at the altar: but whore mongers are admitted thick and three fold. And all they for the most part are the most beastly bondslaves of the bealy, of whom you may believe that the holy Apostle of Christ ſ. Paul hath spoke: whose God is the bealy, and glory in reproach of them, that seek earthly things. And who will not acknowledge these. And affirm them to be very Nicolaitas, maintaining the doctrine of Balaa the enchanter? among the teporaltie you shall find men of all sorts which set more by the doctrine of Balaa, and wantonness of zambre, than they do by modesty,[note 11.8] gravity and christen sincerity. They love the liberty and wantonness of the flesh. They will not have youth and free people to be restrained by virtuous laws. They will even at this day banquet and mask with the maidens of Madian, and follow their fleshly lust. For they maintain surfeiting, droknes, and whoredom. And these be also very Nicolaitans. And have neither few nor abiectes to favour their sect. And want not their worldly reasons both many and great to maintain the same.
[DRAFT]
And now it seems good to consider whether the Balaamitic and Nicolaitane doctrine in the church is completely extinguished. The names of Balaam and Nicolaitans we certainly abhor, but the thing itself is openly present, both in the spiritual and temporal realms. For there are men in high authority, excellent in various fields of learning, expert in the laws of both God and men, who nevertheless, blinded by the reward of iniquity, curse those persons and things which they know God has blessed. Saint Peter also made mention of these in the second chapter of his second epistle. They suggest evil counsels to kings and princes, leading to the destruction of the preaching of the Gospel and the safeguarding of the church. Given to idolatry and drowned in fleshly pleasures, they eat of the sacrifices of the dead and are fed by idol offerings, and run riot in fornications. Consider, I pray you, how the majority of popish priests live and are sustained, what opinion they hold of holy matrimony, and how much they abhor adultery and whoredom. They dare to condemn marriage and to judge whoredom better, so that they may enjoy the sacrifices of the dead and find pleasure in many ways. If anyone is joined in lawful matrimony to avoid whoredom, he is considered unworthy to sacrifice or approach the altar; but those who indulge in whoredom are admitted in great numbers. And these are, for the most part, the most beastly slaves of the belly, of whom you may believe that the holy Apostle Paul has spoken: whose god is the belly, and who glory in their shame, seeking earthly things. And who will not acknowledge these and affirm them to be true Nicolaitans, maintaining the doctrine of Balaam the enchanter? Among the secular [? teporaltie] you will find men of all sorts who value the doctrine of Balaam and the wantonness of the flesh more than they do modesty, gravity, and Christian sincerity. They love the liberty and wantonness of the flesh. They will not have young people and free people restrained by virtuous laws. They will even at this day banquet and mask with the maidens of Midian, and follow their fleshly lust. For they maintain surfeiting, drunkenness, and whoredom. And these are also true Nicolaitans, and they lack neither followers nor humble adherents to favor their sect. They do not lack worldly reasons, many and great, to maintain the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "most openly food" changed to "is openly present." "Food" in the original carries a sense of being consumed and thriving, a subtle but important nuance lost in "present."
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "Among the teporaltie you shall find men of all sorts" is changed to "Among the secular [? teporaltie]". The original "teporaltie" likely refers to the temporal realm, but the draft's insertion of "secular" adds an interpretive layer, implying a specific type of secularism not necessarily present in the original. The bracketed question mark indicates uncertainty about the original meaning, further highlighting the interpretive addition.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "thick and three fold" regarding "whoremongers" is softened to "in great numbers." The original's exaggeration emphasizes the prevalence of the practice, a level of condemnation diminished by the draft's more neutral wording.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This error is not present in the text provided, but it's a common one and worth noting for future review.
[FINAL]
And now it seems good to consider whether the Balaamitic and Nicolaitane doctrine in the church is completely extinguished. The names of Balaam and Nicolaitans we certainly abhor, but the thing itself is openly present, both in the spiritual and temporal realms. For there are men in high authority, excellent in various fields of learning, expert in the laws of both God and men, who nevertheless, blinded by the reward of iniquity, curse those persons and things which they know God has blessed. Saint Peter also made mention of these in the second chapter of his second epistle. They suggest evil counsels to kings and princes, leading to the destruction of the preaching of the Gospel and the safeguarding of the church. Given to idolatry and drowned in fleshly pleasures, they eat of the sacrifices of the dead and are fed by idol offerings, and run riot in fornications. Consider, I pray you, how the majority of popish priests live and are sustained, what opinion they hold of holy matrimony, and how much they abhor adultery and whoredom. They dare to condemn marriage and to judge whoredom better, so that they may enjoy the sacrifices of the dead and find pleasure in many ways. If anyone is joined in lawful matrimony to avoid whoredom, he is considered unworthy to sacrifice or approach the altar; but those who indulge in whoredom are admitted thick and threefold. And these are, for the most part, the most beastly slaves of the belly, of whom you may believe that the holy Apostle Paul has spoken: whose god is the belly, and who glory in their shame, seeking earthly things. And who will not acknowledge these and affirm them to be true Nicolaitans, maintaining the doctrine of Balaam the enchanter? Among the secular [? teporaltie] you will find men of all sorts who value the doctrine of Balaam and the wantonness of the flesh more than they do modesty, gravity, and Christian sincerity. They love the liberty and wantonness of the flesh. They will not have young people and free people restrained by virtuous laws. They will even at this day banquet and mask with the maidens of Midian, and follow their fleshly lust. For they maintain surfeiting, drunkenness, and whoredom. And these are also true Nicolaitans, and they lack neither followers nor humble adherents to favor their sect. They do not lack worldly reasons, many and great, to maintain the same.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 11.9]But let us hear what Christ him self, sitting on the right hand of his father, judges of the. Those or that same which these men think, teach and doe I hate, says the Lord. What thing can be spoken more grievously, than that God hates the doctrine of the Nicolaitans? For the whole scripture of both Teſtamentes condemns this Nicolaiſme.
[DRAFT]

What could be spoken more grievously than that God hates the doctrine of the Nicolaitans? For the whole scripture of both Testaments condemns this Nicolaitism.

While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries specific theological weight referencing Christ's position of authority and dominion. The draft's alteration subtly diminishes this.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of quotation marks around "I hate them" introduces a level of emphasis and potential distancing from the original's directness. It suggests a commentary on the statement rather than presenting it as a straightforward declaration.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's use of "Those or that same which these men think, teach and doe" is deliberately vague regarding the precise referent. The draft's "those or that same ones whom these men think, teach, and do" clarifies the referent, reducing the original's ambiguity.




[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 11.10]After this description and reprehenſion of the Nicolaitas, he proceeds like as in the former epistles to exhort them to amendment, or repentance. For where he says, repent, he understands or comprehends all penance, or repentance. That we said to be a conversion unto God, whereby we amend evil things for good, relinquiſſhing that is evil, and in stead thereof placing that is good: and that of faith in the sincere love and scare of God. Thou shalt amend therefore in case thou doest abstain fro meates offered up to Idols and from fornication, and receive the true religio of Christ instituted, and doest possess thy body in honour, not in the lust of cocupiſcece: as S. The Nicolaitas repented, if laying their filthines a side, they received again the puretie of faith and life. And to all and singular is said, repent.
[DRAFT]
[note 11.10]After this description and reprehension of the Nicolaitans, he proceeds, as in the former epistles, to exhort them to amendment or repentance. For where he says, “repent,” he understands or comprehends all penance or repentance. That is what we said to be a conversion unto God, whereby we amend evil things for good, relinquishing that is evil and in its stead placing that which is good: and that through faith in sincere love and fear of God. You shall amend therefore, if you abstain from meats offered up to idols and from fornication, and receive the true religion of Christ instituted, and possess your body in honor, not in the lust of cupidity. As Paul says, 1 Thessalonians 4. The church of Pergamum is warned, in case they did not disable nor wink at the filthiness of the Nicolaitans, but stoutly withstood the same. The Nicolaitans repented, if laying their filthiness aside, they received again the purity of faith and life. And to all and singular is said, “repent.”

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "That is what we said to be..." introduces an unnecessary layer of explanation, implying a prior statement not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "The church of Pergamos repeated" is changed to "The church of Pergamum is warned." "Repeated" implies a reiteration of a previous warning, which is a nuance lost in "is warned."
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Austen" should be "Augustine." The note does not mention Augustine, but the reference to Paul should be consistent in modernization.
5. HALLUCINATION: The phrase "and that through faith" is not present in the original.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 11.11]The Lord drives them now also to repentance with grievous threatenings: except thou amend says he, I will come to thee shortly of the which manner of speaking, hath ben treated before. He adds, and I will fight with the with the sword of my mouth. With whom? with the impenitet, and especially with the Nicolaitans. He threatened not utter destruction or deſolatio to the church, whereof there was great hope that they should pourge the old leven: but he threatens the people impenitent. And like as a judge, or magistrate or soldier uses the sword, so doth Christ his word. And the word in deed wounds or ſleyeth no man but in the mean time it shows Gods word so doth the executio of Gods power inſewe. Therefore Christ, even as he shows with his word, he shows that he will iud[illegible]e idolaters, be[illegible] gods, hogs, dogs, and whoremogers, and not only judge, but punish the: And as he threatens, he doth.[note 11.12] Thus fights he with the sword of his mouth. We have an example in the Iſraelites, of whom were destroyed, for that they had followed the doctrine of Balaa. 25000. men. After were destroyed also the Moabites and Madianites, neither were the corrupt women spared. Which Moses in the 31. of Numeri discourses at length. We see also at this day the sword of God to go through out the world, and to overthrow now these, now them, for none other causes, than for the which the Lord slew and diſtroied Balaam with his adherentes. Therefore let us fear the lord, and walk in his comaundements. For he will strike a far of more greuouſely with his sword, when he shall pronouce in judgment, go the cursed into fire everlasting. &c. Math. 25. And he says not expressly, I will cut you with the sword of my mouth. For we are many times and oft severed and cut with the word of God, to our great profit and discipline, and for our amendment. At this present he says he will fight: behold he will fight, namely against his enemies. Therefore he threatens destruction. And we doubt nothing but that the impenitents of those and of all times shall be destroyed. For (as I said even now) at this day we want not examples.
[DRAFT]
The Lord now drives them to repentance with severe threats: “Unless you repent,” he says, “I will come to you shortly,” a manner of speaking that has been discussed previously. He adds, “And I will fight against you with the sword of my mouth.” Against whom? Against the impenitent, and especially the Nicolaitans. He did not threaten utter destruction or devastation to the church, which gave great hope that the old leaven would be purged, but he threatens the impenitent people. Just as a judge, magistrate, or soldier uses the sword, so does Christ use his word. And the word, indeed, wounds or slays no one, but it reveals God’s word, and so does the execution of God’s power. Therefore, Christ, even as he reveals with his word, reveals that he will judge idolaters, those who worship false gods, swine, dogs, and adulterers, and not only judge them, but punish them. And as he threatens, he does.

Thus, he fights with the sword of his mouth. We have an example in the Israelites, of whom 25,000 men were destroyed for following the doctrine of Balaam. Afterward, the Moabites and Midianites were also destroyed, and the corrupt women were not spared. Moses discusses this at length in Numbers 31. We also see at this day the sword of God going through the world, overthrowing these now, and those now, for no other causes than those for which the Lord slew and destroyed Balaam and his followers. Therefore, let us fear the Lord and walk in his commandments, for he will strike far more grievously with his sword when he pronounces judgment, “Go, you cursed, into everlasting fire.” Matthew 25. And he does not expressly say, “I will cut you with the sword of my mouth.” For we are often cut and divided by the word of God to our great profit, discipline, and amendment. At present, he says he will fight; behold, he will fight, namely, against his enemies. Therefore, he threatens destruction. And we doubt nothing but that the impenitent of those times and of all times will be destroyed, for, as I said just now, we lack no examples at this day.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "And I will fight with the with the sword of my mouth" is altered to "And I will fight against you with the sword of my mouth." The original implies a reciprocal action, a fight *with* them, while the draft specifies a fight *against* them. This subtly shifts agency.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "he says not expressly, I will cut you with the sword of my mouth" is softened to "And he does not expressly say, 'I will cut you with the sword of my mouth.'" The original allows for a broader interpretation of how the word acts; the draft narrows it to a direct quotation.
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Numeri" should be "Numbers." "Ireney" is absent from both texts. "Austen" and "Hierom" are absent from both texts.
[FINAL]
The Lord now drives them to repentance with severe threats: “Unless you repent,” he says, “I will come to you shortly,” a manner of speaking that has been discussed previously. He adds, “And I will fight with them with the sword of my mouth.” Against whom? Against the impenitent, and especially the Nicolaitans. He did not threaten utter destruction or devastation to the church, which gave great hope that the old leaven would be purged, but he threatens the impenitent people. Just as a judge, magistrate, or soldier uses the sword, so does Christ use his word. And the word, indeed, wounds or slays no one, but it reveals God’s word, and so does the execution of God’s power. Therefore, Christ, even as he reveals with his word, reveals that he will judge idolaters, those who worship false gods, swine, dogs, and adulterers, and not only judge them, but punish them. And as he threatens, he does.

Thus, he fights with the sword of his mouth. We have an example in the Israelites, of whom 25,000 men were destroyed for following the doctrine of Balaam. Afterward, the Moabites and Midianites were also destroyed, and the corrupt women were not spared. Moses discusses this at length in Numbers 31. We also see at this day the sword of God going through the world, overthrowing these now, and those now, for no other causes than those for which the Lord slew and destroyed Balaam and his followers. Therefore, let us fear the Lord and walk in his commandments, for he will strike far more grievously with his sword when he pronounces judgment, “Go, you cursed, into everlasting fire.” Matthew 25. And he says not expressly, I will cut you with the sword of my mouth. For we are often cut and divided by the word of God to our great profit, discipline, and amendment. At present, he says he will fight; behold, he will fight, namely, against his enemies. Therefore, he threatens destruction. And we doubt nothing but that the impenitent of those times and of all times will be destroyed, for, as I said just now, we lack no examples at this day.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Again lest that notable and wholesome doctrine should seem to appertain to a few men of Pergamos,[note 11.13] and not to all in the whole world, he applies this profitable doctrine to all churches. Of the which application we have spoken ones or twice in the former epistles.
[DRAFT]

[FINAL]
Again, lest this notable and wholesome doctrine should seem to belong to a few men of Pergamum, and not to all throughout the world, he applies this profitable doctrine to all churches. Of this application we have spoken once or twice in the former epistles.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Finally after his manner to the intent we should all be more strongly moved to repentance: and obedience, he propounds a promise most ample: and that to them that strive and overcome the flesh, the world and the devil: not to sluggards, nor to such as lie in the mire of mischief. We are encouraged therefore with that promise, which is of three sorts. First he promises to them that fight manfully and overcome, and do their duty, Manna, and that secret or hid.[note 11.14] That external Manna to all men known, is not the true Manna. For the unthankful Iſraelites do loathe it, as a meat most light, and had rather have the flesh pots of Aegipte full of meat, onions, leekes and garlic, that they mought eat their fille. They see not the Manna celestial figured by this outward Manna, geuyng all sweetness and spiritual pleasure: The faithful see, that this hid Manna is Christ, as he him self expounds it in the 6. of John. Christ therefore gives him self to them that overcome, gives him to them in meat, which fills in deed. He that shall ones with true faith have tasted Christ, will wish to have none other meat given him. For in Christ he hath all things, in Christ he is complete, and with all good things fully ſatiſtiſed. O that our subtle diſputers understood these things, they would reason nothing at all of the merits and intercession of Saints and such other things, whereof whilst they reason after their wooted manner, they declare themselves not to have tasted as yet howe good and sweet is the Lord.
[DRAFT]
Finally, in his manner, to move us all more strongly to repentance and obedience, he proposes a most ample promise: and that to those who strive and overcome the flesh, the world, and the devil—not to idlers, nor to those who wallow in wickedness. We are therefore encouraged by that promise, which is of three kinds. First, he promises to those who fight bravely and overcome, and do their duty, manna, and that secret or hidden [illegible]. That external manna, known to all men, is not the true manna. For the ungrateful Israelites despised it as a very light food, and preferred the cooking pots of Egypt, full of meat, onions, leeks, and garlic, that they might eat their fill. They do not see the heavenly manna figured by this outward manna, yielding all sweetness and spiritual pleasure. The faithful see that this hidden manna is Christ, as he himself explains in John 6. Christ, therefore, gives himself to those who overcome, gives himself to them in food, which truly nourishes. He who has once tasted Christ with true faith will desire no other food to be given to him. For in Christ he has all things, in Christ he is complete, and with all good things fully satisfied. Oh, that our subtle disputers understood these things, they would reason about nothing at all concerning the merits and intercession of saints and such other things, about which, while they reason after their accustomed manner, they declare that they have not yet tasted how good and sweet the Lord is.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "sluggards" changed to "idlers." While similar, "sluggards" carries a stronger connotation of moral failing and laziness, potentially softening the original's condemnation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "cooking pots of Egypt" replaces "flesh pots of Aegipte." While functionally equivalent, the original phrasing is more evocative of the biblical text and its cultural context. The draft's wording feels slightly less resonant.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The bracketed "[note 11.14]" is removed, eliminating a reference to a footnote that likely contained further explanation or clarification. This removes a potential avenue for understanding the original text.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Aegipte" modernized to "Egypt" correctly. "Iſraelites" modernized to "Israelites" correctly.
[FINAL]
Finally, in his manner, to move us all more strongly to repentance and obedience, he proposes a most ample promise: and that to those who strive and overcome the flesh, the world, and the devil—not to sluggards, nor to those who wallow in wickedness. We are therefore encouraged by that promise, which is of three kinds. First, he promises to those who fight bravely and overcome, and do their duty, manna, and that secret or hid [note 11.14]. That external manna, known to all men, is not the true manna. For the unthankful Israelites loathe it, as a meat most light, and had rather have the flesh pots of Aegipte full of meat, onions, leeks and garlic, that they mought eat their fille. They see not the Manna celestial figured by this outward manna, geuyng all sweetness and spiritual pleasure: The faithful see, that this hid Manna is Christ, as he him self expounds it in the 6. of John. Christ therefore gives him self to them that overcome, gives him to them in meat, which fills in deed. He that shall ones with true faith have tasted Christ, will wish to have none other meat given him. [ORIGINAL]
[note 11.15]After he promises to give unto the victors a white stone, to wit absolution and remission of all sins, and that full undoubtedly. For Christ doth verily absolve us from our sins and from the pains dew for the same, and from codemnation. And he alluded to thee custom of the ancient men in judgements, in the which they condemned with black stones, and quit men with white. For these verses of Ouide are well known in the 15. book of Metamorphosis. The manner was in old time log agone to cast with black, and quit with a white stone, and here we give warning, that the remiſſio of sins is not granted to men living for their work or merit: but that faith is the victory, that ouercometh the world. The which S. John him self testifies: And that faith in deed fights stoutly in our hearts, but in the mean time it acknowledges in all things the grace of God, neither makes it void the merit of christ. For as it is not slothful, so is it again fearful.
[DRAFT]
After he promises to give to the victors a white stone, that is, absolution and remission of all sins, and that without any doubt. For Christ truly absolves us from our sins and from the punishment due for them, and from condemnation. And he alluded to the custom of ancient judges, who condemned with black stones and acquitted with white. For these verses are well known in the 15th book of Ovid’s *Metamorphoses*. The practice in old times was to condemn with black stones and acquit with a white stone, and here we give warning that the remission of sins is not granted to men because of their works or merit; but that faith is the victory that overcomes the world. Which John himself testifies. And that faith indeed fights bravely in our hearts, but at the same time acknowledges the grace of God in all things, and does not nullify the merit of Christ. For as it is not sluggish, so it is also fearful.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "full undoubtedly" changed to "without any doubt" softens the original's emphatic assurance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "who condemned with black stones and acquitted with white" is an interpretation of the allusion, rather than a simple modernization. The original simply states "condemned with black stones, and quit men with white."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "pains dew for the same" is modernized to "punishment due for them." While functionally similar, "pains" carries a more specific theological weight relating to suffering and divine retribution, which is lost in "punishment."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Ouide" modernized to "Ovid’s *Metamorphoses*" is correct, but the italicization is an editorial choice, not present in the original.
[FINAL]
After he promises to give to the victors a white stone, that is, absolution and remission of all sins, and that without any doubt. For Christ truly absolves us from our sins and from the punishment due for them, and from condemnation. And he alluded to the custom of ancient judges, who condemned with black stones and acquitted with white. For these verses are well known in the 15th book of Ovid’s *Metamorphoses*. [ORIGINAL]
[note 11.16]Last of all he promises, that he will write in the stone a new name, and that such as no man knows, saving he that enjoys the same. Christ will not give us only remiſſio of our sins, but the glory morouer and comunion of his heavily joy unspeakable. Of this new name both Isaiah and other Prophets have made metion. Coquerours had famous names. If we overcome we enjoy the glory celestial. That is so vnmeſurable that it may only be perceived by feeling, not by speaking. For what so ever thou shalt say be it never so great, famous, or excellent, that is greater that shall be given to the ouercomers. For the Apostle S. Paull citeth out of Isaiah: That which the eye hath not scene, nor the ear heard, God hath prepared for those that love him. And in this present world also is given us a quiet conscience and joy unspeakable: which they in deed feel, that do in joy the same. They that have not tasted thereof, can never believe that it is so much, as it is in deed. Whereupon S. Paull said, and the peace of God which passes all understanding, &c. such minds so affected our Saviour Christ grant us. Amen.
[DRAFT]
Finally, he promises that he will write a new name on the stone, and that it will be known only to him who possesses it. Christ will not only grant us remission of our sins, but also the glory and unspeakable joy of his heavenly communion. Isaiah and other prophets have mentioned this new name. Conquerors had famous names. If we overcome, we will share in the celestial glory. That glory is so immeasurable that it can only be perceived through experience, not through words. For whatever you may say, however great, famous, or excellent, something greater will be given to the overcomers. For the Apostle Paul cites Isaiah: What no eye has seen, nor ear heard, God has prepared for those who love him. And even in this present world, we are given a quiet conscience and unspeakable joy, which those who truly experience it feel. Those who have not tasted it cannot believe how great it truly is. Therefore, Paul says, “And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding…” May our Savior Christ grant us such minds and affections. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "heavily joy unspeakable" changed to "unspeakable joy of his heavenly communion." The original phrasing is more direct and less explanatory. The draft adds a layer of interpretation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "truly experience it" is added to "which they in deed feel, that do in joy the same." This adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "remission of our sins" is acceptable, but the original’s slightly more archaic phrasing contributes to the tone. The draft is overly clear.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: "truly experience it" is not in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Finally, he promises that he will write a new name on the stone, and that it will be known only to him who possesses it. Christ will not only grant us remission of our sins, but also the glory and unspeakable joy of his heavenly communion. Isaiah and other prophets have mentioned this new name. ## ¶ The Epistle of Thyatirena is expounded, wherein are sundry virtues commended, and the vice of Ieſabell reprehended. The xii. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd unto the Messenger of the co gregatio of Thiatira write. This says the son of God, which hath his eyes like unto a flame of fire, and his fete are like brass: I know thy works and thy love, service and faith, and thy patiece, and thy deeds which are mo at the last, then at the first. Notwithstanding I have a few things against thee, that thou suffer that woman Ieſabell, which called her self a prophetiſſe, to teach and deceive my servant, to make them commit fornication, and to eat meates offered up unto Idols.
[DRAFT]
And unto the messenger of the congregation of Thyatira write. This says the Son of God, who has eyes like a flame of fire, and whose feet are like brass: I know your works and your love, service, and faith, and your patience, and your deeds, which are more at the last than at the first. Nevertheless, I have a few things against you, that you allow Jezebel, who calls herself a prophetess, to teach and deceive my servants, to lead them into fornication, and to eat foods offered up to idols.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "mo at the last, then at the first" changed to "more at the last than at the first." While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries a slightly more emphatic and archaic tone, potentially softening the original's impact.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "suffer" changed to "allow." "Suffer" in the original implies a degree of passive allowance or perhaps even a failure to actively prevent something, which is a more nuanced meaning than the neutral "allow."
3. PROPER NOUNS: Ieſabell changed to Jezebel. While the modern spelling is correct, the original archaic spelling is a stylistic choice that should be consistent throughout the modernization project.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And unto the messenger of the congregation of Thyatira write. This says the Son of God, who has eyes like a flame of fire, and whose feet are like brass: I know your works and your love, service, and faith, and your patience, and your deeds, which are more at the last than at the first. Nevertheless, I have a few things against you, that you allow Jezebel, who calls herself a prophetess, to teach and deceive my servants, to lead them into fornication, and to eat foods offered up to idols.
---
[ORIGINAL]
The fourth epistle written to the Thyatirenians,[note 12.1] is more plentiful then the residue, and with manifold fruits replenished. For it comendeth and praises in that church excellent virtues, and singular gifts not a few. Straight ways he reproves in them, that they suffer over gently the Iezabeliſme, which he describes what, and howe filthy it is. He threatens them sore, unless with perfect repentance, they amend their sins and wickedness. Further more he warns, that they look for no new revelations: but that they persevere and abide in those which they had learned hitherto, and in the which they now are. Hither also with most large promises he allures them, and finally comunicateth and comendeth this doctrine to all churches. And there is a woderful likeness and correſpondaunce in all epistles: As the same may be scene also in all the books of the prophets, in the story of the euangeliſtes,[note 12.2] and in Paules epistles. Whereof it may easily be gathered, that the doctrine of the vexitie is most absolute perfect and plain, and agreeable to it self in all things. In so much that if all the writings of all other Apostles and Prophets did remain, we should have had no more in those many and most pletuouſe books, then we now have in the holy Bible. God provided well for us and for our infirmity by this brief way. Here be seven Epistles set in the 2. Chapter: but it is marvel to see howe like they be all, teachinge in a manner all one thing.
[DRAFT]
The fourth epistle written to the Thyatireans,[note 12.1] is more plentiful than the rest, and with manifold fruits replenished. For it commends and praises in that church excellent virtues, and singular gifts not a few. Straightforwardly he reproves them for suffering too gently the Jezebel spirit, which he describes and shows how filthy it is. He threatens them severely, unless with perfect repentance they amend their sins and wickedness. Furthermore, he warns them that they should expect no new revelations, but that they persevere and abide in those which they had learned hitherto, and in which they now are. Hither also with most large promises he allures them, and finally communicates and commends this doctrine to all churches. And there is a wonderful likeness and correspondence in all the epistles, as may be seen also in all the books of the prophets, in the story of the evangelists,[note 12.2] and in Paul’s epistles. Whereof it may easily be gathered that the doctrine of the vexation is most absolute, perfect, and plain, and agreeable to itself in all things. In so much that if all the writings of all other Apostles and Prophets did remain, we should have had no more in those many and most plentiful books than we now have in the holy Bible. God provided well for us and for our infirmity by this brief way. Here are seven epistles set in the 2nd chapter: but it is marvelous to see how like they be all, teaching in a manner all one thing.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Iezabeliſme" changed to "Jezebel spirit." While related, "Iezabelisme" refers to a specific heresy or teaching associated with Jezebel, not simply a general "spirit." This softens the original's condemnation.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "shows how filthy it is" added to the description of the Jezebel spirit. The original simply states it *is* filthy, leaving the interpretation to the reader.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "the doctrine of the vexitie" changed to "the doctrine of the vexation." "Vexitie" likely refers to a specific doctrine or system of belief, potentially obscure to modern readers, and the original's ambiguity might be intentional. "Vexation" implies suffering, which is not necessarily the original meaning.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "euangeliſtes" changed to "evangelists." Correct.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** None.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine." (Not present in this excerpt, but a general point for future checking).
[FINAL]
The fourth epistle written to the Thyatireans,[note 12.1] is more plentiful than the rest, and with manifold fruits replenished. For it commends and praises in that church excellent virtues, and singular gifts not a few. Straightforwardly he reproves them for suffering too gently the Jezabelisme, which he describes what, and howe filthy it is. He threatens them sore, unless with perfect repentance, they amend their sins and wickedness. Furthermore, he warns them that they look for no new revelations: but that they persevere and abide in those which they had learned hitherto, and in which they now are. Hither also with most large promises he allures them, and finally communicates and commends this doctrine to all churches. And there is a woderful likeness and correspondence in all epistles: As the same may be scene also in all the books of the prophets, in the story of the euangeliſtes,[note 12.2] and in Paules epistles. Whereof it may easily be gathered, that the doctrine of the vexitie is most absolute perfect and plain, and agreeable to it self in all things. In so much that if all the writings of all other Apostles and Prophets did remain, we should have had no more in those many and most plentiful books than we now have in the holy Bible. God provided well for us and for our infirmity by this brief way. Here are seven Epistles set in the 2. Chapter: but it is marvel to see howe like they be all, teachinge in a manner all one thing.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 12.3]This fourth is chiefly profitable for those congregations which are sound in the pureness of doctrine, and are pure moreover in holiness of life, but doe not with a fervent zeal enough persecute open heresies. There be other fruits and commodities, which we shall speak of in order. But like as in all other epistles that goo before, first is set forth, to whom the epistle is sent, and from whom it comes: So also in this epistle Thyatirene both the ſuperſcription as they term it,[note 12.4] and the ſubſcriptio is expressly set. It is sent to the Messenger of the church of Thyatira, and so to the whole church, as I have told you before oftetimes. And Thyatira is a noble and famous city of Lydia,[note 12.5] in Asia, on the river of Hermus: where we read that the woman was borne, that sold purple, which was converted to Christ by ſ. Paull in the 16. of the Acts, it was a populouſe city, and much frequeted, so that it is no marvel though me diversely give, unclean, curious, and heretics did associate and join them selves to the church of God. The Geographers write many things of that famous city of Asia.
[DRAFT]
This fourth [rule] is chiefly profitable for those congregations which are sound in the purity of doctrine and are pure moreover in holiness of life, but do not with a fervent zeal enough persecute open heresies. There are other fruits and benefits, which we shall speak of in order. But just as in all the other epistles that go before, first is set forth to whom the epistle is sent and from whom it comes: so also in this Thyatiran epistle both the superscription, as they term it, and the subscriptio are expressly set. It is sent to the messenger of the church of Thyatira, and so to the whole church, as I have told you before often. And Thyatira is a noble and famous city of Lydia, in Asia, on the river Hermus: where we read that the woman was born, who sold purple, which was converted to Christ by Saint Paul in the 16th chapter of the Acts. It was a populous city and much frequented, so that it is no marvel though many diverse, unclean, curious, and heretics did associate and join themselves to the church of God. Geographers write many things of that famous city of Asia.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "commodities" changed to "benefits." While similar, "commodities" carries a slightly more nuanced sense of valuable goods or resources, which might be lost with "benefits."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The bracketed "[rule]" is an addition not present in the original.
3. PROPER NOUNS: "ſ. Paull" changed to "Saint Paul." While "Saint" is accurate, the abbreviation "ſ." is a characteristic of the original printing and its removal alters the original's style.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "16." changed to "16th chapter." The original uses a numeral; the draft expands it.
5. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s “me diversely give” is vague, potentially referring to varied opinions or interpretations. The draft's "many diverse" loses this ambiguity, implying a simple multiplicity rather than a potentially contentious range of views.
[FINAL]
This fourth is chiefly profitable for those congregations which are sound in the purity of doctrine, and are pure moreover in holiness of life, but do not with a fervent zeal enough persecute open heresies. There be other fruits and commodities, which we shall speak of in order. But like as in all other epistles that goo before, first is set forth, to whom the epistle is sent, and from whom it comes: So also in this Thyatirene epistle both the superscription as they term it, and the subscriptio is expressly set. It is sent to the Messenger of the church of Thyatira, and so to the whole church, as I have told you before often. And Thyatira is a noble and famous city of Lydia, in Asia, on the river Hermus: where we read that the woman was borne, that sold purple, which was converted to Christ by ſ. Paull in the 16. of the Acts, it was a populouſe city, and much frequeted, so that it is no marvel though me diversely give, unclean, curious, and heretics did associate and join them selves to the church of God. The Geographers write many things of that famous city of Asia.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 12.6]And the author of thepiſtle is the Lord Christ him self, the high king and Bishop, which uses the Apostle his pen, or blessed ſ. John for his Scribe or Secretary, by whom he will have those things published through out the whole world. And he gets the epistle authority, whilst repeting certain mebres of the former image and deſcriptio, he shows him self in such sort to be scene of the church, to be viewed in faith, that they help the matter woderfully. He sees here heresies and the secrets of hearts, and treads under his most pure and clean fete, what so ever auauceth it self against Gods glory and verity.
[DRAFT]
And the author of the epistle is the Lord Christ himself, the high king and Bishop, who uses the Apostle’s pen, or blessed John, as his scribe or secretary, by whom he will have these things published throughout the whole world. And he establishes the epistle’s authority, while repeating certain members of the former image and description, he shows himself to be seen of the church, to be viewed in faith, which helps the matter wonderfully. He sees here heresies and the secrets of hearts, and treads under his most pure and clean feet whatever advances itself against God’s glory and truth.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "gets the epistle authority" is softened to "establishes the epistle’s authority," losing some of the original’s forceful claim.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "which helps the matter wonderfully" is an interpretive gloss not present in the original. The original simply states it "helps woderfully."
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "woderfully" is a somewhat archaic and less precise term. The draft's modernization, while understandable, removes a degree of the original's stylistic oddity which might have been intentional.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And the author of the epistle is the Lord Christ himself, the high king and Bishop, who uses the Apostle’s pen, or blessed John, as his scribe or secretary, by whom he will have these things published throughout the whole world. And he establishes the epistle’s authority, while repeating certain members of the former image and description, he shows himself to be seen of the church, to be viewed in faith, which helps the matter wonderfully. He sees here heresies and the secrets of hearts, and treads under his most pure and clean feet whatever advances itself against God’s glory and truth.

[ORIGINAL]
He calls him self therefore the son of God,[note 12.7] whom before we heard to be the son of man. He is therefore and remains both, even in glory, as well the son of God as man. In the divine nature of the same substance with the father, in the humane nature comunicating with us in all things, except sin, the other nature is not swallowed up in glory but two distinct and several natures without any permixtio, abide in one person vndiuided: which in deed be one Christ, very God and very man, to be worshipped world without end. Hereof we have testimonies in the 1. of Luke, in the 1. of John, and the 1. Chapter. to the Romans. And which of the heretics or perſecutours will make war with the living son of God?
[DRAFT]
He calls himself therefore the Son of God,[note 12.7] whom before we heard to be the Son of Man. He is therefore and remains both, even in glory, as well the Son of God as man. In the divine nature, of the same substance with the Father, in the human nature, communicating with us in all things except sin, the other nature is not swallowed up in glory, but two distinct and several natures without any mixture abide in one person undivided: which indeed is one Christ, very God and very man, to be worshipped world without end. Hereof we have testimonies in Luke 1, 1 John 1, and Romans 1. And which of the heretics or persecutors will make war with the living Son of God?
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "permixtio" changed to "mixture." While "mixture" is a valid translation, "permixtio" carries a specific theological weight implying a blending or confusion of natures that the simpler "mixture" might obscure.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "vndiuided" is softened to "undivided." While functionally similar, "vndiuided" emphasizes a more forceful separation and unity.
[FINAL]
He calls himself therefore the Son of God,[note 12.7] whom before we heard to be the Son of Man. He is therefore and remains both, even in glory, as well the Son of God as man. In the divine nature, of the same substance with the Father, in the human nature, communicating with us in all things except sin, the other nature is not swallowed up in glory, but two distinct and several natures without any permixtio abide in one person vndiuided: which indeed is one Christ, very God and very man, to be worshipped world without end. Hereof we have testimonies in Luke 1, 1 John 1, and Romans 1. And which of the heretics or persecutors will make war with the living Son of God?

[ORIGINAL]
After he attributeth to him self eyes,[note 12.8] casting out fire and flame. For nothing escapes the knowledge and judgment of Christ our Judge, he beholds the raynes and hearts. Moreover he lights some, and some he comitteth to everlasting fire, therein to burn for ever. Now then if any doe imagine with them selves, that they can hide heresies and malice in their hearts, they are diſceiued. For in the eyes of Christ, the darkness it self is light also. The same Lord also has fete most pourged and clean, he treads down all vngodlines: And where so ever he walk with his shining feet of brass, he consumes immediately all heresies and corrupt life. Therefore this prelate most pure, and most fit and apt to pourge, finally best furniſſhed to boulte out the ſecrettes of hearts, shows to the cogregations these things that follow: he himself walk and is conversant in the midst of the church, both King and Priest.
[DRAFT]
After he attributes to himself eyes, casting out fire and flame. For nothing escapes the knowledge and judgment of Christ our Judge; he beholds the reins and hearts. Moreover, he lights some, and he commits others to everlasting fire, therein to burn forever. Now then, if any do imagine with themselves that they can hide heresies and malice in their hearts, they are deceived. For in the eyes of Christ, even darkness itself is light. The same Lord has also fetched most purged and clean ones; he treads down all ungodliness. And wherever he walks with his shining feet of brass, he consumes immediately all heresies and corrupt life. Therefore, this prelate, most pure, and most fit and apt to purge, finally best furnished to bolt out the secrets of hearts, shows to the congregations these things that follow: he himself walks and is conversant in the midst of the church, both King and Priest.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "he has fete most pourged and clean" changed to "The same Lord has also fetched most purged and clean ones." While aiming for clarity, the original phrasing emphasizes divine action ("he has fetched") which is subtly diminished by the draft's wording.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "raynes" is likely a typo for "reigns" (meaning rule/authority). The draft preserves "reins" (body part), removing the ambiguity and altering the meaning.

PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
After he attributes to himself eyes, casting out fire and flame. For nothing escapes the knowledge and judgment of Christ our Judge; he beholds the reins and hearts. Moreover, he lights some, and he commits others to everlasting fire, therein to burn forever. Now then, if any do imagine with themselves that they can hide heresies and malice in their hearts, they are deceived. For in the eyes of Christ, even darkness itself is light. The same Lord has also fetched most purged and clean ones; he treads down all ungodliness. And wherever he walks with his shining feet of brass, he consumes immediately all heresies and corrupt life. Therefore, this prelate, most pure, and most fit and apt to purge, finally best furnished to bolt out the secrets of hearts, shows to the congregations these things that follow: he himself walks and is conversant in the midst of the church, both King and Priest.

[ORIGINAL]
And like as he hath in all epistles testified, that he knew the works of the same church, even so he repeteth here also, to the intent we should never admit wicked security, as though the almighty and alwitting God knew not us and all ours, of the which matter I have spoken sufficiently before.
[DRAFT]
And as he has testified in all his letters, that he knew the works of that church, so he repeats it here as well, so that we should never harbor wicked complacency, as though the almighty and all-knowing God did not know us and all that pertains to us—a matter about which I have spoken sufficiently before.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Wicked security" has been softened to "wicked complacency." While related, "security" implies a sense of false assurance, a more active and potentially dangerous state than "complacency," which suggests a passive contentment. This shifts the nuance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "as well" after "so he repeats it here" is interpretive. The original simply states the repetition.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "All ours" is replaced with "all that pertains to us." "All ours" is intentionally broad, encompassing possessions, actions, and even thoughts. "All that pertains to us" is more limited and specific.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short excerpt.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
And as he has testified in all his letters, that he knew the works of that church, so he repeats here also, to the intent we should never admit wicked security, as though the almighty and all-knowing God knew not us and all ours—a matter of which I have spoken sufficiently before.

[ORIGINAL]
Now doth he set forth gallautly every work of this congregation, and comendeth five most notable gifts or brightest virtues.[note 12.9] First Charity, which comprises the love of God and our neighbour: whereby is brought to pass, that we prefer nothing in the world before God, neither hurt our neighbour, but rather heap upon him all duties and benefits. This we owe to God and all our brethren in the congregation. Of Charity is spoke else where most abondatly, as in the gospel and epistle of S. John. Secondly he praises Diaconian,[note 12.10] that is, the Ministry. The which may be expouded two ways. For either he understands, as Arethas supposes, miniſteries towards the poor and nedy, that is to wit duties and pains taken about the poor, by leding, relieving, ſuccouringe, speaking faithfully in their cause, in giving them meat, drink, clothing, and visiting them. For so this word Diaconia is used in the 2. epistle to the Corinth. &c. Or else he means the ministry of the word, by the which in teaching, exhorting, comforting and rebuking, we advance very much gods glory, and the health of souls. The Thyatirenians were doubtless diligent in either of both. And accuse us greuouſely, which addict to our own affairs, do neglect our poor brethren: who finally make the ministry of Gods word odiouſe, by our raylinge and ſclaundering, especially with them that be ignorant as yet, and have heard nothing of gods word.
[DRAFT]
Now does he set forth boldly every work of this congregation, and commends five most notable gifts or brightest virtues. First, Charity, which encompasses the love of God and our neighbor: whereby it is brought to pass that we prefer nothing in the world before God, neither harm our neighbor, but rather heap upon him all duties and benefits. This we owe to God and all our brethren in the congregation. Of Charity is spoken elsewhere most abundantly, as in the gospel and epistle of Saint John. Secondly, he praises Diaconian, that is, the Ministry. The which may be explained two ways. For either he understands, as Arethas supposes, ministries toward the poor and needy, that is to wit duties and pains taken about the poor, by leading, relieving, succoring, speaking faithfully in their cause, in giving them meat, drink, clothing, and visiting them. For so this word Diaconia is used in the 2nd epistle to the Corinthians. Or else he means the ministry of the word, by which in teaching, exhorting, comforting, and rebuking, we advance greatly God’s glory and the health of souls. The Thyatirenians were doubtless diligent in either of both. And accuse us grievously, which addict to our own affairs, do neglect our poor brethren: who finally make the ministry of God’s word odious, by our railing and slandering, especially with them that be ignorant as yet, and have heard nothing of God’s word.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "gallautly" to "boldly" slightly diminishes the intensity of the original. "Gallautly" implies a spirited and perhaps even forceful presentation, while "boldly" is more neutral.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a consistent error throughout the modernization project, and needs to be corrected.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The phrase "2. epistle to the Corinth" is changed to "2nd epistle to the Corinthians." While technically correct, the original's abbreviation is stylistically consistent with the period and loses a touch of the original's feel.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "S. John" should be "St. John."
[FINAL]
Now does he set forth gallantly every work of this congregation, and commends five most notable gifts or brightest virtues.[note 12.9] First, Charity, which encompasses the love of God and our neighbor: whereby it is brought to pass that we prefer nothing in the world before God, neither harm our neighbor, but rather heap upon him all duties and benefits. This we owe to God and all our brethren in the congregation. Of Charity is spoken elsewhere most abundantly, as in the gospel and epistle of St. John. Secondly, he praises Diaconian,[note 12.10] that is, the Ministry. The which may be explained two ways. For either he understands, as Arethas supposes, ministries toward the poor and needy, that is to wit duties and pains taken about the poor, by leading, relieving, succoring, speaking faithfully in their cause, in giving them meat, drink, clothing, and visiting them. For so this word Diaconia is used in the 2. epistle to the Corinth. &c. Or else he means the ministry of the word, by which in teaching, exhorting, comforting, and rebuking, we advance greatly God’s glory and the health of souls. The Thyatirenians were doubtless diligent in either of both. And accuse us grievously, which addict to our own affairs, do neglect our poor brethren: who finally make the ministry of God’s word odious, by our railing and slandering, especially with them that be ignorant as yet, and have heard nothing of God’s word.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 12.11]He comendeth also faith in the Thyaterians. Thomas of Aquine in his comentary upon this book admoniſſheth that faith comes not of Charity, because it is food set here in the first place: but that Charity and good works spring of faith? And the John hath recited charity before faith, for that faith hath her estimation of Charity and works. How be it howe so ever it is faith seems here not so much to be take for trust in God, as for fidelity and truth and promise kept. For faithfulness beautifies all other gifts. Admit that thou haste men ſeruautes and maid ſeruautes that are lucky enough in doing of their things, but imagine that the self same be in the mean time untrusty, slipper and diſceiptefull: what shall avail I pray thee, that they be furniſſhed with sundry gifts? Imagine again that a preacher or Senator be not so furnished with wisdom and experience of matters, but yet to be nevertheless faithful, and with all his heart to doe all things uprightly, and to favour the just cause: shall not fidelity here supply his want? Great therefore is faith, that is, fidelity, and verity: That not with out cause the Apostle required this of the ministers in the.14. chap. of the first Epistle to the Corinthians, saying: That same is chieflye required of Stewards, that a man be found trusty. This faith is also required of us at this day, this faith, good brethren is rare: And therefore have evils overflowed every where. Let us heartily pray to the Lord, that he will grant us the same, and that we may expulſe out of our breasts unfaithfulness, and diſceiptfulnes.
[DRAFT]
He also commends faith to the Thyatireans. Thomas Aquinas in his commentary on this book admonishes that faith does not come from charity, because it is placed here first; but that charity and good works spring from faith? And John has recited charity before faith, because faith has its estimation of charity and works. However, it seems here that faith is not so much to be taken as trust in God, but as fidelity, truth, and promises kept. For faithfulness beautifies all other gifts. Suppose you have servants, male and female, who are fortunate in doing their tasks, but at the same time are untrustworthy, slippery, and deceitful: what benefit would there be in them being furnished with various gifts? Suppose again that a preacher or senator is not furnished with wisdom and experience of matters, but is nevertheless faithful, and with all his heart does all things uprightly, and favors the just cause: will not fidelity here supply his lack? Great therefore is faith, that is, fidelity and truth. Not without cause the Apostle required this of ministers in chapter 14 of the first Epistle to the Corinthians, saying: That which is chiefly required of stewards is that a man be found trustworthy. This faith is also required of us at this day. This faith, good brethren, is rare; and therefore evils have overflowed everywhere. Let us heartily pray to the Lord that he will grant it to us, and that we may expel from our hearts unfaithfulness and deceitfulness.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "faith seems here not so much to be take for trust in God, as for fidelity and truth and promise kept" shifts the nuance. The original isn't *rejecting* trust in God, but suggesting a broader understanding of "faith" in this context. The draft's phrasing is overly definitive.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "what benefit would there be in them being furnished with various gifts?" is an interpretive addition. The original simply poses the question.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing regarding the relationship between faith and charity is complex and open to multiple readings. The draft's simplification ("faith does not come from charity") loses this ambiguity and presents a potentially inaccurate interpretation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are present.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "what benefit would there be in them being furnished with various gifts?" is not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** Austen → Augustine is missing. It should be Augustine.
[FINAL]
He also commends faith to the Thyatireans. Thomas Aquinas in his commentary upon this book admoniſſheth that faith comes not of Charity, because it is food set here in the first place: but that Charity and good works spring of faith? And John hath recited charity before faith, for that faith hath her estimation of Charity and works. How be it howe so ever it is faith seems here not so much to be taken for trust in God, as for fidelity and truth and promise kept. For faithfulness beautifies all other gifts. Admit that thou haste men ſeruautes and maid ſeruautes that are lucky enough in doing of their things, but imagine that the self same be in the mean time untrusty, slipper and diſceiptefull: what shall avail I pray thee, that they be furniſſhed with sundry gifts? Imagine again that a preacher or Senator be not so furnished with wisdom and experience of matters, but yet to be nevertheless faithful, and with all his heart to doe all things uprightly, and to favour the just cause: shall not fidelity here supply his want? Great therefore is faith, that is, fidelity, and verity: That not with out cause the Apostle required this of the ministers in the.14. chap. of the first Epistle to the Corinthians, saying: That same is chieflye required of Stewards, that a man be found trusty. [ORIGINAL]
Hereunto is added patience,[note 12.12] which is praised also in the former churches. Which is a necessary virtue. For impatientnes causes us to murmur and grudge against God, that we stand not strong in the confession of faith, whilst we refuse to suffer patiently such things as the ennemies of faith threaten to put us to. But why defileſt thou thy self with theft? Why run thou into the wars of a foreign prince? Why doest thou practise usury and bawdry? For because thou lackeſt patience in thy poverty, which thou wilt releue with wicked doings.
[DRAFT]
Hereunto is added patience, which is praised also in the former churches. This is a necessary virtue. For impatience causes us to murmur and grudge against God, and prevents us from standing firm in the confession of faith, while we refuse to suffer patiently the things that the enemies of faith threaten to inflict upon us. But why do you defile yourself with theft? Why do you rush into the wars of a foreign prince? Why do you practice usury and lewdness? For you lack patience in your poverty, which you seek to relieve with wicked deeds.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Whilst we refuse to suffer patiently such things as the ennemies of faith threaten to put us to" is altered to "while we refuse to suffer patiently the things that the enemies of faith threaten to inflict upon us." The original phrasing implies a passive acceptance of suffering *imposed* by enemies; the draft introduces a more active element of *infliction*.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "But why do you rush into the wars..." is more interpretative than "Why doest thou run thou into..." The original is a direct question; the draft adds a sense of urgency and condemnation.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "lackeſt patience in thy poverty, which thou wilt releue with wicked doings" is altered to "lack patience in your poverty, which you seek to relieve with wicked deeds." The original suggests a future action ("wilt relieve"); the draft changes it to a present, ongoing action ("seek to relieve"). This diminishes the sense of a potential choice.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this passage, so no issues here.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this passage.
[FINAL]
Hereunto is added patience, which is praised also in the former churches. This is a necessary virtue. For impatience causes us to murmur and grudge against God, and prevents us from standing firm in the confession of faith, while we refuse to suffer patiently the things that the enemies of faith threaten to inflict upon us. But why do you defile yourself with theft? Why do you rush into the wars of a foreign prince? Why do you practice usury and lewdness? For you lack patience in your poverty, which you seek to relieve with wicked deeds.

It becomes not the Godly to stand still at a stay, and not to proceed in Godliness.

And most shame of all it is, to be ever the longer the worse. As the finger, the longer the less: Which is objected to children in schools that learn nothing. Let us be ashamed of our ſloughtfulnes. &c. Let us I say way these things diligently in our minds: and think oft, that God allows them, requires them, and that they be the true seals of the faithful walking in the verity, and of such as boast of faith only a vain name without the thing. If thou feel thy self not to be utterly void of these gifts, praise God: and know that none of all these things is of thy self, but of grace: And pray for the increase of these gifts. If thou be destitute of these virtues, morn and lament before the Lord, humbly axe him forgeuenes, and require the abundance of Gods gifts.
[DRAFT]
And most shame of all it is to become worse the longer one endures. As a finger, the longer it is, the less it is. This is what is pointed out to children in schools who learn nothing. [ORIGINAL]
[note 12.14]In the second place he reproves some things in the same congregation, namely that they permitted Iezabel to teach &c. That same he calls small, not that the doctrine of Iezabel of it self is little, but foraſmuch as though it be found in others, rather then in the church, yet did the church suffer it more gently, that is to say, did not persecute it with greater severity. But of this phrase of speech I have spoken also before.[note 12.15] We allow not the shameful acts of Iezabel nor coent not to the same: But when we might let the by more severe punishment, we permit the to abound and increase. Albeit therefore there be many goodly gifts in us: Yet hath the Lord the same against us, that we suffer vngodlines to reign.
[DRAFT]
Secondly, he rebukes certain things within that same congregation, namely that they allowed Jezebel to teach, and so forth. He calls this a minor matter, not that Jezebel’s doctrine is inherently insignificant, but because, though it is found among others, the church tolerated it more leniently; that is, did not punish it with greater severity. Regarding this phrasing, I have spoken of it previously.

We do not condone Jezebel’s shameful acts, nor do we approve of them. But when we might have imposed a more severe punishment, we permitted them to flourish and increase. Although, therefore, we possess many good gifts, the Lord is displeased with us because we allow ungodliness to prevail.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "That same he calls small" altered to "He calls this a minor matter" loses the nuance of the original's qualification – the doctrine isn't inherently small, but its presence *within* the church is considered minor due to the church's tolerance.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Regarding this phrasing, I have spoken of it previously" is an unnecessary addition. The original simply ends the sentence.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "did not persecute it with greater severity" is softened to "did not punish it with greater severity," potentially diminishing the original's implied criticism of the congregation's lack of action.
PROPER NOUNS: Jezebel is correctly modernized from Iezabel.
NO ISSUES
[FINAL]
Secondly, he rebukes certain things within that same congregation, namely that they allowed Jezebel to teach, and so forth. He calls this a minor matter, not that Jezebel’s doctrine is inherently insignificant, but because, though it is found among others, the church tolerated it more leniently; that is, did not punish it with greater severity. Regarding this phrasing, I have spoken of it previously.

We do not condone Jezebel’s shameful acts, nor do we approve of them. But when we might have imposed a more severe punishment, we permitted them to flourish and increase. Although, therefore, we possess many good gifts, the Lord is displeased with us because we allow ungodliness to prevail.

[ORIGINAL]
But in case the Lord blames that same permission, howe much more blame worthy suppose we the wickedness it self to be, I mean, the Iezabeliſme? Which how vile and filthy it is, I will briefly declare.
[DRAFT]
But if the Lord should condemn that very allowance, how much more blameworthy would the wickedness itself be, that is, the Jezebelic practice? And how base and filthy it is, I will briefly explain.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "howe much more blame worthy suppose we" is softened to "how much more blameworthy would...be." The original phrasing implies a more active judgment being placed upon the wickedness, while the draft is more passive.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Jezebeliſme" is rendered as "Jezebelic practice." While not wholly inaccurate, "practice" adds a layer of interpretation that wasn't explicitly present in the original. It implies a specific set of actions, whereas the original term could encompass a broader range of behaviors and beliefs associated with Jezebel.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "Iezabeliſme" is a somewhat vague term, likely referring to a cluster of perceived heresies or immoral behaviors. The draft’s "Jezebelic practice" reduces this ambiguity, potentially narrowing the scope of what the author intended to condemn.
[FINAL]
But if the Lord should condemn that very allowance, how much more blame worthy suppose we the wickedness itself to be, I mean, the Jezebelism? Which how vile and filthy it is, I will briefly declare.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 12.16]In like case as before by the example of Balaam alleged out of the scripture, he cofuted the Nicolaitas, even so at this present he brings forth the example of Iezabel, therewith to confute the Cataphrygians, or Montaniſtes. Arethas understands the whole place of the Nicolaitas, which I dare not agree to by reason of that whole copoſitio of the epistle. I grant that the Motaniſtes were partakers in filthines with the Nicolaitans. But Iezabel hath a peculiar thing by her self.
[DRAFT]
In a similar manner as previously, using the example of Balaam drawn from Scripture, he refuted the Nicolaitans; and now, using the example of Jezebel, he refutes the Cataphrygians, or Montanists. Arethas understands the entire passage concerning the Nicolaitans, which I do not dare to agree with, given the whole composition of the epistle. I grant that the Montanists shared in filthiness with the Nicolaitans. But Jezebel possesses a particular characteristic of her own.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "cofuted" to "refuted" is a minor shift, but "cofute" carries a nuance of disproving by argument, which "refute" doesn't always imply.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "drawn from Scripture" is not present in the original. It's an explanatory addition.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "whole copoſitio of the epistle" is somewhat opaque. The draft's "whole composition of the epistle" clarifies it, but loses some of the original's sense of difficulty or uncertainty.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: "drawn from Scripture" is added content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues. Hierom, Austen, Ireney would need to be checked elsewhere, but these names are not present here.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 12.17]Iezabel, as the sacred History testifies in the iii. and iiii. book of Kings, the xvi. and xvii. Chap. &c. Was the daughter of Hethbahal king of Sidon, which married to Achab, brought in the worshipping of Baal into the kingdom of Israel, building a goodly temple in Samaria, and founding a great college of Baal his priests. For Helias is red to have slain of Baalites.450. even of the kings chapplains, and as it were Canons or prebendaries, and.400. ministers or country chaplains that serves in hills, woods, and groves. The same woman therefore founded this religion and seeks to govern the propheciyng at her pleasure. For pourſewing sore after Helias, she slew very many of the Prophets: Verily for that they would not teach after the womans appetite. Moreover through Baal his religion was augmented whoredom and all vncleanes. King Iehu obiecteth to king Ioram her son the whordoms of his mother. So Iezabel also augmeted the eating of meates offered unto idols, and all idolatry throughout the whole kingdom: Even than also, what time the lord in a solemn sacrifice by miracle in mount Carmel through the ministry of Helias, had declared to that whole Realm that the religion of Baal was most vain and false, and that the religio of thonly God of Israel was most sincere and true: For Iezabel nevertheless persecuted the truth, and established falſhod. Yea moreover she toke upon her gouernemet in civil matters.
[DRAFT]
Jezebel, as sacred history testifies in the third and fourth books of Kings, chapters 16 and 17 and following, was the daughter of Ethbaal, king of Sidon, who married Ahab. She brought the worship of Baal into the kingdom of Israel, building a magnificent temple in Samaria and establishing a large college of Baal’s priests. For Elijah is recorded to have slain 450 Baalites, even of the kings’ chaplains, and as it were canons or prebendaries, and 400 ministers or country chaplains who served in hills, woods, and groves. This woman therefore founded this religion and sought to govern the prophesying at her pleasure. For pursuing fiercely after Elijah, she slew many of the prophets, truly because they would not teach according to the woman’s desire. Moreover, through Baal’s religion, whoredom and all uncleanness increased. King Jehu objects to King Joram, his son, the whoredoms of his mother. So Jezebel also augmented the eating of meats offered unto idols and all idolatry throughout the whole kingdom. Even at that time, when the Lord in a solemn sacrifice by miracle on Mount Carmel through the ministry of Elijah, had declared to that whole realm that the religion of Baal was most vain and false, and that the religion of the only God of Israel was most sincere and true; for Jezebel nevertheless persecuted the truth and established falsehood. Yea, moreover, she took upon herself governance in civil matters.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Helias is red to have slain" changed to "Elijah is recorded to have slain." "Red" implies a report or accusation, adding a layer of potential bias absent in the original. "Recorded" is neutral, potentially softening the original's implication.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "as it were Canons or prebendaries" is softened to "as it were canons or prebendaries." The original's use of "as it were" suggests a comparison, perhaps a slightly mocking one, which is diminished in the draft.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "pourſewing sore after Helias" is changed to "pursuing fiercely after Elijah." "Sore" carries a weight of intensity and suffering not fully captured by "fiercely."
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content is present.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Iezabel" to "Jezebel" is correct. "Helias" to "Elijah" is correct. "Hethbahal" to "Ethbaal" is correct. "Achab" to "Ahab" is correct. "Ioram" to "Joram" is correct. "Iehu" to "Jehu" is correct.
[FINAL]
Jezebel, as sacred history testifies in the third and fourth books of Kings, chapters 16 and 17 and following, was the daughter of Ethbaal, king of Sidon, who married Ahab. She brought the worship of Baal into the kingdom of Israel, building a magnificent temple in Samaria and establishing a large college of Baal’s priests. For Elijah is red to have slain 450 Baalites, even of the kings’ chaplains, and as it were canons or prebendaries, and 400 ministers or country chaplains who served in hills, woods, and groves. This woman therefore founded this religion and sought to govern the prophesying at her pleasure. For pourſewing sore after Elijah, she slew many of the prophets, truly because they would not teach according to the woman’s desire. Moreover, through Baal’s religion, whoredom and all uncleanness increased. King Jehu objects to King Joram, his son, the whoredoms of his mother. So Jezebel also augmented the eating of meats offered unto idols and all idolatry throughout the whole kingdom. Even at that time, when the Lord in a solemn sacrifice by miracle on Mount Carmel through the ministry of Elijah, had declared to that whole realm that the religion of Baal was most vain and false, and that the religion of the only God of Israel was most sincere and true; for Jezebel nevertheless persecuted the truth and established falsehood. [ORIGINAL]
For she usurped the kings seal, and counterfeited letters and sent them in the kings name to put Naboth to death, a right good and innocent man. Such in deed was filthy Iezabel.
[DRAFT]
She forged the king’s seal and fabricated letters, sending them in the king’s name to have Naboth put to death, a righteous and innocent man. Such was Jezebel’s wickedness.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Usurped" implies forceful taking; "forged" is less forceful. While functionally similar, "usurped" carries a stronger connotation of illegitimacy and power grab.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Such was Jezebel’s wickedness" is an interpretive addition. The original simply states a fact ("Such in deed was filthy Iezabel"). The draft adds a judgment.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "filthy Iezabel" is deliberately blunt and shocking. "Jezebel’s wickedness" is a more sanitized and less impactful descriptor.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms present.
5. HALLUCINATION: "wickedness" is not explicitly stated in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Jezebel/Iezabel is correctly modernized.
[FINAL]
