[ORIGINAL]
Now after the example of this defiled woman,[note 12.18] were women in the church of Thyatira which challenged to the selves a prerogative in the religion and teaching in the congregation, taking upon the the spirit of prophecy: whereof they taught in deed but corrupt doctrine seducing the, who God by his doctrine had prepared to be his servants. But these false prophetiſſes, corrupted their minds, and brought forth a new doctrine and prophecy and many things not set forth in the scriptures, but fetched out of their own deueliſh dreams and diſceiptfulnes. And amongst other things they communicated with the Nicolaitans, in whordo, and participating of meates offred to idols. Whereof hath ben spoke before. And the lord seems plainly to speak of the Cataphrygias or Motaniſtes, whose foundation being laid in the time of S. John, after in process of time, and especially in thempire of Antoninus lx. years after the Apocalypse set forth, brake out more strongly and plentifully. They say howe Montanus had prophetiſſes Priſcilla and Maximilla, which had visions, and brought in woderful reuelatios into the church. Of whom Eusebius treats at large in the fifth book of theccleſ. story, Chap. 16. And Epiphanius in the.48. heresy. in Panario. Certes John, or Christ him self by John, going about at the first beginning to pluck up and destroy the rotes of this heresy, by the example of that wicked woman Iezabel, hath co demned that same heresy. The scripture also elsewhere prohibiteth a woman to rule, teach or minister in the congregatio. By and by the Lord him self will confute the new prophecies, when he shall admonish us, that he will reveal none other new kind of doctrine, besides that which he hath co mitted or delivered to his church. Now also fornication, and the eating of meates offered to Idols, are condemned else wherein the scripture most ſeueeely, as before is said.
[DRAFT]
Following the example of this defiled woman,[note 12.18], there were women in the church of Thyatira who asserted authority for themselves in the religion and teaching within the congregation, claiming to have the spirit of prophecy. They taught in fact, but corrupt doctrine, seducing those whom God had prepared by his doctrine to be his servants. But these false prophetesses corrupted their minds and brought forth a new doctrine and prophecy, and many things not found in the scriptures, but drawn from their own devilish dreams and deceitfulness. And among other things, they communed with the Nicolaitans, in word and in participating of meats offered to idols, as has been spoken of before. And the Lord seems plainly to speak of the Cataphrygians or Montanists, whose foundation was laid in the time of Saint John, but after a time, and especially in the empire of Antoninus, sixty years after the Apocalypse was published, broke out more strongly and abundantly. They say how Montanus had prophetesses Priscilla and Maximilla, who had visions and brought in wonderful revelations into the church. Eusebius treats of them at length in the fifth book of ecclesiastical history, chapter 16. And Epiphanius in the 48th heresy, in Panarion. Certainly, John, or Christ himself by John, seeking at the beginning to uproot and destroy the roots of this heresy, by the example of that wicked woman Jezebel, has condemned that same heresy. Scripture also elsewhere prohibits a woman from ruling, teaching, or ministering in the congregation. Immediately, the Lord himself will refute the new prophecies, when he admonishes us that he will reveal no other new kind of doctrine besides that which he has committed or delivered to his church. Now also fornication and the eating of meats offered to idols are condemned elsewhere in scripture most severely, as before is said.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "challenging to the selves a prerogative" changed to "asserted authority for themselves." While conveying a similar idea, "prerogative" carries a nuance of inherent right that "authority" lacks, potentially softening the original's condemnation.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "in whordo" changed to "in word." The original's phrasing, though unusual, might have intended a more specific, perhaps ritualistic, participation. The draft loses this potential specificity.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes detected.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No changes detected.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
[FINAL]

They taught in fact, but corrupt doctrine, seducing those whom God had prepared by his doctrine to be his servants. [ORIGINAL]
But since that those things so afflicted and troubl[illegible] [illegible]he church of God in the time of the Apostles, it is not hard to gether how indiscreet they be, which at this day (as I showed you before) for the hatred of the true religion restored, accuse it of ſectes, which boyle up in such plenty, as though that filthines did prove, that the Gospel that we preach were not the Gospel. For the Gospel that was preached of John and the rest of the Apostles, was the most true and most pure Gospel, how so ever of the false Goſpellers crept up the Nicolaitans, Cataphrygians, and other ſectes innumerable. Where nevertheless the Gospel impugned and condemns all such manner of ſectes, and mainteineth the Christian verity and unity of the catholic church. Praise be to the Lord our God. Amen.
[DRAFT]
But since those things so troubled the church of God in the time of the Apostles, it is not hard to see how indiscreet those are who, as I showed you before, for the sake of suppressing the true religion, accuse it of sects, as though they abounded so greatly that the filthiness proves that the gospel we preach is not the true gospel. For the gospel that was preached by John and the rest of the Apostles was the most true and most pure gospel, even though false gospel proponents like the Nicolaitans, Cataphrygians, and other sects innumerable crept in. Nevertheless, the gospel refutes and condemns all such sects, and maintains the Christian truth and unity of the catholic church. Praise be to the Lord our God. Amen.

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s “filthines did prove” is intentionally ambiguous, allowing for multiple readings about the nature of the perceived problem. The draft’s “the filthiness proves” is more direct and less open to interpretation.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

For the gospel that was preached by John and the rest of the Apostles was the most true and most pure gospel, even though false gospel proponents like the Nicolaitans, Cataphrygians, and other sects innumerable crept in. Nevertheless, the gospel refutes and condemns all such sects, and maintains the Christian truth and unity of the catholic church. Praise be to the Lord our God. Amen.

And all congregations shall know, that I am he which searches the reins and hearts, and I will give unto every one of you according to your works.

[ORIGINAL]
To the former errors and sins of Iezabell he adds an other sin nothing light, to wit,[note 13.1] the abuse and even the contempt of God his long suffering. God doth not by and by and out of hand destroy such as be in error and sins also most grievous. But sinners are wont for the most part to abuse that long animitie of God unto the occaſio and pretence of sinning more impudetly, saying: If god did so much abhor these offences, he had destroyed us long or this: But now he nourishes us benignly, therefore doth he not so greatly miſlike it. But this is an abuse of Gods long suffering. For the Lord saith at this present, I have given Iezabell a time to repent her, and to leave her fornication, and turn to the Lord: How be it she hath not converted. Which thing the Lord takes in most evil part, that his grace should be verily despised and set at naught. Wherefore S. Paul to the Romains. Whether doest thou contemn the riches of God his goodness, long suffering and lenity, knowing not that the goodness of God provokes thee to repentance. &c. If than the Lord hath not suddenly in our sins oppressed us, let us not thereof take unto us a liberty to sin, but let us rather amend. S. Peter saith, the Lord is patient towards us, whilst he will destroy none, but receive all to repentance. ii. Petri. iii. Certes Iezabel her self, when after the death of her husband Achab, and the mortal fall of her son Ocoſias she did not amend, nor within the xii. years of her son Ioram, wherein he is red to have reigned, did repeat her: Felt the wrath of God so much more grievous, for that it was long or it came.
[DRAFT]
To the former errors and sins of Jezebel, he adds another sin, nothing slight, namely, the abuse and even the contempt of God’s long-suffering. God does not immediately destroy those who are in error and sin, even the most grievous. But sinners are usually wont to abuse God’s long patience into an opportunity and pretext for sinning more openly, saying: “If God did so much abhor these offenses, he would have destroyed us long ago. But now he nourishes us kindly, therefore he does not greatly dislike it.” But this is an abuse of God’s long-suffering. For the Lord says at this present, “I have given Jezebel a time to repent of her fornication and turn to the Lord.” However, she has not converted. And the Lord takes this in the worst part, that his grace should be truly despised and set at naught. Therefore, Paul to the Romans: “Do you despise the riches of God’s goodness, his long-suffering and gentleness, not knowing that the goodness of God provokes you to repentance?” And so forth. If then the Lord has not suddenly oppressed us in our sins, let us not take this as license to sin, but rather let us amend. Peter says, “The Lord is patient toward us, not wishing to destroy any, but receiving all to repentance.” 2 Peter 3. Certainly, Jezebel herself, when after the death of her husband Ahab, and the mortal fall of her son Ocozias, she did not amend, nor within the twelve years of her son Joram, wherein he is said to have reigned, did repeat her [illegible]: felt the wrath of God so much more grievous, for that it was long in coming.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "animitie" is modernized to "patience." While related, "animitie" carries a sense of God's forbearance and unwillingness to punish, a nuance lost in "patience."
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The addition of "namely" in "namely, the abuse and even the contempt of God’s long-suffering" injects an interpretive element not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing "long or it came" is softened to "long in coming." The original suggests a delayed, almost inevitable wrath, while the draft diminishes the sense of foreboding.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of significant theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The bracketed "[illegible]" is a note on the original's condition, not added content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be Irenaeus. This is not present in the text, but is a general point to note.
[FINAL]
To the former errors and sins of Jezebel, he adds another sin, nothing slight, to wit,[note 13.1] the abuse and even the contempt of God his long animitie. God doth not by and by destroy such as be in error and sins also most grievous. But sinners are wont for the most part to abuse that long animitie of God unto the occaſio and pretence of sinning more impudetly, saying: “If god did so much abhor these offences, he would have destroyed us long or this: But now he nourishes us benignly, therefore doth he not so greatly miſlike it.” But this is an abuse of Gods long animitie. For the Lord saith at this present, “I have given Iezabell a time to repent her, and to leave her fornication, and turn to the Lord: How be it she hath not converted.” Which thing the Lord takes in most evil part, that his grace should be verily despised and set at naught. Wherefore S. Paul to the Romains: Whether doest thou contemn the riches of God his goodness, long suffering and lenity, knowing not that the goodness of God provokes thee to repentance. &c. [ORIGINAL]
And in the text following the Lord Jesus in deed threatens most grievously the Iezabelines, that is to wit,[note 13.2] the Cataphrigians or Montaniſtes, unless they will yet repeat in time. For he opens again the gates of his grace to the penitent, reciting, how he will plague the impenitet. Whereby verily he assays to drive them into repentance by threatenings. For in reciting the kinds or degrees of puniſhementes, he shows also diverse kinds of them that be in errors, and declares to every one his judgment, which they may by repentance eschew. And he is thought to have rehearsed those kinds for this consideration, least any man happily should think him self giltles and free, in case he be never so little partaker with Iezabel.
[DRAFT]
And in the text following the Lord Jesus severely threatens the followers of Jezebel—that is to say, the Cataphrygians or Montanists—unless they repent. For he opens again the gates of his grace to the penitent, recounting how he will punish the impenitent. Thus, he seeks to drive them to repentance through threats. By recounting the kinds or degrees of punishments, he also shows diverse kinds of those who are in error, and declares to each his judgment, which they may avoid through repentance. And it is thought that he rehearsed those kinds for this consideration, lest any man should happily think himself blameless and free, even if he is in the smallest measure partaker with Jezebel.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "most grievously" softened to "severely." While not a drastic change, it diminishes the original's intensity.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Thus, he seeks to drive them to repentance through threats" is interpretive; the original states he *assays* to do so, implying an attempt rather than a guaranteed outcome.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "giltles" is modernized to "blameless." "Giltles" carries a nuance of guilt and responsibility that "blameless" lacks.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And in the text following the Lord Jesus severely threatens the followers of Jezebel—that is to say, the Cataphrygians or Montanists—unless they repent. For he opens again the gates of his grace to the penitent, recounting how he will punish the impenitent. Thus, he seeks to drive them to repentance through threats. By recounting the kinds or degrees of punishments, he also shows diverse kinds of those who are in error, and declares to each his judgment, which they may avoid through repentance. And it is thought that he rehearsed those kinds for this consideration, lest any man should happily think himself blameless and free, even if he is in the smallest measure partaker with Jezebel.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 13.3]First the Lord threatens Iezabel her self, that he will cast her into a bed. He speaks of the first authors of the evil and of the heresy, upon whom he menaces to send a sickness. For the bed in many times in the scripture taken for the very diseases wherewith they be vexed that lie in bed. And we Germans say, that he is taken with a most grievous and deadly disease. And the Lord plageth the archheretickes with sickness of body and soul. In the mean ſeaſo also he weakens the force of therrour, to the intent it might by little and little vanish away.
[DRAFT]
[note 13.3]First, the Lord threatens Jezebel herself, that he will cast her into a bed. He speaks of the first authors of the evil and of the heresy, upon whom he menaces to send a sickness. For the bed in many times in the scripture is taken for the very diseases with which those who lie in bed are afflicted. And we Germans say that it signifies a most grievous and deadly disease. And the Lord afflicts the archheretics with sickness of body and soul. In the meantime also, he weakens the force of their terror, so that it might by little and little vanish away.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "taken for the very diseases wherewith they be vexed that lie in bed" is softened to "is taken for the very diseases with which those who lie in bed are afflicted." While subtle, the original phrasing carries a more direct, almost visceral connection between the bed and the illness.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: "And we Germans say that it signifies a most grievous and deadly disease" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original simply states "And we Germans say, that he is taken with a most grievous and deadly disease." The addition of "it signifies" introduces an explanatory element.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing regarding the German saying is less definitive. The draft's "it signifies" makes the connection between the bed and disease more explicit and less open to alternative interpretations.
[FINAL]
[note 13.3]First, the Lord threatens Jezebel herself, that he will cast her into a bed. He speaks of the first authors of the evil and of the heresy, upon whom he menaces to send a sickness. For the bed in many times in the scripture is taken for the very diseases wherewith they be vexed that lie in bed. And we Germans say, that he is taken with a most grievous and deadly disease. And the Lord afflicts the archheretics with sickness of body and soul. In the meantime also he weakens the force of their terror, to the intent it might by little and little vanish away.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 13.4]Secondly he threatens great affliction to such as have to do with Iezabel: that is to say, to such as cleave unto false doctrine, receive errors, delight in heresies, and go about to set forth the same. To these, I say, he threatens most grievous afflictios, to wit of body and soul, of this present and of the life to come. He seems to have said somewhat more, than if he had recited certain kinds of punishment.
[DRAFT]

To these, I say, he threatens most grievous afflictions, namely of body and soul, both in this present life and in the life to come. He seems to have said somewhat more than if he had merely recited certain kinds of punishment.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "go about to set forth the same" is softened to "seek to promote them." While similar, "set forth" implies a more active and potentially public dissemination, which the draft's "promote" diminishes.
2. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original's "To these, I say" is unnecessary and slightly awkward. Its removal in the draft isn't inherently problematic, but it eliminates a subtle rhetorical flourish.
3. **No Issues with Theological Terms:** No theological terms are present in this short excerpt.
4. **Hallucination:** No content is added.
5. **Proper Nouns:** Jezebel is correctly maintained.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 13.5]Finally he menaces death to the children borne of this copulation and fornicatio, to wit whore sons, and bastards. And those are chiefly the children of heretics, which ſtire up a fresh, and restore newly again, heresies already condened, meakened, and wearing away. These the Lord diſtroied with temporal and eternal death. And the ecclesiastical story doth testify, that the Lord hath in deed punished most grievously not only the heresy of the Cataphrigias, but all heresies in general. And certain things cocerning the Cataphrygias or Mo taniſtes, are touched of Euſeb. lib. 1. of the cl. story, Cap. 16.
[DRAFT]
Finally, he threatens death to the children born of this union and fornication, namely, illegitimate sons and bastards. These are chiefly the children of heretics, who stir up new and restore previously condemned, weakened, and fading heresies. The Lord destroyed these with temporal and eternal death. And church history testifies that the Lord has indeed severely punished not only the heresy of the Cataphrigias but all heresies in general. Certain things concerning the Cataphrigias or Montanists are addressed by Eusebius, book 1 of the ecclesiastical history, chapter 16.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "meakened, and wearing away" is softened to "weakened, and fading," diminishing the sense of decline and decay inherent in the original.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "namely, illegitimate sons and bastards" adds a layer of explanation not present in the original. While the meaning is clear, the explicit clarification is interpretive.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "ſtire up a fresh" is rendered as "stir up new." "Fresh" implies novelty and suddenness, a nuance lost in "new."
PROPER NOUNS: Eusebius is correctly modernized.
NO ISSUES
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 13.6]The Lord seems here to me to have alluded to the old story of Iezabel and Achab for them, as it were cast in a bed, fro day to day, ever since they began to worship Baal, he vexed with sickness, and brought them lowe: And the people that received the religion of Baal, be put to much sorrow, evils and afflictions. Finally their children he brought to a shameful death. Their partakers also were slain, that would have had Baal his religion, safe and sound, and even to have been restored again. For after the death of Achab his father, not many days after. Ochoſias the son of Achab and Iezabel, bruised with an unhappy fall and cast in bed, died iiii. Reg. Cap. 1. And Ioram an other son of Achab and Iezabel, stricken through with an arrow of Iehu was slain. Athalia the daughter of Achab and Iezabel, the wife of Ioram king of Iuda, the son of Ioſaphat, being divided with that swore of Ioiadas, fell down before the gates of the temple. And Ochoſias king of Iuda, the son of Athalia and Ioram, was slain also by the power of Iehu. And after were put to death by the same Iehu, the lxx. sons of Achab: And all the priſtes of Baal are slain together in the temple, and before the Altar of Baal, and not one of so great a number escaped. Yea the temple, the idol, and the service of Baal were quite and clean overthrown. This old maruelouſe, and wonderful history the Lord calls to memory, ſignifiyng that he lives yet a revenger, and a puniſher: Who will neither overpass the just limit, nor touche the same out of time. For he adds and all congregations shall know. &c.
[DRAFT]
The Lord seems to me here to have alluded to the old story of Jezebel and Ahab for them, as it were cast in a bed, from day to day, ever since they began to worship Baal; he vexed them with sickness and brought them low. And the people that received the doctrine of Baal were put to much sorrow, evils, and afflictions. Finally, their children he brought to a shameful death. Their partakers also were slain, those who would have had Baal’s religion safe and sound, and even restored again. For after the death of Ahab his father, not many days after, Jehoaz the son of Ahab and Jezebel, bruised with an unhappy fall and cast in bed, died (2 Kings 1:4). And Jehoram, another son of Ahab and Jezebel, stricken through with an arrow of Jehu, was slain. Athalia, the daughter of Ahab and Jezebel, the wife of Jehoram king of Judah, the son of Jehoshaphat, being divided with that oath of Joaz, fell down before the gates of the temple. And Jehoaz king of Judah, the son of Athalia and Jehoram, was slain also by the power of Jehu. And after were put to death by the same Jehu, the seventy sons of Ahab. And all the priests of Baal were slain together in the temple, and before the altar of Baal, and not one of so great a number escaped. Yea, the temple, the idol, and the service of Baal were quite and clean overthrown. This old marvelous and wonderful history the Lord calls to memory, signifying that he lives yet a revenger and a punisher, who will neither overpass the just limit, nor touch the same out of time. For he adds, “and all congregations shall know.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Ochoſias" is consistently rendered as "Jehoaz," a significant error as they are distinct individuals. This alters the narrative sequence and familial relationships.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "(2 Kings 1:4)" is an interpretive addition, explicitly referencing a biblical verse. While helpful for some readers, it's an unwarranted editorial insertion.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s vagueness regarding Joaz’s oath is clarified in the draft, potentially removing a layer of intended mystery or open interpretation.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Ioſaphat" is incorrectly modernized as "Jehoshaphat" multiple times.
5. PROPER NOUNS: "Ochoſias" should be modernized as "Ahaziah."
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Ioram" is inconsistently modernized as "Jehoram." Maintain consistency.
[FINAL]
The Lord seems to me here to have alluded to the old story of Jezebel and Ahab for them, as it were cast in a bed, from day to day, ever since they began to worship Baal; he vexed them with sickness and brought them low. [ORIGINAL]
Notwithstanding this thing is notable and most worthy to bee remembered, and no less full of comfort,[note 13.7] that in this rehearsal of puniſhementes, he puts in as it were in the midst a mention of repentance, as though he should say, let no man think him self that he must be destroyed and perish through a certain fatal necessity. For if any will repent, the gates of the grace of God are set open, his sins shall be forgeuen, and he shall be taken in to favour, and shall be delivered from all those evils? And after this sort have taught also the Prophets, Ieremy in the xviii. Chapter, and Ezech. in the xviii. Chapter.
[DRAFT]

For if any will repent, the gates of the grace of God are set open, his sins shall be forgiven, and he shall be taken into favor, and shall be delivered from all those evils. And in this manner the Prophets have also taught, Jeremiah in chapter eighteen, and Ezekiel in chapter eighteen.

3. PROPER NOUNS: "Ieremy" changed to "Jeremiah." While technically a correction, the original spelling is a key marker of the period and its modernization should be handled with care. Consistency in modernization is needed.
4. LOST AMBIGUITY: No significant ambiguity is lost.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 13.10]Neither doth Christ know only all the thoughts of men whatsoever they be, but gives moreover to every man after his own works. And so the Apostle S. Paul teaches saying: The just judgment of God shall be opened, which will reward every man according to his deeds, that is to say: Praise, honour, and immortality, to them which continue in well doing, and seek eternal life: But unto them that are rebelliouſe and disobey the truth, and follow iniquity, shall come indignation, and wrath, tribulatio, and angwiſh. &c. In the ii. to the Roma. For works be the touchstones of faith and infidelity: And works whether they be good or evil, be judged of God and the Godly men, according as they proceed of faith, or of infidelity. Therefore what so ever any of us shall sow, the same shall he reap also. For God is the most just rewarder of good, and revenger of evil. The same sentence as it is most true, so is it the foundation of the true and Godly Religion. Glory be to God.
[DRAFT]
Nor does Christ know only all the thoughts of men, whatever they may be, but also gives to each man according to his own works. And so the Apostle Paul teaches, saying: “The just judgment of God will be revealed, which will reward each man according to his deeds—praise, honor, and immortality to those who continue in good works and seek eternal life, but indignation and wrath, tribulation, and anguish to those who are rebellious and disobedient to the truth, and follow iniquity.” This is in the second letter to the Romans. For works are the tests of faith and infidelity. And works, whether good or evil, are judged by God and by godly men, according to whether they proceed from faith or from infidelity. Therefore, whatever any of us sows, that he will also reap. For God is the just rewarder of good and the revenger of evil. This statement, as it is most true, is the foundation of the true and godly religion. Glory be to God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "touchstones of faith and infidelity" is subtly altered to "tests of faith and infidelity." While related, "touchstones" implies a more discerning, critical evaluation than "tests" suggests.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of quotation marks around Paul's words ("The just judgment of God will be revealed...") introduces an interpretive layer, implying a direct quote rather than a paraphrase, which the original does not.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original uses "&c." indicating continuation of the list. The draft abruptly ends the list, removing the original's implication of further consequences.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: S. Paul is correctly modernized to Paul. Hierom, Austen, and Ireney are not present in this passage.
[FINAL]

## ¶ That the doctrine of pity is so fully ſ [illegible][illegible] forth to the church, that there needs no new Revelations. And of the most large promeſſes of Christ made unto the church. The.xiiij. Sermon.

[ORIGINAL]
ANd to you I say, and to others that be of Thiatira: Who so ever have not this doctrine, and which have not known the depenes of Satan, as they say, I will put upon you none other burden, but that which the have already: Hold fast till I come. And whosoever ouercometh and kepeth my works unto the end, to him will I give power over nations, and he shall rule them with a rod of Iron: And as the vessels of a potter shall he break the to ſheuers. Even as I received of my father, so will I give him the morning star. Let him that hath ears, hear what the spirit says to the congregations.

LOST AMBIGUITY: "to the ſheuers" changed to "to pieces" - "ſheuers" is archaic for "shewers" or "showers," potentially referring to a display or revealing of something. "To pieces" is a more straightforward, less evocative interpretation.

[ORIGINAL]
He speaks here now to the Cataphrygians,[note 14.1] and also to the faithful of the church of Thyatira that believed rightly in Christ, and heals their diseases. Wherein appears the unspeakable mercy of God, which cease not to speak unto such as are yet entangled with heresy, and to heal their peſtiferous diseases. And he admonishes all men, that they look for no new revelations, but know rather that God hath through Christ and his Apostles set forth a most perfect doctrine, whereunto he will add nothing: And therefore that they keep fast in memory such things as they had learned already, and wherein they were now exercised.[note 14.2] For the Cataphrigians, called also Montaniſtes, bragged of a new comforter, and a new revelation: As though all things had not ben fully set forth by the Apostles, but that many things were left as yet to be revealed of them. As also at this day the maynteiners of the Popiſhe church most stiffly do affirm. And like as the Cataphrygians covered their trifles under the pretence of the holy ghost: So do the Papists likewise cloak the vain constitutions of men, and set the forth under a false colour of the holy ghost. As though the Lord spake of their decrees, when he said: I have yet many things to say unto you, which now the can not bear. Nevertheless the faithful people of Thyatira, which had not the doctrine of Iezabell, but rather detested it, notwithstanding as doubtful, said, that the Devil was a certain depenes, and had a thousand crafts, which could also transform him into an angel of light: And that they were but simple men, who being ignorant of these his wonderful crafts and ſubtilties, knew not what they might chiefly follow, whilst the false Prophets also make their boast of the holy ghost, and shine in miracles, and with great constancy avouch their doctrine to be true. Ye shall find at this day which will say, I am a plain simple man, and know not whether part I should cleave to, since the doctors of both parts affirm with great costa cie that they have the truth on their side, and therefore will some say, they shall agree better or ever I will believe any of the all. &c.
[DRAFT]
He speaks now to the Cataphrygians,[note 14.1] and also to the faithful of the church of Thyatira who rightly believed in Christ, and heals their diseases. In this appears the unspeakable mercy of God, which does not cease to speak to those still entangled with heresy, and to heal their pestiferous diseases. And he admonishes all men that they look for no new revelations, but know rather that God through Christ and his Apostles has set forth a most perfect doctrine, to which he will add nothing; and therefore that they keep fast in memory such things as they had already learned and in which they were now exercised.[note 14.2] For the Cataphrygians, called also Montanists, boasted of a new comforter and a new revelation, as though all things had not been fully set forth by the Apostles, but that many things were left yet to be revealed by them. As also at this day the maintainers of the Popish church most stiffly affirm. And just as the Cataphrygians covered their trifles under the pretense of the Holy Spirit, so do the Papists likewise cloak the vain constitutions of men and set them forth under a false color of the Holy Spirit, as though the Lord spoke of their decrees when he said, “I have yet many things to say unto you, which now you cannot bear.” Nevertheless, the faithful people of Thyatira, who did not have the doctrine of Jezebel but rather detested it as doubtful, said that the Devil was a certain dependence [?] and had a thousand crafts, which could also transform him into an angel of light; and that they were but simple men, who, being ignorant of his wonderful crafts and subtleties, did not know what they should chiefly follow, while the false prophets also make their boast of the Holy Spirit, and shine in miracles, and with great constancy avouch their doctrine to be true. You shall find at this day those who will say, “I am a plain simple man, and know not whether part I should cleave to, since the doctors of both parts affirm with great cost [?] that they have the truth on their side, and therefore will some say, they shall agree better ere I will believe any of them.” &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "depenes" changed to "dependence [?]" alters the original's more archaic and potentially more specific meaning. The original suggests a state of being dependent or reliant, whereas "dependence" is more general.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "costa cie" changed to "cost [?]" loses the original's slightly unusual phrasing which might have been intended to emphasize the expense or effort involved in the doctors' claims.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Iezabell" should be "Jezebel."
4. **HALLUCINATION:** The bracketed question marks after "dependence" and "cost" are not present in the original. They indicate uncertainty in the modernization process, but are not part of the original text.
[FINAL]
He speaks now to the Cataphrygians,[note 14.1] and also to the faithful of the church of Thyatira who rightly believed in Christ, and heals their diseases. In this appears the unspeakable mercy of God, which does not cease to speak to those still entangled with heresy, and to heal their pestiferous diseases. And he admonishes all men that they look for no new revelations, but know rather that God through Christ and his Apostles has set forth a most perfect doctrine, to which he will add nothing; and therefore that they keep fast in memory such things as they had already learned and in which they were now exercised.[note 14.2] For the Cataphrygians, called also Montanists, boasted of a new comforter and a new revelation, as though all things had not been fully set forth by the Apostles, but that many things were left yet to be revealed by them. As also at this day the maintainers of the Popish church most stiffly affirm. And just as the Cataphrygians covered their trifles under the pretense of the Holy Spirit, so do the Papists likewise cloak the vain constitutions of men and set them forth under a false color of the Holy Spirit, as though the Lord spoke of their decrees when he said, “I have yet many things to say unto you, which now you cannot bear.” Nevertheless, the faithful people of Thyatira, who did not have the doctrine of Jezebel but rather detested it as doubtful, said that the Devil was a certain depenes and had a thousand crafts, which could also transform him into an angel of light; and that they were but simple men, who, being ignorant of his wonderful crafts and subtleties, did not know what they should chiefly follow, while the false prophets also make their boast of the Holy Spirit, and shine in miracles, and with great constancy avouch their doctrine to be true. You shall find at this day those who will say, “I am a plain simple man, and know not whether part I should cleave to, since the doctors of both parts affirm with great costa cie that they have the truth on their side, and therefore will some say, they shall agree better ere I will believe any of them.” &c.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.3]The Lord therefore answering to both, shows what they should do: To you, saith he, I say that follow the doctrine of Iezabel, I say also to the rest of the Thyatiremas, that follow not the Iezabeliſme, yet nevertheless complain in such diſſentions, and wonderful crafts of the devil, that they se not what is best: To you all I say, if the be simple in deed, as you pretend, if the will with all your heart embrace the truth, give your selves to the simplicity Apoſtolical, cleaving fast to such things as you have once learned of the Apostles, neither looking for nor receiving any new religions, or additions, conſtitucions or any other thing moreover, than that you have learned of the Apostles. For these things which you have received are sufficient to obtain salvation.
[DRAFT]
The Lord, therefore, answering both parties, shows what they should do. To you, he says, who follow the doctrine of Jezebel, I say also to the rest of the Thyatirans, who do not follow Jezebel’s doctrine, yet nevertheless complain in such disagreements and wondrous schemes of the devil, that they do not see what is best: To you all I say, if you are simple in deed, as you claim, if you will with all your heart embrace the truth, devote yourselves to the simplicity of the apostolic faith, cleaving fast to those things you have once learned from the Apostles, neither seeking nor receiving any new religions, or additions, constitutions, or anything else moreover, than that which you have learned from the Apostles. For these things which you have received are sufficient to obtain salvation.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Iezabeliſme" is rendered as "Jezebel’s doctrine." While functionally similar, the original term carries a specific theological weight referring to a system or ideology, not just the actions of Jezebel.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "apostolic faith" is added. The original uses "Apoſtolical" modifying "simplicity," implying a return to the simple, original practices of the apostles, not necessarily a formalized "faith."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's vagueness regarding the nature of the "disſentions" and "crafts of the devil" is diminished by the draft's use of "disagreements" and "wondrous schemes." The original allows for a broader range of interpretation regarding the source of the conflict.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Iezabel" is modernized to "Jezebel." Correct. "Iezabeliſme" is not modernized. See point 1.
5. NO ISSUES
[FINAL]
The Lord, therefore, answering both parties, shows what they should do. To you, he says, who follow the doctrine of Jezebel, I say also to the rest of the Thyatirans, who do not follow Jezebel’s doctrine, yet nevertheless complain in such disagreements and wondrous schemes of the devil, that they do not see what is best: To you all I say, if you are simple in deed, as you claim, if you will with all your heart embrace the truth, devote yourselves to the simplicity of the apostolic faith, cleaving fast to those things you have once learned from the Apostles, neither seeking nor receiving any new religions, or additions, constitutions, or anything else moreover, than that which you have learned from the Apostles. For these things which you have received are sufficient to obtain salvation.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.4]And these words of the Lord must be weighed more diligently, to the end we may perceive the great fruicte that is in the [illegible]: that is I will lay upon you none other weight or burthe, besides this that you have. The Lord affirms that he will add nothing more to the doctrine evangelical set forth by the Apostles, as to that which is most perfect. Certes if the doctrine of Moiſes were so perfect, that the Lord him self did prohibit, that no man should add or take away any thing fro the same, but only should doe that which was commanded, as we read in the.4. and.12. Chap. of Deuteronomy. Who would doubt that there should want any thing in the doctrine of Christ the son of God? He therefore now affirms, that he will lay nothing upon them, more then he had laid, and the which they bear at that time.
[DRAFT]
And these words of the Lord must be weighed more diligently, so that we may perceive the great benefit in them: that is, I will lay upon you no other weight or burden besides what you already have. The Lord affirms that he will add nothing more to the evangelical doctrine set forth by the Apostles, as to that which is most perfect. Certainly, if the doctrine of Moses were so perfect that the Lord himself prohibited anyone from adding or taking away anything from it, but only doing what was commanded, as we read in the fourth and twelfth chapters of Deuteronomy. Who would doubt that the doctrine of Christ, the Son of God, would be lacking in anything? He therefore now affirms that he will lay nothing upon them more than he had already laid, and which they were bearing at that time.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "fruicte" changed to "benefit." While related, "fruicte" implies a more substantial, potentially spiritual reward than "benefit" suggests.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "so that" after "diligently" adds a layer of explanation not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "[illegible]" is removed, eliminating the original's ambiguity. While a modern editor might attempt to reconstruct it, its absence is a loss.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]

The Lord affirms that he will add nothing more to the doctrine evangelical set forth by the Apostles, as to that which is most perfect. Certes if the doctrine of Moiſes were so perfect, that the Lord him self did prohibit, that no man should add or take away any thing fro the same, but only should doe that which was commanded, as we read in the.4. and.12. Chap. of Deuteronomy. Who would doubt that there should want any thing in the doctrine of Christ the son of God? He therefore now affirms, that he will lay nothing upon them, more then he had laid, and the which they bear at that time.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.5]A burden in the ſermons of the Prophets is take for doctrine of grave and weighty matters. The Apostles also call the law a yocke and burden. Where therefore the Lord saith that he will not lay upon the church any other burden, he saith howe he will not reveal any other doctrine, nor further charge the, with other rites or ceremonies, than such as he had ordained and imposed already. And with these words of Christ accord those things very well, which are red in thapoſtles epistle Sinodical Act xv. For by the common consent of the congregation, and after the mind of the holy ghost, they say they will impose nothing moreover upon the church, than such things as they had received already of S. Paul, and a few things that they added for a declaration of the same. Wherupo S. Paul said to the Galath. If an angel from heaven preach unto you an other Gospel, besides that which is preached, let him be accursed.
[DRAFT]
A burden in the sermons of the Prophets is taken as doctrine concerning grave and weighty matters. The Apostles also call the law a yoke and burden. Therefore, when the Lord says that he will not lay any other burden upon the church, he means that he will not reveal any other doctrine, nor further charge them with other rites or ceremonies, than those he had already ordained and imposed. These words of Christ accord well with what is read in the apostles' epistle, the Synodical Act of xv. For by the common consent of the congregation, and after the mind of the Holy Spirit, they say they will impose nothing moreover upon the church, than such things as they had received already from Paul, and a few things that they added for a declaration of the same. Whereupon Paul said to the Galatians: If an angel from heaven preach unto you another gospel besides that which is preached, let him be accursed.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "take for doctrine" changed to "taken as doctrine concerning," subtly shifts from a descriptive observation to an interpretive assertion.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "he means that" inserted after "he will not lay any other burden upon the church" adds an explanatory layer not present in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "Wherupo" is a slightly archaic connector. While the draft's "Whereupon" is acceptable, the original's phrasing allows for a broader range of possible connections that the more direct "Whereupon" eliminates.
PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paul" changed to "Paul." While acceptable, the original's abbreviation is a stylistic choice reflecting the period and its removal homogenizes the text.
HALLUCINATION: None.
[FINAL]
A burden in the sermons of the Prophets is take for doctrine of grave and weighty matters. The Apostles also call the law a yocke and burden. Where therefore the Lord saith that he will not lay upon the church any other burden, he saith howe he will not reveal any other doctrine, nor further charge the, with other rites or ceremonies, than such as he had ordained and imposed already. What than? [illegible] hold fast,[note 14.6] namely that which you have received, suffering it not to be plucte out of your hades: Hold fast I say, with touth and nail, till I come: that is to say unto the last judgement. Therefore he testifies expressly, that this doctrine shall be perpetual, and unchangeable, and therefore to be kept most stiffly of all me, and not to be ſhroke fro, though all the world cry out, and persuade the contrary. Arethas Biſh. of Caesarea He required of the nothing else, saith he, but that they wold keep safely the godly pledge of faith until his coming. This if we shall do, we may easily eschew the crafts of the devil, and diſceauable clouds. For whatsoever they shall bring forth, whatsoever they shall forge and fine, or die with the cou terfeited colour of the holy ghost, we shall have always recourse to the simple doctrine of Christ set forth by thapoſtles, wherein alone we shall rest, rejecting althigs that shall not acerd with the same.
[DRAFT]
What then? Hold fast, namely that which you have received, suffering it not to be plucked out of your hands: Hold fast I say, with tooth and nail, until I come; that is to say, unto the last judgment. Therefore he testifies expressly that this doctrine shall be perpetual and unchangeable, and therefore to be kept most steadfastly by all of us, and not to be shaken from, though all the world cry out and persuade the contrary. Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, required of them nothing else, he says, but that they would keep safely the godly pledge of faith until his coming. This if we shall do, we may easily avoid the crafts of the devil and deceivable clouds. For whatever they shall bring forth, whatever they shall forge and refine, or dye with the counterfeited color of the Holy Spirit, we shall always have recourse to the simple doctrine of Christ set forth by the apostles, wherein alone we shall rest, rejecting all things that shall not accord with the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "stiffly of all me" changed to "steadfastly by all of us." The original phrasing, while archaic, emphasizes personal responsibility and adherence. The draft's wording shifts the focus to a collective obligation, potentially diluting the individual commitment implied in the original.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a consistent error needing correction throughout the modernized text.
3. HALLUCINATION: The phrase "die with the cou terfeited colour of the holy ghost" in the original is rendered as "dye with the counterfeited color of the Holy Spirit" in the draft. The original uses "die," suggesting a deceptive appearance or false representation, while the draft uses "dye," referring to a coloring process. This alters the intended meaning.
[FINAL]
What then? Hold fast, namely that which you have received, suffering it not to be plucked out of your hands: Hold fast I say, with tooth and nail, until I come; that is to say, unto the last judgment. Therefore he testifies expressly that this doctrine shall be perpetual and unchangeable, and therefore to be kept most stiffly of all me, and not to be shaken from, though all the world cry out and persuade the contrary. Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, required of them nothing else, he says, but that they would keep safely the godly pledge of faith until his coming. This if we shall do, we may easily avoid the crafts of the devil and deceivable clouds. For whatever they shall bring forth, whatever they shall forge and refine, or die with the counterfeited colour of the holy ghost, we shall always have recourse to the simple doctrine of Christ set forth by the apostles, wherein alone we shall rest, rejecting all things that shall not accord with the same.

[ORIGINAL]
And this wholesome doctrine of Christ confounds all traditions,[note 14.7] and subverts all conſtinitios made since the time of the Apostles. The godly may always object this ſayin of Christ to the traditioners. I will lay none other burthe upon you, besides that you have: That same hold fast until the last judgment. They shall allege that same also, that the Apostles deny that they will add nothing more. Act. 15. Christ spake this in the time of S. John, in the year of our Lord lxxxxvii. Therefore what so ever laws, traditios, decrees have been made since that time, we know they were not imposed of Christ, which saith so expressly that he will lay none other burden on the faithful. Where then become the decrees and constitutions of worshipping Images in the church, for the consecration and celebrating of masses. What shall we say to the decretalles of the Bishop of Rome? They are all overthrown and stricken down as it were with a thonderbolte, by this only sentence of Christ. I will impose none other burden, the that you have, keep that until the judgment. Behold, he saith, unto the judgment least any should imagine in the mean season, that another thing had pleased the holy ghost. Let us therefore persevere in the same.
[DRAFT]
This wholesome doctrine of Christ confounds all traditions and subverts all constitutions made since the time of the Apostles. The godly may always object this saying of Christ to the traditioners. I will lay no other burden upon you, besides that which you have: hold fast to that until the last judgment. They shall allege this also, that the Apostles denied that they would add anything more. Acts 15. Christ spoke this in the time of Jerome, in the year of our Lord 97. Therefore, whatever laws, traditions, decrees have been made since that time, we know they were not imposed by Christ, who says so expressly that he will lay no other burden on the faithful. Where then become the decrees and constitutions of worshipping images in the church, for the consecration and celebrating of masses? What shall we say to the decretals of the Bishop of Rome? They are all overthrown and stricken down as it were with a thunderbolt, by this only sentence of Christ: I will impose no other burden than that which you have, keep that until the judgment. Behold, he says, unto the judgment, lest any should imagine in the meantime that another thing had pleased the Holy Spirit. Let us therefore persevere in the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "lxxxxvii" changed to "97." This significantly alters the timeline and context. The original likely refers to a specific year within a historical understanding of John's time, which is lost with the simplification.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "S. John" changed to "Jerome." This is a critical error. Jerome is a different figure entirely.
3. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of commas around "Acts 15" disrupts the flow and implies a pause for reflection that isn't present in the original.
4. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "That same hold fast" is simplified to "hold fast to that." While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries a subtle emphasis on maintaining the existing burden.
5. PROPER NOUNS: "Austen" should be "Augustine" (not present in this excerpt, but a common error in modernized texts).
[FINAL]
This wholesome doctrine of Christ confounds all traditions[note 14.7] and subverts all constitutions made since the time of the Apostles. The godly may always object this saying of Christ to the traditioners. I will lay none other burthe upon you, besides that you have: That same hold fast until the last judgment. They shall allege that same also, that the Apostles deny that they will add nothing more. Act. 15. Christ spake this in the time of S. John, in the year of our Lord lxxxxvii. Therefore what so ever laws, traditios, decrees have been made since that time, we know they were not imposed of Christ, which saith so expressly that he will lay none other burden on the faithful. Where then become the decrees and constitutions of worshipping Images in the church, for the consecration and celebrating of masses. What shall we say to the decretalles of the Bishop of Rome? They are all overthrown and stricken down as it were with a thonderbolte, by this only sentence of Christ. I will impose none other burden, the that you have, keep that until the judgment. Behold, he saith, unto the judgment least any should imagine in the mean season, that another thing had pleased the holy ghost. Let us therefore persevere in the same.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.8]Hereunto he annexeth after his wonted manner most ample promeſſes, that through hope of so great rewards he might pluck them from errors, and join them to the true religion. And like as in the former epistles he hath said, he that ouercometh, so here he repeteth the same, admonishing us not to sleep but to watch, and fight mafully. And he ouercometh that kepeth the works of Christ unto the end. The works of Christ by a privy opposition are set against the inventions and works of men.[note 14.9] The works of Christ fignifie both doctrine and faith, and whatsoever good works inſew upon the same the service of worshipping of God, and the obſeruatio of Gods word. For in the.28. chap. of S. Math. The Lord saith to his disciples, teace you them to keep those things which I have commanded you. He speaks with an emphaſie, which I have commanded you, not such as you shall have invented of your own brain. For the Lord alleges out of the Propheth in the xv. of the same S. Math. saying: In vain do they worship me, teaching the doctrines of men. Therefore these works have no promise: But the works of Christ, which he him self hath ordained, and the which are done of his spirit, and of true faith, whilst we forsake our errors and cleave to the truth, they have a promise most ample.
[DRAFT]
Hereunto he adds, as was his custom, most ample promises, that through hope of so great rewards he might draw them from errors and join them to the true religion. And just as in the former epistles he has said, “He who overcomes,” so here he repeats the same, admonishing us not to sleep but to fight valiantly. He overcomes who keeps the works of Christ until the end. The works of Christ, by a subtle opposition, are set against the inventions and works of men.

The works of Christ signify both doctrine and faith, and whatever good works flow from them, the service of worshiping God, and the observance of God’s word. For in the twenty-eighth chapter of Saint Matthew, the Lord says to his disciples, “Teach them to keep those things which I have commanded you.” He speaks with emphasis, “those things which I have commanded you,” not such as you shall have invented of your own brain. For the Lord quotes from the prophet in the fifteenth chapter of the same Saint Matthew, saying, “In vain do they worship me, teaching the doctrines of men.” Therefore these works have no promise. But the works of Christ, which he himself has ordained, and which are done by his Spirit and by true faith, while we forsake our errors and cleave to the truth, they have a most ample promise.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "pluck them from errors" changed to "draw them from errors." While similar, "pluck" implies a more forceful, potentially reluctant extraction, which is subtly altered.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "a subtle opposition" is added to describe the relationship between the works of Christ and the works of men. The original simply states "a privy opposition." The addition introduces an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s "mafully" is softened to "valiantly." "Mafully" carries a sense of fierce, determined, and even brutal fighting, a nuance lost in "valiantly."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: "a subtle opposition" (as noted above).
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Hereunto he adds, as was his custom, most ample promises, that through hope of so great rewards he might draw them from errors and join them to the true religion. And just as in the former epistles he has said, “He who overcomes,” so here he repeats the same, admonishing us not to sleep but to fight valiantly. He overcomes who keeps the works of Christ until the end. The works of Christ, by a subtle opposition, are set against the inventions and works of men.

The works of Christ signify both doctrine and faith, and whatever good works flow from them, the service of worshiping God, and the observance of God’s word. For in the twenty-eighth chapter of Saint Matthew, the Lord says to his disciples, “Teach them to keep those things which I have commanded you.” He speaks with emphasis, “those things which I have commanded you,” not such as you shall have invented of your own brain. For the Lord quotes from the prophet in the fifteenth chapter of the same Saint Matthew, saying, “In vain do they worship me, teaching the doctrines of men.” Therefore these works have no promise. But the works of Christ, which he himself has ordained, and which are done by his Spirit and by true faith, while we forsake our errors and cleave to the truth, they have a most ample promise.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.10]And promises two notable things. The first: like as my father hath promised me victory, and performed it, that I overcome all my ennemies, and triumphed over them, the same being broke in pieces like vessels of clay or earth with out any difficulty, so will I give unto you also power and victory against all ungodly. And that same promise at the last shall be fully accomplished in the last judgment, in the which all the ennemies of Godliness shall be caste under the feet of Christ: As it is declared in the Pſalmes, especially in the ii. and Cx. Pſalme. And in this world also Christ affirms that his servants shall spiritually rule over his ennemies: Like as Christ, although he were tormented and died, yet nevertheless he overcame his ennemies. The holy and ecclesiastical stories bear witness of these things sufficiently.
[DRAFT]
And promises two notable things. The first: just as my Father has promised me victory, and fulfilled it, that I overcome all my enemies, and triumphed over them, shattering them like vessels of clay or earth without any difficulty, so will I give unto you also power and victory against all the ungodly. And that same promise at the last shall be fully accomplished in the final judgment, in which all the enemies of godliness shall be cast under the feet of Christ: As it is declared in the Psalms, especially in the second and one hundredth Psalm. And in this world also Christ affirms that his servants shall spiritually rule over his enemies: just as Christ, although he was tormented and died, yet nevertheless overcame his enemies. The holy and ecclesiastical histories bear witness of these things sufficiently.

The latter: I will give him the morning star. Peter is red to have used this allegory in the ii. Epistle first Chapter, or at the least he promised a clerenes most bright. For Daniel saith, howe the faithful in the resurrection shall shine like the firmament. The which thing also the Lord Christ alleges the xiii. of Math. And the Apostle alluding hereunto said, that one star was brighter then an other: So likewise in the resurrection one shall be made brighter then an other. These promises be most great, neither can I think that any greater can be given us. God grant us grace, that we may be made partakers of so great things.

The latter: I will give him the morning star. And he understood the knowledge of Christ increasing daily more and more, and so even Christ himself, [note 14.11] in like case as the day in the rising of the morning star waxes brighter and brighter. [ORIGINAL]
Finally he applies this epistle to all churches and ages of the world. Whereof since we have spoken oftener than once, there is no cause that by oft repeating I should be tedious to any man. To the Lord our God be praise and glory.
[DRAFT]
Finally, he applies this epistle to all churches and ages of the world. Concerning which, since we have spoken of this more than once, there is no need for me to be tedious to anyone by repeating it often. To the Lord our God be praise and glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "oft repeating I should be tedious to any man" subtly shifts from a concern about the speaker's potential boredom to a statement of unnecessary repetition. The original implies a potential imposition on the reader; the draft simply states it's unnecessary.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "by repeating it often" is an interpretation of "oft repeating." The original is more direct and less explanatory.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "oft repeating" allows for a broader interpretation of the speaker's actions. The draft's "repeating it often" is more specific.
[FINAL]
Finally, he applies this epistle to all churches and ages of the world. Concerning which, since we have spoken of this more than once, there is no cause that by oft repeating I should be tedious to any man. To the Lord our God be praise and glory.

## ¶ He blames certain things in the congregation of Sardis: notwithstanding he shows straight ways a remedy, whereby they may be healed, and be safe. The xv. Sermon.
