[ORIGINAL]
[note 17.6]Last he adds, which hath the key of David. I spake of the key in the first chapter. He alludeth to the 22. Chapter. of Isaiah. Whereby is signified the divine and almighty power of Christ, by the which he brings us purified into the kingdom of heaven: which work verily neither deuilles, nor any power can let. The same casts down the unclean into hell, neither is there that can deliver or differ the same. He says therefore aptely and expressly, he hath, not had or shall have, but he hath now. For he alone hath this power, which he comunicateth with no man else. The Pope of Rome lies, which says that he has this power. The only son of God excells in this prerogative.[note 17.7] Thapoſtles as ministers and preachers, have received the keys of knoweledge and of utterance, of learning, instruction and introduction, by the which also in threatening they exclude infidels out of the kingdom of God, bind them in their sins: almighty God which hath the high power ratifiyng the judgment of the minister, which he pronounced not of him self, but of Christ's words. But these things agree right well with those that follow of the opened door, which no man can shut, and so to the whole matter.
[DRAFT]
Finally, he adds, “He has the key of David.” I spoke of the key in the first chapter. He alludes to chapter 22 of Isaiah, signifying the divine and almighty power of Christ, by which he brings us purified into the kingdom of heaven; a work that neither devils nor any power can hinder. It casts down the unholy into hell, and there is no one who can prevent it. Therefore, he says aptly and expressly, “he has,” not “he has had” or “he will have,” but “he has now.” For he alone has this power, which he does not communicate to anyone else. The Pope of Rome lies, claiming that he has this power. The only Son of God excels in this prerogative.

The apostles, as ministers and preachers, have received the keys of knowledge and of utterance, of learning, instruction, and introduction, by which they also, in threatening, exclude unbelievers from the kingdom of God and bind them in their sins. Almighty God, who has the ultimate authority, ratifies the judgment of the minister, which he pronounces not of himself, but of Christ’s words. But these things agree well with what follows about the opened door, which no one can shut, and with the entire matter.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "deuilles" changed to "devils" – a minor semantic shift, but potentially softening the original's condemnation.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "a work that neither devils nor any power can hinder" is a slight expansion on "let" in the original, adding emphasis.
LOST AMBIGUITY: "differ the same" is changed to "prevent it." "Differ" in the original carries a nuance of separation or distinction, which is lost in "prevent."
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Finally, he adds, “He has the key of David.” I spoke of the key in the first chapter. He alludes to chapter 22 of Isaiah, signifying the divine and almighty power of Christ, by which he brings us purified into the kingdom of heaven; a work that neither devils nor any power can hinder. It casts down the unholy into hell, and there is no one who can prevent it. Therefore, he says aptly and expressly, “he has,” not “he has had” or “he will have,” but “he has now.” For he alone has this power, which he does not communicate to anyone else. The Pope of Rome lies, claiming that he has this power. The only Son of God excels in this prerogative.

[ORIGINAL]
And he commends so the perseverance in faith in this congregation,[note 17.8] that he signifies with all, that the same also did proceed of the grace of Christ. Thou haste, says he, little power, and as it were no force and strength, which this world regards, as power, riches, wordly wisdom, lucky success, plenty of friends, and such other like things. Therefore thou can attribute nothing to thy self, nothing to thine own strength, not so much as this that thou art a church, and that the verity of the gospel is freely preached with thee.[note 17.9] For I set open this door. And by my strength I keep open the same, that no man can shut the same door: to wit the preachinge and grace ones granted, by any means to prohibit, let, or take away. To open the door, is a common phrase of speaking, used of the Apostle, in the 1. to the Corinth. 16. and the 2. Corinth. the 2. He opens the door, which gives an occasion, and prepares the way to enter in. By the word therefore was opened the door of life. The faithful might enter in: the infidels could not stop this way. For the hand of Christ held the door open. And these things in deed do declare,[note 17.10] whereof it is that in cities, towns, and villages not greatly furnished with any force or power, the course of the gospel proceeds with so lucky success: And where many go about by layng waite, crafts and policies, threatenings and persecutions to shut the door, they ca not. These things are not done through our cunning and wisdom, but of the grace of God.
[DRAFT]
And he praises the perseverance in faith within this congregation, so that he indicates to all that it proceeds from the grace of Christ. “You have little power, and seemingly no force or strength, which the world regards as power, riches, worldly wisdom, fortunate success, plenty of friends, and similar things. Therefore, you can attribute nothing to yourself, nothing to your own strength, not even this that you are a church, and that the truth of the gospel is freely preached among you. For I have opened this door. And by my strength I keep the same door open, so that no one can shut it—that is, the preaching and grace once granted, by any means to prohibit, hinder, or take away. To open the door is a common phrase used by the Apostle in 1 Corinthians 16 and 2 Corinthians 2. He opens the door, which provides an opportunity and prepares the way to enter. Therefore, by the word the door of life was opened. The faithful could enter in; the unbelievers could not stop this way. For the hand of Christ held the door open. And these things indeed declare, which is why in cities, towns, and villages not greatly furnished with any force or power, the course of the gospel proceeds with such fortunate success. And where many attempt by waiting, schemes, and policies, threats, and persecutions to shut the door, they cannot. These things are not done through our cunning and wisdom, but by the grace of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "lucky success" changed to "fortunate success." While semantically close, "lucky" carries a nuance of chance and divine favor absent in "fortunate," potentially softening the original's theological emphasis.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "let, or take away" is softened to "hinder or take away." "Let" implies permission, a crucial element of the original's argument about divine allowance, now lost.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
4. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "Therefore, by the word the door of life was opened" is not present in the original. It is an interpretive addition.
[FINAL]

Therefore, you can attribute nothing to yourself, nothing to your own strength, not even this that you are a church, and that the truth of the gospel is freely preached among you. For I have opened this door. And by my strength I keep the same door open, so that no one can shut it—that is, the preaching and grace once granted, by any means to prohibit or take away. [ORIGINAL]
How be it if any man list to understand those things and such as follow herafter peculiarly of the pastor or bishop of the church, I will not be against it. For where he was humble, and instruct with no worldly wisdom, yet furnished with God his grace, he opened the way of salvation, which now they could not shut up, as many as sought to abrogate the preaching of the gospel. The virtue of Christ kept him.
[DRAFT]
However, if any person desires to understand those things and what follows particularly concerning the pastor or bishop of the church, I will not object. For where he was humble and instructed with no worldly wisdom, yet furnished with God’s grace, he opened the way of salvation, which those who seek to abolish the preaching of the gospel now cannot shut. The power of Christ preserved him.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: "particularly concerning" is an unnecessary addition, clarifying a point that was already clear in the original.

[ORIGINAL]
And now more expressly he preaches or comendeth the faithful constancy in faith of the pastor and congregation:[note 17.11] Thou hast kept, says he, my word, and hast not denied my name. When the Lord opened the door, lighted the candel, and gave heavenly gifts, the pastor with the congregation received them, and received kept the, and so kept denied them not, neither trod the under foot. This is an excellent praise. Would God there were many such churches found at this day. Here may thou learn also, O thou church of Christ, here may you learn all and singular, what is the duty of pastors, of the church, and of all and singular godly men and women. Thy merit was none at all, God of his grace shone unto thee. Thy worthiness was non, thy desert, power nor authority: Christ of his mercy hath revealed him self unto thee. Embrace him therefore that offers him self to thee, hold fast and never at any time let him go. &c.
[DRAFT]
And now he more explicitly urges or commends the faithful constancy in faith of the pastor and congregation:[note 17.11] “You have kept my word,” he says, “and have not denied my name.” When the Lord opened the door, lit the lamp, and gave spiritual gifts, the pastor and the congregation received them and kept them, and so did not deny them, nor trample them underfoot. This is excellent praise. Would God there were many such churches found today. Here also, O church of Christ, you may learn all things—each and every one—concerning the duty of pastors, of the church, and of all godly men and women. Your merit was none at all; God shone upon you through grace. Your worthiness, power, or authority were none; Christ revealed himself to you through mercy. Embrace him who offers himself to you, hold fast, and never at any time let him go. &c.
[CRITIQUE]

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "lit the lamp" adds a sense of agency ("lit") not present in "lighted."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "heavenly gifts" changed to "spiritual gifts." "Heavenly" retains a broader theological scope; "spiritual" is more restrictive.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.

Embrace him who offers himself to you, hold fast, and never at any time let him go. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 17.12]And note that the Lord says, my word, not every mans word, but mine. What the word of Christ is, it is known to all men. For that which is written in the Gospel, and first in deed by the Prophets, and after by the Apostles was set forth in holy writ, is the word of christ. It is not Christ's word that strives with the same, although it be set forth by Counsels and holy fathers. Christ doth not acknowledge that word: he acknowledges his for his own. And this must be observed and kept. The word of Christ is observed, what time it is not corrupted with additions,[note 17.13] detractions, and wraſtinges, but in case it be kept sincere in his natural sense. It is not kept, when it is corrupted or depraved with men's inventions, and perverse interpretations. The word of Christ is kept, when it is commended not with the mouth alone, but is also expressed with godly works in the whole life, and beautified with holiness. It is not observed, when with out repentance men live most filthily. Finally the word of Christ is observed and kept, when it is not with any lotheſomneſſe of ours or impatiece cast away, denied and forsaken. And therefore he annereth incotinently, and thou hast not denied my name. I have spoken else where largely of confessing and deniyng of Christ's name. These things verily did the Philadelphias, and with these virtues through faith pleased the lord. By these also may we comende our selves to our Saviour.
[DRAFT]
And note that the Lord says, my word, not every man's word, but mine. What the word of Christ is, it is known to all men. For that which is written in the Gospel, and first indeed by the Prophets, and after by the Apostles was set forth in holy writ, is the word of Christ. It is not Christ's word that contends with the same, although it be set forth by councils and holy fathers. Christ does not acknowledge that word; he acknowledges his as his own. And this must be observed and kept. The word of Christ is observed, when it is not corrupted with additions, detractions, and wrappings, but in case it be kept sincere in his natural sense. It is not kept when it is corrupted or depraved with men's inventions and perverse interpretations. The word of Christ is kept when it is commended not with the mouth alone, but is also expressed with godly works in the whole life, and beautified with holiness. It is not observed when, without repentance, men live most filthily. Finally, the word of Christ is observed and kept when it is not with any loathing of ours or impatience cast away, denied and forsaken. And therefore he affirms immediately, and you have not denied my name. I have spoken elsewhere largely of confessing and denying of Christ's name. These things truly did the Philadelphians, and with these virtues through faith pleased the Lord. By these also may we commend ourselves to our Savior.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "strives with the same" (original) changed to "contends with the same" (draft). While similar, "strive" implies a more active, potentially difficult relationship, which "contend" softens.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "truly" before "These things did the Philadelphians" adds an unwarranted emphasis and interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "wraſtinges" (original) is modernized to "wrappings." "Wraſtinges" likely refers to distortions or misrepresentations, a more nuanced meaning than simply "wrappings." The draft loses this specific nuance.
4. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And note that the Lord says, my word, not every man's word, but mine. What the word of Christ is, it is known to all men. For that which is written in the Gospel, and first indeed by the Prophets, and after by the Apostles was set forth in holy writ, is the word of Christ. It is not Christ's word that contends with the same, although it be set forth by councils and holy fathers. Christ does not acknowledge that word; he acknowledges his as his own. And this must be observed and kept. The word of Christ is observed, when it is not corrupted with additions, detractions, and wrappings, but in case it be kept sincere in his natural sense. It is not kept when it is corrupted or depraved with men's inventions and perverse interpretations. The word of Christ is kept when it is commended not with the mouth alone, but is also expressed with godly works in the whole life, and beautified with holiness. It is not observed when, without repentance, men live most filthily. Finally, the word of Christ is observed and kept when it is not with any loathing of ours or impatience cast away, denied and forsaken. And therefore he affirms immediately, and you have not denied my name. I have spoken elsewhere largely of confessing and denying of Christ's name. These things truly did the Philadelphians, and with these virtues through faith pleased the Lord. By these also may we commend ourselves to our Savior.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 17.14]Furthermore the Lord shows, with how great a reward he would honour that constancy of the godly in faith. Ye have now, says he, many enemies by reason of your pure religion: but in case the thus hold one, I will cause that those same enemies shall become your friends, and finally fellows of your religion: In so much that they that have hitherto condemned you for wicked doers and heretics, shall come unto you with great humility, to axe you forgeuenes, ready to receive your religion, and to worship him whom they have blasphemed. And they shall come in most humble wise, and with the greatest humility that may be. For so said Isaiah before that it should so come to pass in 49. chap. whereunto the Lord alluded at this present.
[DRAFT]
Furthermore, the Lord shows with how great a reward he would honor the constancy of the faithful in faith. “You now have many enemies because of your pure religion,” he says, “but if you persevere, I will cause those same enemies to become your friends and ultimately companions in your faith. So that those who have previously condemned you as evildoers and heretics will come to you with great humility, asking for forgiveness, ready to receive your religion and to worship him whom they have blasphemed. And they will come in the most humble manner, with the greatest humility possible. For so Isaiah foretold that it would come to pass in chapter 49, to which the Lord alludes at this time.”
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "fellows of your religion" changed to "companions in your faith" subtly shifts the sense of shared practice and community to a more abstract adherence to belief.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "with the greatest humility possible" is an unnecessary addition. The original simply states "with the greatest humility."
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "axe forgiuenes" (ask for forgiveness) is softened to "asking for forgiveness," losing the slightly archaic and more direct tone.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Furthermore, the Lord shows with how great a reward he would honor the constancy of the faithful in faith. “You now have many enemies because of your pure religion,” he says, “but if you persevere, I will cause those same enemies to become your friends and ultimately companions in your faith. So that those who have previously condemned you as evildoers and heretics will come to you with great humility, asking for forgiveness, ready to receive your religion and to worship him whom they have blasphemed. And they will come in the most humble manner, with the greatest humility possible. For so Isaiah foretold that it would come to pass in chapter 49, to which the Lord alludes at this time.”

[ORIGINAL]
In the mean season he touches the Jews the singular enemies of the saith,[note 17.15] whom he calls the Sinagoge of Satan. For their teacher was none other but the Devil, as in deed they have no better at this day. He calls them false Jews and liars. For neither they confessed the Lord, nor glorified god, nor believed in Christ their Messiah. But they that are Jews in deed, be not such: as the Apostle S. Paull said in the 2. Chapter to the Romans. The power of God co strained many of them, forsaking their Iewiſheneſſe, to go to the Christen religion.
[DRAFT]
In the meantime, he addresses the Jews, the singular enemies of the faith,[note 17.15], whom he calls the Synagogue of Satan. For their teacher was none other than the Devil, as indeed they have no better at this day. He calls them false Jews and liars. For neither did they confess the Lord, nor glorify God, nor believe in Christ their Messiah. But those who are Jews in deed are not such, as the Apostle Saint Paul said in the second chapter to the Romans. The power of God compelled many of them, forsaking their Jewishness, to embrace the Christian religion.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "co strained" to "compelled" subtly alters the nuance; "co strained" suggests a more complex or persuasive influence than "compelled," which implies force.
LOST AMBIGUITY: "Iewiſheneſſe" to "Jewishness" removes a degree of archaic formality and potentially a subtle distancing from the concept.
PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paull" to "Saint Paul" is acceptable, but the original abbreviation is characteristic of the period and its removal is a minor stylistic loss.
[FINAL]
In the meantime, he addresses the Jews, the singular enemies of the faith,[note 17.15], whom he calls the Synagogue of Satan. For their teacher was none other than the Devil, as indeed they have no better at this day. He calls them false Jews and liars. For neither did they confess the Lord, nor glorify God, nor believe in Christ their Messiah. But those who are Jews in deed are not such, as the Apostle S. Paull said in the second chapter to the Romans. The power of God constrained many of them, forsaking their Iewiſheneſſe, to embrace the Christian religion.

[ORIGINAL]
Therefore if we covet or go about to reteyne also in our Churches the pure word of God,[note 17.16] and to receive our enemies humble, we shall not attain to these things by wars or wrongs, by raylyng and approbriouſe words, but by constant faith. But if either we profess our faith not purely, or beautify not the same with virtues, what marvel is it, though enemies abide enemies still, and continue to hate us every day more haynouſly than other, and at length oppress us, and extinguish the light of God his word with many? let us learn dere brethren, by godlynes, constancy and holiness to win our brethren. The Lord Jesus grant us his grace to perform the same.
[DRAFT]
Therefore, if we desire or seek to retain also in our churches the pure word of God, and to receive our enemies humbly, we shall not achieve these things through wars or wrongs, through railing and reproachful words, but by steadfast faith. But if either we do not profess our faith purely, or do not adorn it with virtues, what is it to be wondered at, though enemies remain enemies still, and continue to hate us every day more heinously than before, and at length oppress us, and extinguish the light of God’s word with many? Let us learn, dear brethren, by godliness, constancy, and holiness to win our brethren. The Lord Jesus grant us his grace to accomplish the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "raylyng and approbriouſe words" to "railing and reproachful words" - While largely synonymous, "approbriouſe" carries a stronger sense of public scorn and derision than "reproachful" does. This slightly softens the original's condemnation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "beautify not the same with virtues" to "do not adorn it with virtues" - "Adorn" introduces a visual metaphor not explicitly present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "more haynouſly than other" to "more heinously than before" - The original phrasing is ambiguous; "other" could refer to other groups or other times. "Before" is more specific and limits the possible interpretations.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Therefore, if we desire or seek to retain also in our churches the pure word of God, and to receive our enemies humbly, we shall not achieve these things through wars or wrongs, through railing and reproachful words, but by steadfast faith. But if either we do not profess our faith purely, or do not adorn it with virtues, what is it to be wondered at, though enemies remain enemies still, and continue to hate us every day more heinously than before, and at length oppress us, and extinguish the light of God’s word with many? Let us learn, dear brethren, by godliness, constancy, and holiness to win our brethren. The Lord Jesus grant us his grace to accomplish the same.

## ¶ He exhorts them to persevere in the true faith propoundyng most ample rewards. The.xviij. Sermon.

[ORIGINAL]
ANd they shall know, that I have loved thee: because thou hast kept the words of my patience: therefore will I keep thee from the hour of temptation, which will come upon all the world, to tempte them that dwell upon the earth behold I come shortly. Hold fast that thou hast that noma take away thy crown.
[DRAFT]
And they shall know that I have loved you, because you have kept the words of my patience. Therefore, I will keep you from the hour of temptation, which will come upon all the world to test those who dwell on the earth. Behold, I am coming shortly. Hold fast to what you have, so that no one takes away your crown.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "noma" changed to "so that no one takes away your crown" significantly alters the original's meaning. "Noma" is likely a corruption of "now," intending "hold fast now." The draft introduces a possessive and adversarial element absent in the original.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft’s phrasing “to test those who dwell on the earth” adds an interpretive layer. The original simply states "to tempte them that dwell upon the earth," which is more straightforward.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s "noma" is ambiguous, potentially referring to a specific time or action. The draft eliminates this ambiguity with a specific interpretation.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short excerpt, so this is not applicable.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.
[FINAL]
And they shall know that I have loved you, because you have kept the words of my patience. Therefore, I will keep you from the hour of temptation, which will come upon all the world to tempte them that dwell upon the earth. Behold, I come shortly. Hold fast now that you have, so that no one takes away your crown.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.1]An excellent virtue is commended in the congregation of Philadelphia, that they have kept the word of Christ, not every word, but the word of Christ, and have not denied it. And he hath begone to rehearse most large rewards, which both he hath given to this church, and is also ready to give to any other like in the zeal of godly religion. For we are allured by rewards.
[DRAFT]
A commendable virtue is praised in the congregation of Philadelphia: that they have kept the word of Christ, not every word, but the word of Christ, and have not denied it. And he has begun to recount great rewards, which both he has given to this church, and is also ready to give to any other like in the zeal of godly religion. For we are drawn by rewards.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "Allured" changed to "drawn." While similar, "allured" carries a connotation of temptation or attraction that "drawn" lacks, subtly altering the motivation presented.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "we are allured by rewards" is a somewhat ambiguous statement about human motivation. The draft's "we are drawn by rewards" is less evocative and removes some of the original's potential for multiple interpretations regarding the nature of that attraction.
[FINAL]
A commendable virtue is praised in the congregation of Philadelphia: that they have kept the word of Christ, not every word, but the word of Christ, and have not denied it. And he has begun to recount great rewards, which both he has given to this church, and is also ready to give to any other like in the zeal of godly religion. For we are allured by rewards.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.2]First I will convert, says he, thine enemies, that they may be made thy friends and brethren, that coming into the congregation, they may worship Christ, whom they have blasphemed hitherto, yea that they shall submit them selves hue bly and lowly: As we read of S. Paull, which in the 15. Chapter. of the 1. epistle to the Corinthians says that he is unworthy to be called an Apostle. &c. And this is a wonderful benefit. For God is glorified by such as are couerted, and the truth is set forth, lying and superstition are confounded. Whereof the Saints can not but be exceedingly glad. The faithful also are delivered out of the Devils claws, and are saved.
[DRAFT]
First, I will convert your enemies, so that they may become your friends and brethren, and come into the congregation to worship Christ, whom they have blasphemed until now, and submit themselves willingly and humbly. As we read of Jerome, who in the 15th chapter of the first epistle to the Corinthians says that he is unworthy to be called an apostle, and so forth. And this is a wonderful benefit. For God is glorified by those who were converted, and the truth is proclaimed, while lies and superstition are confounded. The saints cannot but be exceedingly glad. The faithful are also delivered out of the Devil’s clutches and are saved.

6. PROPER NOUNS: Austen changed to Jerome is incorrect. It should be Jerome.
[FINAL]

As we read of Jerome, who in the 15th chapter of the first epistle to the Corinthians says that he is unworthy to be called an apostle, and so forth. And this is a wonderful benefit. The faithful are also delivered out of the Devil’s clutches and are saved.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.4]But of this love of God, whereby he, provoked by no deserts of ours, but of his only grace and native goodness has joined him self to the church, all virtues doe proceed: That chiefly which immediately follows, that the church hath kept the word of patiece. The same John in his canonical epistle: not that we, says he, have loved God, but that he hath loved us &c. Therefore where the observation of the word of patience is annexed as the cause of love, it must be religiouſely expouded, that the favour of god, and all our gifts be verily of grace, but yet that he of the same grace doth as it were requite and reward us for our pains. Whereof the Saints are not proud, but humbly acknowledge and preach grace every where and in all things.
[DRAFT]
Note 18.4 But of this love of God, by which he, provoked by no merits of ours, but by his only grace and innate goodness, has joined himself to the church, all virtues proceed. The saints are not proud of this, but humbly acknowledge and preach grace everywhere and in all things.

Note 18.4 But of this love of God, by which he, provoked by no merits of ours, but by his only grace and innate goodness, has joined himself to the church, all virtues proceed. Chiefly that which immediately follows: that the church has kept the word of patience. [ORIGINAL]
Again he comendeth the perseverance of the faithful in the true religion. Thou hast kept, says he,[note 18.5] the word of my patience. The word of patience is the Gospel of eternal salvation: which is otherwise called of S. Paull the word of the cross, and that for two considerations. First for because he describes the cross and patience of Christ whereby we are saved. And again he persuades us also to bear the cross, and patiently to suffer with Christ, Matthew. 16.2. Timoth. 2. Neither must any man look for any perseverance of him that is impatient. The Lord says in the 12. of Luke, in your patience you shall possess your souls. Therefore hath either the pastor or the church of Philadelphia kept the word of patience, to wit in retaining in their hearts the patience of Christ through faith, and in showing patience in words or sayings, and ſuſteyning much travel in body. Which in deed is the best way to keep churches safe and sound, and every one of the faithful. Let them keep, I say, the word of Christ his patience, and the rest commit to the Lord.
[DRAFT]
Again he commends the perseverance of the faithful in the true religion. “You have kept,” he says, [note 18.5], “the word of my patience.” The word of patience is the Gospel of eternal salvation, which Paul also calls the word of the cross, and that for two considerations. First, because he describes the cross and patience of Christ by which we are saved. And again, he urges us also to bear the cross and patiently to suffer with Christ, Matthew 16:24; 2 Timothy 2:3. Neither must any man expect perseverance from one who is impatient. The Lord says in Luke 12:36, “In your patience you shall possess your souls.” Therefore, either the pastor or the church of Philadelphia has kept the word of patience, namely, by retaining in their hearts the patience of Christ through faith, and by showing patience in words and sayings, and sustaining much hardship in body. This indeed is the best way to keep churches safe and sound, and every one of the faithful. Let them keep, I say, the word of Christ’s patience, and the rest commit to the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "ſuſteyning much travel in body" changed to "sustaining much hardship in body." "Travel" implies journey or labor, which could have a broader spiritual connotation. "Hardship" is more narrowly physical.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of semicolons and rephrasing of "Matthew. 16.2. Timoth. 2" to "Matthew 16:24; 2 Timothy 2:3" adds a layer of interpretation, suggesting a direct connection between the verses that might not have been explicitly intended in the original. Verse 16:24 is a more specific reference than simply "Matthew 16."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "the rest commit to the Lord" is open to interpretation regarding what "the rest" refers to. The draft maintains this, which is appropriate.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Hierom→Jerome, Austen→Augustine, Ireney→Irenaeus are not present in this passage. The modernization of "Paull" to "Paul" is correct.
[FINAL]
Again he commends the perseverance of the faithful in the true religion. “You have kept,” he says, [note 18.5], “the word of my patience.” The word of patience is the Gospel of eternal salvation, which Paul also calls the word of the cross, and that for two considerations. First, because he describes the cross and patience of Christ whereby we are saved. And again, he urges us also to bear the cross and patiently to suffer with Christ, Matthew 16:24; 2 Timothy 2:3. Neither must any man expect perseverance from one who is impatient. The Lord says in Luke 12:36, “In your patience you shall possess your souls.” Therefore, either the pastor or the church of Philadelphia has kept the word of patience, namely, by retaining in their hearts the patience of Christ through faith, and by showing patience in words and sayings, and sustaining much travel in body. This indeed is the best way to keep churches safe and sound, and every one of the faithful. Let them keep, I say, the word of Christ’s patience, and the rest commit to the Lord.
---
[ORIGINAL]
For it follows: And I will keep thee again from the hour of temptation, &c.[note 18.6] The hour of temptation is expou ded two ways. For either he speaks of heresies and of heretics, by whose talk, and crafty iuggelynge, leudenes, and diſceiptfulnes is tempted the faith, simplicity, and integrity of the faytheful. Whereof the Lord treats much in the 13. Chapter of Deuteronomie. Or else he speaks verily of the persecutions, which the emperors of Rome have inflicted, among whom Traiane a most mighty Prince set forth sore proclamations against the Christians. Whereof Plinie also made mention in the 10. book of Epistles, the ho dreth and one. But Christ preserved the church of Philadelphia, and kepeth also at this day the faithful by his word and power in the perils of heretics and heresies, and finally of persecutions also: so that the faithful may stand sure in all controversies and receive nothing of heretics that is strange from gods word, and also give no place in persecutions. Christ causes many times that the burden of persecution presses not so heavily. Therefore let us always be constant in gods word, and permit the defence to our Lord God. He will not neglect us, &c.
[DRAFT]
For it follows: And I will keep you again from the hour of temptation, &c.[note 18.6] The hour of temptation is explained in two ways. For either he speaks of heresies and heretics, by whose talk, and crafty juggling, lewdness, and deceptiveness tempts the faith, simplicity, and integrity of the faithful. Of this, the Lord treats much in the 13th Chapter of Deuteronomy. Or else he speaks truly of the persecutions which the Roman emperors have inflicted, among whom Trajan, a most mighty prince, issued severe proclamations against the Christians. Of this, Pliny also made mention in the 10th book of Epistles, the hundred and one. But Christ preserved the church of Philadelphia, and keeps also at this day the faithful by his word and power in the perils of heretics and heresies, and finally of persecutions also: so that the faithful may stand firm in all controversies and receive nothing of heretics that is strange from God’s word, and also give no place in persecutions. Christ causes many times that the burden of persecution presses not so heavily. Therefore let us always be steadfast in God’s word, and permit the defense to our Lord God. He will not neglect us, &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "iuggelynge" modernized to "juggling" loses some of the original's sense of deceptive trickery and subtle manipulation. "leudenes" modernized to "lewdness" is acceptable but less nuanced.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None apparent.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None apparent.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: None apparent.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Austen" should be "Augustine" (not present in this passage, but a general point). "Plinie" modernized to "Pliny" is correct. "Traiane" modernized to "Trajan" is correct.
[FINAL]
For it follows: And I will keep you again from the hour of temptation, &c.[note 18.6] The hour of temptation is explained in two ways. For either he speaks of heresies and heretics, by whose talk, and crafty jugglery, lewdness, and deceptiveness tempts the faith, simplicity, and integrity of the faithful. Of this, the Lord treats much in the 13th Chapter of Deuteronomy. Or else he speaks truly of the persecutions which the Roman emperors have inflicted, among whom Trajan, a most mighty prince, issued severe proclamations against the Christians. Of this, Pliny also made mention in the 10th book of Epistles, the hundred and one. But Christ preserved the church of Philadelphia, and keeps also at this day the faithful by his word and power in the perils of heretics and heresies, and finally of persecutions also: so that the faithful may stand firm in all controversies and receive nothing of heretics that is strange from God’s word, and also give no place in persecutions. Christ causes many times that the burden of persecution presses not so heavily. Therefore let us always be steadfast in God’s word, and permit the defense to our Lord God. He will not neglect us, &c.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.7]But for as much as in temptations and afflictions the Lord seems many times to our flesh to tarry overlong, and in manner to neglect his: for we say the Lord prevents, and adds, behold I come shortly. Shortly, I say, that is to say in time: not to late nor to hastily. The which we say, neither to soon nor to late, but in dew time and season. If the lord therefore shall seem to be over slow, despair not: for he will come timely enough, when he shall see it good. Doe not thou prescribe unto him the manner and mean of deliverance, but abide the Lords leisure. Read what goodly and wholesome things S. Paull hath written concerning this matter in the end of the 10. chapter to the Hebrews, where a place also out of the 2. Chapter. of Abachuc is alleged.
[DRAFT]
But since in trials and afflictions the Lord often appears to our flesh to delay, and to seem to neglect his own work: for we say the Lord anticipates, and adds, “Behold, I come shortly.” Shortly, I say, that is to say, in time: neither too late nor too hastily. This we say, neither too soon nor too late, but in due time and season. If the Lord therefore seems to be slow, do not despair: for he will come in due time, when he sees it fitting. Do not you prescribe to him the manner and means of deliverance, but wait for the Lord’s appointed time. Read what excellent and wholesome things Saint Paul has written concerning this matter in the end of the tenth chapter to the Hebrews, where a passage also from the second chapter of Habakkuk is cited.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "prevents" is altered to "anticipates." While related, "prevents" in this context implies foreknowledge and proactive action, a nuance lost in "anticipates."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "wait for the Lord’s appointed time" is an interpretation not explicitly present in the original "abide the Lords leisure." The original emphasizes patience; the draft adds a sense of a preordained schedule.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "in dew time and season" is softened to "in due time and season," losing some of the poetic and slightly archaic quality.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paull" is correctly modernized to "Saint Paul," "Abachuc" to "Habakkuk."
[FINAL]
But since in trials and afflictions the Lord often appears to our flesh to delay, and to seem to neglect his: for we say the Lord prevents, and adds, “Behold, I come shortly.” Shortly, I say, that is to say in time: not too late nor too hastily. The which we say, neither too soon nor too late, but in dew time and season. If the Lord therefore shall seem to be over slow, despair not: for he will come timely enough, when he shall see it good. Do not thou prescribe unto him the manner and mean of deliverance, but abide the Lords leisure. Read what goodly and wholesome things Saint Paul has written concerning this matter in the end of the tenth chapter to the Hebrews, where a place also out of the second chapter of Habakkuk is alleged.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.8]And now he exhorts in few but most evident words to perseverance in piety, wherein they had excelled hitherto. And he says two things hold [illegible] hold fast that thou hast. They had the gospel of Christ, and the word of eternal life, the true faith and godly religion. These things he commands to hold fast, and to persist in the religion ones received. And whilst he commanded them to keep that they had, he signifies by the way, that no new or other doctrine is to be looked for: but that this ones received doth suffice. Let us not think therefore in the government of the church upon other laws, upon other traditions, than of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. This is sufficient for the church. After reaſonynge as it were of the loss, he says: Therefore must thou watch diligently and hold strongly the gospel, for this being taken away thou art spoiled of thy crown. The crown is a token of virtue and victory. We say.
[DRAFT]
And now he exhorts in few but most evident words to perseverance in piety, wherein they had excelled hitherto. And he says two things must be held fast: that which you have. They had the gospel of Christ, and the word of eternal life, the true faith and godly religion. These things he commands to hold fast, and to persist in the religion one has received. And whilst he commanded them to keep that which they had, he signifies by the way that no new or other doctrine is to be looked for, but that this one has received doth suffice. Let us not therefore think in the government of the church upon other laws, upon other traditions, than of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. This is sufficient for the church. After reasoning as it were of the loss, he says: Therefore must you watch diligently and hold strongly the gospel, for if this is taken away you are spoiled of your crown. The crown is a token of virtue and victory. We say.
[CRITIQUE]

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "this ones received" is more subtly ambiguous regarding the source of the faith. The draft's "this one has received" is more direct and potentially limits interpretation.

[ORIGINAL]
Conquerors and worthy of the Empire, are crowned. The virgin loses her crown, that is defiled. Therefore heretics, false prophets, and seducers take away the crown, what time they seduce and corrupt: therefore says the Lord: Thou haste gotten honour and glory, see that no man take it from thee. So we read that S. Paull spake in the 2. to the Coloſs. Let no man take from you the victory. In the 18. of Ezekiel the Lord testifies, that he will not impute rightuouſnes to the just, in case he forsake and leave his rightouſenes let us pray therefore, that we may evermore persevere in the word of the Lord.
[DRAFT]
Conquerors and those worthy of the Empire are crowned. The virgin loses her crown when she is defiled. Therefore, heretics, false prophets, and seducers take away the crown when they seduce and corrupt. Thus says the Lord: “You have gained honor and glory; see that no one takes it from you.” So we read that Paul spoke in the second letter to the Colossians: “Let no one take away the victory from you.” In the eighteenth chapter of Ezekiel, the Lord testifies that he will not impute righteousness to the just if he forsakes and abandons his righteousness. Let us pray, therefore, that we may always persevere in the word of the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "Forsake and leave" changed to "forsakes and abandons" subtly alters the tone; "leave" implies a more passive abandonment than "abandons."
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "rightuousnes" is plural, suggesting a broader concept than the singular "righteousness" in the draft. This removes a potential nuance regarding multiple aspects of righteousness.
PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paull" modernized to "Paul" is acceptable, but the lack of a full stop after "Paull" in the original is lost.
[FINAL]
Conquerors and those worthy of the Empire are crowned. The virgin loses her crown when she is defiled. Therefore, heretics, false prophets, and seducers take away the crown when they seduce and corrupt. Thus says the Lord: “You have gained honor and glory; see that no one takes it from you.” So we read that S. Paull spake in the second letter to the Colossians: “Let no one take away the victory from you.” In the eighteenth chapter of Ezekiel, the Lord testifies that he will not impute righteousness to the just, in case he forsake and leave his rightuousnes. Let us pray, therefore, that we may always persevere in the word of the Lord.

## ¶ He proceeds in recityng most great rewards. The xix. Sermon.

## ¶ He proceeds in recityng most great rewards. The xix. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
HIm that ouercometh will I make a pillar in the temple of my God, and he shall go no more out. And I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God, new Ieruſale, which comes down out of Heaven from my God, and I will write upon him my new name. Let him that has ears hear, what the spirit says to the congregations.
[DRAFT]

And I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God, New Jerusalem, which comes down out of Heaven from my God, and I will write upon him my new name. Let him who has ears hear what the Spirit says to the congregations.

NO ISSUES
[FINAL]
He who overcomes, I will make a pillar in the temple of my God, and he shall go no more out. And I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God, New Jerusalem, which comes down out of Heaven from my God, and I will write upon him my new name. Let him who has ears hear what the Spirit says to the congregations.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Our Lord proceeds in raccomptynge much more ample rewards, which he would give to them that overcome: And so he tempers his words, that we may easily perceive, this promise not only to appertain to the congregation of Philadelphia, but to all the Churches in the whole world, yea and to every of the faythefull. And as we have oftentimes repeated already (for I am not aſſhamed to repeat, saying that the Lord him self so greatly urges the victory) again we say, that those things are promised not to such as fight lightly or negligently (for diverse fight,[note 19.1] and by and by run away) but to those that overcome and persevere to the end. For our life is a warfare upon earth: which Iob also hath confessed. The soldier hath a sure purpose to overcome his enemies. Our enemies are, the devil, the world, and the flesh. Against these we must earnestly fight: of none other intent, but that we may overcome. The Apostle in the 6. to the Epheſ. described the armour of the faithful. In victory the Saints consider ſinceries and integrity, that we lose nothing of the verity known: but let us reteyne the pure word of God, and sincere faith, and let us keep our bodies and our souls clean from all pollution, and that to our lives end. He propounds most ample rewards by promise.[note 19.2] Whereby he alludeth to the manner of Greeks and Romans, who decreed Images to such as deserved well of the common welthe in the which also they wrote their virtues, for whose cause they were set up either in the court or market place or else where. They seemed by this mean to deliver to their posterity as it were by hand the glory of their elders, which they made also as it were everlasting.[note 19.3] Otherwise the use of this vocable Column, or pillar is diverse. Ieremie is called of God a pillar for his constancy. The Apostles are called of S. Paull in the 2. to the Galath. chief pillars, for their excellency, and that the churches leaned upon them, for the preaching of the verity. The church it self also is called the pillar and base of verity, for as much as it is grouded upon the sure rock Christ. In the Temple of Salomo stood two columns or pillars, figures of the everlasting kingdom of Christ,[note 19.4] and of the holy church. In this place a pillar is taken for a man, in glory and beauty excellent. For he says not that he will erecte a pillar for a godly man: but I will, says he, make him a pillar, that is to say, I will beautify him with honours and glory everlasting.
[DRAFT]
Our Lord proceeds in recounting much more ample rewards, which he would give to those who overcome: and so he tempers his words, that we may easily perceive that this promise not only pertains to the congregation of Philadelphia, but to all the churches in the whole world, yes, and to every of the faithful. And as we have oftentimes repeated already (for I am not ashamed to repeat, saying that the Lord himself so greatly urges the victory), again we say, that those things are promised not to such as fight lightly or negligently (for diverse fight, and by and by run away) but to those that overcome and persevere to the end. For our life is a warfare upon earth: which Job also has confessed. The soldier has a sure purpose to overcome his enemies. Our enemies are the devil, the world, and the flesh. Against these we must earnestly fight: of none other intent, but that we may overcome. The Apostle in the 6th to the Ephesians described the armor of the faithful. In victory the Saints consider sincerity and integrity, that we lose nothing of the verity known: but let us retain the pure word of God, and sincere faith, and let us keep our bodies and our souls clean from all pollution, and that to our lives’ end. He propounds most ample rewards by promise. Whereby he alludes to the manner of Greeks and Romans, who decreed images to those who deserved well of the common wealth, in which also they wrote their virtues, for whose cause they were set up either in the court or marketplace or else where. They seemed by this means to deliver to their posterity as it were by hand the glory of their elders, which they made also as it were everlasting. Otherwise the use of this vocable Column, or pillar is diverse. Jeremiah is called by God a pillar for his constancy. The Apostles are called by Paul in the 2nd to the Galatians chief pillars, for their excellency, and that the churches leaned upon them, for the preaching of the verity. The church itself also is called the pillar and base of verity, for as much as it is grounded upon the sure rock Christ. In the Temple of Solomon stood two columns or pillars, figures of the everlasting kingdom of Christ, and of the holy church. In this place a pillar is taken for a man, in glory and beauty excellent. For he says not that he will erect a pillar for a godly man: but I will, says he, make him a pillar, that is to say, I will beautify him with honors and glory everlasting.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "raccomptynge" is modernized to "recounting." While functionally similar, "raccomptynge" carries a sense of careful accounting or detailing, which "recounting" lacks. This subtle nuance is lost.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The phrase "to our lives end" is changed to "to our lives’ end.” The original’s phrasing is slightly ambiguous – does it refer to the end of one's life, or the end of a specific period? The addition of the possessive apostrophe removes this ambiguity, making it definitively about the end of one's life, which might not have been the original intent.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
[FINAL]
Our Lord proceeds in raccomptynge much more ample rewards, which he would give to those who overcome: and so he tempers his words, that we may easily perceive that this promise not only pertains to the congregation of Philadelphia, but to all the churches in the whole world, yes, and to every of the faithful. [ORIGINAL]
But where shall this pillar be set? where shall the glory of Saints be famous?[note 19.5] not in Court, or market place: but in the Temple of my God. And the Temple of God, is heaven it self, and in our world the holy Church. Gloriouſe therefore shall he be in the church of Saints, as well militaunt, as triumphaunt. Albeit therefore that the very godly hear evil in this world: yet this world shall peryſhe, Christ shall reign for ever, and the Saints shall reign with him. Their glory then shall remain for ever and ever. And where he says, of my God, Arethas expounds and says: This ſaiynge of my God, takes not away the divine nature, that is scene in Christ, but establishes, as I may say, the conſubſtantialitie. For it declares the union of two natures, which are in the person of our Lord Jesus, to wit of his deity and humanity, even after understanding, yet not confuſely, to be indiſſobible. For they answer mutualy one another, because of the aſſumptio of the humane nature, the divine to the humane, and likewise the humane to the divine proprieties. &c.
[DRAFT]
But where shall this pillar be set? where shall the glory of the saints be renowned? not in a court or marketplace, but in the temple of my God. And the temple of God is heaven itself, and in this world, the holy Church. Therefore, he will be glorious in the church of the saints, both those engaged in warfare and those who have already triumphed. Although the very godly hear evil in this world, this world will perish, Christ will reign forever, and the saints will reign with him. Their glory will remain forever and ever. And where he says, “of my God,” Arethas explains and says: This saying “of my God” does not diminish the divine nature seen in Christ, but establishes, as it were, the consubstantiality. For it declares the union of two natures that are in the person of our Lord Jesus, namely, his deity and his humanity, even after understanding, yet not confusedly, to be inseparable. For they answer one another mutually because of the assumption of the human nature, the divine to the human, and likewise the human to the divine properties. &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "famous" changed to "renowned." While synonyms, "famous" carries a slightly more established, historical weight appropriate to the original context. "Renowned" feels more modern and less weighty.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "both those engaged in warfare and those who have already triumphed" is an interpretation of "militaunt, as triumphaunt." The original leaves the distinction more open to reader understanding.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "indiſſobible" is softened to "inseparable." "Indiſſobible" implies a more profound, theological impossibility of separation, a nuance lost in "inseparable."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

And the temple of God is heaven itself, and in this world, the holy Church. Therefore, he will be glorious in the church of the saints, both those engaged in warfare and those who have already triumphed. Although the very godly hear evil in this world, this world will perish, Christ will reign forever, and the saints will reign with him. Their glory will remain forever and ever. For they answer one another mutually because of the assumption of the human nature, the divine to the human, and likewise the human to the divine properties. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
Here follows of the inscription of the pillar,[note 19.7] of what sort it shall be. Three things chiefly are written in the Saints, to wit the name of God, the name also of the city of God, and the new name of God or of Christ. Which we shall discuss in order. First the name of God is ascribed to the godly, that is, they them selves are called by the name of God, and be the children and heirs of God. Which is discoursed at large in the first of John, and the viii. to the Romains. [note 19.8] And what can you devise to be more honourable, than to be, and be called the son, not of king nor Emperor, but of the living God? But this same noble grace the Lord grants to the that overcome. In the first of John the iii. and v. [note 19.9] Chap. Secondly to the ouercomers is inscribed the name of the city of God: that is to say, the Godly man is written in the no ber of the citez ns of the city of God, and is verily a citezen of the city of God, I say even of the city of God. It was a great matter in times past, to be a citezen of Rome: But it is far greater to be a citezen of the city of God. The citizens enjoy all privileges and commodities, finally the glory of the city.[note 19.10] But this is greater and more than that it can be declared at few words.
[DRAFT]
Here follows a description of the inscription on the pillar,[note 19.7] of what sort it shall be. Three things chiefly are written on it, namely, the name of God, the name also of the city of God, and the new name of God or of Christ. Which we shall discuss in order. First, the name of God is ascribed to the godly, that is, they themselves are called by the name of God, and are the children and heirs of God. This is discussed at length in 1 John, and in Romans 8. [note 19.8] And what can you devise to be more honorable than to be, and be called the son, not of a king nor emperor, but of the living God? But this same noble grace the Lord grants to those who overcome. In 1 John 3 and 5 [note 19.9] chapters. Secondly, the name of the city of God is inscribed for the overcomers: that is to say, the godly man is written in the number of the citizens of the city of God, and is truly a citizen of the city of God, I say even of the city of God. It was a great matter in times past to be a citizen of Rome: but it is far greater to be a citizen of the city of God. The citizens enjoy all privileges and commodities, finally the glory of the city.[note 19.10] But this is greater and more than that it can be declared in few words.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "to the Romains" changed to "in Romans 8." While technically correct, the original phrasing implies a broader discussion within the book of Romans, not just chapter 8. The draft's specificity narrows the intended scope.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "to the that overcome" changed to "to those who overcome." The original phrasing, while archaic, carries a slightly more nuanced sense of ongoing struggle and triumph. The draft's wording is more straightforward and loses some of that implication.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "truly a citizen" added after "a citizen of the city of God" is an interpretation not present in the original.
6. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
[FINAL]
Here follows a description of the inscription on the pillar,[note 19.7] of what sort it shall be. Three things chiefly are written on it, namely, the name of God, the name also of the city of God, and the new name of God or of Christ. Which we shall discuss in order. First, the name of God is ascribed to the godly, that is, they themselves are called by the name of God, and are the children and heirs of God. This is discussed at length in 1 John, and in Romans 8. [note 19.8] And what can you devise to be more honorable than to be, and be called the son, not of a king nor emperor, but of the living God? But this same noble grace the Lord grants to those who overcome. In 1 John 3 and 5 [note 19.9] chapters. Secondly, the name of the city of God is inscribed for the overcomers: that is to say, the godly man is written in the number of the citizens of the city of God, and is truly a citizen of the city of God, I say even of the city of God. It was a great matter in times past to be a citizen of Rome: but it is far greater to be a citizen of the city of God. The citizens enjoy all privileges and commodities, finally the glory of the city.[note 19.10] But this is greater and more than that it can be declared in few words.
---
[ORIGINAL]
But the church, is the city of God: And the city of God, is the church. Which is here set forth with three epithets or titles, of the which it is easy to judge, what the church is, or what we should think thereof. The church is the city of God. For like as the city, is the fellowship of citizens: Even so is the church the communion of saints. The Prince of the is Christ, the head of the church. The round world it self was a figure of this church, and the very setting up of the tents, in the midst whereof was scene the Tabernacle, a token of the deity present, as it were a cohabiter. &c. For the Lord is in the midst of the church. As we have red in the xxvi. of Leuit. And in the.2. to the Corinth. the.6. Secondly the church is called new Jerusalem. For the old was a figure of the new. This corporal church is new Hieruſale, that is to say spiritual. Which S. Paul also affirms in the iiii. to the Galath. For in the third place is expounded that newnes. It is not builded of men, but comes down from heaven aboue. For unless we be borne from aboue of spirit and of ſede immortal to wit the word of God, we can not be members of the church. And we are borne by a spiritual regeneration, the children of Christ and of the church. Whereof the Lord himself discourses at large in the iii. of John.
[DRAFT]
But the church is the city of God: and the city of God is the church. This is presented here with three epithets or titles, from which it is easy to judge what the church is, or what we should think of it. The church is the city of God. For just as a city is the fellowship of citizens, so the church is the communion of saints. The Prince of it is Christ, the head of the church. The entire world itself was a figure of this church, and the very setting up of the tents, in the midst of which was seen the Tabernacle, a token of the deity present, as it were a cohabiter. And so on. For the Lord is in the midst of the church, as we have read in Leviticus 26 and 2 Corinthians 6. Secondly, the church is called New Jerusalem. For the old [?] was a figure of the new. This earthly church is New Jerusalem, that is to say, spiritual. Paul also affirms this in Galatians 4. For the third place explains this newness. It is not built by humans, but comes down from heaven above. For unless we are born from above by spirit and by immortal seed, namely the word of God, we cannot be members of the church. And we are born by a spiritual regeneration, the children of Christ and of the church, as the Lord himself discusses at length in John 3.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "It is easy to judge, what the church is, or what we should think thereof" changed to "it is easy to judge what the church is, or what we should think of it." While seemingly minor, the original phrasing implies a process of discernment or evaluation, which the draft loses.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "This is presented here with three epithets or titles" is an interpretive addition not present in the original. The original simply states the existence of the titles.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "old" is bracketed with a question mark in the draft. The original text leaves open what "old" refers to (the old covenant, old Jerusalem, etc.). The draft's hesitation removes this ambiguity without justification.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: "And so on" is an added phrase not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
But the church is the city of God: and the city of God is the church. This is presented here with three epithets or titles, from which it is easy to judge what the church is, or what we should think of it. The church is the city of God. For just as a city is the fellowship of citizens, so the church is the communion of saints. The Prince of it is Christ, the head of the church. The entire world itself was a figure of this church, and the very setting up of the tents, in the midst of which was seen the Tabernacle, a token of the deity present, as it were a cohabiter. And so on. For the Lord is in the midst of the church, as we have read in Leviticus 26 and 2 Corinthians 6. Secondly, the church is called New Jerusalem. For the old [?] was a figure of the new. This earthly church is New Jerusalem, that is to say, spiritual. Paul also affirms this in Galatians 4. For the third place explains this newness. It is not built by humans, but comes down from heaven above. For unless we are born from above by spirit and by immortal seed, namely the word of God, we cannot be members of the church. And we are born by a spiritual regeneration, the children of Christ and of the church, as the Lord himself discusses at length in John 3.

[note 19.11]Finally in them that overcome is written a new name, and that in deed the new name of Christ: Not only that they should be called Christians of Christ, but because the name is a brief description of every thing and nature, and a new name is promised: It follows that we should understand that men shall be renewed, chiefly by glorifiyng. He promises therefore a glorifiyng to the Godly. Whereof is spoke else where in the xvii. of S. Math. 1. Cor. xv. Phil. iii. And the first of John the iii. These most ample rewards the saints may verily look for, if they fight that they may overcome.

These most ample rewards the saints may truly look for, if they fight that they may overcome.

Hereunto is annexed the wonted acclamation, by the which both this doctrine is applied and communicated to all churches through out the world. And is declared that it came not of men as vain, but of the very spirit of God most true. This spirit the Lord grant us.
[DRAFT]
Hereunto is annexed the customary affirmation, by which both this doctrine is applied and communicated to all churches throughout the world. And it is declared that it did not come from men as a frivolous thing, but from the very spirit of God, most true. This spirit the Lord grant us.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "wonted acclamation" to "customary affirmation" subtly shifts the sense. "Wonted" implies a familiar, traditional greeting, while "customary" is more neutral and could suggest a formal declaration rather than a heartfelt expression.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "vain" to "frivolous thing" adds a layer of interpretation. "Vain" in 1561 could imply empty, deceptive, or lacking substance – a broader critique than simply "frivolous." The draft's choice narrows the potential meaning.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "came not of men as vain" leaves open the possibility that human involvement, however flawed, was present. The draft's "did not come from men as a frivolous thing" more strongly asserts a purely divine origin, removing a potential nuance.
[FINAL]
Hereunto is annexed the wonted acclamation, by which both this doctrine is applied and communicated to all churches throughout the world. And it is declared that it came not of men as vain, but of the very spirit of God most true. This spirit the Lord grant us.

## ¶ The Lord blames sore the church of Laodicea. The xx. Sermon.

[ORIGINAL]
AND unto the Angel of the congregation, which is in Laodices write: This saith Amen the faithful and true witness, the beginning of the creatures of God. I know thy works, that thou art neither cold nor hot: I would thou were cold or hoate. So then because thou art between both, and neither cold nor hoate, I will spew thee out of my mouth: Because thou say: I am rich and increased with goods, and have need of nothig: And know not that thou art wretched, and miserable, poor, blind and naked.
[DRAFT]
And unto the angel of the congregation which is in Laodicea, write: This saith Amen, the faithful and true witness, the beginning of the creation of God. I know your works, that you are neither cold nor hot; I wish you were cold or hot. So then, because you are lukewarm, neither cold nor hot, I will spit you out of my mouth. Because you say, ‘I am rich and increased with goods, and have need of nothing,’ and do not know that you are wretched, miserable, poor, blind, and naked.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "creatures of God" changed to "creation of God." While related, "creatures" implies active beings, whereas "creation" is broader, encompassing all that is made. This subtly shifts the emphasis.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "lukewarm" inserted to explain "between both, and neither cold nor hoate." The original leaves the state ambiguous, allowing for varied interpretation. This addition imposes a specific understanding.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "hoate" is unusual and potentially carries a nuance lost in "hot." It's not simply about temperature but a more intense, possibly zealous, state.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And unto the angel of the congregation which is in Laodicea, write: This saith Amen, the faithful and true witness, the beginning of the creation of God. I know your works, that you are neither cold nor hot; I wish you were cold or hot. So then, because you are lukewarm, neither cold nor hot, I will spit you out of my mouth. Because you say, ‘I am rich and increased with goods, and have need of nothing,’ and do not know that you are wretched, miserable, poor, blind, and naked.

[ORIGINAL]
The seventh and last Epistle of our Saviour Christ,[note 20.1] is written by the hand of S. John, to the Bishop of Laodicea. The same is a great reproach of that people, in nothing commendable: And nevertheless a faithful admonition or exhortation to repentance. And after his accustomed manner, he signifies to whom he writes, and from whom the Epistle proceeds. The Epistle is indicted of Christ to the Bishop of Laodicea, and to the whole congregation. Therefore some thing is to be said of the Laodiceans, whereby the rest may the better be understand and considered.
[DRAFT]

[ORIGINAL]
Laodicea the chief city of Caria, after Strabo and Plinie, stands by the river of Lycus. Antiochus Theos, [note 20.2] builded the city, and named it after his wife. It was the welthieſt city of Asia: Which Vadiane also hath noted in his Epitome. It had by making of woollen clothe a most plentiful gain. Unto whom S. Paul seems also to have preached the gospel. For he mentions of Laodicea, from whence also some men think he wrote the first Epistle unto Timothee.[note 20.3] Certainly it appears that the Laodiceas had received the gospel, even by this Epistle, but corruptely. For they went about to match the world and the church together, and to join together Christ and Mammon: And as it is said at this day.
[DRAFT]
Laodicea, the chief city of Caria, stands by the river Lycus, as Strabo and Pliny report. Antiochus Theos built the city and named it after his wife. It was the wealthiest city in Asia, as Vadian also noted in his Epitome. It had a very profitable trade in woolen cloth. It appears that Paul also preached the gospel there. For he mentions Laodicea, and some believe he wrote his first letter to Timothy from there. Certainly, it seems the Laodiceans had received the gospel, even through this letter, but in a corrupted way. For they sought to blend the world and the church together, and to join Christ and Mammon, as is said today.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "welthieſt" to "wealthiest" is a minor shift but loses some of the original's emphasis on extreme wealth.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "It appears that Paul also preached the gospel there" is an interpretation, not directly stated in the original. The original says "Unto whom S. Paul seems also to have preached the gospel," which is more tentative.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "from whence also some men think he wrote the first Epistle unto Timothee" is slightly more indirect than "and some believe he wrote his first letter to Timothy from there." The original allows for more doubt.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: "It had a very profitable trade in woolen cloth" is not explicitly stated in the original; it's an elaboration.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

It was the wealthiest city in Asia, as Vadian also noted in his Epitome. It had a very profitable trade in woolen cloth. It appears that Paul also preached the gospel there. [ORIGINAL]
[note 20.4]Therefore they laid not aside their avarice, and their immoderate traffic (to use moderately the trade of merchandise without diſceptfulnes, no religion doth forbid) and exceading great riot and pride, neither seemed they to want any thing, but to have and seem to have all things, for that they were rich. Against these me the Lord inueigheth grievously, declaring them to be very miserable, and more than nedy and plain beggars. For as in the church of Philadelphia he blamed nothing, so in this he commends nothing at all.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 20.5]You shall find at this day many like, to whom this is common and ever in their mouth: I have learned both to be a goſpeller, and to be a soldier, to drink, to play the whoremonger, and live at pleasure. You shall find like churches, serving both Christ and Mammon, or merchandise, Bacchus, Venus, and God of battle. Both they and all these here are confuted, and are called to repentance. Which argues, that the mercy of God is greatest, not forsaking nor rejecting so corrupt churches, and men full of so great filthines.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 20.6]Christ is here again most plentifully described, who he is as in the former titles. Certes it may be gathered of all that this is the best and most perfect description of Christ, that there is no need to beg of any other, to wit of humane matters. He sets forth him self with a new name, and calls him, Ho amen, that Amen. That same is an Hebrewe word, and most commonly used in the Euangeliſtes, especially in John, S. Paul in the ii. to the Corinthians the first Chapter. Christ the son of God, saith he, which by us is preached amongst you, by me and by Syluanus and Timothee was not yea and nay, but in him it was yea. For all the promeſſes of God are in him yea, and in him are amen to the praise of God by us. &c. But the Lord expounds him self, why he called him self that Amen. For I am, saith he, that witness, I mean that trusty or faithful or constant and true. For Christ is given us of the father, that he should testify of the will of God the father. And his testimony, as he him self repeteth oftener than once in the Gospel of S. John, is firm, constant, sure, certain and true, having no falſetie, doubtfulnes, nor inconstancy. And these things accorde right well to this argument, wherein he reproves the Laodicians of sin, and exhorts them to repentance. It is a grievous matter for the flesh to hear such a doctrine: but where the certentie, aſſurednes, or verity of the teacher is perceived, it will commonly move men's minds if they be not altogether abject and desperate.
[DRAFT]
Christ is here again most plentifully described, who he is as in the former titles. Certainly, it may be gathered from all that has been said that this is the best and most perfect description of Christ, so that there is no need to seek further clarification from human sources. He sets forth himself with a new name, and calls him, “Ho Amen,” that is Amen. That same is a Hebrew word, and most commonly used in the Evangelists, especially in John, Paul in 2 Corinthians 1:20. “Christ is the Son of God,” he says, “whom we preach among you, I and Sylvanus and Timothy, was not yea and nay, but in him it was yea. For all the promises of God are in him yea, and in him are amen to the praise of God by us.” And the Lord explains why he called himself that Amen: “For I am he who is a witness,” that is, a trustworthy, faithful, constant, and true one. For Christ is given to us by the Father to testify of the will of God the Father. And his testimony, as he himself repeats often in the Gospel of John, is firm, constant, sure, certain, and true, having no falsehood, doubtfulness, or inconstancy. And these things accord well with this argument, wherein he reproves the Laodiceans for sin and exhorts them to repentance. It is a grievous matter for the flesh to hear such doctrine, but where the certainty, assuredness, or truth of the teacher is perceived, it will commonly move men's minds if they are not altogether abject and desperate.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "It is a grievous matter for the flesh to hear such a doctrine" is softened to "It is a grievous matter for the flesh to hear such doctrine." The original carries a stronger sense of moral struggle.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "so that there is no need to seek further clarification from human sources" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. It injects a theological perspective.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "certentie, aſſurednes, or verity" is flattened to "certainty, assuredness, or truth." While not a major issue, the original’s repetition emphasizes the multifaceted nature of truth being described.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** Syluanus is misspelled as Sylvanus.
[FINAL]
