[ORIGINAL]
He adds moreover an other thing,[note 20.7] which declares his dignity. For he calls him self the beginning of the creatures of God. Neither ought the Arrians to seek here any defence for them selves. For neither is it mete by any one place, much less by a little word, to ſubuerte the whole scripture, and to strive with the articles of the cred, the lively tradition of the Apostles. Our saviour Christ is considered after his deity and after his humanity. After his deity, he hath no beginning, but is rather the beginning (actively as it is commonly said, not passively of all things and creatures. Neither is he a creature: For all things are made by him. Which thing both the Evangelical and Apostolical scriptures prove John. 1. Coloſ. 1. and the Hebr. 1. where thou hast places expoſitours of this same one. After his humanity he is called the beginning of the creature of God (namely man, which is called a creature by reason of his excellency, and for that he is the Lord of creatures, for whom all things were made) as he is called the first begotten of the dead. For in Christ mankynde is repared, that it hath not perished: God looked upon the countenance of his Christ, when he first made man. For Christ is the beginning, that is to say, the preserver of the humane nature: As it hath else where been told you at large. Hitherto we have had the description of Christ, which is called Amen, and the beginning of the creature of God, by whom verily all things are made, which is very and true God, witness of the divine will of God. &c.
[DRAFT]
He adds moreover another thing,[note 20.7] which declares his dignity. For he calls himself the beginning of the creatures of God. Neither ought the Arians to seek here any defense for themselves. For neither is it fitting by any one place, much less by a little word, to subvert the whole scripture and to strive with the articles of the creed, the lively tradition of the Apostles. Our savior Christ is considered after his deity and after his humanity. After his deity, he has no beginning, but is rather the beginning (actively as it is commonly said, not passively of all things and creatures. Neither is he a creature: for all things are made by him. Which thing both the Evangelical and Apostolic scriptures prove, John 1 and Colossians 1, and the Hebrews 1, where you have passages expounding this same one. After his humanity he is called the beginning of the creature of God (namely man, which is called a creature by reason of his excellency, and for that he is the Lord of creatures, for whom all things were made) as he is called the firstborn of the dead. For in Christ mankind is repaired, that it has not perished: God looked upon the countenance of his Christ when he first made man. For Christ is the beginning, that is to say, the preserver of the human nature: as it has elsewhere been told you at large. Hitherto we have had the description of Christ, which is called Amen, and the beginning of the creature of God, by whom verily all things are made, which is very and true God, witness of the divine will of God. &c.

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "passages expounding this same one" in the draft is an unnecessary interpretation. The original simply states "places expoſitours of this same one," which is a less assertive statement.

4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals detected.

For in Christ mankind is repaired, that it has not perished: God looked upon the countenance of his Christ when he first made man. For Christ is the beginning, that is to say, the preserver of the human nature: as it has elsewhere been told you at large. Hitherto we have had the description of Christ, which is called Amen, and the beginning of the creature of God, by whom verily all things are made, which is very and true God, witness of the divine will of God. &c.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Now he tells the church what opinion he hath of her, and what she is, that is to say blames her. And as he hath beaten in to all the former, that he knew all their works: so doth he to this also. And first he shows, that he knows this of the church of Laodicea, and especially of the Bishop thereof that he is neither cold, nor hot. He adds, [illegible], I would it were better, if thou were altogether cold, or thoroughly hot: But now thou art lewke warm, or blood warm. An Allegory taken of men's meat, or of cold, hot, or warm water, and it is in a manner applied prouerbially. He is cold, that openly follows the world,[note 20.8] being wrapped in heathenniſſhe errors and sins of this world, he boasts no thing, or will seem to have any thing to doe with the true Religion.[note 20.9] He is hot, whose breast inflames with the holy ghost, contemns the world, loves the true Religion exceedingly, and lives an holy life. He is warm or between both,[note 20.10] which hath neither forsaken the world, his errors, and sins, nor hath fully received Christ, his verity and rightuouſnes, but serves partly the world, partly Christ: In outward things he shows him self to be a Christian, in resorting to holy aſſemblees, and receiving the Sacramentes, but inwardly he is so besieged of the world, that he lives a worldly life, rather than a Christian. Such a mixture the Lord allows not,[note 20.11] which else where forbids to plow with an Ox and an Ass, and to make a garment of linen and woollen: To pour new wine into old bottels, and to patch an old garment with new cloth.
[DRAFT]
Now he tells the church what opinion he has of her, and what she is, that is to say, blames her. And as he has impressed upon all the former, that he knew all their works, so he does to this also. First, he shows that he knows this of the church of Laodicea, and especially of the Bishop thereof, that he is neither cold nor hot. He adds, I would it were better if thou were altogether cold, or thoroughly hot; but now thou art lukewarm, or blood warm. An allegory taken of men's food, or of cold, hot, or warm water, and it is applied in a manner proverbially. He is cold, who openly follows the world, being wrapped in heathenish errors and sins of this world, boasts nothing, or will seem to have nothing to do with the true religion. He is hot, whose breast inflames with the Holy Spirit, contemns the world, loves the true religion exceedingly, and lives a holy life. He is warm, or between both, which has neither forsaken the world, his errors, and sins, nor has fully received Christ, his truth and righteousness, but serves partly the world, partly Christ: In outward things he shows himself to be a Christian, in resorting to holy assemblies, and receiving the Sacraments, but inwardly he is so besieged of the world, that he lives a worldly life, rather than a Christian. Such a mixture the Lord allows not, which elsewhere forbids to plow with an ox and an ass, and to make a garment of linen and woollen: to pour new wine into old bottles, and to patch an old garment with new cloth.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "beaten in to" changed to "impressed upon." While conveying a similar idea, "beaten in" suggests forceful repetition and emphasis, a nuance potentially lost with "impressed upon."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "his truth and righteousness" is added to "his verity and rightuouſnes." While not incorrect, it adds a layer of interpretation not explicitly present.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "blood warm" is changed to "lukewarm, or blood warm." The original leaves "blood warm" as a less common, potentially more evocative descriptor. The draft's addition of "lukewarm" diminishes this ambiguity.
4. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Now he tells the church what opinion he has of her, and what she is, that is to say, blames her. And as he has impressed upon all the former, that he knew all their works, so he does to this also. First, he shows that he knows this of the church of Laodicea, and especially of the Bishop thereof, that he is neither cold nor hot. [ORIGINAL]
[note 20.12]In Religions and rytes that lightness and mixture can be less allowed of God. For you shall have, that will temper together sundry Religions, and of many compile some one. Mahomet composed his Religion of the Iewyſhe and christian Religion. Many at this day make an hogepotche of papiſtrie and the Gospel, or bake a chuchurnullis as the Germans call a cake of sundry grains. If a Papiſte see this kind of service, he knows it not for his: And if the Goſpeler see it, he knows it for none of his. For it is a mixture of whole and corrupt, where the sound part hath no more strength, the corrupt for the most part hath the greater. Of such sort are the masses that are used at this day of many, neither altogether Papiſticall, nor yet wholly Evangelical. For the Lords supper appears not in them: The Popiſhe mass also is cut of and altered in the same. If we believe that Christ set forth the best rule of Religion and living, why follow we not that same only Master? But we set more by the favour of men, which in no wise we will lose. For we set not so much by the favour of Christ, as to call that saying of the Apostle to memory, if I should please men I should not be Christ's servant.
[DRAFT]
In religious practices and rites, a lessening of God’s allowance can come from lightness and mixture. For you will find those who attempt to blend various religions, compiling many into one. Muhammad composed his religion from Jewish and Christian teachings. Many today create a mishmash of Roman Catholicism and the Gospel, or bake a “chuchurnullis,” as the Germans call a cake of diverse grains. If a Roman Catholic sees this kind of service, he does not recognize it as his; and if an adherent of the Gospel sees it, he does not recognize it as belonging to him. For it is a mixture of the pure and the corrupt, where the pure part has no more strength, and the corrupt part generally has the greater. Masses used by many today are of this sort, neither entirely Roman Catholic nor wholly Evangelical. The Lord’s Supper does not appear in them; the Roman Catholic mass is also cut off and altered in the same. If we believe that Christ established the best rule for religion and living, why do we not follow only that Master? But we value the favor of men more, which we will in no way lose. For we do not value the favor of Christ as much as to recall the Apostle’s saying: “If I please men, I am not Christ’s servant.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "lightness and mixture" is softened to "a lessening of God’s allowance can come from lightness and mixture." The original implies a more severe judgment.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft inserts "Roman Catholicism" where "Papiſtrie" originally stood, adding a modern denominational label.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "Goſpeler" is modernized to "adherent of the Gospel," losing the original's more general reference to those following the Gospel.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "Papiſtrie" is changed to "Roman Catholicism." While not incorrect, it alters the nuance of the original's broader critique of Papal authority.
5. NO ISSUES regarding hallucination.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Hierom/Jerome is not present. Austen/Augustine is not present. Ireney/Irenaeus is not present. Mahomet/Muhammad is correct.
[FINAL]
In religious practices and rites, a lessening of God’s allowance can come from lightness and mixture. For you will find those who attempt to blend various religions, compiling many into one. Muhammad composed his religion from Jewish and Christian teachings. Many today create a mishmash of Roman Catholicism and the Gospel, or bake a “chuchurnullis,” as the Germans call a cake of diverse grains. If a Roman Catholic sees this kind of service, he does not recognize it as his; and if an adherent of the Gospel sees it, he does not recognize it as belonging to him. For it is a mixture of the pure and the corrupt, where the pure part has no more strength, and the corrupt part generally has the greater. Masses used by many today are of this sort, neither entirely Roman Catholic nor wholly Evangelical. The Lord’s Supper does not appear in them; the Roman Catholic mass is also cut off and altered in the same. If we believe that Christ established the best rule for religion and living, why do we not follow only that Master? But we value the favor of men more, which we will in no way lose. For we do not value the favor of Christ as much as to recall the Apostle’s saying: “If I please men, I am not Christ’s servant.”

[ORIGINAL]
But hear what the Lord saith to these mungerelles.[note 20.13] It were better, saith he, thou were either cold or hot. It were better thou were a ſynner or an heathen, than an Hipocrite, and a mongerell. For so might thou be more easily help, according to that saying of the Lord, If you were blind, the should have no sin.[note 20.14] Now where the seem to your selves just and sufficiently taught and furnished with Godly rites and cultes that please God, you leave no place to further instruction, but contemning the word of God, and Christ's institution, the prefer your mixtures before all the justifications of God. The Lord also in the Gospel saith unto the Pharyſeis: Amen I say unto you, that Publicanes and co mon harlottes go before you into the kingdom of God.[note 20.15]
[DRAFT]
But hear what the Lord says to these mongrels. It would be better, he says, if you were either cold or hot. It would be better if you were a sinner or a heathen than a hypocrite and a mongrel. For then you might be more easily helped, according to that saying of the Lord, “If you were blind, you would have no sin.” Now, when you seem to yourselves to be just and sufficiently taught and furnished with godly rites and cults that please God, you leave no place for further instruction, but, contemning the word of God and Christ's institution, you prefer your mixtures before all the justifications of God. The Lord also in the Gospel says to the Pharisees: “Truly, I say to you, that tax collectors and prostitutes go before you into the kingdom of God.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "It were better thou were a sinner or an heathen" is softened to "It would be better if you were a sinner or a heathen." While functionally similar, the original carries a stronger, more condemnatory tone.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of quotation marks around "If you were blind, you would have no sin" and "Truly, I say to you" introduces an interpretive layer, suggesting direct quotation that may not have been intended in the original.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: The addition of "Truly" before "I say to you" is not present in the original.
6. LOST AMBIGUITY: No issues.

The other membre is plain enough, that it were better they were hot, namely with the spirit of God, which thing the Apostle requires in the xii. Chapter. to the Romains.

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "hot" is somewhat ambiguous, allowing for multiple interpretations of desired spiritual quality. "Fervent" narrows this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present to assess.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
The other member is clear enough; it would be better if it were hot, namely with the Spirit of God, which thing the Apostle requires in the twelfth chapter to the Romans.

[ORIGINAL]
Furthermore he threatens to plague them, if they continue, as they have begone to be newters: [illegible].[note 20.16] I will spew thee out of my mouth. By the which manner of speaking two things are signified: Both the lothſomes which God conceaueth of this newtralitie or warmnes: And the vomiting out, which punishes the same. warm water provokes a vomit. Wheruto he appears to have alluded, as likewise to that old phrase of speaking, the land hath vomyted the Chananites, and the same shall vomit up you also. Therefore these composers or mongerelles with their temperature and mixture doe so displease God, that they engender in him a lothſomnes, be unto him an abomination, that finally he shakes them of, the same we understand of them that join together Christ and Mammon. And the phrase of speech is to be noted, [illegible] now therefore, or so forasmuch as, or now seeing it is so, &c. Moreover the longaminitie, or lunge suffering of God is here noted, which plageth not immediately, unless there appear nowher any hope of amedemet.
[DRAFT]
Furthermore, he threatens to plague them if they continue as they have begun to be neutrals. I will spew you out of my mouth. By this manner of speaking, two things are signified: both the loathsomeness which God conceives of this neutrality or lukewarmness, and the vomiting out, which punishes it. Warm water provokes a vomit. To this, he appears to have alluded, as well as to that old phrase of speaking: “the land has vomited the Canaanites,” and the same shall vomit you up also. Therefore, these composers or mongers with their temperance and mixture so displease God that they engender in him loathsomeness, are an abomination to him, that finally he shakes them off. The same we understand of those who join together Christ and Mammon. And the phrase of speech is to be noted, either “now therefore” or “so forasmuch as” or “now seeing it is so,” etc. Moreover, God’s longanimity, or suffering, is here noted, which does not plague immediately unless there appears nowhere any hope of amendment.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "newters" is modernized to "neutrals." While close, "newters" carried a specific connotation of sexual ambiguity and moral laxity beyond simple neutrality, a nuance lost in "neutrals."
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "temperance and mixture" replaces "temperature and mixture." While intending to clarify, "temperature" in the original likely referred to a lack of zeal or fervor, a theological concept, not simply moderation. The draft's "temperance" shifts the meaning.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "[illegible]" is retained in the draft, but the surrounding explanation of phrases like "now therefore" is overly prescriptive. The original allowed for reader interpretation of the phrase's function within the text. The draft's explanation limits that possibility.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]
Furthermore, he threatens to plague them if they continue as they have begun to be neutrals. I will spew you out of my mouth. By this manner of speaking, two things are signified: both the loathsomeness which God conceives of this neutrality or lukewarmness, and the vomiting out, which punishes it. Warm water provokes a vomit. To this, he appears to have alluded, as well as to that old phrase of speaking: “the land has vomited the Canaanites,” and the same shall vomit you up also. Therefore, these composers or mongers with their temperance and mixture so displease God that they engender in him loathsomeness, are an abomination to him, that finally he shakes them off. [ORIGINAL]
[note 20.17]He expounds more fully the sin of the Laodiceans, and what is the cause of their tepiditie: Because they love riches, wherein they trust, supposing them selves to want nothing: They think them selves to be wise, and to se all things, and to be sufficiently furnished with things spiritual and temporal. It is less, where they say, we are rich. More that follows, I am increased with goods: That is to say, I have gotten so much riches, that I want nothing.
[DRAFT]
He explains more fully the sin of the Laodiceans, and what is the cause of their lukewarmness: because they love riches, in which they trust, supposing themselves to want nothing. They think themselves to be wise, and to see all things, and to be sufficiently furnished with spiritual and temporal things. It is less, where they say, “we are rich.” More follows, “I am increased with goods”: that is to say, I have gotten so much riches that I want nothing.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "tepiditie" to "lukewarmness" is a softening of the original's condemnation. "Tepiditie" implies a moral failing, while "lukewarmness" is more neutral.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The inclusion of quotation marks around "we are rich" and "I am increased with goods" implies a direct quote, which isn't indicated in the original. This subtly frames the phrases as distinct statements rather than part of a continuous description.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing allows for a degree of interpretation regarding the Laodiceans' self-perception. The draft's more direct wording reduces this ambiguity.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 20.18]That same he now confuteth, and shows that they are utterly diſceiued, and to be miserable people. For he rebukes them grievously, and saith, thou know not that thou art such, as thou art. That ignorance, is a great evil, and the beginning of desperate blindness, when a man thinks to have, that he hath not. For such persevere in their error, and admit no counſellour. Therefore saith the Lord, thou know not that thou art [illegible] miserable, wretched, wearied and worn with evils. For they are toiled with many labours, that serve this world. [illegible] miserable. Thou see not thine own misery: Others that se, are full sorry. Thou see not in what case thou art. This kind of speech signifies a man very wretched and desperate, whose misery others see, but he him self sees nothing, [illegible] poor or a beggar. Thou think thy self very rich, but thou art a stark beggar. Covetous rich men, are poor: They are poor also in virtues. The people of Laodicea were blind, as the Phariſeis were called blind in the ix. of John. Well sighted in wordly matters, in heavily blind as betels. Naked, or destitute of good works. Void of thy wedding garment. They notwithstanding were richly arrayed with garments of most five wolle. But before God they appeared naked. Let the gallants of this world, or proud pecockes rather, so well eyed, and gorgeously appareled, mark these things well. The Lord give them understanding.
[DRAFT]
That same he now refutes, and shows that they are utterly deceived, and are to be miserable people. For he rebukes them severely, and says, “You do not know that you are what you are.” That ignorance is a great evil, and the beginning of desperate blindness, when a man thinks he has something that he does not. For such persevere in their error, and admit no counselor. Therefore the Lord says, “You do not know that you are poor, wretched, weary, and worn with evils.” For they are toiled with many labors that serve this world. They are miserable. You do not see your own misery; others who see it are deeply sorry. You do not see in what condition you are. This kind of speech signifies a man very wretched and desperate, whose misery others see, but he himself sees nothing, a poor man or a beggar. You think yourself very rich, but you are a stark beggar. Covetous rich men are poor; they are poor also in virtues. The people of Laodicea were blind, as the Pharisees were called blind in John 9. Well-sighted in worldly matters, yet heavily blind as betels. Naked, or destitute of good works. Void of your wedding garment. They nevertheless were richly arrayed with garments of the finest wool. But before God they appeared naked. Let the gallants of this world, or proud peacocks rather, so well-sighted and gorgeously apparelled, mark these things well. The Lord give them understanding.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "grievously" changed to "severely." While similar, "grievously" carries a weight of moral seriousness absent in "severely."
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "[illegible] miserable, wretched, wearied and worn with evils” changed to “You do not know that you are poor, wretched, weary, and worn with evils.” The original’s illegibility allowed for a broader interpretation of the Lord’s statement; the draft's phrasing narrows it to a specific claim of poverty.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "heavily blind as betels" is unclear in the original and likely a reference to a specific, obscure idiom. The draft's rendering loses this obscurity and potential for varied interpretation.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "ix. of John" changed to "John 9." While functionally equivalent, the original’s Roman numeral is more consistent with the period’s citation style.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is added.
[FINAL]

## ¶ The Lord gives wholesome counsel to the Laodiceans, admonishing them to repent. The.xxj. Sermon.
[DRAFT]
(heading preserved)

## ¶ The Lord gives wholesome counsel to the Laodiceans, admonishing them to repent. The.xxj. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
I Counsel thee to bye of me gold tried in the fire, that thou may be rich: And white raiment, that thou may be clothed, that the shame of thy nakedness do not appear: And annoynte thine eyes with eye salve, that thou may see. As many as I love, I rebuke and chasten. Be fervent therefore and repent.
[DRAFT]
I counsel you to buy from me gold refined in the fire, so that you may be rich; and white garments, so that you may be clothed, and the shame of your nakedness may not be revealed; and anoint your eyes with ointment, so that you may see. As many as I love, I rebuke and chasten. Be zealous, therefore, and repent.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "bye" to "buy" is a standard modernization and doesn't alter meaning. "annoynte" to "anoint" is also acceptable.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None detected. The draft maintains a straightforward rendering.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None detected. The original's phrasing is relatively clear, and the draft doesn't introduce unnecessary specificity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short passage to assess.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.
[FINAL]
I counsel you to buy of me gold refined in the fire, so that you may be rich; and white raiment, so that you may be clothed, that the shame of your nakedness do not appear; and anoint your eyes with eye salve, so that you may see. As many as I love, I rebuke and chasten. Be zealous therefore and repent.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Foraſmuch as God wills not the death of a ſynner,[note 21.1] but rather that he should convert and live: Therefore after a grievous blaming of the church of Laodicea, he gives her holſom counsel, admonishing and exhorting the same to repentance, and signifies with all what is true repentance.
[DRAFT]
Since God does not desire the death of a sinner, but rather that he should repent and live: Therefore, after severely rebuking the church of Laodicea, he gives her wholesome counsel, admonishing and exhorting her to repentance, and indicates with all clarity what true repentance is.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Grievous blaming" softened to "severely rebuking." While similar, "grievous" carries a weight of sorrow and judgment absent in "severely."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Signifies with all what is true repentance" changed to "indicates with all clarity what true repentance is." The original phrasing is more evocative and less explanatory. The draft introduces a didactic element.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing allows for multiple interpretations of *how* God signifies true repentance. The draft’s "indicates with all clarity" removes this ambiguity, implying a direct and unambiguous revelation.
[FINAL]
Since God does not desire the death of a sinner, but rather that he should repent and live: Therefore, after a grievous blaming of the church of Laodicea, he gives her wholesome counsel, admonishing and exhorting her to repentance, and signifies with all what is true repentance.
---
[ORIGINAL]
The Lord uses the word of counselling,[note 21.2] not of commanding, to thintet to confound the madness of them, which unless they be violently drawn, think not them selves admonished, allured or called of the Lord. And whilst they look for such a drawing, they neglect all gods counsel, and fall from the true salvation. God counsels his elect such things as are wholesome: The chosen obey good counsels. God touches their hearts inwardly, and outwardly by preaching of the word, and by sundry admonitions he pooleth and drives man from evil to good. This counsel of God is not to be despised, and an other violet vocation to be imagined. God his word must be heard. To day, saith the Prophet, if the hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. When the Lord counsels with his word, and the hearers harden their minds, they do that through their own fault, and are made authors of their own destruction. But they that receive Gods counsel, received it not by the force of free will, but of the grace of God, which works in us to will and to perform.
[DRAFT]
The Lord uses the word of counseling,[note 21.2] not of commanding, to attempt to confound the madness of those who, unless they are forcefully drawn, do not think themselves admonished, allured, or called by the Lord. And while they look for such a drawing, they neglect all of God’s counsel and fall from true salvation. God counsels his elect with things that are wholesome; the chosen obey good counsel. God touches their hearts inwardly, and outwardly by preaching of the word, and by sundry admonitions he pulls and drives man from evil to good. This counsel of God is not to be despised, and another violent vocation to be imagined. God’s word must be heard. Today, says the Prophet, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. When the Lord counsels with his word, and the hearers harden their minds, they do so through their own fault and are made authors of their own destruction. But those who receive God’s counsel receive it not by the force of free will, but by the grace of God, which works in us to will and to perform.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "thintet" is rendered as "attempt" which alters the nuance. "Thintet" implies an intention or purpose, but "attempt" suggests a possible failure.
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's vagueness regarding the nature of the "drawing" is flattened by the draft's implication of forceful coercion. The original leaves room for a more nuanced understanding of divine invitation.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
The Lord uses the word of counseling,[note 21.2] not of commanding, to attempt to confound the madness of those who, unless they are forcefully drawn, do not think themselves admonished, allured, or called by the Lord. And while they look for such a drawing, they neglect all of God’s counsel and fall from true salvation. God counsels his elect with things that are wholesome; the chosen obey good counsel. God touches their hearts inwardly, and outwardly by preaching of the word, and by sundry admonitions he pulls and drives man from evil to good. This counsel of God is not to be despised, and another violent vocation to be imagined. God’s word must be heard. Today, says the Prophet, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. When the Lord counsels with his word, and the hearers harden their minds, they do so through their own fault and are made authors of their own destruction. But those who receive God’s counsel receive it not by the force of free will, but by the grace of God, which works in us to will and to perform.

Therefore when the Lord counsels wholesome things, the chosen pray that they may receive the same: And they receive them through grace, obeiyng the counsels of God.
[DRAFT]
When the Lord advises beneficial things, the elect pray that they may receive them; and they receive them through grace, obeying the counsel of God.
[CRITIQUE]

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Wholesome" is more nuanced than "beneficial." "Wholesome" implies spiritual health and goodness, while "beneficial" is more neutral.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing allows for a broader understanding of "the counsels of God." The draft's singular "counsel of God" is more restrictive.

[note 21.3]And the some of the wholesome counsel is this: By of me, saith the Lord, gold tried in the fire, that thou may be rich, may by apparel, and may get eye salve to anoint thine eyes. He sets these things as a medicine against the diseases, which he discovered before, calling the church of the Laodiceans, poor, naked, and blind. Now therefore he teaches them how they may be rich, may be clothed, and may receive their eyes or sight again, if they verily get them selves gold tried, or concoct or purified.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 21.4]And gold tried in the fire, is gold most purified and clean, having in it no groſſenes or mettle, but pure and clean gold. Hereby is shadowed the word of God, whereof the Prophet sang: The word of the Lord, is a pure word, silver tried in the fire, seven times pourged in a vessel of earth. Certainly the word of God is light, coming of the eternal and most pure light, having no part of humane filthines or affections, ſauouring of none errors, teaching nothing that is corrupt. How be it of it self it shall profit a man nothing, unless it be received with a true and sincere faith. Therefore do I not separate faith from the word, and say therefore that the pure and sincere faith is signified by gold. Whereof S. Peter said, that the faith of our hearts be pourged. For although there be in us spots and infirmities, yet is faith, by reason of the subject whereupon he rests, most pure.
[DRAFT]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 21.5]For no man shall imagine, that there is bargayning before God, as there is with men: As though the spiritual gifts of God mought be bought for money. This is repugnant to the whole scripture, and specially against the determination of S. Peter pronounced against Simon Magus. But this our exposition the Prophet Essay. Approves in the.55. Chapter. Where amongst other things come, saith he, bye without money, and without price, or exchange. And by and by: I[illegible] hearing hear me, incline your care, &c. Therefore the Romish Chananite hath no hold hereof, I mean the Pope that great merchant, which sells all things in the church, even those things which he hath not, the greatest diſceiuer in the world. Hereunto is added moreover, that like as it is in Essay plainly expressed, of whom such graces or gifts are to be bought: So Christ also here saith expressly, I counsel thee to by of me. Behold he saith of me: Not of the Pope, of Monks, Freers, or priests. For Christ alone hath the things, which we may require. He alone doth satisfy, he alone grants those gifts. And therefore he saith in the Gospel of S. John: Let him that hath honger or thirst, come unto me: To me, I say, let him come. John the iiii. vi. and.vii And S. Peter saith, Lord, to whom shall we go? Thou hast the words of eternal life. As though he should say: If we will live, we can go to none other, but unto thee. Thou art the life and fountain of all goodness.
[DRAFT]
For no man shall imagine that there is bargaining before God, as there is with men: as though the spiritual gifts of God might be bought for money. This is repugnant to the whole scripture, and especially against the determination of Saint Peter pronounced against Simon Magus. But this our exposition the Prophet Isaiah approves in the fifty-fifth chapter, where among other things comes, “Say, ‘Without money, without price, or exchange.’” And immediately following: “If you hear, give heed; incline your ear.” Therefore, the Roman Canaanite has no hold hereof, I mean the Pope, that great merchant, who sells all things in the church, even those things which he does not have, the greatest deceiver in the world. Hereunto is added moreover, that just as it is in Isaiah plainly expressed, of whom such graces or gifts are to be bought: so Christ also here says expressly, “I counsel you to buy of me.” Behold, he says, “of me,” not of the Pope, of monks, friars, or priests. For Christ alone has the things which we may require. He alone does satisfy, he alone grants those gifts. And therefore he says in the Gospel of Saint John: “Let him who has hunger or thirst, come to me.” To me, I say, let him come. John 4:6 and 7. And Saint Peter says, “Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life.” As though he should say: If we will live, we can go to none other, but unto you. You are the life and fountain of all goodness.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Romish Chananite" changed to "Roman Canaanite." "Chananite" is a deliberate derogatory term, likely intended to evoke biblical imagery of a foreign, corrupt religious figure. "Canaanite" loses this specific, pointed theological nuance.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The insertion of quotation marks around "Say, 'Without money, without price, or exchange.'" adds an interpretive layer, suggesting direct speech that may not be explicitly indicated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "I[illegible] hearing hear me, incline your care, &c." is rendered as "If you hear, give heed; incline your ear." The original is more elliptical and potentially open to multiple interpretations; the draft's phrasing is more directive.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of key theological terms are evident.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** Hierom should be Jerome, Austen should be Augustine, Ireney should be Irenaeus. None of these appear in the provided text.
[FINAL]

The first fruicte, is welthe or riches, to wit spiritual.[note 21.7] For the word and faith is not a false imagination, and a vain dream of things most excellent. And besides this, they that are destitute of the light of God his word, and lack faith, ca not use rightly nor well these earthly riches. Therefore heavenly riches, are the true riches.

For he who believes the word feels joy in his heart and enjoys spiritual gifts; and possessing Christ through faith possesses all goodness. These shall be judged by the wisdom of God, as it is manifest in the twelfth chapter of Saint Luke. [ORIGINAL]
But least they should say, we hear these things in vain,[note 21.11] as they which have heard before that we shall be spewed out of the Lords mouth: yea and are so sharply shaken up with bitter words and sentences, that we are constrained to despair: He prevents that same and saith: whom soever I love, [illegible] I rebuke and chaste. The first word signifies to accuse and reprove openly, which is done with sharper words: The latter is referred to discipline, whereby children are kept in awe with the Palmer, least they forget them selves through wantonness. The Lord therefore alluding to the words of Solomon in the iii. Chap. signifies that a sharpe rebuke, or severe chastening is not always a sign that God is angry, but oftener a token that he is pleased and loves us. Therefore he saith, first I rebuked you sharply of love, and so sought your salvation. Therefore it is now also an wholesome sign if the preachers rebuke the church with sharpe words: And again it is an unlucky sign, if a fox tail be stricken over faults intolerable. It is a token of love also, if a man suffer sundry mishaps. Which thing the Apostle discourses at large in the xii. Chapter. to the Hebre.
[DRAFT]
But lest they should say, we hear these things in vain, as those who have heard before that we shall be spewed out of the Lord’s mouth; yea, and are so sharply shaken up with bitter words and sentences that we are constrained to despair: he prevents that same and saith: whomsoever I love, I rebuke and chaste. The first word signifies to accuse and reprove openly, which is done with sharper words; the latter is referred to discipline, whereby children are kept in awe with the rod, lest they forget themselves through wantonness. The Lord therefore alluding to the words of Solomon in the third chapter, signifies that a sharp rebuke or severe chastening is not always a sign that God is angry, but oftener a token that he is pleased and loves us. Therefore he saith, first I rebuked you sharply of love, and so sought your salvation. Therefore it is now also a wholesome sign if the preachers rebuke the church with sharp words; and again it is an unlucky sign, if a fox’s tail be stricken over faults intolerable. It is a token of love also, if a man suffer sundry mishaps. Which thing the Apostle discourses at large in the twelfth chapter to the Hebrews.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "The Palmer" changed to "the rod." While a rod is a form of discipline, "the Palmer" carries a specific, potentially archaic, connotation of a traveling disciplinarian, a figure with a particular cultural weight. Replacing it with "the rod" loses this nuance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The explanation of "The first word signifies to accuse and reprove openly..." is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. It's an explanatory gloss.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's vagueness regarding the "illegible" portion is removed. While modernization often necessitates filling gaps, this eliminates a potential point of scholarly discussion about the original text's condition.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
But lest they should say, we hear these things in vain, as those who have heard before that we shall be spewed out of the Lord’s mouth; yea, and are so sharply shaken up with bitter words and sentences that we are constrained to despair: he prevents that same and saith: whomsoever I love, I rebuke and chaste. The first word signifies to accuse and reprove openly, which is done with sharper words; the latter is referred to discipline, whereby children are kept in awe with the rod, lest they forget themselves through wantonness. The Lord therefore alluding to the words of Solomon in the third chapter, signifies that a sharp rebuke or severe chastening is not always a sign that God is angry, but oftener a token that he is pleased and loves us. Therefore he saith, first I rebuked you sharply of love, and so sought your salvation. Therefore it is now also a wholesome sign if the preachers rebuke the church with sharp words; and again it is an unlucky sign, if a fox’s tail be stricken over faults intolerable. It is a token of love also, if a man suffer sundry mishaps. Which thing the Apostle discourses at large in the twelfth chapter to the Hebrews.

[ORIGINAL]
Upon these things he infers the some of the matter,[note 21.12] and saith: Where thou see God so earnestly seek thy salvation, I pray thee continue not always to be thus in a mammering nether hot nor cold. [illegible], be zelouſe, take unto thee a fervent zeal to follow and apprehend thy salvation. For now he sets the feruentnes of faith conceived of the word and spirit of God, against this newtralitie or warmnes. After he adds, and repent, in ſorſaking thine evil conversation, and being of Christ tried gold: That is purefied and pourged, whereby thou may be rich, be arrayed in white, and may have a medicine wherewith to annointe thine eyes, that thou may see. To God be glory.
[DRAFT]
Upon these things he infers some of the matter. And he says: Where you see God so earnestly seeking your salvation, I pray you do not always remain in a hesitant state, neither lukewarm nor fervent. Be zealous, take to yourself a fervent zeal to follow and grasp your salvation. For now he sets the fervor of faith, conceived by the word and spirit of God, against this neutrality or lukewarmness. After this, he adds, “Repent, in forsaking your evil ways, and be like gold tried by Christ: that is, purified and cleansed, so that you may be rich, arrayed in white, and may have a medicine with which to anoint your eyes, that you may see.” To God be glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "mammering nether hot nor cold" altered to "hesitant state, neither lukewarm nor fervent" loses the original's specific nuance of indecision and tepidity. "Mammering" suggests a stammering, halting approach, which is more than just hesitancy.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "be like gold tried by Christ" after "being of Christ tried gold" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "being of Christ tried gold" is somewhat metaphorical and open to interpretation. The draft's clarification "be like gold tried by Christ" removes this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: "be like gold tried by Christ" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
Upon these things he infers some of the matter.[note 21.12] And he says: Where you see God so earnestly seeking your salvation, I pray you do not always remain in a stammering state, neither hot nor cold. Be zealous, take to yourself a fervent zeal to follow and grasp your salvation. For now he sets the fervor of faith, conceived of the word and spirit of God, against this neutrality or lukewarmness. After he adds, and repent, in forsaking thine evil conversation, and being of Christ tried gold: That is purefied and purged, whereby thou may be rich, be arrayed in white, and may have a medicine wherewith to anoint thine eyes, that thou may see. To God be glory.

## ¶ He draws them also hereby unto repentance. The.xxij. Sermon.

[FINAL]
## ¶ He draws them also hereby unto repentance. The.xxij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Behold I stand at the door and knock: If any man hear my voice and open the door, I will come in unto him, and will sup with him, and he with me: To him that ouercometh will I grant to sit with me on my seat even as I overcame, and have sat with my father on his seat. Let him that hath ears, hear, what the spirit saith to the congregations.
[DRAFT]
Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in to him and will share a meal with him, and he with me. To the one who overcomes, I will grant the privilege of sitting with me on my throne, just as I overcame and have sat with my Father on his throne. Let the one who has ears hear what the Spirit says to the congregations.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Seat" changed to "throne." While related, "seat" implies a more humble position than "throne," potentially altering the nuance of shared authority and intimacy.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "the privilege" before "of sitting" introduces an interpretation of the reward as a special favor, not explicitly present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "congregations" is plural, suggesting multiple groups. The draft's "the congregations" implies a single, unified body, diminishing the potential for diverse interpretations of the intended audience.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is present in the draft not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.
[FINAL]
Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in to him and will share a meal with him, and he with me. To him that overcomes, I will grant to sit with me on my seat even as I overcame, and have sat with my Father on his seat. Let the one who has ears hear what the Spirit says to the congregations.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Hereby also the Lord allures the Laodiceans to repentance, showing that every time is mete for conversion, and that God is evermore ready to receive sinners, and provokes them always that they should amede and live. And this matter he expounds in an allegorical and goodly speech,[note 22.1] taken out of the fifth Chapter. of the book of Canticles. For he feigns the Lord to stand at the door and knock, yea and to promise to them that open the greatest familiarity and joys unspeakable.
[DRAFT]
Hereby the Lord also draws the Laodiceans to repentance, showing that every time is suitable for conversion, and that God is always ready to receive sinners, and constantly urges them to repent and live. And this matter he explains in an allegorical and beautiful speech [note 22.1], taken from the fifth chapter of the Song of Songs. For he portrays the Lord as standing at the door and knocking, indeed promising to those who open it the greatest intimacy and unspeakable joys.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "allures" changed to "draws." While similar, "allure" carries a stronger sense of temptation or persuasive charm, potentially softening the original's nuance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "indeed" added before "promising." This adds a degree of emphasis not present in the original and subtly shapes the reader's interpretation of the Lord's promise.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "familiarity" changed to "intimacy." "Familiarity" in 1561 could encompass a range of closeness, including a spiritual connection. "Intimacy" is more specifically romantic or deeply personal, narrowing the potential meaning.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
Hereby the Lord also allures the Laodiceans to repentance, showing that every time is mete for conversion, and that God is evermore ready to receive sinners, and provokes them always that they should amende and live. And this matter he expounds in an allegorical and goodly speech [note 22.1], taken out of the fifth Chapter of the book of Canticles. For he feigns the Lord to stand at the door and knock, yea and to promise to them that open the greatest familiarity and joys unspeakable.
---
[ORIGINAL]
First therefore is declared the benevolence of God towards sinners, and his most ready will always to receive the same, yea and his infinite study to move men to repentance, that they might live. For the Lord stands at the door, and knocks. The word of standing doth signify, that God is always prepared, always watches over our salvation. For he sits not ſtyll, nor lies not on the one side like a sluggard: He stands buſely to his work. And I stand saith he, not I stood, or shall stand: But I stand evermore ready, evermore loving and gentle. What doth he? He knocks, and that in deed at the door, desiring to be let in. For like as he that knocks at the door seriously, covets to be let in: so God desires earnestly to be of us received. And God uses sundry kinds of knocking. For he warns, and exciteth with his word by the Prophets, again by signs and wonders, and also by sundry chauces and mouings.[note 22.2] These things may be scene in the city of Ieruſale. He sends to them his Prophets and Apostles. He shows divers wonders. He brings on the sorrowful chances, that they might admonish the: Such as are reported, Luke. 13. of the Galileans, and of those whom the tour of Siloe had overwhelmed. We may se the like at this day, how the Lord knocks. Therefore he said truly, Hieruſale, Hieruſale. &c. Math. 23. These are doubtless the parts and doings of god which will not that a sinner should die, but rather convert and live.
[DRAFT]
First, therefore, is declared the benevolence of God toward sinners, and his most ready will always to receive them, yes, and his infinite study to move men to repentance, that they might live. For the Lord stands at the door, and knocks. The word "standing" signifies that God is always prepared, always watches over our salvation. For he does not sit still, nor lie on one side like a sluggard; he stands busily to his work. And "I stand," he says, not "I stood" or "I shall stand," but "I stand" evermore ready, evermore loving and gentle. What does he do? He knocks, and that indeed at the door, desiring to be let in. For just as he who knocks at the door seriously covets to be let in, so God desires earnestly to be received by us. And God uses sundry kinds of knocking. For he warns and excites with his word by the Prophets, again by signs and wonders, and also by sundry chances and movements. [note 22.2] These things may be seen in the city of Jerusalem. He sends to them his Prophets and Apostles. He shows divers wonders. He brings on the sorrowful chances, that they might admonish them—such as are reported in Luke 13, of the Galileans and of those whom the tower of Siloam had overwhelmed. We may see the like at this day, how the Lord knocks. Therefore he said truly, “Jerusalem, Jerusalem.” &c. Matthew 23. These are doubtless the parts and doings of God, which will not that a sinner should die, but rather convert and live.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** The insertion of "by us" after "received" in "God desires earnestly to be of us received" alters the original's more general appeal for acceptance.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The explanation "The word 'standing' signifies that God is always prepared..." is an interpretation, not a modernization. It imposes a specific meaning that isn't explicitly stated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "mouings" is softened to "movements." "Mouings" carries a stronger sense of divine prompting or influence, a nuance lost in the more neutral "movements."
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ieruſale" should be "Jerusalem," not "Jeruſale."
5. **HALLUCINATION:** None.
[FINAL]
First, therefore, is declared the benevolence of God toward sinners, and his most ready will always to receive them, yes, and his infinite study to move men to repentance, that they might live. For the Lord stands at the door, and knocks. The word standing doth signify, that God is always prepared, always watches over our salvation. For he does not sit still, nor lie on one side like a sluggard; He stands buſely to his work. And "I stand," he says, not "I stood" or "I shall stand," but "I stand" evermore ready, evermore loving and gentle. What doth he do? He knocks, and that indeed at the door, desiring to be let in. For just as he that knocks at the door seriously covets to be let in, so God desires earnestly to be received. And God uses sundry kinds of knocking. For he warns and exciteth with his word by the Prophets, again by signs and wonders, and also by sundry mouings. [note 22.2] These things may be seen in the city of Jerusalem. He sends to them his Prophets and Apostles. He shows divers wonders. He brings on the sorrowful chances, that they might admonish them—such as are reported in Luke 13, of the Galileans and of those whom the tower of Siloam had overwhelmed. We may see the like at this day, how the Lord knocks. Therefore he said truly, “Jerusalem, Jerusalem.” &c. Matthew 23. These are doubtless the parts and doings of God, which will not that a sinner should die, but rather convert and live.

[ORIGINAL]
Than must we se, what is required of us,[note 22.3] verily that we should hear the knocking and noise of the knocker, and that also we open and receive such as desire to come in. Here are they confuted, which speak of man as though he were a block, and image I know not what manner of drawing, saying: It is neither in that runner nor in the willer. &c. Certain altogether abstain from well doing, saying, if I be chosen, it is enough. But the scripture requires every where hearing and obedience. We know that thelect are only saved, and that thelect in Christ: In Christ to be they that believe: That faith is of hearing, hearing by the word of god. Therefore saith the Prophet, this day if you hear his voice. &c. This same is recited of the Apostle. Heb. 4. The Apostle also. 2. Tim. 2. In a great house there be not only vessels of gold, but of earth also. If any pourge him self. &c. And therefore the Lord saith, I knock: It shall be thy part, not to despise him that knocks, but to open unto him. And he recites in deed two things, to hear, which both in the.8. and.9. of John is required of the children of God, and of the true sheep: And to open, that is to receive the Lord, or believe, to obey, and to frame them selves after the will of God, and to do penance. Notwithstanding we must here beware, that we think not that man hath power of him self to receive the Lord. The Lord illumines his elect, and by him we can do all things, without who we can do nothing. Other places must be coferred with this as John. 15.2. Corinth. 3. Philip. 2. They therefore that open, do open by the grace of God. They that open not being wrapped in their sins, through their own fault open not, and not through any fault of God.
[DRAFT]
Then we must see what is required of us, truly that we should hear the knocking and noise of the knocker, and also open and receive those who desire to come in. Here are those refuted, who speak of humans as though they were a block and an image of some unknown drawing, saying: It is neither in the runner nor in the willer. &c. Some entirely abstain from doing good, saying, if I am chosen, it is enough. But Scripture requires everywhere hearing and obedience. We know that people are only saved, and that in Christ: in Christ to be those who believe: that faith is of hearing, hearing by the word of God. Therefore, the prophet says, “This day if you hear his voice.” &c. This is recited by the Apostle in Hebrews 4. The Apostle also, in 2 Timothy 2, says, “In a great house there are not only vessels of gold, but of earth also. If anyone cleanses himself.” &c. And therefore the Lord says, “I knock.” It shall be your part, not to despise him who knocks, but to open to him. And he recounts indeed two things, to hear, which is required both in the 8th and 9th verses of John, of the children of God and of the true sheep: and to open, that is to receive the Lord, or believe, to obey, and to shape ourselves after the will of God, and to do penance. Nevertheless, we must be on our guard here, so that we do not think that humans have power of themselves to receive the Lord. The Lord illuminates his elect, and by him we can do all things, without whom we can do nothing. Other passages must be consulted with this, such as John 15:2, Corinthians 3, and Philippians 2. Those therefore who open, do open by the grace of God. Those who do not open are wrapped in their sins, not through their own fault, but through a fault of God [?]
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Than" to "Then" is a minor change, but "Than must we se" is slightly more archaic and conveys a sense of urgency that "Then we must see" lacks.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "Here are they confuted, which speak of man as though he were a block" becomes "Here are those refuted, who speak of humans as though they were a block." The original implies a specific group being refuted; the draft generalizes.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Thelect" to "people" loses the theological specificity of the original. "Thelect" refers to the elect, a distinct group within Christian theology. Replacing it with "people" dilutes the meaning.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "penance" is a reasonable modernization, but the original’s use of "thelect" is significantly altered.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** "through a fault of God [?]" is not present in the original. The original states "not through any fault of God." The addition introduces a contradictory and inaccurate interpretation.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Corinth" should be "Corinthians."
[FINAL]
Then must we se,[note 22.3] what is required of us, verily that we should hear the knocking and noise of the knocker, and that also we open and receive such as desire to come in. Here are they confuted, which speak of man as though he were a block, and image I know not what manner of drawing, saying: It is neither in that runner nor in the willer. &c. Certain altogether abstain from well doing, saying, if I be chosen, it is enough. But the scripture requires every where hearing and obedience. We know that thelect are only saved, and that thelect in Christ: In Christ to be they that believe: That faith is of hearing, hearing by the word of god. Therefore saith the Prophet, this day if you hear his voice. &c. This same is recited of the Apostle. Heb. 4. The Apostle also. 2. Tim. 2. In a great house there be not only vessels of gold, but of earth also. If any pourge him self. &c. And therefore the Lord saith, I knock: It shall be thy part, not to despise him that knocks, but to open unto him. And he recites in deed two things, to hear, which both in the.8. and.9. of John is required of the children of God, and of the true sheep: And to open, that is to receive the Lord, or believe, to obey, and to frame them selves after the will of God, and to do penance. Notwithstanding we must here beware, that we think not that man hath power of him self to receive the Lord. The Lord illumines his elect, and by him we can do all things, without who we can do nothing. Other places must be coferred with this as John. 15.2. Corinthians 3. Philip. 2. They therefore that open, do open by the grace of God. They that open not being wrapped in their sins, through their own fault open not, and not through any fault of God.

[ORIGINAL]
Let us hear moreover what the Lord promises to the that open, that is to say, to such as receive Christ with true faith.[note 22.4] The Lord promises to them two things chiefly. First, I will go into him, saith he. The scripture signifies, the Christ dwells every where through faith in the hearts of the faithful, and with a most ſtrayte knot to be joined unto them. He that eats my flesh and drinks my blood, abides in me, and I in him. These things are spoken of the Lord in the vi. of John. And in the xiiii. Chapter. he saith. He that loves me, will keep my word: And my father and I will come unto him, and will make abode with him. S. Paul saith that he lives not now, but that Christ lives in him. The same affirms that Christ through faith dwells in the hearts of the faithful. And so the Lord enters the hearts of them that let him in. Not the least part of felicity consists in this conjunction. For to be united with God, is blessedness, which begins here, and is made perfect in an other life. And therefore in the second place the Lord saith: And I will sup with him, and he with me. Whereby he notes not only again a most dere friendship and familiarity (for the table is consecrate to amity) but rather the fruition of eternal glory. For by the supper are signified, the joys celestial, greatest, and unspeakable, which after their souls the Godly receive immediately after death: But more fully in the end of times, when the bodies shall arise again. Therefore is it not applied to a diner, but to a supper, as it is also in the.14. of Luke. The if we receive Christ, we shall have him dwelling with us continually, whilst we live in this world. And in the world to come we shall have the full fruition of all the joys celestial. These things be certain and true. For otherwise in the life to come there shall be no riotouſe bankettes, such as the Turks do imagine.
[DRAFT]
Let us hear moreover what the Lord promises to those who open [illegible], that is to say, to such as receive Christ with true faith. The Lord promises to them two things chiefly. First, “I will go into him,” he says. Scripture signifies that Christ dwells everywhere through faith in the hearts of the faithful, and with a most tight knot to be joined unto them. “He who eats my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him.” These things are spoken of the Lord in the sixth chapter of John. And in the fourteenth chapter, he says, “He who loves me will keep my word, and my Father and I will come unto him and will make abode with him.” Paul says that he no longer lives, but that Christ lives in him. He affirms that Christ through faith dwells in the hearts of the faithful. And so the Lord enters the hearts of those who let him in. Not the least part of felicity consists in this conjunction. For to be united with God is blessedness, which begins here and is made perfect in another life. And therefore, in the second place, the Lord says, “And I will sup with him, and he with me.” Whereby he notes not only again a most dear friendship and familiarity (for the table is consecrated to amity) but rather the fruition of eternal glory. For by the supper are signified the celestial joys, greatest and unspeakable, which the godly receive immediately after death, but more fully in the end of times, when the bodies shall arise again. Therefore it is applied to a supper, not a dinner, as it is also in the fourteenth chapter of Luke. If we receive Christ, we shall have him dwelling with us continually while we live in this world. And in the world to come we shall have the full fruition of all the celestial joys. These things are certain and true. For otherwise in the life to come there shall be no riotous banquets, such as the Turks do imagine.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Let us hear moreover what the Lord promises to the that open" is altered to "Let us hear moreover what the Lord promises to those who open [illegible]". The original phrasing, while archaic, implies a receptive state or willingness, which is lost with the generic "those who open." The bracketed "[illegible]" is an inappropriate editorial decision.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "Scripture signifies" is an interpretive addition not present in the original. It imposes a layer of explanation that the original leaves to the reader.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "a most ſtrayte knot" is modernized to "a most tight knot." While functionally similar, "straight" (as in, direct, unwavering) carries a theological weight of commitment and certainty absent in "tight."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to core theological terms are noted, though the nuance of "straight" is lost.
5. HALLUCINATION: No new content is present.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Let us hear moreover what the Lord promises to those who open [illegible], that is to say, to such as receive Christ with true faith. The Lord promises to them two things chiefly. First, “I will go into him,” he says. Scripture signifies that Christ dwells everywhere through faith in the hearts of the faithful, and with a most tight knot to be joined unto them. “He who eats my flesh and drinks my blood abides in me, and I in him.” These things are spoken of the Lord in the sixth chapter of John. And in the fourteenth chapter, he says, “He who loves me will keep my word, and my Father and I will come unto him and will make abode with him.” Paul says that he no longer lives, but that Christ lives in him. He affirms that Christ through faith dwells in the hearts of the faithful. And so the Lord enters the hearts of those who let him in. Not the least part of felicity consists in this conjunction. For to be united with God is blessedness, which begins here and is made perfect in another life. And therefore, in the second place, the Lord says, “And I will sup with him, and he with me.” Whereby he notes not only again a most dear friendship and familiarity (for the table is consecrated to amity) but rather the fruition of eternal glory. For by the supper are signified the celestial joys, greatest and unspeakable, which the godly receive immediately after death, but more fully in the end of times, when the bodies shall arise again. Therefore it is applied to a supper, not a dinner, as it is also in the fourteenth chapter of Luke. If we receive Christ, we shall have him dwelling with us continually while we live in this world. And in the world to come we shall have the full fruition of all the celestial joys. These things are certain and true. For otherwise in the life to come there shall be no riotous banquets, such as the Turks do imagine.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 22.5]He annexeth also an other general promise, whereby he exhorts and moves to the study of godly religion and to repentance. For to him that ouercometh is promised the kingdom of heaven. And he saith to him that ouercometh (whereof I have spoken in tother epistles) not to him the flees or to a coward. &c. He propounds also thexaple of the coquerour Christ. For we must overcome, as he hath overcome. He in deed overcame most perfectly: we after our little strength, fight, and overcome. And verily the true victory in us, is the lively virtue of Christ: that is to say, by him they overcome, whosoever overcome. And like as he having overcome death, and vanquished the world and the devil, ascended into heaven, and sate on the right hand of the father: so he promises us also overcoming, that he will give us the seat of his father: not that we sitting on the right hand of God, should judge over all flesh, being made Christ's: but that being made pertakers of everlasting glory, and delivered from all judgment, we may appear in glory, when he shall come to judge the quick and the dead. We read of a like promise made to the disciples Matthew. 19. and Luke. 22. And so assuredly shall this glory come unto us, as Christ him self did verily ascend into Heaven, and sate in the glory celestial.
[DRAFT]
He adds another general promise, by which he exhorts and encourages the study of godly religion and repentance. For to him who overcomes is promised the kingdom of heaven. And he says to him who overcomes—of which I have spoken in other epistles—not to him who flees or is a coward, and so forth. He presents the example of conquering Christ. For we must overcome, as he has overcome. He indeed overcame most perfectly; we, with our limited strength, fight and overcome. And truly, the true victory in us is the lively virtue of Christ: that is to say, by him they overcome, whoever overcomes. And just as he, having overcome death and vanquished the world and the devil, ascended into heaven and sat at the right hand of the Father, so he promises us, upon overcoming, that he will give us the seat of his Father: not that we, sitting at the right hand of God, should judge over all flesh, being made Christ's, but that being made partakers of everlasting glory and delivered from all judgment, we may appear in glory when he shall come to judge the living and the dead. We read of a similar promise made to the disciples in Matthew 19 and Luke 22. And so certainly will this glory come to us, as Christ himself did truly ascend into heaven and sit in celestial glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "He annexeth also" changed to "He adds another." "Annexeth" implies a connection to preceding points, which "adds" loses.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "and so forth" added to the end of the second sentence. The original leaves the reader to consider the implications of the phrase "and &c."
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "little strength" is more specific than the original's "little strength," which could imply a variety of limitations.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** "and so forth" is not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]
He adds another general promise, by which he exhorts and encourages the study of godly religion and repentance. For to him who overcomes is promised the kingdom of heaven. And he says to him who overcomes—of which I have spoken in other epistles—not to him who flees or is a coward, and so forth. He presents the example of conquering Christ. For we must overcome, as he has overcome. He indeed overcame most perfectly; we, with our limited strength, fight and overcome. And truly, the true victory in us is the lively virtue of Christ: that is to say, by him they overcome, whoever overcomes. And just as he, having overcome death and vanquished the world and the devil, ascended into heaven and sat at the right hand of the Father, so he promises us, upon overcoming, that he will give us the seat of his Father: not that we, sitting at the right hand of God, should judge over all flesh, being made Christ's, but that being made partakers of everlasting glory and delivered from all judgment, we may appear in glory when he shall come to judge the living and the dead. We read of a similar promise made to the disciples in Matthew 19 and Luke 22. And so certainly will this glory come to us, as Christ himself did truly ascend into heaven and sit in celestial glory.

[ORIGINAL]
And here we must note a special thing, that Christ gives here that thing, which in the 20. of Matthew. he denies that he can give to James and John, that is to sit in the glory celestial. Therefore this place expounds that.[note 22.6] For Christ after his deity gives that, which after his humanity he denies that he ca give. This place then proves, that Christ is very God, geuer of eternal life, &c.
[DRAFT]
And here we must note a special thing, that Christ gives here that which in the twentieth chapter of Matthew he denies he can give to James and John, namely, to sit in celestial glory. Therefore this passage explains that. [note 22.6] For Christ, after declaring his deity, gives what he denies he can give after declaring his humanity. This passage then proves that Christ is very God, giver of eternal life, and so forth.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "sit in the glory celestial" changed to "sit in celestial glory." While seemingly minor, "glory celestial" carries a slightly more elevated, almost poetic, sense of heavenly splendor absent in the draft's phrasing.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's "namely, to sit in celestial glory" is a slight interpretive addition. The original simply states what Christ denies, leaving the reader to infer the significance. The draft explicitly labels it as the meaning.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing regarding Christ's deity and humanity is slightly more elliptical, allowing for a broader range of interpretations regarding the interplay between them. The draft's "after declaring his deity, gives what he denies he can give after declaring his humanity" is more direct and potentially less nuanced.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
And here we must note a special thing, that Christ gives here that which in the twentieth chapter of Matthew he denies he can give to James and John, that is to sit in the glory celestial. Therefore this place expounds that. [note 22.6] For Christ after his deity gives that, which after his humanity he denies that he ca give. This place then proves that Christ is very God, giver of eternal life, &c.

[ORIGINAL]
He adds after his manner an acclamation, whereby he applies this epistle to all congregations, and affirms it to be inspired of the spirit of Christ. Whereof we have spoken before.
[DRAFT]
He adds, in his characteristic way, an affirmation whereby he applies this epistle to all congregations, and affirms it to be inspired by the Spirit of Christ. Of this we have spoken previously.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "acclamation" to "affirmation" subtly alters the tone. "Acclamation" suggests a more enthusiastic or celebratory declaration, while "affirmation" is more neutral.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "in his characteristic way" is an interpretive addition. The original simply states he adds something.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's vagueness about *what* the "acclamation" consists of is removed by the more direct "affirmation" in the draft.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short passage.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
He adds an acclamation, whereby he applies this epistle to all congregations, and affirms it to be inspired of the spirit of Christ. Of this we have spoken before.

[ORIGINAL]
And we have treated hitherto of the second part of this work wherein are declared the most excellent points of our religion, who and of what sort is Christ,[note 22.7] sitting in the glory of the father, howe he is present in his church, and governs the same as king and priest, by his spirit, by his word and Sacraments. What also and of what sort is the church of Christ: what is the true and right doctrine of the church: what opinios are wicked. What is to be done with erroneouſe doctrines and seducers: howe the church fallen and afflicted may be repared: what is true repetaunce, and what are the duties of the godly, and many other things of like sort. To God the father be praise, thanks geuinge, and glory, through Jesus Christ our Lord.
[DRAFT]
