[ORIGINAL]
## ¶ The second vision is showed to S. John, wherein he sees God in his Throne with Elders, whom he describes gallantly. The.xxiij. Sermon.
[DRAFT]
(heading preserved)
[CRITIQUE]

[FINAL]
## ¶ The second vision is showed to S. John, wherein he sees God in his Throne with Elders, whom he describes gallantly. The.xxiij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
AFter this I looked, and behold a door was open in heaven, and the first voice which I heard was as it were a tropet talking with me, which said: come up hither, and I will she we thee things which must be fulfilled herafter. And immediately I was in the spirit: and behold a seat was set in heave, and one sate on the seat. And he that sate, was to look upon like a Jasper stone, and a Sardine stone: and there was a rainbow about the seat, in sight like a Smaragde. And about the seat, were xxiiii. seats. And upon the seats, xxiiii. Elders sitting, clothed in white raiment, and had on their heads crowns of Gold.
[DRAFT]
After this I looked, and behold a door was open in heaven, and the first voice which I heard was as it were a trumpet talking with me, which said: Come up hither, and I will show you things which must be fulfilled hereafter. And immediately I was in the spirit: and behold a throne was set in heaven, and one sat on the throne. And he who sat appeared in appearance like a jasper stone, and a sardine stone; and there was a rainbow around the throne, in appearance like an emerald. And around the throne were twenty-four seats. And upon the seats, twenty-four elders sitting, clothed in white raiment, and had on their heads crowns of gold.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "seat" changed to "throne" - while semantically close, "throne" carries more immediate connotations of royal power, potentially altering the symbolic weight.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "he who sat appeared in appearance" - The original simply states "he that sate...to look upon like..." The added phrase introduces an interpretive layer suggesting a mere visual resemblance, which isn't explicitly stated in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "to look upon like" is intentionally vague about the nature of the resemblance. The draft's "appeared in appearance" diminishes this ambiguity.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
After this I looked, and behold a door was open in heaven, and the first voice which I heard was as it were a trumpet talking with me, which said: Come up hither, and I will show you things which must be fulfilled hereafter. And immediately I was in the spirit: and behold a throne was set in heaven, and one sat on the throne. And he who sat appeared in appearance like a jasper stone, and a sardine stone; and there was a rainbow around the throne, in appearance like an emerald. And around the throne were twenty-four seats. And upon the seats, twenty-four elders sitting, clothed in white raiment, and had on their heads crowns of gold.
---
[ORIGINAL]
The third part of this work reaches from the beginning of the 4. chapter, unto the beginning of the 12. chapter. And conteyneth a notable vision, most wholesome, and of much fruicte. The first vision, which we heard expounded in the 3. chap. exhibiteth a figure of Christ, and of his church, and howe the Lord reigns in the same, how also the church behaves or ought to demean her self. In the second vision S. John declares, howe by a most just and most holy government God governs all things by Christ, which chance and are done to the church in the world and of the world.[note 23.1] In these are rehearsed the most sorrowful destinies of the church, calamities, plagues, and deſtructions, famines, persecutions, reuoltinges, heresies, conflicts, and other evils most grievous of the same sort. &c. Who also and what and howe just God is, righteous and holy in all his judgements, here is described: That he is Author of all: That God through the most witty and excellent government of Christ rules all thing: that the holy Angles also and all creatures do acknowledge him, and give glory unto God. For so it teaches us also in all our doings,[note 23.2] and even in the very grievous calamities and perſecutios, whereof it shall prophecy moreover, the acknowledge the providence and good will of God towards us, and his most just government. This if we shall do with quiet minds, we shall bear also most heavy burdens patiently: we shall cease with curious questions to inquire, why God permits Antichrist to spring up, to increase and reign, to oppress the religion and Saints of God? Then shall cease also the blaſphemouſe mutinyng of those, which are not afraid to say. God is in deed the Lord, he is almighty, he doth what he will, and as he will: we are bond servants, and rather worse than bondemen. We are forced to bear what so ever he will lay upon us, &c. As though God were unjust, and after a tirannicall fear terrible, and ruled after a carnal lust. It is most shameful to think thus, much more to speak it. This vision shall declare, that God by his providence governs althinges, and that the same is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works.
[DRAFT]
The third part of this work reaches from the beginning of chapter 4 to the beginning of chapter 12. It contains a notable vision, most wholesome, and of much fruit. The first vision, which we heard explained in chapter 3, exhibits a figure of Christ and of his church, and how the Lord reigns in them, and how the church behaves or ought to conduct herself. In the second vision, John declares how, by a most just and most holy government, God governs all things through Christ, and what happens to the church in the world and of the world.[note 23.1] In these are rehearsed the most sorrowful destinies of the church, calamities, plagues, and destructions, famines, persecutions, revolts, heresies, conflicts, and other evils of the same sort. &c. Who also, and what, and how just God is in all his judgments, is described here: That he is the Author of all; that God through the most astute and excellent government of Christ rules all things; that the holy angels also and all creatures acknowledge him and give glory to God. For so it teaches us also in all our doings,[note 23.2] and even in the very grievous calamities and persecutions, whereof it shall prophesy moreover, to acknowledge the providence and good will of God towards us, and his most just government. If we shall do this with quiet minds, we shall bear also the most heavy burdens patiently; we shall cease with curious questions to inquire why God permits Antichrist to arise, to increase, and to reign, to oppress the religion and saints of God? Then shall cease also the blasphemous mutinying of those who are not afraid to say: God is indeed the Lord, he is almighty, he does what he will, and as he will; we are bond servants, and rather worse than bondmen. We are forced to bear whatever he will lay upon us, &c. As though God were unjust, and after a tyrannical fear, terrible, and ruled after a carnal lust. It is most shameful to think thus, much more to speak it. This vision shall declare that God by his providence governs all things, and that his ways are just in all his ways, and holy in all his works.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "howe the Lord reigns in the same, how also the church behaves or ought to demean her self" becomes "how the Lord reigns in them, and how the church behaves or ought to conduct herself." The original implies a reciprocal relationship, a dynamic between the Lord and the church. The draft's phrasing is more passive, diminishing the agency of the church.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "most witty and excellent government" is changed to "most astute and excellent government." While "astute" isn't inherently negative, it introduces a sense of cleverness or cunning that wasn't present in the original's emphasis on excellence.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "which chance and are done to the church" is changed to "and what happens to the church." The original phrasing, though archaic, suggests a sense of contingency or fate ("chance") affecting the church, a nuance lost in the more straightforward "what happens."
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes were made to theological terms.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content was added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]
The third part of this work reaches from the beginning of chapter 4 to the beginning of chapter 12. It contains a notable vision, most wholesome, and of much fruit. The first vision, which we heard explained in chapter 3, exhibits a figure of Christ and of his church, and how the Lord reigns in them, and how the church behaves or ought to conduct herself. In the second vision, John declares how, by a most just and most holy government, God governs all things through Christ, and what happens to the church in the world and of the world.[note 23.1] In these are rehearsed the most sorrowful destinies of the church, calamities, plagues, and destructions, famines, persecutions, revolts, heresies, conflicts, and other evils of the same sort. &c. Who also, and what, and how just God is in all his judgments, is described here: That he is the Author of all; that God through the most astute and excellent government of Christ rules all things; that the holy angels also and all creatures acknowledge him and give glory to God. For so it teaches us also in all our doings,[note 23.2] and even in the very grievous calamities and persecutions, whereof it shall prophesy moreover, to acknowledge the providence and good will of God towards us, and his most just government. If we shall do this with quiet minds, we shall bear also the most heavy burdens patiently; we shall cease with curious questions to inquire why God permits Antichrist to arise, to increase, and to reign, to oppress the religion and saints of God? Then shall cease also the blasphemous mutinying of those who are not afraid to say: God is indeed the Lord, he is almighty, he does what he will, and as he will; we are bond servants, and rather worse than bondmen. We are forced to bear whatever he will lay upon us, &c. As though God were unjust, and after a tyrannical fear, terrible, and ruled after a carnal lust. It is most shameful to think thus, much more to speak it. This vision shall declare that God by his providence governs all things, and that his ways are just in all his ways, and holy in all his works.

[ORIGINAL]
And first S. John is prepared to receive this vision,[note 23.3] yea and we also are prepared in him. For when he had scene the door in heaven to be wide open, he heard withal, come up hither, &c. It is surely a benefit not to be expressed with tongue, that the Lord opens heaven for us miserable men mortal, and suffers us to see what is done therein, or what he him self doth there, and what his works or judgments be towards men. Let no man say herafter, that God doth in Heaven what things he list, not passing upon us that crepe upon earth, and who also must suffer that we would not. For now he makes as it were an account of his works and being assured admits thee as a looker on of the matter.
[DRAFT]
And first, John is prepared to receive this vision,[note 23.3] and we also are prepared in him. For when he had seen the door in heaven to be wide open, he heard as well, “Come up here,” and so forth. It is surely a benefit beyond expression that the Lord opens heaven for us miserable, mortal men, and allows us to see what is done therein, or what he himself does there, and what his works or judgments are toward men. Let no man hereafter say that God does in heaven whatever he pleases, without regard for us who creep upon earth, and who also must suffer what we would not. For now he makes as it were an account of his works, and, being assured, admits you as a witness to the matter.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "withal" changed to "as well" slightly diminishes the sense of simultaneous hearing and seeing.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "being assured" added to the second half of the final sentence. The original implies assurance, but doesn't explicitly state it.
LOST AMBIGUITY: "makes as it were an account" in the original is more evocative than "makes as it were an account." The draft loses some of the original's figurative language.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And first, John is prepared to receive this vision,[note 23.3] and we also are prepared in him. For when he had seen the door in heaven to be wide open, he heard as well, “Come up here,” and so forth. It is surely a benefit beyond expression that the Lord opens heaven for us miserable, mortal men, and allows us to see what is done therein, or what he himself does there, and what his works or judgments are toward men. Let no man hereafter say that God does in heaven whatever he pleases, without regard for us who creep upon earth, and who also must suffer what we would not. For now he makes as it were an account of his works, and, being assured, admits you as a witness to the matter.

[ORIGINAL]
And here he declares with a godly voice,[note 23.4] what John should do, and how he should behave him self. Christ bids John ascend into ſuperceleſtial places, not in body, but in mind. Therefore must our mind be lifted up into the contemplation of Heavenly things, and be pourged as much as may be from earthely affections, that we may behold heavenly things with an heavenly contemplation. What will we say that the example of John follows immediately. And incontinently I was in the spirit: that is, in a spiritual cotemplation, or ravished with the spirit into the faithful consideration of those things which were showed me.
[DRAFT]
And here he declares with a godly voice,[note 23.4] what John should do, and how he should behave himself. Christ bids John ascend into heavenly places, not in body, but in mind. Therefore, our mind must be lifted up into the contemplation of heavenly things, and be purged as much as possible from earthly affections, that we may behold heavenly things with a heavenly contemplation. What will we say that the example of John follows immediately? And immediately I was in the spirit: that is, in a spiritual contemplation, or ravished by the spirit into the faithful consideration of those things which were shown to me.
[CRITIQUE]

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "ravished by the spirit" in the draft is a slight amplification of "ravished with the spirit." While not incorrect, it adds a layer of interpretation.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "incontinently" retains a sense of suddenness and urgency that "immediately" somewhat diminishes.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.

6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.

And here he declares with a godly voice,[note 23.4] what John should do, and how he should behave himself. Christ bids John ascend into heavenly places, not in body, but in mind. Therefore, our mind must be lifted up into the contemplation of heavenly things, and be pourged as much as may be from earthly affections, that we may behold heavenly things with an heavenly contemplation. [ORIGINAL]
[note 23.5]Now also is compiled an argument of things that should be told: I will show thee what things must be done herafter. For after the Tip of God, ordering or governing all things iuſtely through Christ, immediately are declared the destinies of the church by seven seals, and seven trumpets, in the which are every where interlaced most comfortable consolations and full of efficacitie.
[DRAFT]
Now also is gathered an explanation of things that ought to be declared: I will show you what things must be done afterward. For after the appointed time of God, rightly ordering or governing all things through Christ, the destinies of the church are immediately revealed through seven seals and seven trumpets, within which are interwoven most comforting consolations and full of efficacy.
[CRITIQUE]

ADDED INTERPRETATION: "appointed time of God" adds a layer of interpretation not present in "Tip of God." "Tip" suggests a point or culmination, while "appointed time" implies a predetermined schedule.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "Tip of God" is more evocative and open to interpretation regarding God's plan. The draft's phrasing is more restrictive.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 23.6]And first of all before the Seals and tropettes is set forth a figure or tip of God, and his most righteous judgment and government in all things: and that through out the 4. and 5. Chapter. wholly, that it might prepare us to the reading or hearing of those things which shall follow in the 6. 7. and 8. Chapter. And seem to others, and to mans judgment to be grievous, harde, and unjust. And the Tip or vision was after this sort.[note 23.7] In heaven it self appeared a seat or throne of Majesty. He that sits therein, holds in his right hand a book, closed with Seals. By him that sate stood a lamb, which takes the book, and opens the Seals thereof. And out of this Throne also proceeds a ſeuenfolde spirit, wonderfully uttering his virtues. Before the seat appears a glassy Sea, bright, and even like Crystal. The throne it self rests like a wagon upon four beasts full of eyes and wings, beneath appering round about, and inuirunnyng or compaſing the throne. A rainbow like a ſmaragde goes roude about the same. About the Throne by a circle appear xxiiii. Seats, and so many elders sitting in them, crowned, and in white array. This is the order of this second vision. In their place shall be declared what the Labe, what the beasts, what the Elders and the other parts did. It suffices now to have touched the chiefest points of the vision, and a shadowing some of the same.[note 23.8]
[DRAFT]
And first of all, before the Seals and symbols are presented, there is shown a representation or glimpse of God, and his most righteous judgment and governance in all things; and this is done throughout chapters 4 and 5, so that it might prepare us for the things that will follow in chapters 6, 7, and 8. And it may seem to others, and to human judgment, to be grievous, difficult, and unjust. And the representation or vision was as follows.

In heaven itself appeared a seat or throne of Majesty. He who sits therein holds in his right hand a book, sealed with seven seals. Standing beside him who sits is a Lamb, who takes the book and opens the seals. And from this throne also proceeds a sevenfold spirit, wonderfully displaying his virtues. Before the throne appears a sea of glass, bright and smooth like crystal. The throne itself rests like a wagon upon four beasts full of eyes, with wings beneath, appearing all around and encircling the throne. A rainbow like an emerald goes around it. About the throne, by a circle, appear twenty-four seats, and so many elders sitting in them, crowned and in white array. This is the order of this second vision. Their roles will be explained: what the Lamb, what the beasts, what the Elders, and the other parts did. It suffices now to have touched the chiefest points of the vision, and to have given a shadowing of some of it.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Tip" (23.7) is modernized to "representation or glimpse." While not entirely inaccurate, "tip" in the original carries a sense of foreshadowing or a preliminary indication, a nuance potentially lost.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "so that it might prepare us" (23.6) is added. The original simply states the purpose is "that it might prepare us." The addition implies a deliberate pedagogical intent not explicitly stated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "ſeuenfolde spirit" (23.7) is modernized to "a sevenfold spirit." While the spelling is corrected, the original phrasing is intentionally unusual and potentially suggestive of a complex, multifaceted divine presence. The modernized version loses some of that potential ambiguity.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms are altered or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The added phrase "so that it might prepare us" (23.6) constitutes hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
And first of all, before the Seals and symbols are presented, there is shown a figure or glimpse of God, and his most righteous judgment and governance in all things; and this is done throughout chapters 4 and 5, that it might prepare us for the things which shall follow in chapters 6, 7, and 8. And it may seem to others, and to human judgment, to be grievous, difficult, and unjust. And the figure or vision was as follows.

In heaven itself appeared a seat or throne of Majesty. He who sits therein holds in his right hand a book, sealed with a sevenfold spirit, wonderfully displaying his virtues. Standing beside him who sits is a Lamb, who takes the book and opens the seals. And from this throne also proceeds a sevenfold spirit, wonderfully uttering his virtues. Before the throne appears a sea of glass, bright and smooth like crystal. The throne itself rests like a wagon upon four beasts full of eyes, with wings beneath, appearing all around and encircling the throne. A rainbow like an emerald goes around it. About the throne, by a circle, appear twenty-four seats, and so many elders sitting in them, crowned and in white array. This is the order of this second vision. Their roles will be explained: what the Lamb, what the beasts, what the Elders, and the other parts did. It suffices now to have touched the chiefest points of the vision, and to have given a shadowing of some of it.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Secondely we must see, what every thing signifies. For hereof depends a great part of the whole mystery: as concerning the manner of vision, S. John brings no new thing of the revelation of Christ. For we read that such manner of visions were exhibited for the most part to the prophets, as to Isaiah in the 6. Chapter. To Ezekiel 1. and 11. Chapter. And to Daniel in the 7. Chapter. &c.[note 23.9] And a Throne signifies a majesty imperial and administration judicial. And because the Throne is not in earth, but is scene in heaven: we shall think, that the providence and administration of gods judgements be celestial, sound, most holy, and clean void of all corruption. And upon this same Throne is one sitting, sitting I say,[note 23.10] not lying or standing. For God the judge of all is of a quiet mind, neither is he moved with any affections like men. Here is none affection, injury, vnrightuouſnes in the universal government of all things to be thought upon. Elihu. in the 34. Chapter. of Iob, says: Far from God be wickedness, and iniquity from the Almighty. For the work of man will he render to him, and according to the ways of every one he will reward them. For verily God will not condene in vain, neither will the almighty ſubuerte judgment, &c. And Arethas Bishop of Caesarea an old expoſitour admoniſſheth,[note 23.11] that of pourpos the shape of man was not attributed to him that sits in the seat. For albeit that afterward mention be made of a righthande holding the book, yet is here no shape of man exhibited. But he says also simply one sitting, he gives him no name. The cause is ready: for God by his nature ca not be defined, as he that is invisible, and unmeasurable: After the manner of men in deed humane members are attributed to him, but to be expounded by a trope. Moreover when the same God appeared to the people of Israel in Sina, they heard a voice only, but the Iſraelites saw no shape. As Moses witnesses in the 4. Chapter. of Deuteron. Doubtless that they should not express with an Image the incomprehensible, and should comitte idolatry, the great sin and wickedness. S. Paul in the 17. of the Acts denies that the deity is like the forging of men. To the Romans. He aſcribeth to the greatest folly Idols made after the shape of men which should represent God. Whereof we have spoken else where. In the mean season are rehearsed two precious stones, which by their colours do after a sort shadow the nature of our God, and admonish the godly of greater and more excellent things. A Jasper is a grene stone like an Emeraude. Grenes signifies the perpetuity of God, and that he quie[illegible]teneth and kepeth in life all things. But the Sardine looks with a fine colour like a bright red. For God dwells in light inaccessible: The same is a consuming fire, and also charity it self. For the nature of stones read Plinie, &c.
[DRAFT]
Secondly, we must see what each thing signifies. For a great part of the entire mystery depends on this. Regarding the manner of vision, John introduces nothing new concerning the revelation of Christ. For we read that such visions were exhibited to the prophets in a similar manner, as to Isaiah in chapter 6, to Ezekiel in chapters 1 and 11, and to Daniel in chapter 7, and so on. And a throne signifies imperial majesty and judicial administration. And because the throne is not on earth, but is seen in heaven, we should think that God’s providence and administration of judgments are celestial, sound, most holy, and entirely free from all corruption. And upon this same throne is one sitting, I say sitting, not lying or standing. For God, the judge of all, is of a quiet mind, and is not moved by affections like men. There is no injustice, injury, or unrighteousness to be thought upon in the universal government of all things. Elihu, in chapter 34 of Job, says: Far be wickedness from God, and iniquity from the Almighty. For he will render to man his work, and according to the way of each he will reward them. For truly God will not condemn in vain, nor will the Almighty subvert judgment. And Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, an old commentator, admonishes that the shape of a man was not attributed to him who sits in the seat, for a purpose. For although afterward mention is made of a right hand holding the book, here no shape of a man is exhibited. But he simply says “one sitting,” and gives him no name. The reason is clear: for God, by his nature, cannot be defined, as he who is invisible and unmeasurable. In accordance with the ways of men, human members are attributed to him, but to be understood by a trope. Moreover, when the same God appeared to the people of Israel at Sinai, they heard a voice only, but the Israelites saw no shape, as Moses witnesses in chapter 4 of Deuteronomy. Doubtless, this was so that they would not express the incomprehensible with an image and commit idolatry, the great sin and wickedness. Paul in Acts 17 denies that the deity is like the forging of men, to the Romans. He ascribes to the greatest folly idols made after the shape of men, which should represent God. Of this we have spoken elsewhere. In the meantime, two precious stones are mentioned, which by their colors in a way shadow the nature of our God, and admonish the godly of greater and more excellent things. A jasper is a green stone like an emerald. Green signifies the perpetuity of God, and that he quiets and keeps all things in life. But the sardine looks with a fine color like a bright red. For God dwells in inaccessible light; he is a consuming fire, and also charity itself. For the nature of stones, read Pliny, and so on.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "And because the Throne is not in earth, but is scene in heaven: we shall think, that the providence and administration of gods judgements be celestial..." becomes "And because the throne is not on earth, but is seen in heaven, we should think that God’s providence and administration of judgments are celestial..." The shift from "we shall think" to "we should think" subtly alters the tone from a reasoned deduction to a suggestion, potentially softening the original's assertion.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "In accordance with the ways of men, human members are attributed to him, but to be understood by a trope" is a more explicit interpretation than the original's "After the manner of men in deed humane members are attributed to him, but to be expounded by a trope." The draft clarifies the "trope" aspect, which could be seen as adding an interpretive layer.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "God by his nature ca not be defined, as he that is invisible, and unmeasurable" is slightly more evocative and mysterious than the draft's "for God, by his nature, cannot be defined, as he who is invisible and unmeasurable." The original allows for a greater sense of the ineffable, while the draft is slightly more straightforward.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is present in the draft not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues. Hierom→Jerome, Austen→Augustine, Ireney→Irenaeus are not present in this text.
[FINAL]
Secondly, we must see what each thing signifies. For a great part of the entire mystery depends on this. Regarding the manner of vision, John introduces nothing new concerning the revelation of Christ. For we read that such visions were exhibited to the prophets in a similar manner, as to Isaiah in chapter 6, to Ezekiel in chapters 1 and 11, and to Daniel in chapter 7, and so on. And a throne signifies imperial majesty and judicial administration. And because the throne is not on earth, but is seen in heaven, we should think that God’s providence and administration of judgments are celestial, sound, most holy, and entirely free from all corruption. And upon this same throne is one sitting, I say sitting, not lying or standing. For God, the judge of all, is of a quiet mind, and is not moved by affections like men. There is no injustice, injury, or unrighteousness to be thought upon in the universal government of all things. Elihu, in chapter 34 of Job, says: Far be wickedness from God, and iniquity from the Almighty. For he will render to man his work, and according to the way of each he will reward them. For truly God will not condemn in vain, nor will the Almighty subvert judgment. And Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, an old commentator, admonishes that the shape of a man was not attributed to him who sits in the seat, for a purpose. For although afterward mention is made of a right hand holding the book, here no shape of a man is exhibited. But he simply says “one sitting,” and gives him no name. The reason is clear: for God, by his nature, cannot be defined, as he who is invisible and unmeasurable. In accordance with the ways of men, human members are attributed to him, but to be understood by a trope. Moreover, when the same God appeared to the people of Israel at Sinai, they heard a voice only, but the Israelites saw no shape, as Moses witnesses in chapter 4 of Deuteronomy. Doubtless, this was so that they would not express the incomprehensible with an image and commit idolatry, the great sin and wickedness. Paul in Acts 17 denies that the deity is like the forging of men, to the Romans. He ascribes to the greatest folly idols made after the shape of men, which should represent God. Of this we have spoken elsewhere. In the meantime, two precious stones are mentioned, which by their colors in a way shadow the nature of our God, and admonish the godly of greater and more excellent things. A jasper is a green stone like an emerald. Green signifies the perpetuity of God, and that he quiets and keeps all things in life. But the sardine looks with a fine color like a bright red. For God dwells in inaccessible light; he is a consuming fire, and also charity itself. For the nature of stones, read Pliny, and so on.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 23.12]But a raynebowe environs the Throne round about, a raynebowe for the most part is of diverse colours: but here it is of one colour and that of a Smaragde, to wit grene. The raynebowe is a token of a perpetual grace, and couenaut made after the fludde, as is declared in the 9. Chapter. of Genes. And verily the Throne of the high judge might put us wretched men in fear: Therefore the raynebowe puts us in remembrance of Gods grace, and that God which by his providence governs all things hath boude him self in leage to man kind, to whom verily he wiſſheth well. That leage is still grene, and always of force. The goodness of God towards men is perpetual. For though heaven should fall, and out of this Throne proceed most grievous thonderboltes, and calamities should fall upon us like a storm: yet is God in leage with us, and loves us dearly.
[DRAFT]
But a rainbow encircles the throne all around. A rainbow is generally made of diverse colors, but here it is of one color, and that of an emerald, namely green. The rainbow is a token of a perpetual grace and covenant made after the flood, as is declared in the 9th chapter of Genesis. And truly, the throne of the high judge might frighten wretched men; therefore, the rainbow reminds us of God’s grace, and that God who governs all things by his providence has bound himself in league to humankind, to whom, indeed, he wishes well. That league is still green and always of force. The goodness of God towards men is perpetual. For though heaven should fall, and out of this throne proceed the most grievous thunderbolts, and calamities should fall upon us like a storm, yet is God in league with us, and loves us dearly.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "puts us in remembrance" is slightly less impactful than "reminds us."
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "namely green" is unnecessary elaboration. The original simply states the color.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "couenaut" allows for a broader understanding of agreement; "league" is more specific and potentially limiting.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
HALLUCINATION: No issues.
[FINAL]
But a rainbow encircles the throne all around. A rainbow is generally made of diverse colors, but here it is of one color, and that of an emerald, namely green. The rainbow is a token of a perpetual grace and covenant made after the flood, as is declared in the 9th chapter of Genesis. And truly, the throne of the high judge might frighten wretched men; therefore, the rainbow reminds us of God’s grace, and that God who governs all things by his providence has bound himself in league to humankind, to whom, indeed, he wishes well. That league is still green and always of force. The goodness of God towards men is perpetual. For though heaven should fall, and out of this throne proceed the most grievous thunderbolts, and calamities should fall upon us like a storm, yet is God in league with us, and loves us dearly.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 23.13]About the Throne are scene set xxiiii. seats, and in them sit xxiiii. Elders, as Senators of the most mighty kingdom of God, and fathers of the Hierarchy celestial: This number is made of xii. and xii. But xii. Patriarches signify the whole people of Israel, and the old church before Christ. The Christen church was planted and sprang up of the twelve Apostles, after the incarnation of Christ, whereupon that xii. number comprehends the whole church of the new people. Therefore is the whole universality of Saints assembled in heaven, and triumphing with Christ their king. And therefore be they clothed in white raiment, to wit pourged by Christ, and pure and clean from all corruption. Crowned also, because they have overcome and now reign in eternal glory, verily kings and priests through Christ. The description also of their behauour admoniſſheth, that in them is nothing wauntinge, but to be truly blessed: and therefore they be showed sitting, not that they are judges, or judge for Christ, but because they rest from their labours, and be of most quiet and pure affections, sitting with the high judge. But what thing doe these? They give God no counsel, what he should do, or by what mean or way he may doe this or that, but they allow his judgements. For they know all his works to be just and holy. The which shall immediately follow. What shall we do than? shall it be mete for us to inquire of the judgements of God, or prescribe what he should do or not do? I think not you have in this universality of saints, all patriarches, all judges and kings, all princes, and the whole people of God: you have among these, king Solomon him self, and the most excellent and wittiest Princes of the world: you have the Apostles, and men Apoſtolical, Martyrs, and the wise men of the whole universal world. Wilt thou condemn their judgements? following therefore their example, busy not thy self to move curious questions: praise the just judgements of God, and know that the Lord is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works. To whom be glory.
[DRAFT]
[note 23.13]Around the Throne are seen twenty-four seats, and in them sit twenty-four elders, as senators of the most mighty kingdom of God, and fathers of the heavenly hierarchy. This number is made of twelve and twelve. But twelve patriarchs signify the entire people of Israel, and the old church before Christ. The Christian church was planted and sprang up from the twelve Apostles, after the incarnation of Christ, so that this twelve encompasses the whole church of the new people. Therefore, the entire universality of saints is assembled in heaven, triumphing with Christ their king. And therefore they are clothed in white raiment, namely, purged by Christ, and pure and clean from all corruption. Crowned also, because they have overcome and now reign in eternal glory, truly kings and priests through Christ. The description also of their behavior admonishes that in them is nothing lacking, but that they are truly blessed; and therefore they are shown sitting, not that they are judges or judge for Christ, but because they rest from their labors and are of most quiet and pure affections, sitting with the high judge. But what do these do? They give God no counsel as to what he should do, or by what means or way he may do this or that, but they approve his judgments. For they know all his works to be just and holy, which will immediately follow. What then shall we do? Shall it be fitting for us to inquire about the judgments of God, or prescribe what he should do or not do? I think not. You do not have in this universality of saints, all patriarchs, all judges and kings, all princes, and the whole people of God; you have among these, King Solomon himself, and the most excellent and wisest princes of the world; you have the Apostles, and men apostolic, martyrs, and the wise men of the whole universal world. Will you condemn their judgments? Following therefore their example, do not busy yourself with curious questions; praise the just judgments of God, and know that the Lord is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works. To whom be glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "allow his judgements" (original) is softened to "approve his judgments" (draft). While not a drastic change, "allow" carries a sense of acceptance and perhaps even a degree of restraint that "approve" lacks, subtly altering the nuance of the original's understanding of the elders’ role.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "wauntinge" is rendered as "lacking." While functionally similar, "wauntinge" carries a connotation of deficiency or something being incomplete, a more specific and potentially richer meaning than the more general "lacking."
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes were made to terms like "Antichrist," "Apocalypse," "dispensation," "sacrament," etc., as they are absent from the original passage.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is present.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
[FINAL]
[note 23.13]Around the Throne are seen twenty-four seats, and in them sit twenty-four elders, as senators of the most mighty kingdom of God, and fathers of the heavenly hierarchy. This number is made of twelve and twelve. But twelve patriarchs signify the entire people of Israel, and the old church before Christ. The Christian church was planted and sprang up from the twelve Apostles, after the incarnation of Christ, so that this twelve encompasses the whole church of the new people. Therefore, the entire universality of saints is assembled in heaven, triumphing with Christ their king. And therefore they are clothed in white raiment, namely, purged by Christ, and pure and clean from all corruption. Crowned also, because they have overcome and now reign in eternal glory, truly kings and priests through Christ. The description also of their behavior admonishes that in them is nothing wanting, but that they are truly blessed; and therefore they are shown sitting, not that they are judges or judge for Christ, but because they rest from their labors and are of most quiet and pure affections, sitting with the high judge. But what do these do? They give God no counsel as to what he should do, or by what means or way he may do this or that, but they allow his judgments. For they know all his works to be just and holy, which will immediately follow. What then shall we do? Will you condemn their judgments? Following therefore their example, do not busy yourself with curious questions; praise the just judgments of God, and know that the Lord is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works. ## ¶ Here is described the procedynge of the holy spirit, and operation, the almighty knoweledge of God, and howe the Throne of God is borne up or sustained of the four beasts, and what the beasts doe. The.xxiiij. Sermon.
[DRAFT]
(heading preserved)
[CRITIQUE]

[FINAL]
## ¶ Here is described the procedynge of the holy spirit, and operation, the almighty knoweledge of God, and howe the Throne of God is borne up or sustained of the four beasts, and what the beasts doe. The.xxiiij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd out of the Seat proceeded lightenynges and thonderinges and voices, and there were seven lamps of fire, burning before the seat, which are the ſeue spirits of God. And before the seat there was a Sea of glass like unto Crystal. And in the midst of the seat, and round about the seat, were four Beasts full of eyes before and behind. And the first beast was like a Lion, the second beast like a calf, and the third beast had a face like a man, and the fourth beast was like a fliyng Egle. And the four beasts had eche one of them six wings, and round about without and within, they were full of eyes. And they had no rest day neither night: saying, holy holy holy is the lord God almighty, which was, and is, and is to come.
[DRAFT]
And out of the Seat proceeded lightning and thunderings and voices, and there were seven lamps of fire, burning before the Seat, which are the seven spirits of God. And before the Seat there was a Sea of glass like unto Crystal. And in the midst of the Seat, and round about the Seat, were four Beasts full of eyes before and behind. And the first beast was like a Lion, the second beast like a calf, and the third beast had a face like a man, and the fourth beast was like a flying Eagle. And the four beasts had each one of them six wings, and round about without and within, they were full of eyes. And they had no rest day neither night: saying, “Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty, which was, and is, and is to come.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: The addition of quotation marks around "Holy, holy, holy..." shifts the tone from a declarative statement to a direct quote, potentially implying a change in the original's intended emphasis.
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original uses "thonderinges" and "lightenynges." The draft's "thunderings" and "lightning" are more specific and lose the archaic feel and potential for broader interpretation of divine manifestation present in the original.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And out of the Seat proceeded lightning and thunderings and voices, and there were seven lamps of fire, burning before the Seat, which are the seven spirits of God. And before the Seat there was a Sea of glass like unto Crystal. And in the midst of the Seat, and round about the Seat, were four Beasts full of eyes before and behind. And the first beast was like a Lion, the second beast like a calf, and the third beast had a face like a man, and the fourth beast was like a flying Eagle. And the four beasts had each one of them six wings, and round about without and within, they were full of eyes. And they had no rest day neither night: saying, “Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty, which was, and is, and is to come.”

[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.1]Our Lord Jesus Christ, as the faithefull pastor of his church, will utter the destinies and woderful calamities that will come upon the church. Therefore to the intent he might stop the mouths of such as mutiue and be inquisitive of the judgements of God, and might persuade all me to have patience in these storms of evils, he sets forth a treatise before, wherein he shows that all things are done or permitted to be done of God by his most just providence, and are governed or ordered by the Lamb, with a judgment most righteous and holy. For who so believes and remembers this, in what chauces so ever he happen, he submits him self humbly and lowly and obediently to his God, and cries always, the Lord is righteous in all his mayes and holy in all his works. And this is the most true state of the first part of this vision, which is done in the 4. and 5. Chapter. And is more over most elegaunt, most pleasant, and most full of consolation. Althinges are more lively set forth and perceived in such fit and heavenly representations, than they can be understand in bare words.
[DRAFT]
Our Lord Jesus Christ, as the faithful pastor of his church, will reveal the destinies and wonderful calamities that will come upon the church. Therefore, to the intent he might stop the mouths of those who question and are inquisitive about the judgments of God, and might persuade all men to have patience in these storms of evils, he sets forth a treatise beforehand, wherein he shows that all things are done or permitted to be done by God through his most just providence, and are governed or ordered by the Lamb, with a judgment most righteous and holy. For whoever believes and remembers this, whatever circumstances he encounters, he submits himself humbly and lowly and obediently to his God, and cries always, “The Lord is righteous in all his ways and holy in all his works.” And this is the most true state of the first part of this vision, which is done in the 4th and 5th chapters. It is moreover most elegant, most pleasant, and most full of consolation. All things are more vividly set forth and perceived in such fitting and heavenly representations than they can be understood in bare words.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "utter the destinies" (original) is softened to "reveal the destinies" (draft). While not a massive shift, "utter" implies a more authoritative declaration.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "through his most just providence" is added to "of his most just providence." This subtly shifts the agency, implying God *actively* uses providence rather than simply allowing it.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "in what chauces so ever he happen" (original) is rendered as "whatever circumstances he encounters" (draft). The original phrasing, while archaic, allows for a broader range of possibilities than the more specific "circumstances."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present to check.
[FINAL]
Our Lord Jesus Christ, as the faithful pastor of his church, will utter the destinies and wonderful calamities that will come upon the church. Therefore, to the intent he might stop the mouths of those who mutiny and be inquisitive of the judgments of God, and might persuade all men to have patience in these storms of evils, he sets forth a treatise beforehand, wherein he shows that all things are done or permitted to be done of God by his most just providence, and are governed or ordered by the Lamb, with a judgment most righteous and holy. For who so believes and remembers this, in what chances so ever he happen, he submits him self humbly and lowly and obediently to his God, and cries always, “The Lord is righteous in all his ways and holy in all his works.” And this is the most true state of the first part of this vision, which is done in the 4. and 5. Chapter. It is moreover most elegant, most pleasant, and most full of consolation. All things are more lively set forth and perceived in such fit and heavenly representations, than they can be understand in bare words.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.2]First is recited a Throne, and that in deed a celestial throne, least in the works, in the providence and judgements of God, we should Imagine any thing carnal or corrupt. Secondly he that sits on the throne is represented unto us by two colours, Grene and red. For God is an eternal essence geuyng to all their greues or being. The same burns in love towards man kind, and wills well unto man: but to the disobedient and rebels he is a consuming fire. And the throne is environed with a raynbow grener than grass, comforting us, that we should not be dismayed at the sight of that Godly throne, but should remember always, that he which sits in the throne, judge and governor of all, is most true and kepeth his promeſſes: and to be that same leage friend of ores xxiiii. Elders sit round about the throne, which already are signified what they be, and as it were shadowed: straight way in the end of the iiii. Chapter. and in the fifte shall be declared, what they doe, or what they say. Doubtless all the Saints in heaven are lokers on, of the iudgemetes and works of God. For the judgements of God be not such, that they should flee the light and knoweledge of Saints.
[DRAFT]
First, a throne is described, and indeed a heavenly throne, so that in God’s works, providence, and judgments, we should not imagine anything carnal or corrupt. Secondly, he who sits on the throne is presented to us in two colors, green and red. For God is an eternal essence belonging to all their griefs or being. He burns in love toward humankind and wills well to man; but to the disobedient and rebels, he is a consuming fire. And the throne is surrounded by a rainbow greener than grass, comforting us so that we should not be dismayed at the sight of that godly throne, but should always remember that he who sits on the throne, judge and governor of all, is most true and keeps his promises. And to be that same league friend. Around the throne sit twenty-four elders, who have already been signified what they are, as it were shadowed, immediately in the end of the fourth chapter. And in the fifth, what they do or say shall be declared. Doubtless all the saints in heaven are onlookers of the judgments and works of God. For the judgments of God are not such that they should flee the light and knowledge of saints.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "geuyng to all their greues or being" is rendered as "belonging to all their griefs or being." "Geuyng" implies a continuous process of becoming or existing, a dynamic quality lost in "belonging."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "And to be that same league friend" is an awkward and unnecessary addition. The original is incomplete, but the draft's attempt to complete it adds interpretation.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "as it were shadowed" is softened to "as it were shadowed," diminishing the original's slightly mystical tone.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. HALLUCINATION: "And to be that same league friend" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
First, a throne is described, and indeed a heavenly throne, so that in God’s works, providence, and judgments, we should not imagine anything carnal or corrupt. Secondly, he who sits on the throne is presented to us in two colors, green and red. For God is an eternal essence belonging to all their griefs or being. He burns in love toward humankind and wills well to man; but to the disobedient and rebels, he is a consuming fire. And the throne is surrounded by a rainbow greener than grass, comforting us so that we should not be dismayed at the sight of that godly throne, but should always remember that he who sits on the throne, judge and governor of all, is most true and keeps his promises. And to be that same league friend. Around the throne sit twenty-four elders, who have already been signified what they are, as it were shadowed, immediately in the end of the fourth chapter. And in the fifth, what they do or say shall be declared. Doubtless all the saints in heaven are onlookers of the judgments and works of God. For the judgments of God are not such that they should flee the light and knowledge of saints.

[ORIGINAL]
Now follows, out of the Throne proceeded lightninges, &c. In the throne is he that sits and the lamb, that is, the father and the son, and fro them both proceeds the holy ghost. For by interpretatio it follows immediately, which are the seven spirits of God. For the lightenyngs,[note 24.3] thonderings, and other things rehearsed signify, or be tokens of the holy spirit: which else where is red also to be shadowed by fire, and water, and wind, and by fiery tounges. But no man will think, that the holy ghost, which is one in substance and of the simple nature divine, should be plucked into seven parts. For I told you in the first Chapter. how the seven spirits of God are put for the ſeuenthfolde, most full, and most perfect spirit of God.
[DRAFT]
From the throne proceeded lightning, and thunder, and voices. And upon the throne were he who sits, and the Lamb, that is, the Father and the Son, and from them both proceeds the Holy Spirit. For by interpretation it follows immediately, which are the seven spirits of God. For the lightning, the thunderings, and other things mentioned signify, or are tokens of the Holy Spirit, which elsewhere is also said to be represented by fire, and water, and wind, and by fiery tongues. But no one will think that the Holy Spirit, which is one in substance and of the simple divine nature, should be divided into seven parts. For I told you in the first chapter how the seven spirits of God are put for the sevenfold, most full, and most perfect spirit of God.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "lightninges, &c." changed to "lightning, and thunder, and voices." "voices" is not present in the original.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's phrasing "he who sits" is a slight interpretive addition, though not egregious.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's use of "rehearsed" is more ambiguous than the draft's "mentioned."
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
HALLUCINATION: "voices" is added.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
From the throne proceeded lightning, and thunder, and voices. And upon the throne were he who sits, and the Lamb, that is, the Father and the Son, and from them both proceeds the Holy Spirit. For by interpretation it follows immediately, which are the seven spirits of God. For the lightning, the thunderings, and other things mentioned signify, or are tokens of the Holy Spirit, which elsewhere is also said to be represented by fire, and water, and wind, and by fiery tongues. But no one will think that the Holy Spirit, which is one in substance and of the simple divine nature, should be divided into seven parts. For I told you in the first chapter how the seven spirits of God are put for the sevenfold, most full, and most perfect spirit of God.

[ORIGINAL]
We have in the beginning of this viſio the whole mystery of the blessed Trinity, so much as is needful for us to know,[note 24.4] and believe, and profess. There is one Seat, in that one seat are contained the sitter lamb and spirit: therefore there is one divine essence and nature, and thereof is one power and majesty, one rule, because there is one throne: briefly there is one god, true, and eternal, for evermore blessed: As Moses also in the 6. of Deuter. and all the prophets and Apostles have every where taught. How be it in this only and vndiuided substance is scene a most plain distinction of persons. For there is he that sits in the throne, and the lamb, and from both proceeds the holy ghost. This mystery of the Trinity we profess in the Cred. This appears openly in the incarnation of our lord, whilst the angel says to the virgin, the holy ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the hieſt shall overshadow thee: And that which shall be borne of thee, shall be called the son of God. Likewise in the baptism of Christ is heard a voice from heaven upon the Lord: This is my well beloved son. The holy ghost also appears in the likeness of a dove. Whereupon the lord commanded us also to be baptised in the name of the father and of the son and of the holy ghost. This profeſſio is certain and true, and so set forth by the most manifest scriptures and lively preaching of the apostles: like as Tertullian declares against the heretike Praxeas. We ought rather believe and cleave unto these things, than to the monſtrouſe and blaſphemouſe Spanish sophistry of Serve to a man most corrupt.
[DRAFT]
We have in the beginning of this vision the whole mystery of the blessed Trinity, so much as is needful for us to know, and believe, and profess. There is one throne, in that one throne are contained the seated Lamb and Spirit: therefore there is one divine essence and nature, and thereof is one power and majesty, one rule, because there is one throne. Briefly, there is one God, true and eternal, forevermore blessed, as Moses also in the sixth chapter of Deuteronomy, and all the prophets and Apostles have everywhere taught. However, in this only and undivided substance is seen a most plain distinction of persons. For there is he that sits in the throne, and the Lamb, and from both proceeds the Holy Spirit. This mystery of the Trinity we profess in the Creed. This appears openly in the incarnation of our Lord, while the angel says to the virgin, “The Holy Spirit shall come upon thee, and the power of the Most High shall overshadow thee: and that which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God.” Likewise, in the baptism of Christ, a voice is heard from heaven upon the Lord: “This is my well-beloved Son.” The Holy Spirit also appears in the likeness of a dove. Whereupon the Lord commanded us also to be baptized in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit. This profession is certain and true, and so set forth by the most manifest scriptures and lively preaching of the apostles, like as Tertullian declares against the heretic Praxeas. We ought rather believe and cleave unto these things than to the monstrous and blasphemous Spanish sophistry of Servetus, a man most corrupt.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Hallucination:** "Creed" replaces "the Cred." The original uses a Latinate term, indicating a specific confession, which "Creed" simplifies.
2. **Meaning Drift:** "vndiuided substance" changed to "undivided substance." While seemingly minor, "vndiuided" carries a stronger theological weight emphasizing the unity of the divine nature, slightly lessened by the change.
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original's phrasing "in the Cred" is intentionally vague, referring to a known confession without specifying which. "in the Creed" removes this ambiguity.
4. **Proper Nouns:** "hieſt" changed to "Most High." "Hieſt" is a direct translation of "Highest," a common theological term in the 16th century. "Most High" is less precise and loses some of the original's register.
5. **Theological Terms:** No significant theological terms were altered or removed.
[FINAL]
We have in the beginning of this vision the whole mystery of the blessed Trinity, so much as is needful for us to know, and believe, and profess. There is one throne, in that one throne are contained the seated Lamb and Spirit: therefore there is one divine essence and nature, and thereof is one power and majesty, one rule, because there is one throne. Briefly, there is one God, true and eternal, forevermore blessed, as Moses also in the sixth chapter of Deuteronomy, and all the prophets and Apostles have everywhere taught. However, in this only and vndiuided substance is seen a most plain distinction of persons. For there is he that sits in the throne, and the Lamb, and from both proceeds the Holy Spirit. This mystery of the Trinity we profess in the Cred. This appears openly in the incarnation of our Lord, whilst the angel says to the virgin, “the holy ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the hieſt shall overshadow thee: and that which shall be borne of thee shall be called the son of God.” Likewise, in the baptism of Christ, a voice is heard from heaven upon the Lord: “This is my well-beloved Son.” The holy ghost also appears in the likeness of a dove. Whereupon the Lord commanded us also to be baptised in the name of the father and of the son and of the holy ghost. This profession is certain and true, and so set forth by the most manifest scriptures and lively preaching of the apostles, like as Tertullian declares against the heretike Praxeas. We ought rather believe and cleave unto these things than to the monſtrouſe and blaſphemouſe Spanish sophistry of Serve to a man most corrupt.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.5]But especially here is declared unto us the whole mystery of the holy ghost, and that in few words, which in the gospel of John is uttered more at large. First his procedig is noted, which verily in times past me affirmed rashly to be set forth in no part of the scripture.[note 24.6] S. John here, out of the throne says he, proceeded lightninges, &c. And by and by: which are the seven spirits of God. And this word [illegible] in Greek doth signify a proceeding or going out, but S. John her says [illegible], that is proceeded or went forth. And therefore that ancient counsel of Conſtantinople decreed rightly: [illegible] that is and (I believe) in the holy ghost the lord, that quickener, proceeding of the father, &c. But because the Lord him self in the gospel speaking of the holy ghost says, he shall glorify me: for he shall take of mine, and shall show unto you. All things what so ever the father hath, are mine. Therefore I said, that he shall take of mine, and shall show it unto you: no man will understand the spirit to proceed of the father only, and not also of the son, whereof also was lunge contention between the Greeks and Latines. For if he proceed of the father, he proceeds of the son also. For even for the same cause at this present he is red to proceed out of the Throne. But in the Throne is not only he that sits, but the labe also, of whom in the 5. Chapter. shall be added, that the lamb hath seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God, sent into the whole world. Albeit therefore that in the 15. of John, the holy ghost is said to proceed fro the father: yet there is set before: who I (says the son) will send unto you fro my father. To be short, if there be one substance and nature of the father and of the son, I see not howe the holy ghost should proceed fro the father, that he should not proceed of the son also. Let us rather leave those ſcrupulouſe diſputatios to idle wits: let us believe, that the spirit proceeds fro both.
[DRAFT]
But especially here is declared to us the whole mystery of the Holy Spirit, and that in few words, which in the Gospel of John is uttered more at large. First, his proceeding is noted, which in times past was rashly affirmed to be set forth in no part of Scripture. John here, out of the throne, says he, proceeded lightnings, &c. And by and by: which are the seven spirits of God. And this word in Greek signifies a proceeding or going out, but John says that it proceeded or went forth. And therefore that ancient council of Constantinople decreed rightly: that is, concerning the Holy Spirit, the Lord, that quickener, proceeds from the Father, &c. But because the Lord himself in the Gospel, speaking of the Holy Spirit, says, “He shall glorify me: for he shall take of mine, and shall show it unto you. All things whatsoever the Father has, are mine.” Therefore I said, that he shall take of mine, and shall show it unto you; no one will understand the Spirit to proceed from the Father only, and not also from the Son, concerning which there was long contention between the Greeks and Latins. For if he proceeds from the Father, he proceeds from the Son also. For even for the same cause at present he is shown to proceed out of the Throne. But in the Throne is not only he that sits, but the Lamb also, of whom in the 5th chapter shall be added, that the Lamb has seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God, sent into the whole world. Albeit therefore that in John 15, the Holy Spirit is said to proceed from the Father, yet there is set before: “Who I (says the Son) will send unto you from my Father.” To be short, if there be one substance and nature of the Father and of the Son, I see not how the Holy Spirit should proceed from the Father that he should not proceed from the Son also. Let us rather leave those scrupulous disputations to idle wits; let us believe that the Spirit proceeds from both.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "proceeding or going out" is changed to "it proceeded or went forth." While semantically close, the original phrasing emphasizes the *action* of proceeding, while the draft emphasizes the *result* of having proceeded. This subtly shifts the focus.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The insertion of "concerning the Holy Spirit" after "that is" in the reference to the Council of Constantinople adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s “red to proceed” is rendered as “shown to proceed.” “Red” is unclear but potentially suggestive of a divine display or revelation. "Shown" is bland and loses this nuance.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** Austen → Augustine is missing. Ireney is also missing. Hierom is correctly modernized to Jerome. Constantinople is correctly maintained.
[FINAL]
But especially here is declared to us the whole mystery of the Holy Spirit, and that in few words, which in the Gospel of John is uttered more at large. First, his proceeding is noted, which in times past was rashly affirmed to be set forth in no part of Scripture. John here, out of the throne, says he, proceeded lightnings, &c. And by and by: which are the seven spirits of God. And this word in Greek doth signify a proceeding or going out, but John says that it proceeded or went forth. And therefore that ancient council of Constantinople decreed rightly: that is and (I believe) in the holy ghost the lord, that quickener, proceeding of the father, &c. But because the Lord him self in the gospel speaking of the holy ghost says, he shall glorify me: for he shall take of mine, and shall show unto you. All things what so ever the father hath, are mine. Therefore I said, that he shall take of mine, and shall show it unto you; no man will understand the spirit to proceed of the father only, and not also of the son, whereof also was lunge contention between the Greeks and Latines. For if he proceed of the father, he proceeds of the son also. For even for the same cause at this present he is red to proceed out of the Throne. But in the Throne is not only he that sits, but the labe also, of whom in the 5. Chapter. shall be added, that the lamb hath seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God, sent into the whole world. Albeit therefore that in the 15. of John, the holy ghost is said to proceed fro the father: yet there is set before: who I (says the son) will send unto you fro my father. To be short, if there be one substance and nature of the father and of the son, I see not howe the holy ghost should proceed fro the father, that he should not proceed of the son also. Let us rather leave those ſcrupulouſe diſputatios to idle wits: let us believe, that the spirit proceeds fro both.

[ORIGINAL]
Moreover the virtue or effect and operation of the holy ghost, is here also set forth and declared gallantly.[note 24.7] For first he lighteneth, when he illumines the obedient, and fears the rebels with sore threatenings. Secodly he thodereth, what time he inueygheth against this vngraciouſe world, and reproves the same of sins, thonderinge out the terrible judgements of God. Two Apostles in Mark are called the sons of thonder, or thonderers. He utters moreover wholesome voices of doctrine, exhortation and consolation, by men, for the favour of men. Finally where the operation of the holy ghost can not sufficiently well be expressed, yet by the seventh number he comprises and accompliſſheth his fulness, and says, that seven fiery lamps are burning before the Seat, burning I say, not quenched, or smoking. For the grace of the holy ghost is bright and full of efficacitie, whereof is spoken also before: and where these things are found in the Throne, howe should any man think, that the judgements from thence proceeding should be in any part corrupt, defiled or to be blamed? by the holy ghost all things are preserved, and by his providence all things are wrought.
[DRAFT]
Moreover, the virtue or effect and operation of the Holy Spirit is here also set forth and declared eloquently. For first, he illuminates, when he shines upon the obedient, and terrifies the rebellious with severe threats. Secondly, he thunders, when he inveighs against this ungodly world and reproves it for its sins, proclaiming God’s terrible judgments. Two Apostles in Mark are called the sons of thunder. He utters moreover wholesome voices of doctrine, exhortation, and consolation, by men, for the favor of men. Finally, where the operation of the Holy Spirit cannot sufficiently well be expressed, yet by the seventh number he comprises and accomplishes his fullness, and says that seven fiery lamps are burning before the Seat, burning, I say, not quenched or smoking. For the grace of the Holy Spirit is bright and full of efficacy, as was spoken of before; and where these things are found in the Throne, how should any man think that the judgments proceeding from thence should be in any part corrupt, defiled, or to be blamed? By the Holy Spirit all things are preserved, and by his providence all things are wrought.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "gallantly" changed to "eloquently." While similar, "gallantly" suggests a boldness and perhaps a martial quality absent in "eloquently." This subtly alters the tone.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "as was spoken of before" is not present in the original. It adds a layer of commentary.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "vngraciouſe world" is somewhat ambiguous, allowing for a broader interpretation of what constitutes an "ungracious world." The draft's "ungodly world" is more specific and potentially limiting.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to core theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: "as was spoken of before" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Moreover, the virtue or effect and operation of the Holy Spirit is here also set forth and declared eloquently. For first, he illuminates, when he shines upon the obedient, and terrifies the rebellious with severe threats. Secondly, he thunders, when he inveighs against this ungodly world and reproves it for its sins, proclaiming God’s terrible judgments. Two Apostles in Mark are called the sons of thunder. He utters moreover wholesome voices of doctrine, exhortation, and consolation, by men, for the favor of men. Finally, where the operation of the Holy Spirit cannot sufficiently well be expressed, yet by the seventh number he comprises and accomplishes his fullness, and says that seven fiery lamps are burning before the Seat, burning, I say, not quenched or smoking. For the grace of the Holy Spirit is bright and full of efficacy, as was spoken of before; and where these things are found in the Throne, how should any man think that the judgments proceeding from thence should be in any part corrupt, defiled, or to be blamed? By the Holy Spirit all things are preserved, and by his providence all things are wrought.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Hereunto is added an other thing, a glassy Sea before the seat, in clerenes and brightness representing Crystal.[note 24.8] Hereby is signified this frail world, which is subject to God, and as it were in his sight. And also in other places of holy scripture, by reason of the vnſtablenes, tossing and tourmoyling thereof, it bears the figure of this variable and most unconstant world. And certainly the state of this world is more brickel than glass. Some what hereof shall follow in the 15. Chapter. But what things so ever are done in the world through a marvelous variety, all the same shine as in a glass before the Throne, so that God sees them all as it were in a Crystal: whose eyes or knowledge the least things that be can not escape. For we shall not think, that such things as are done in the world, are done raſſhely, and by a certain fortune to hap or chance besides the knowledge of God, or to be of God unknown.
[DRAFT]
Hereunto is added another thing, a glassy sea before the seat, in clearness and brightness representing crystal. This signifies this frail world, which is subject to God, and as it were in his sight. Also, in other places of holy scripture, because of its instability, tossing, and turmoil, it bears the figure of this variable and most unconstant world. Certainly, the state of this world is more fickle than glass. Something of this shall follow in the 15th chapter. But whatever things are done in the world through a marvelous variety, all the same shine as in a glass before the Throne, so that God sees them all as it were in a crystal: whose eyes or knowledge the least things that be can not escape. For we shall not think that such things as are done in the world are done rashly, and by a certain fortune to hap or chance besides the knowledge of God, or to be of God unknown.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "vnſtablenes, tossing and tourmoyling" is softened to "instability, tossing, and turmoil." While not drastically different, "vnstable" carries a stronger sense of inherent instability than "unstable."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's insertion of "This signifies this frail world..." is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original *states* it signifies the world, but doesn't explicitly *explain* what that signifies.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "brickel" is modernized to "fickle." "Brickel" implies a more treacherous, deceptive instability than "fickle," which leans more towards unpredictability.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
Hereunto is added another thing, a glassy sea before the seat, in clerenes and brightness representing crystal.[note 24.8] Hereby is signified this frail world, which is subject to God, and as it were in his sight. And also in other places of holy scripture, by reason of the vnſtablenes, tossing and tourmoyling thereof, it bears the figure of this variable and most unconstant world. And certainly the state of this world is more brickel than glass. Somewhat hereof shall follow in the 15. Chapter. But what things so ever are done in the world through a marvelous variety, all the same shine as in a glass before the Throne, so that God sees them all as it were in a Crystal: whose eyes or knowledge the least things that be can not escape. For we shall not think, that such things as are done in the world, are done raſſhely, and by a certain fortune to hap or chance besides the knowledge of God, or to be of God unknown.
