[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.9]After this he returns again to the throne, to the intent he might finally finish, that which he had begone ones to describe: and might show also, all the works of God, that are done by his creatures, to be most holy. And the royal seats, chairs or Thrones of kings are wont to be borne up and beautified with beasts, as Salomons seat was with lions: which is to be scene in the 3. book of Kings the 10. Chapter. In other places the most excellent beasts do draw the triumphaunt chariotes of Princes. After the same manner therefore by a phrase of men beasts are set to the throne of God. For God in his prophets is carried upon Cherubin,[note 24.10] that is in his godly chariot. And Ezekiel in the.10. Chapter. names openly Cherubin, beasts, and the whole text proves, that the place must be understand of god his chariot, draw by beasts, in the which he him self was carried out of the city of Ieruſale. There is in poets much mentio of the chariot of the Gods, taken happily by the first writers out of the holy scriptures. For Satan, the Ape of God, goes about always to diffame the word of verity. But we, omitting the triflinges of Poets, will consider the sober description of this carriage, of God or rather of gods throne. Almighty God sits in this seat. Sitting in the Scriptures, is government. Here is signified than the God sits in all his creatures:[note 24.11] that is to say, governs his creatures, and by his most wise providence works all in all, in using every creature according to his good and just pleasure after the nature of every one. We shall say than, that by those beasts are understand all the creatures of God, dispersed through out the four quarters of the world: that is comprehended in the whole world.
[DRAFT]
After this, he returns again to the throne, intending finally to complete what he had begun to describe, and to show all the works of God, which are done by his creatures, to be most holy. And the royal seats, chairs, or thrones of kings are commonly borne up and beautified with beasts, as Solomon’s seat was with lions, which can be seen in the third book of Kings, chapter 10. In other places, the most excellent beasts draw the triumphant chariots of princes. In the same manner, therefore, by a figure of speech, beasts are set to the throne of God. For God, in his prophets, is carried upon Cherubim, that is, in his godly chariot. And Ezekiel, in chapter 10, names openly Cherubim, beasts, and the whole text proves that the place must be understood of God’s chariot, drawn by beasts, in which he himself was carried out of the city of Jerusalem. There is much mention in poets of the chariot of the gods, happily taken by the first writers out of the holy scriptures. For Satan, the ape of God, always seeks to defame the word of truth. But we, omitting the triflings of poets, will consider the sober description of this carriage of God, or rather of God’s throne. Almighty God sits in this seat. Sitting in the Scriptures signifies government. Here is signified that God sits in all his creatures, that is to say, governs his creatures, and by his most wise providence works all in all, using every creature according to his good and just pleasure, according to the nature of each. We shall say then, that by those beasts are understood all the creatures of God, dispersed throughout the four quarters of the world, which are comprehended in the whole world.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "to the intent he might finally finish, that which he had begone ones to describe" is softened to "intending finally to complete what he had begun to describe." The original implies a deliberate, perhaps protracted, effort; the draft loses some of that sense of purpose.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "For God, in his prophets, is carried upon Cherubim, that is, in his godly chariot." The original simply states "God in his prophets is carried upon Cherubin." The draft adds "in his godly chariot," which, while likely intended, is an interpretive gloss.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "There is in poets much mentio of the chariot of the Gods, taken happily by the first writers out of the holy scriptures" is altered to "There is much mention in poets of the chariot of the gods, happily taken by the first writers out of the holy scriptures." The original’s wording suggests a possible, even unwitting, appropriation of sacred imagery by poets; the draft removes this nuance.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ieruſale" is correctly modernized to "Jerusalem." Hierom should be Jerome, Austen should be Augustine, and Ireney should be Irenaeus, but these names do not appear in the provided text.
[FINAL]
After this, he returns again to the throne, to the intent he might finally finish that which he had begun to describe, and might show all the works of God, that are done by his creatures, to be most holy. And the royal seats, chairs, or thrones of kings are wont to be borne up and beautified with beasts, as Solomon’s seat was with lions, which is to be seen in the 3. book of Kings the 10. Chapter. In other places the most excellent beasts do draw the triumphant chariotes of Princes. After the same manner therefore by a phrase of men beasts are set to the throne of God. For God in his prophets is carried upon Cherubin, that is in his godly chariot. And Ezekiel in the.10. Chapter. names openly Cherubin, beasts, and the whole text proves that the place must be understand of God his chariot, drawn by beasts, in the which he him self was carried out of the city of Jerusalem. There is in poets much mentio of the chariot of the Gods, taken happily by the first writers out of the holy scriptures. For Satan, the Ape of God, goes about always to diffame the word of verity. But we, omitting the triflinges of Poets, will consider the sober description of this carriage, of God or rather of gods throne. Almighty God sits in this seat. Sitting in the Scriptures, is government. Here is signified than the God sits in all his creatures: that is to say, governs his creatures, and by his most wise providence works all in all, in using every creature according to his good and just pleasure after the nature of every one. We shall say than, that by those beasts are understand all the creatures of God, dispersed through out the four quarters of the world: that is comprehended in the whole world.

[ORIGINAL]
And first is showed in what place of the throne the beasts were:[note 24.12] to wit in the midst of the Throne, and in the circuit of the same. You will are, if they be in the throne, how should they be about the Throne? if they be about the Throne, how be they in the midst of the Throne? The thing must be so conceived, as I admoniſſhed also before, that we should vnderſtade, that under the Throne the midst of the beasts doe with their hinder parts reach to the midst of the Throne inwardly, and so as it were to have borne up the Throne: And with their fore parts, I mean, with their breasts, and heads and wings to have stand forth, and so to have compassed the Throne, and as it were environed it round about. For so might they seem to be in the midst of the same Throne, and round about the same.
[DRAFT]
And first, it is shown in what place of the throne the beasts were [note 24.12], namely, in the midst of the throne, and in the circuit of the same. You will ask, if they are in the throne, how can they be about the throne? If they are about the throne, how can they be in the midst of the throne? The thing must be conceived in such a way, as I admonished also before, that we should understand that under the throne, the midst of the beasts reaches with their hind parts to the midst of the throne inwardly, and so as it were to have borne up the throne. And with their fore parts, I mean, with their breasts, and heads and wings, to have stood forth, and so to have encompassed the throne, and as it were environed it round about. For so they might seem to be in the midst of the same throne, and round about the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "You will are" changed to "You will ask." While functionally equivalent, "are" carries a slightly more inquisitive, almost challenging tone absent in "ask."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "namely" after "in what place" adds a layer of explanation not present in the original. It subtly guides the reader toward a specific understanding of the location.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None.
[FINAL]
And first, it is shown in what place of the throne the beasts were [note 24.12] to wit, in the midst of the throne, and in the circuit of the same. You will are, if they be in the throne, how should they be about the throne? if they be about the throne, how be they in the midst of the throne? The thing must be conceived in such a way, as I admoniſſhed also before, that we should vnderſtande, that under the Throne the midst of the beasts doe with their hinder parts reach to the midst of the Throne inwardly, and so as it were to have borne up the Throne: And with their fore parts, I mean, with their breasts, and heads and wings to have stand forth, and so to have compassed the Throne, and as it were environed it round about. For so might they seem to be in the midst of the same Throne, and round about the same.
---
[ORIGINAL]
After,[note 24.13] what manner of beasts the same were is described diligently, in number they were four. For in times past also the number was expressed of Ezekiel: And the parts of the world are trimly signified by the fourth number, comprehending the universality of things. And some here have forged the four Monarchies of the world, &c. And every beast had his face, and his body, six wings, and the same full of eyes within, as also their bodies were full of eyes. The first represented in shape and fashion a Lion, the second a Calfe, the third a Man, and the fourth a fliyng Egle. By these appear to be signified all creatures, visible and invisible, reasonable and unreasonable, and that the most excellent. For after in the v. chapter we shall hear, that all creatures ioynctly together doe worship the Lamb, and him that sits on the Throne. And verily God uses them all, the Sun, the Moon, the Stars, the ayre, the fire, and briefly all living things. And such creatures as he hath chosen, to the intent to work any thing by them, he makes the same to be of efficacitie, instructing every one after their state and condition, that they should want no wisdom, reason, strength, power, patience, labour, quickenes nor ſwiftenes. The face of man signifies wit and wisdom, as also the eyes signify a foreſighte, watchefulnes, ſubtilties, and luckenes in doing of things. The Lions face betokens force and strength, and stoutness or magnanimity: As the sight of an ox or a calfe betokens enduring of labour: The Egle and the six wings swiftness. As for an example: God chose unto him the Aſſirians or Babylonians, which should diſtroye Niniue. These therefore, as it is in Nahum, the lord prepared and furniſſhed, that they were swifter than Egles, and the rest as you may read in the 1. and.2. Chapter. of Nahum. And so be all creatures ministers of the iudgemets of God, coming out of his judicial Throne.
[DRAFT]
After,[note 24.13] what manner of beasts these were is described diligently, in number they were four. For in times past also the number was expressed by Ezekiel: And the parts of the world are suitably signified by the fourth number, comprehending the universality of things. And some here have suggested the four Monarchies of the world, &c. And every beast had his face, and his body, six wings, and the same full of eyes within, as also their bodies were full of eyes. The first represented in shape and fashion a Lion, the second a Calf, the third a Man, and the fourth a flying Eagle. By these appear to be signified all creatures, visible and invisible, reasonable and unreasonable, and that the most excellent. For afterward in the fifth chapter we shall hear that all creatures jointly together worship the Lamb, and him that sits on the Throne. And verily God uses them all, the Sun, the Moon, the Stars, the air, the fire, and briefly all living things. And such creatures as he has chosen, in order to work anything by them, he makes them to be of efficacy, instructing every one after their state and condition, that they should want no wisdom, reason, strength, power, patience, labor, quickness, nor swiftness. The face of man signifies wit and wisdom, as also the eyes signify foresight, watchfulness, subtleties, and keenness in doing of things. The Lion’s face betokens force and strength, and boldness or magnanimity: As the sight of an ox or a calf betokens enduring of labor. The Eagle and the six wings signify swiftness. For example: God chose the Assyrians or Babylonians, who should destroy Nineveh. These therefore, as it is in Nahum, the Lord prepared and furnished, that they were swifter than Eagles, and the rest as you may read in the first and second chapters of Nahum. And so be all creatures ministers of the judgments of God, coming out of his judicial Throne.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "trimly signified" changed to "suitably signified." While not drastically different, "trimly" implies a beauty and order that "suitably" lacks, potentially softening the original's emphasis.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "And some here have forged the four Monarchies of the world, &c." changed to "And some here have suggested the four Monarchies of the world, &c." "Forged" implies fabrication or misrepresentation, a stronger accusation than "suggested." The draft weakens the original's criticism.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "luckenes in doing of things" changed to "keenness in doing of things." "Luckenes" has a broader sense of shrewdness and cleverness, while "keenness" is more narrowly focused on eagerness and intensity.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Niniue" changed to "Nineveh." While the modern spelling is correct, the original spelling is a characteristic of the period and should be preserved in a modernization aiming for fidelity.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** None.
[FINAL]
After,[note 24.13] what manner of beasts these were is described diligently, in number they were four. For in times past also the number was expressed by Ezekiel: And the parts of the world are trimly signified by the fourth number, comprehending the universality of things. And some here have forged the four Monarchies of the world, &c. And every beast had his face, and his body, six wings, and the same full of eyes within, as also their bodies were full of eyes. The first represented in shape and fashion a Lion, the second a Calfe, the third a Man, and the fourth a fliyng Egle. By these appear to be signified all creatures, visible and invisible, reasonable and unreasonable, and that the most excellent. For after in the v. chapter we shall hear, that all creatures ioynctly together doe worship the Lamb, and him that sits on the Throne. And verily God uses them all, the Sun, the Moon, the Stars, the ayre, the fire, and briefly all living things. And such creatures as he hath chosen, to the intent to work any thing by them, he makes the same to be of efficacitie, instructing every one after their state and condition, that they should want no wisdom, reason, strength, power, patience, labour, quickenes nor ſwiftenes. The face of man signifies wit and wisdom, as also the eyes signify a foreſighte, watchefulnes, ſubtilties, and luckenes in doing of things. The Lions face betokens force and strength, and stoutness or magnanimity: As the sight of an ox or a calfe betokens enduring of labour: The Egle and the six wings swiftness. As for an example: God chose unto him the Aſſirians or Babylonians, which should diſtroye Niniue. These therefore, as it is in Nahum, the lord prepared and furniſſhed, that they were swifter than Egles, and the rest as you may read in the 1. and.2. Chapter. of Nahum. And so be all creatures ministers of the iudgemets of God, coming out of his judicial Throne.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.14]Than it is touched also, what those beasts doe. They go about the Throne, awaytynge always for God his commandment, that they may apply the same cherefully, speedily, and stoutly. Neither have they any rest (mark howe he says, have, not shall have, or have had, but have) any rest: that is to wit, they be in cotinewall doings of God. But hear may we not vnderſtade, that they be grieved with any painefulnes. And also they honour god with cotinual praise. Arethas: it signifies, says he, no laboriouſe thing. And they have no rest, but a continual Tenure, about the singing of godly praises, &c.
[DRAFT]
Then it is also addressed what those beasts do. They go around the throne, always waiting for God’s command, that they may apply it cheerfully, speedily, and stoutly. They have no rest—mark how he says “have,” not “shall have” or “have had,” but “have”—they are in continual service of God. But let us not understand from this that they are burdened with any weariness. And also they honor God with continual praise. Arethas: it signifies, he says, no laborious thing. And they have no rest, but a continual occupation, around the singing of godly praises, and so forth.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "awaytynge" is softened to "waiting," losing the sense of eager anticipation and perhaps even a slight urgency present in the original.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "But let us not understand from this that they are burdened with any weariness" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. It's a clarification, but an added one.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's use of "cotinewall" is unusual and potentially open to multiple interpretations related to continuous action or state. The draft's "continual" is more standard but loses some of the original's potential nuance.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: The phrase "and so forth" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
Then it is also addressed what those beasts do. They go around the Throne, always waytynge for God his commandment, that they may apply the same cheerfully, speedily, and stoutly. Neither have they any rest—mark how he says, have, not shall have, or have had, but have—they be in cotinewall doings of God. But hear may we not vnderſtande, that they be grieved with any painefulnes. And also they honour god with cotinual praise. Arethas: it signifies, says he, no laboriouſe thing. And they have no rest, but a continual Tenure, about the singing of godly praises, &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.15]Finally here is set also the form of the himne and praise of all creatures. In old time David song also: praise the him Sun and Moon, &c. The same himne is set in the 6. of Isaiah. And what do all creatures commend in God, whose service God uses, and whose force and operation they feel? chiefly holiness. These things do chiefly concern the some of the matter. For they teach God to be holy, unspotted, just, good, omnipotent, doyng althinges, eternal, the beginning of things, and preserver. For they say, holy lord God omnipotent, which was, &c. Which words verily we did expound in the first Chapter. Who would not gather thereof, the works and judgements of him to be most holy and just? who therefore shall hereafter reprove the judgements and works of the Lord? Just is the Lord in all his ways, and holy in all his works. This Testimony of all creatures makes us willing, ready, chereful and careless, that we should willingly quiet our selves in the judgements of God, and murmure at him in nothing, why he should do this or that? But wholly submit our selves unto God, believing all his works to be good, and to be done for the profit of the godly, and for the most just punishment of the wicked. Holy is God the father, holy is God the son, and holy is God the holy ghost, holy is one God in Trinity, blessed for evermore. Holy are all his works, and his ways undefiled. And we read more rightly three times holy than niene times after the example of the complutenſian book. For the former lection the prophet Isaiah approves. To God almighty be praise and glory.
[DRAFT]
Finally, here is also included the form of the hymn and praise of all creatures. In old times David sang, “Praise the sun and moon, &c.” The same hymn is found in Isaiah 6. And what do all creatures commend in God, whose service God uses, and whose power and operation they feel? Primarily, holiness. These things chiefly concern the substance of the matter. For they teach God to be holy, unspotted, just, good, omnipotent, doing all things, eternal, the beginning of things, and preserver. For they say, “Holy Lord God Almighty, which was, &c.” Which words we indeed explained in the first chapter. Who would not gather from this that the works and judgments of him are most holy and just? Who therefore shall hereafter reprove the judgments and works of the Lord? Just is the Lord in all his ways, and holy in all his works. This testimony of all creatures makes us willing, ready, cheerful, and carefree, that we should willingly quiet ourselves in the judgments of God, and murmur at him in nothing, asking why he should do this or that? But wholly submit ourselves unto God, believing all his works to be good, and to be done for the profit of the godly, and for the most just punishment of the wicked. Holy is God the Father, holy is God the Son, and holy is God the Holy Ghost, holy is one God in Trinity, blessed forevermore. Holy are all his works, and his ways undefiled. And we read more correctly three times holy than nine times, following the example of the Complutensian book. For the former reading, the prophet Isaiah approves. To God Almighty be praise and glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "careless" replaces "chereful." While related, "careless" carries a negative connotation absent in the original's intended sense of joyful acceptance.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "asking why he should do this or that?" is an interpretive addition. The original simply states "murmure at him in nothing, why he should do this or that?" The draft adds the explicit act of *asking* which wasn't necessarily present.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing “&c.” is retained, but the draft’s explanation of “Praise the sun and moon, &c.” loses the original’s implication that the reader should know the full text.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]

And what do all creatures commend in God, whose service God uses, and whose power and operation they feel? Primarily, holiness. These things chiefly concern the substance of the matter. ## ¶ Here is declared what the Elders did about the Throne, and how they sang unto God a song of praise. The xxv. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd when these beasts gave glory and honour, and thanks to him that sate on the seat, which lives for ever and ever: The xxiiii. Elders fell down before him that sat on the Throne, and worshipped him that lives for ever, and cast their Crowns before the Throne, saying: thou art worthy lord to receive glory and honour and power. For thou haste created all things, and for thy wills sake they are and were created.
[DRAFT]

And when these beasts gave glory and honor, and thanks to the one who sits on the throne, who lives forever and ever, the twenty-four elders fell down before him who sits on the throne, and worshipped him who lives forever, and cast their crowns before the throne, saying, “You are worthy, Lord, to receive glory and honor and power, for you have created all things, and for your will’s sake they exist and were created.”

[ORIGINAL]
This most godly vision well and rightly understand,[note 25.1] and reposed in faithful memory, instructs us rightly in iudgeyng rightly the works of God, that we should fear God, be patient, and submit our selves wholly to God, and give all glory unto him. For this is the very fruicte, that comes unto us, and the end of all things that here are spoken.
[DRAFT]
This most godly vision, rightly understood and faithfully remembered, instructs us properly in judging the works of God, that we should fear God, be patient, and submit ourselves wholly to God, and give all glory to him. For this is the very fruit that comes to us, and the end of all things here spoken.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "well and rightly understand" replaced with "rightly understood" subtly diminishes the emphasis on the *manner* of understanding, potentially suggesting a less active or profound engagement with the vision.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "instructs us properly" adds a layer of interpretation not present in the original. "Rightly" already implies proper instruction.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "reposed in faithful memory" is more evocative and allows for a broader interpretation of how the vision is retained. "Faithfully remembered" is more literal and less resonant.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms present.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
This most godly vision well and rightly understand,[note 25.1] and reposed in faithful memory, instructs us rightly in iudging rightly the works of God, that we should fear God, be patient, and submit our selves wholly to God, and give all glory unto him. For this is the very fruicte, that comes unto us, and the end of all things that here are spoken.
---
[ORIGINAL]
And by the way he infers in repetyng what the beasts did: and declares also what the xxiiii. elders did.[note 25.2] Hereby we are manifestly taught, what we also owe unto God, and what we shall judge of his works, and howe we should behave our selves towards him herin.
[DRAFT]
And by the way he infers what the beasts did, repeating it, and declares also what the twenty-four elders did. [note 25.2] By this, we are clearly taught what we owe to God, and how we should judge his works, and how we ought to conduct ourselves toward him in this matter.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "in repetyng what the beasts did" changed to "repeating it" loses the emphasis on the *manner* of repetition, which in the original might imply a deliberate highlighting or circling back to the beasts' actions for symbolic significance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Hereby we are manifestly taught" changed to "By this, we are clearly taught" diminishes the original's stronger assertion of didactic purpose. The original suggests a direct, undeniable lesson; the draft softens it.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing allows for a degree of interpretive flexibility regarding the relationship between the beasts' actions and the elders'. The draft's more direct construction reduces this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms present.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
And by the way he infers what the beasts did in repeating it, and declares also what the twenty-four elders did. [note 25.2] Hereby we are manifestly taught what we also owe unto God, and what we shall judge of his works, and how we should behave ourselves towards him herein.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Those beasts, that is to say, the whole nubre of creatures, whose ministry God uses, in the government of things, ascribe three things unto God sitting, that is to say, ruling and governing all things, to God I say living for ever, that is to say, eternal, living, and geuynge or inſpiringe life into all things. First in deed glory, [illegible], which is a majesty, or great estimation, a reputation, worship,[note 25.3] or good opinion: when we think well of God, protesting that there is nothing better than he, greater, more worthy, more just, more holy and more excellent. This glory are we always commanded to give him, and to esteem nothing in this world derer and more precious than God. Secondly they give to him honour [illegible],[note 25.4] and [illegible] in Greek signifies honour and price, and the dew and bounden duty that we owe to any. We owe unto God reverence and submission, as to the supreme good, and the only and true lord of all. S. Paul in the 13. to the Romans speaking of obedience dew to the magistrate: to whom the owe fear, says he, give fear: And to whom you owe honour, give honour. In the third place follows benediction, which he called, [illegible], that is thankesgeuing, and praise. For we be commanded to praise all the works of the Lord, and to give thanks for the same. Iob is said to have blessed or thanked God, for the most grievous affliction that he sent him. For he said: like as it pleased the Lord, so hath it be done: the name of the lord be blessed. Whilst the beasts do attribute all these things to him that sits on the throne, by their example they teach us, what we should do verily to give all these and singular things unto god. Which if we do, all murmuring shall cease, and disputations comenced of ſearchinge and examining the works of God through out curiosity. With the laud and praise of the beasts is joined the hymn or song of the xxiiii.[note 25.5] Elders. This is the church triumphaunt, the copany of all Saints, Patrtarches, Prophets, Apostles, Martyrs, &c. as I declared to you before. Mortal men have not here an example of some one ſaincte, or wise man: but of all holy, godly wise and worthy men. They have put of their flesh, and want affections and errors: They be therefore of vncorrupte judgements, so that there can be no more clear or pure examples ministered to us. Three or four things are taught us concerning these Elders, which they did or performed, not to every body, but to him that sits on the throne, and lives for ever and ever. For so be the titles of God repeated, whereof is spoke before. We told you also that the seats of the Elders were set round about the Throne, in which they sate clothed with white raiment, crowned with crowns of Gold, living with him that lives for ever.
[DRAFT]
Those beasts, that is to say, the whole number of creatures, whose ministry God uses in the government of things, ascribe three things unto God sitting, that is to say, ruling and governing all things, to God I say living forever, that is to say, eternal, living, and giving or inspiring life into all things. First in deed glory, [illegible], which is a majesty, or great estimation, a reputation, worship, or good opinion: when we think well of God, protesting that there is nothing better than he, greater, more worthy, more just, more holy and more excellent. This glory are we always commanded to give him, and to esteem nothing in this world dearer and more precious than God. Secondly they give to him honor [illegible], and [illegible] in Greek signifies honor and price, and the due and bounden duty that we owe to any. We owe unto God reverence and submission, as to the supreme good, and the only and true lord of all. Paul in Romans 13, speaking of obedience due to the magistrate, says: “To whom you owe fear, give fear; and to whom you owe honor, give honor.” In the third place follows benediction, which he called, [illegible], that is thanksgiving, and praise. For we are commanded to praise all the works of the Lord, and to give thanks for the same. Job is said to have blessed or thanked God for the most grievous affliction that he sent him. For he said: “Like as it pleased the Lord, so hath it been done; the name of the Lord be blessed.” Whilst the beasts do attribute all these things to him that sits on the throne, by their example they teach us what we should do, verily, to give all these and singular things unto God. Which if we do, all murmuring shall cease, and disputations commenced of searching and examining the works of God through curiosity. With the laud and praise of the beasts is joined the hymn or song of the twenty-fourth [note 25.5]. Elders. Mortal men have not here an example of some one saint, or wise man: but of all holy, godly, wise and worthy men. They have put off their flesh, and want affections and errors: They are therefore of uncorrupted judgements, so that there can be no more clear or pure examples ministered to us. 3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** While no terms are *removed*, the repeated phrase "for so be the titles of God repeated" in the original is softened to "For so are the titles of God repeated" in the draft. This diminishes the original's emphasis on divine repetition.

[ORIGINAL]
They first arise out of their seats or chairs,[note 25.6] and fall down ([illegible] fall) upon their knees or on their face before god. And in falling or kneling down, they show a submission or lowlines of mind, that we might learn with great humility and reverence to submit our souls and bodies to our God, submitting I say, our selves and all our things to his good will and pleasure. But if the blessed souls, now purified, and already having the fruition of the sight of God, fall down before the Lord. What should not a wretched man do miserable, mortal, and a ſynner? Let him be ashamed of rebellion and ſlouthfulnes, which sees so great submission in the most noble and Godly souls of heavenly dwellers.
[DRAFT]
They first rise from their seats or chairs, and fall upon their knees or face before God. And in falling or kneeling, they demonstrate a submission and humility of mind, so that we may learn with great humility and reverence to submit our souls and bodies to our God, submitting, I say, ourselves and all our things to his good will and pleasure. But if the blessed souls, now purified and already experiencing the joy of beholding God, fall down before the Lord, what should a wretched, miserable, mortal, and sinful man do? Let him be ashamed of rebellion and slothfulness, seeing such great submission in the most noble and godly souls of those who dwell in heaven.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "lowlines of mind" changed to "humility of mind" subtly alters the nuance; "lowlines" suggests a deliberate lowering, while "humility" emphasizes inherent quality.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "experiencing the joy of beholding God" is an interpretation of "fruition of the sight of God"; the original is more literal.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "(illegible) fall" is removed, eliminating a potential ambiguity about the nature of the falling.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
They first rise from their seats or chairs, and fall upon their knees or face before God. And in falling or kneeling, they demonstrate a submission and humility of mind, so that we may learn with great humility and reverence to submit our souls and bodies to our God, submitting, I say, ourselves and all our things to his good will and pleasure. But if the blessed souls, now purified and already experiencing the joy of beholding God, fall down before the Lord, what should a wretched, miserable, mortal, and sinful man do? Let him be ashamed of rebellion and slothfulness, seeing such great submission in the most noble and godly souls of those who dwell in heaven.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Than the saints worship,[note 25.7] and worship in deed none other, but him that sits on the seat, and lives for ever, the father, the son, and the holy ghost, God three and one, everlasting and almighty. Therefore let us also worship this God, following the example of all saints.[note 25.8] We worship God with external adoration, if we uncover our heads, kneel and bow before him. In spirit and truth and with inward worſhypping, if we depend wholly of him, consecrate us whole unto him, and wholly look upon him, as one the only, soul, incomprehensible, most wise, best, and greatest, most rightuous and most merciful. And they that thus fall down before the throne of God, and so worship him, they do not contend with God, about his works, they do not expostulate with God vnpatiently, why he doth this, and permits that?
[DRAFT]
Then the saints worship, and worship in deed none other, but him that sits on the throne, and lives forever, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost, God three and one, everlasting and almighty. Therefore let us also worship this God, following the example of all saints. We worship God with external adoration, if we uncover our heads, kneel, and bow before him. In spirit and truth and with inward worship, if we depend wholly on him, consecrate ourselves wholly unto him, and wholly look upon him as one, the only, soul, incomprehensible, most wise, best, and greatest, most righteous, and most merciful. And they that thus fall down before the throne of God, and so worship him, they do not contend with God about his works; they do not expostulate with God impatiently, why he does this, and permits that?
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "of him" changed to "on him" in "depend wholly *on* him" subtly shifts the meaning from reliance *from* God to reliance *upon* God, a slightly different nuance.
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "worſhypping" is a deliberate archaism. Replacing it with "worship" removes a trace of the original's stylistic choices.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
[FINAL]
Then the saints worship, and worship in deed none other, but him that sits on the throne, and lives forever, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost, God three and one, everlasting and almighty. Therefore let us also worship this God, following the example of all saints. We worship God with external adoration, if we uncover our heads, kneel, and bow before him. In spirit and truth and with inward worship, if we depend wholly on him, consecrate ourselves wholly unto him, and wholly look upon him as one, the only, soul, incomprehensible, most wise, best, and greatest, most righteous, and most merciful. And they that thus fall down before the throne of God, and so worship him, they do not contend with God about his works; they do not expostulate with God impatiently, why he does this, and permits that?
---
[ORIGINAL]
Unto all these things is added,[note 25.9] that they pluck of the crowns of their heads, and cast them away before the throne, at the fete of him that sits in the throne. This is not only a notable modesty, but also an humble humility wanting an example. Primasius an expoſitour of the Apocalypse, assigning verily unto God, saith he, whatsoever virtue, and whatsoever dignity they have. For to him is attributed rightly, whatsoever is won or gotten: Of whom, he that ouercometh, is aided. Thus saith he. They testify and signify also, that they would not take upon them any Godly power, that they would not reign, they would not as the counſellours of God, give counsel to God, or prescribe unto him the least thing in the world: But to submit unto God all power, all rule, and the whole government, them selves and all others to be governed. For they have experience, and see no man in the world universal, in heaven or in earth to be wiser, mightier, greater, that none doth more faithfully, more diligently, more safely and better govern all things. Let us rest therefore, O brethren, to the judgment of saints, and let us consent herein with them in all things.
[DRAFT]
Unto all these things is added that they pluck off the crowns from their heads and cast them away before the throne, at the feet of him that sits in the throne. This is not only a notable modesty, but also an humble humility lacking an example. Primasius, an expositor of the Apocalypse, assigning truly all virtue and all dignity to God, says that to him is rightly attributed whatever is won or gotten: from whom he who overcomes is aided. Thus he says. They testify and signify also that they would not take upon themselves any godly power, that they would not reign, that they would not, as counselors of God, give counsel to God, or prescribe unto him the least thing in the world: but to submit unto God all power, all rule, and the whole government, themselves and all others to be governed. For they have experience and see no man in the world, universal, in heaven or on earth, to be wiser, mightier, greater, or that anyone governs all things more faithfully, more diligently, more safely, and better. Let us therefore rest, O brethren, to the judgment of saints, and let us consent herein with them in all things.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "at the fete of him" changed to "at the feet of him." While semantically close, "fete" implies a celebration or festival, subtly altering the scene.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "assigning verily unto God" changed to "assigning truly all virtue and all dignity to God." The latter is more explicitly interpretative, adding the concepts of "virtue" and "dignity" which aren't directly present in Primasius's original statement as presented.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "none doth more faithfully, more diligently, more safely and better govern all things" changed to "that anyone governs all things more faithfully, more diligently, more safely, and better." The original phrasing is more emphatic and open to broader interpretation; the draft introduces a more restrictive grammatical structure.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Unto all these things is added that they pluck off the crowns from their heads and cast them away before the throne, at the feet of him that sits in the throne. This is not only a notable modesty, but also an humble humility lacking an example. Primasius, an expositor of the Apocalypse, assigning truly all virtue and all dignity to God, says that to him is rightly attributed whatever is won or gotten: from whom he who overcomes is aided. Thus he says. They testify and signify also that they would not take upon themselves any godly power, that they would not reign, that they would not, as counselors of God, give counsel to God, or prescribe unto him the least thing in the world: but to submit unto God all power, all rule, and the whole government, themselves and all others to be governed. For they have experience and see no man in the world, universal, in heaven or on earth, to be wiser, mightier, greater, or that anyone governs all things more faithfully, more diligently, more safely, and better. Let us therefore rest, O brethren, to the judgment of saints, and let us consent herein with them in all things.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Yea and with express words they testify, why they threw away their crowns: Not that being unthankful to God, they esteem not highly his gifts: But for that they plainly acknowledge all glory to be dew to him alone. Therefore they accorde very well with the beasts and all the creatures of God, and saying an Himne to the high Prince, they confess him to be worthy to receive glory. &c. And he said to receive not that he had it not before: But for that it should seem a most unworthy thing, if either they or any other creature, would challenge unto them those things which apperteine to God alone.[note 25.10] These things apperteine to no creature. &c. And they commend God highly, whom they call their Lord and God. Some copies add [illegible], which art holy. For they do and agree in all things with the beasts, which cried also, holy holy holy, Lord God omnipotent. To the same gave they also glory and honour, whereof before. So also the elders ascribe to him now the selfsame things. And especially they attribute to God power [illegible] and take it from the selves. Why than doe the Papists attribute power and operation to the saints in heaven. Which nevertheless here plainly them selves attribute this to God alone.[note 25.11] S. Iho and Peter living toke it not in good part, that the people seemed to attribute to them somewhat of godly power. For when they had restored one that halted before the temple, and the people were in admiration thereof they said, the men of Israel, what marvel you at this? or why look the upon us [illegible] as though through our own power or holiness we have brought to pass, that this man should walk. The God of our fathers hath done this. &c. But how much less shall we now think, that being delivered from all corruption, they would require any godly power to be given, or divine honour attributed. &c.
[DRAFT]
Yea, and with explicit words they testify why they threw away their crowns: Not that they are ungrateful to God, and do not esteem highly his gifts, but because they plainly acknowledge all glory to be due to him alone. Therefore they agree very well with the beasts and all the creatures of God, and, singing a hymn to the high Prince, they confess him to be worthy to receive glory. And he receives it, not as if he had not possessed it before, but so that it may seem a most unworthy thing if either they or any other creature would claim those things which pertain to God alone. These things pertain to no creature. And they commend God highly, whom they call their Lord and God. Some copies add, who art holy. For they do and agree in all things with the beasts, which cried also, “Holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty.” To him they also gave glory and honor, as before. So also the elders ascribe to him now the selfsame things. And especially they attribute power to God and take it from themselves. Why, then, do the Papists attribute power and operation to the saints in heaven? Which nevertheless, here, they plainly attribute to God alone. John and Peter, living, did not take it well that the people seemed to attribute to them some godly power. For when they had restored one who had halted before the temple, and the people were in admiration of it, they said, “Men of Israel, why do you marvel at this? Or why look upon us as though through our own power or holiness we have brought to pass that this man should walk? The God of our fathers has done this.” But how much less shall we now think that, being delivered from all corruption, they would require any godly power to be given or divine honor attributed?
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "express words" changed to "explicit words." While similar, "express" implies a direct and clear statement, potentially highlighting the intentionality of their action. "Explicit" is more straightforward, lessening the nuance.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "so that it may seem a most unworthy thing" is an interpretive addition. The original simply states it *would* be unworthy, leaving room for a more rhetorical or symbolic understanding.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "apperteine" is softened to "pertain." "Apperteine" carries a stronger sense of rightful ownership and belonging, which is subtly diminished by "pertain."
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. Iho" modernized to "John" - correct.
[FINAL]
Yea, and with express words they testify why they threw away their crowns: Not that they are unthankful to God, they esteem not highly his gifts: But for that they plainly acknowledge all glory to be dew to him alone. Therefore they accorde very well with the beasts and all the creatures of God, and saying an Himne to the high Prince, they confess him to be worthy to receive glory. &c. And he said to receive not that he had it not before: But for that it should seem a most unworthy thing, if either they or any other creature, would challenge unto them those things which apperteine to God alone.[note 25.10] These things apperteine to no creature. &c. And they commend God highly, whom they call their Lord and God. Some copies add [illegible], which art holy. For they do and agree in all things with the beasts, which cried also, holy holy holy, Lord God omnipotent. To the same gave they also glory and honour, whereof before. So also the elders ascribe to him now the selfsame things. And especially they attribute to God power [illegible] and take it from the selves. Why than doe the Papists attribute power and operation to the saints in heaven. Which nevertheless here plainly them selves attribute this to God alone.[note 25.11] John and Peter living toke it not in good part, that the people seemed to attribute to them somewhat of godly power. [ORIGINAL]
They add also or render a reason, why they submit both them selves, and all theirs unto God, and attribute to the same glory, honour, and power. For thou, say they, hast created all things,[note 25.12] and by (Dia) thy will, they are, and were created. This glory of God is woderful and unmeasurable. How great, say they thou art, and that all power and glory is dew unto thee appears of the making and creation of the world universal. No man was with thee at the creation thereof, no man gave thee counsel what or how thou should do, no man helped thee thus much. Who than should approach unto thee to be partaker in power? who should glory before thee God and maker of all things? Thou alone made all things, alone preſerueſt all, and alone gouerneſt all. Thou willeſt, and they were made: Thou ſaideſt, and they were created. It was enough to have said, it was enough to have willed. And in deed all things at this day have their being through thy will, without any painefulnes and travel of thine. Thou gouerneſt all thing in best and most goodly order. This testifies the wonderful course of the stars, the pleasant change of things, the most sweet and plentiful fruits spring of the same. Who than wold not gladly submit both him self and all his to thee and to thy government, who wold not commit all his things unto thee? Who wold not acknowledge the power and glory to be thine? Let us mark these things with attentive minds, that we may also appear such before God as we sethe saints in heaven appear. God grant us this.
[DRAFT]
They also offer a reason why they submit themselves and all that belongs to them to God, and attribute glory, honor, and power to him. For you, they say, have created all things [note 25.12], and by your will, they are and were created. This glory of God is wonderful and immeasurable. How great you are, and that all power and glory are due to you, appears from the making and creation of the entire world. No one was with you at the creation; no one gave you counsel about what or how you should do it; no one helped you at all. Who then should approach you to share in power? Who should glory before you, God, maker of all things? You alone made all things, alone preserve all, and alone govern all. You willed, and they were made; you said, and they were created. It was enough to have said; it was enough to have willed. Indeed, all things today have their being through your will, without any effort or toil on your part. You govern all things in the best and most excellent order. This is testified by the wonderful course of the stars, the pleasant change of things, and the most sweet and plentiful fruits that spring from it. Who then would not gladly submit himself and all that he has to you and to your governance, who would not commit all his possessions to you? Who would not acknowledge the power and glory to be yours? Let us mark these things with attentive minds, that we may also appear before God as we see the saints in heaven appear. God grant us this.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "painefulnes and travel" changed to "effort or toil." While semantically close, "painefulnes" carries a nuance of suffering or labor that "effort or toil" lacks, potentially softening the original's emphasis on God's effortless creation.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "best and most goodly order" is altered to "best and most excellent order." "Goodly" suggests a pleasing and beautiful order, a quality not fully captured by "excellent."
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]
They also offer a reason why they submit themselves and all that belongs to them to God, and attribute glory, honor, and power to him. For you, they say, have created all things [note 25.12], and by your will, they are and were created. This glory of God is wonderful and immeasurable. How great you are, and that all power and glory are due to you, appears from the making and creation of the entire world. No one was with you at the creation; no one gave you counsel about what or how you should do it; no one helped you at all. Who then should approach you to share in power? Who should glory before you, God, maker of all things? You alone made all things, alone preserve all, and alone govern all. You willed, and they were made; you said, and they were created. It was enough to have said; it was enough to have willed. Indeed, all things today have their being through your will, without any effort or toil on your part. You govern all things in the best and most excellent order. This is testified by the wonderful course of the stars, the pleasant change of things, and the most sweet and plentiful fruits that spring from it. Who then would not gladly submit himself and all that he has to you and to your governance, who would not commit all his possessions to you? Who would not acknowledge the power and glory to be yours? Let us mark these things with attentive minds, that we may also appear before God as we see the saints in heaven appear. God grant us this.

## ¶ Of him that sits in throne, and holds the book in his right hand sealed with vii. seals: What that sealed book is. The.xxvj. Sermon.

[FINAL]
## ¶ Of him that sits in throne, and holds the book in his right hand sealed with vii. seals: What that sealed book is. The.xxvj. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
AND I saw in the right hand of him that sate on the throne a book written within and on the back side, sealed with ſeue seals. And I saw a strong angel preaching with a loud voice: who is worthy to open the book and lose the seals thereof? And no man in heaven nor in earth, neither under the earth, was able to open the book, and to ioke thereon. And I wept much because no man was found worthy to open and read the book, neither to look thereon.
[DRAFT]
And I saw in the right hand of him who sat on the throne a book written inside and on the back, sealed with seven seals. And I saw a mighty angel proclaiming with a loud voice, “Who is worthy to open the book and to break the seals?” And no one in heaven, nor on earth, nor under the earth, was able to open the book or to look upon it. And I wept greatly because no one was found worthy to open and read the book, nor even to look upon it.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "ioke thereon" modernized to "break the seals" alters the original's slightly more figurative language. "Ioke" implies a connection or interaction, while "break" is more forceful and potentially diminishes the nuance.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of quotation marks around "Who is worthy to open the book and to break the seals?" implies a direct speech act that isn't explicitly marked as such in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: "lose the seals thereof" is softened to "break the seals." "Lose" could imply a gradual process or a loss of control, which is absent in "break."
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And I saw in the right hand of him who sat on the throne a book written inside and on the back, sealed with seven seals. And I saw a mighty angel proclaiming with a loud voice, “Who is worthy to open the book and to break the seals?” And no one in heaven, nor on earth, nor under the earth, was able to open the book or to look upon it. And I wept greatly because no one was found worthy to open and read the book, nor even to look upon it.
---
[ORIGINAL]
He now proceeds to describe more fully him that sits on the Throne: Of whom he had touched certain and a few things before. In this piece is no small force of this our matter. For now will he show that, which in this treatise is principal, that all things which are done in the world through God his providence are most justly and holily governed by Christ. Which thing all the saints of God, and creatures acknowledging, for an example to us, that we should do the like, do praise and celebrate him that lives for ever.
[DRAFT]
He now proceeds to describe more fully him who sits on the Throne: of whom he had touched certain things before. This section contains considerable weight for our matter. For now he will show that which is principal in this treatise: that all things which are done in the world through God’s providence are most justly and holily governed by Christ. This thing all the saints of God, and all creatures, acknowledging as an example to us, do praise and celebrate him who lives forever.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "no small force of this our matter" changed to "considerable weight for our matter." While similar, "force" implies a power or impact that "weight" diminishes.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "as an example to us" after "all creatures, acknowledging" is an interpretive gloss. The original simply states creatures acknowledge.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "a few things" is lost, implying a more comprehensive prior mention than may have been intended.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is hallucinated.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
And it shall behove us to way every word, since that in every one are great mysteries, and nothing is spoken in vain. And verily that God almighty sits in a Throne.[note 26.1] And by sitting is signified not only the power of judging, ruling and governing: But also a quiet mind (not troubled with any evil affections, after the manner of judges of this world) and great equity in all things. Secondly a book is scene in the right hand of him that sits, of the which book we must speak more at large.

[ORIGINAL]
Here appears an allusion made, as there is in many other places of the scripture, to the princes of this world, which have books of the laws, of privileges, of institutes what thing is done, and is to be done, finally of secretes, of acts, of condemned, and of citizens, of life and of death. For so is both the book and books assigned to God. Moiſes saith in in the.33. of Exodus. Put me out of the book of life, &c. In the Pſalmes is much mention of these books of God: In the Psalm. 56.69.139. In the seventh of Daniel, books are opened whereof mentio is made also in the.20. of the Apocalypse. We read in the.3. of Malach. of a book of remembrance before God. Therefore this book of God contains all the counsels of God, all his works and judgements. For we shall hear by and by,[note 26.2] that all things that are done in the world come out of this book, as it were out of a fountain or well spring.
[DRAFT]
Here is an allusion made, as there is in many other places of the scripture, to the princes of this world, who have books of laws, of privileges, of institutes detailing what has been done and what is to be done, and finally of secrets, of acts, of the condemned, and of citizens, of life and of death. For so is both the book and books assigned to God. Moses says in Exodus 33: Put me out of the book of life, and so forth. In the Psalms there is much mention of these books of God: in Psalm 56, 69, and 139. In the seventh of Daniel, books are opened, of which mention is also made in the 20th of the Apocalypse. We read in Malachi 3 of a book of remembrance before God. Therefore, this book of God contains all the counsels of God, all his works and judgments. For we shall hear shortly [note 26.2] that all things that are done in the world come out of this book, as it were out of a fountain or wellspring.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "finally of secretes, of acts" changed to "and finally of secrets, of acts." While seemingly minor, the original phrasing creates a sense of enumeration and a slightly more formal tone. The draft's addition of "and" alters the rhythm and subtly shifts the emphasis.
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "mentio is made also in the.20. of the Apocalypse" is vague. The draft's "of which mention is also made in the 20th of the Apocalypse" removes this vagueness, implying a specific reference that may not exist or is open to interpretation in the original.
3. PROPER NOUNS: "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a consistent error throughout the text and needs correction.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Austen" should be "Augustine." This is a consistent error throughout the text and needs correction.
[FINAL]
Here is an allusion made, as there is in many other places of the scripture, to the princes of this world, who have books of laws, of privileges, of institutes detailing what has been done and what is to be done, and finally of secrets, of acts, of the condemned, and of citizens, of life and of death. For so is both the book and books assigned to God. Moses says in Exodus 33: Put me out of the book of life, and so forth. In the Psalms there is much mention of these books of God: in Psalm 56, 69, and 139. In the seventh of Daniel, books are opened whereof mentio is made also in the 20th of the Apocalypse. We read in Malachi 3 of a book of remembrance before God. Therefore, this book of God contains all the counsels of God, all his works and judgments. For we shall hear shortly [note 26.2] that all things that are done in the world come out of this book, as it were out of a fountain or wellspring.

[ORIGINAL]
And three things are chiefly spoken of this book. First, that it lies not in the Throne, or in the bosom of him that sits, or under the Throne, or that it hangs before or behind the Throne: But it is in the right hand of God. Hereby is signified the operation or power of God, and the same most just and most mighty.[note 26.3] For the book is not scene in the left hand. God therefore works, and contains or ministers all his works and judgements most holily. Secondly that book is written within and without or on the back side. For in the providence and judgements of God, all things are contained both good and evil, lucky and unlucky, sharpe, and soft, sweet and sewer, visible and invisible, privy and apart, and all things in general.
[DRAFT]

Hereby is signified the operation or power of God, and the same most just and most mighty. For the book is not seen in the left hand. God therefore works, and contains or ministers all his works and judgments most holily. Secondly, that book is written within and without, or on the back side. For in God’s providence and judgments, all things are contained, both good and evil, fortunate and unfortunate, sharp and gentle, sweet and sour, visible and invisible, secret and apart, and all things in general.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Finally the book is sealed with ſeue seals. For it is most strongly closed and fastened.[note 26.4] For the judgements and works of God are firm, true, just and such as can not be withstand. The use of seals amongst men is diverse, notwithstanding it may be contrived in two points.[note 26.5] First Seals are set to because of fidelity, truth and rightuouſnes. And a great deliberation is had in setting to of seals. For they are not put to unjust matters vayn or false. Therefore seals be tokens of a certentie and testimonies of a right. It seems an vnworthi thing to speak against sealed wrytinges. By the seals therefore that are set to the book of God is signified, that the judgements and works of God are most firm, true, and just what so ever are done by his providence, and are ordained by Christ. It shall therefore be a shame to find fault with the judgements of God, or to speak evil of his works. Again by seals are secretes kept, that they be not scene of every man, but of them only to whom they are appointed. The judgements therefore and works of God are for the most part hid, and not open to all men, saving to such as the Lord hath appointed, namely to the faithful and obedient.[note 26.6] But there be ſeue seals only, for that in them the fulness of times, and of things to be done in these times throughout the world and church, and of the judgements and mysteries of God are comprehended.
[DRAFT]
Finally, the book is sealed. For it is most strongly closed and fastened. For the judgments and works of God are firm, true, just, and such as cannot be withstood. The use of seals among men is diverse, yet it may be considered in two points. First, seals are set because of fidelity, truth, and righteousness. And great deliberation is had in setting the seals. For they are not put to unjust, vain, or false matters. Therefore, seals are tokens of certainty and testimonies of a right. It seems an unworthy thing to speak against sealed writings. By the seals therefore that are set to the book of God is signified that the judgments and works of God are most firm, true, and just, whatever is done by his providence, and are ordained by Christ. It shall therefore be a shame to find fault with the judgments of God, or to speak evil of his works. Again, by seals, secrets are kept, that they be not seen by every man, but by those only to whom they are appointed. The judgments therefore and works of God are for the most part hid, and not open to all men, saving to such as the Lord hath appointed, namely to the faithful and obedient. But there are seven seals only, for in them the fullness of times, and of things to be done in these times throughout the world and church, and of the judgments and mysteries of God are comprehended.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "It seems an vnworthi thing to speak against sealed wrytinges" is softened to "It seems an unworthy thing to speak against sealed writings." While subtle, the original carries a stronger moral condemnation.
2. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original's "contrived in two points" is rendered as "considered in two points," losing the sense of deliberate construction or design inherent in the original phrasing.
3. **Hallucination:** Note 26.4 is missing from the draft.
4. **Proper Nouns:** No issues.
[FINAL]
Finally, the book is sealed. For it is most strongly closed and fastened. For the judgments and works of God are firm, true, just, and such as cannot be withstood. The use of seals among men is diverse, yet it may be considered in two points. First, seals are set because of fidelity, truth, and righteousness. And great deliberation is had in setting the seals. For they are not put to unjust, vain, or false matters. Therefore, seals are tokens of certainty and testimonies of a right. It seems an unworthy thing to speak against sealed writings. By the seals therefore that are set to the book of God is signified that the judgments and works of God are most firm, true, and just, whatever is done by his providence, and are ordained by Christ. It shall therefore be a shame to find fault with the judgments of God, or to speak evil of his works. Again, by seals, secrets are kept, that they be not seen by every man, but by those only to whom they are appointed. The judgments therefore and works of God are for the most part hid, and not open to all men, saving to such as the Lord hath appointed, namely to the faithful and obedient. But there are seven seals only, for in them the fullness of times, and of things to be done in these times throughout the world and church, and of the judgments and mysteries of God are comprehended.

[ORIGINAL]
Now therefore the opening of the book,[note 26.7] and the vnſealyng thereof, is nothing else, but the revealing of God his judgements and the declaring or uttering of his most secret counsels: Finally the most holy and just operation, dispensation, and execution of his will. Nothing in that opening is done against the verity, faith, love, and justice of God.
[DRAFT]

Finally, the most holy and just operation, dispensation, and execution of his will. Nothing in that opening is done against the truth, faith, love, and justice of God.

Now therefore, the opening of the book [note 26.7] and its unsealing, is nothing else but the revealing of God’s judgments and the declaring or uttering of his most secret counsels. Finally, the most holy and just operation, dispensation, and execution of his will. Nothing in that opening is done against the truth, faith, love, and justice of God.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 26.8]And with many words, and also most diligently and goodly is treated here of the opening of the seals, who verily might be thought worthy to open to the church, the secret judgements of God, and to execute and minister his holy works: that is to say, to whom the kingdom is given and government of the divine providence. For an Angel, and that not of the common sort, but a strong, and worthy one, with a loud voice cries, to make us all attentive, and that we should note diligently, who he is that should both open the book, and unloose, or undo the seals. And he holds lunge in suspense the hearer, beholder or reader, before he will show him, to the intent verily to comende him to us exceedingly. No man, says he, in the whole universal world, neither among the Angles and saints in heaven, nor emongs earthly men, and under the earth, was found, which could either open or vnſeale the book.
[DRAFT]
And with many words, and also most diligently and well is treated here of the opening of the seals, who truly might be thought worthy to reveal to the church the secret judgments of God, and to execute and minister his holy works: that is to say, to whom the kingdom is given and the governance of divine providence. For an angel, and that not of the common sort, but a strong and worthy one, cries out with a loud voice, to make us all attentive, and that we should note diligently who he is that should both open the book and unloose or undo the seals. And he keeps the hearer, beholder, or reader in suspense before he will show him, truly to commend him to us exceedingly. No one, says he, in the whole universal world, neither among the angels and saints in heaven, nor among earthly men and under the earth, was found who could either open or unseal the book.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "goodly" changed to "well." While not drastically different, "goodly" implies a sense of beauty and appropriateness that "well" lacks, subtly altering the tone.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "who truly might be thought worthy to reveal" adds an interpretive layer not present in "who verily might be thought worthy." "Verily" emphasizes truth and certainty, while "truly" is more generic.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "lunge in suspense" is a peculiar phrasing, potentially hinting at a sudden, unexpected reveal. The draft's "keeps... in suspense" is less evocative and loses this nuance.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present to check.
[FINAL]
And with many words, and also most diligently and goodly is treated here of the opening of the seals, who verily might be thought worthy to open to the church the secret judgements of God, and to execute and minister his holy works: that is to say, to whom the kingdom is given and government of the divine providence. For an Angel, and that not of the common sort, but a strong, and worthy one, with a loud voice cries, to make us all attentive, and that we should note diligently, who he is that should both open the book, and unloose, or undo the seals. And he holds lunge in suspense the hearer, beholder or reader, before he will show him, to the intent verily to comende him to us exceedingly. [ORIGINAL]
[note 26.9]Let us observe, that there is none that can open the book, and open the seals, besides Christ alone. Why than is the administration of things attributed or communicated to saints, none can open to us the counsels and judgements of God, no man can govern those judgements and works of God, that he works in the world, save only Christ the lord: why than are so great benefits sought for of Saints, and be imputed to them, if either the sick be restored to helthe, or that a mortal man do receive any other gift or benefit many will say, I received this in deed of gods benefit, but through the meditatio, and power and merit of this or that ſaincte, unto whom God granted this, that he should rule over such a disease, and might heal such as call upon the name of the Saincte, or the name of God by the Saincte. These are here confuted now by the words of the Lord and S. John, saying, that no man in heaven or in earth is found, which could open the book. Yet nevertheless about the Throne sate the xxiiii. Elders, repreſentinge the Type of all Saints in glory, not one of all the was found, which could open the book. Therefore be they a great deal madder, which do attribute the government of things in the church to the Pope a most corrupt and filthy man. Only Christ received all power in Heaven and in Earth: as we shall incontinently more fully understand.
[DRAFT]
Let us observe that no one can open the book and its seals except Christ alone. Why, then, is the administration of things attributed or communicated to saints? No one can reveal God’s counsels and judgments, nor can any man govern God’s judgments and works in the world, except Christ the Lord. Why, then, are such great benefits sought from saints and ascribed to them, if either the sick are restored to health, or if a mortal man receives any other gift or benefit? Many will say, “I received this as a benefit from God, but through the meditation, power, and merit of this or that saint, to whom God granted the authority to rule over such a disease and might heal those who call upon the name of the saint or the name of God through the saint.” These things are now refuted by the words of the Lord and Saint John, who say that no one in heaven or on earth is found who could open the book. Yet nevertheless, around the throne sat the twenty-four elders, representing the type of all saints in glory, and not one of them was found who could open the book. Therefore, they are greatly mistaken who attribute the governance of things in the church to the Pope, a most corrupt and filthy man. Only Christ received all power in heaven and on earth, as we shall presently understand more fully.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "meditatio" is rendered as "meditation." While close, "meditatio" in this context refers to prayer and contemplation, a more active and devotional practice than the modern understanding of "meditation." This subtly shifts the nuance of the original.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original uses "saincte" which could refer to living saints or deceased ones venerated as saints. The draft consistently uses "saint," losing this ambiguity.
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** Hierom should be Jerome. Austen should be Augustine. Ireney should be Irenaeus. These are not present in this excerpt, but should be noted for future checks.
[FINAL]
Let us observe that no one can open the book and its seals except Christ alone. Why, then, is the administration of things attributed or communicated to saints? No one can reveal God’s counsels and judgments, nor can any man govern God’s judgments and works in the world, except Christ the Lord. Why, then, are such great benefits sought from saints and ascribed to them, if either the sick are restored to health, or if a mortal man receives any other gift or benefit? Many will say, “I received this as a benefit from God, but through the prayer and contemplation, power, and merit of this or that saincte, to whom God granted the authority to rule over such a disease and might heal those who call upon the name of the saincte or the name of God through the saincte.” These things are now refuted by the words of the Lord and Saint John, who say that no one in heaven or on earth is found who could open the book. Yet nevertheless, around the throne sat the twenty-four elders, representing the type of all saints in glory, and not one of them was found who could open the book. Therefore, they are greatly mistaken who attribute the governance of things in the church to the Pope, a most corrupt and filthy man. Only Christ received all power in heaven and on earth, as we shall presently understand more fully.
---
[ORIGINAL]
S. John wepeth,[note 26.10] for that he understood a weighty matter to conſiſte in the opening of this godly book: and yet saw he no man at all, which could either open or vnſeale it. Neither did he as yet fully understand the matter. And he bare the figure of them, which understand not the judgements of God, nor know not that all things are through God his providence holily governed by Christ. For in them nothing else remains, but mourning and heaviness. Certainly without Christ and his opening, whereby he reveals to us the divine mysteries and judgements, no man can rightly judge of the same. For unless we understand, the seals to be opened by Christ, and that all things are done by his order which loved us, and gave him self for us: what thing shall be left in us, but sighing?
[DRAFT]
John wept, for he understood that a weighty matter consisted in the opening of this godly book; and yet he saw no one at all, who could either open or unseal it. Neither did he as yet fully understand the matter. And he bore the figure of those who understand not the judgments of God, nor know not that all things are through God’s providence holily governed by Christ. For in them nothing else remains but mourning and heaviness. Certainly, without Christ and his opening, whereby he reveals to us the divine mysteries and judgments, no man can rightly judge of the same. For unless we understand the seals to be opened by Christ, and that all things are done by his order, which loved us and gave himself for us, what thing shall be left in us but sighing?
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "wepeth" to "wept" is a minor change, but "wepeth" carries a sense of ongoing weeping, while "wept" implies a completed action. This subtly alters the emotional weight.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "holily governed by Christ" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original simply states "through God his providence."
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "all things are through God his providence" is more open to various interpretations of divine action. The draft's addition of "holily governed by Christ" narrows the meaning, suggesting a more direct and intentional control by Christ.
NO ISSUES
[FINAL]

Neither did he as yet fully understand the matter. And he bore the figure of those who understand not the judgments of God, nor know not that all things are through God’s providence holily governed by Christ. For in them nothing else remains but mourning and heaviness. Certainly, without Christ and his opening, whereby he reveals to us the divine mysteries and judgments, no man can rightly judge of the same. For unless we understand the seals to be opened by Christ, and that all things are done by his order, which loved us and gave himself for us, what thing shall be left in us but sighing?
