[ORIGINAL]
But he rehearsed three things, to open, rede and look upon. No man living opens, for that no man is mete for so great a charge, save only the son of God. No man reads or understands fully the judgements of God, but the son, and to whom he hath revealed, so much as any man has. No man looks on it, that is to say, can behold the works and judgements of God, but he shall be offended, except he be indewed with the spirit, and purified with the same. Therefore we must axe grace of him, that we may understand so much of Gods judgements as shall suffice, and may judge well of the same.
[DRAFT]
But he rehearsed three things, to read, to rede, and to look upon. No living man opens [it], for no man is fit for so great a responsibility, except only the Son of God. No man reads or fully understands God’s judgments, but the Son, and to whom he has revealed as much as any man has. No man looks upon [it], that is to say, can behold the works and judgments of God, but he will be offended, unless he is endowed with the Spirit and purified by it. Therefore, we must ask grace of him, that we may understand so much of God’s judgments as will suffice, and may judge them well.

No man reads or fully understands God’s judgments, but the Son, and to whom he has revealed as much as any man has. [ORIGINAL]
Arethas Bishop of Caesarea, an expoſitour of this book: neither any, says he, of those that lack flesh, nor any of them that are in the flesh, nor yet of such as are deperted leaving their flesh behind them, hath received a perfect knoweledge of godly matters. And by and by after: neither only is there any which could open it, but also not so much as look upon it: that is to say, could not look attentively on the judgments of God. and so forth. And the cotexte of the whole place proves sufficiently, that S. John speaks here of the iudgmets verily: but chiefly of the government of things. The Lord Jesus be glorified for ever. Amen.
[DRAFT]
Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, an expositor of this book, says that neither those who lack physical bodies, nor those who possess them, nor those who have departed, leaving their bodies behind, have received a perfect understanding of divine matters. And immediately afterward: neither is there anyone who could open it, nor even one who could look upon it—that is to say, could not attentively consider the judgments of God, and so forth. And the context of the entire passage proves sufficiently that John speaks here of judgments truly: but chiefly of the governance of things. The Lord Jesus be glorified forever. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "godly matters" changed to "divine matters" - subtle shift, but "godly" carries a more specific religious connotation.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of an em-dash after "it" in "could not attentively consider it—that is to say" adds a slight emphasis that isn't present in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: "verily" changed to "truly" - "verily" in 1561 had a broader range of meaning, sometimes implying certainty, sometimes emphasis. "Truly" is more straightforward.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, an expositor of this book, says that neither those who lack physical bodies, nor those who possess them, nor those who have departed, leaving their bodies behind, have received a perfect understanding of divine matters. And immediately afterward: neither is there anyone who could open it, nor even one who could look upon it—that is to say, could not attentively consider the judgments of God, and so forth. And the context of the entire passage proves sufficiently that John speaks here of judgments truly: but chiefly of the governance of things. The Lord Jesus be glorified forever. Amen.

## ¶ Here is lively described the Lamb in the throne of God, receiving the book of the hand of him that sits and opening it. The.xxvij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
AND one of the Elders said unto me, weep not: Behold the Lion, which is of the tribe of Iuda, the root of David, hath obtained to open the book, to louse the seven seals thereof. And I beheld, and lo, in the midst of the seat, and of the four beasts, and in the midst of the Elders, stood a labe as though he had been killed, which had seven horns, and seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God, sent into all the world, and he came and toke the book out of the right hand of him that sate on the Throne.
[DRAFT]
And one of the Elders said to me, “Do not weep.” Behold, the Lion from the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has prevailed to open the book and to loose its seven seals. And I looked, and there, in the midst of the throne, and of the four living creatures, and in the midst of the Elders, stood a Lamb as though he had been slain, who had seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God sent into every world. And he came and took the book out of the right hand of him who sat on the throne.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "louse" changed to "loose." "Louse" in 1561 meant to release or unfasten, which is the intended meaning. "Loose" has a different connotation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "living creatures" added for "four beasts." While a common interpretation, it's an addition.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "all the world" changed to "every world." The original phrasing allows for a more universal interpretation; "every world" implies multiple worlds.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: "living creatures" is added content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And one of the Elders said to me, “Do not weep.” Behold, the Lion from the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has prevailed to open the book and to loose its seven seals. And I looked, and there, in the midst of the throne, and of the four living creatures, and in the midst of the Elders, stood a Lamb as though he had been slain, who had seven horns and seven eyes, which are the seven Spirits of God sent into every world. And he came and took the book out of the right hand of him who sat on the throne.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 27.1]Foraſmuch as John had wept, that no man was worthy so much as to look on the book of him that sate on the seat, much less to open it: One of the xxiiii. Elders comfort him. His name is not expressed, wherefore it seems to be required both vaynely and curiously. Notwithstanding there be some of the expoſitours, which suppose him to be the patriarch Iacob: Verily for that shortly after his oracle or prophecy is recited. And so the author descends in a most goodly order unto the description also of the son of God, by whom the celestial father, as all the scripture every where approves, governs all things. Hitherto hath he described him that sits on the seat, and before that the holy ghost. Wherefore these are wholesome and most profitable doctrines for the church, whereby the true faith is confirmed.
[DRAFT]
Since John had wept, that no one was worthy even to look upon the book of the one who sat on the throne, much less to open it, one of the twenty-four elders comforted him. His name is not expressed, and it seems unnecessary and curious to inquire about it. Nevertheless, some commentators suppose him to be the patriarch Jacob, because shortly afterward his oracle or prophecy is recited. And so the author proceeds in a most orderly fashion to the description also of the Son of God, by whom the heavenly Father, as all Scripture affirms everywhere, governs all things. Hitherto he has described the one who sits on the throne, and before that, the Holy Spirit. Therefore, these are wholesome and most profitable doctrines for the church, by which the true faith is confirmed.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "vaynely and curiously" changed to "unnecessary and curious." While similar, "vainly" carries a stronger sense of futility and potential sinfulness absent in "unnecessary."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "it seems unnecessary and curious to inquire about it" is a more direct judgment than the original's "wherefore it seems to be required both vaynely and curiously." The original implies a question of appropriateness, the draft a statement of judgment.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing allows for the possibility that the elder's name *should* be known, even if it isn't. The draft’s wording ("it seems unnecessary and curious to inquire about it") removes this ambiguity, suggesting the inquiry is inherently improper.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Since John had wept, that no one was worthy even to look upon the book of the one who sat on the throne, much less to open it, one of the twenty-four elders comforted him. His name is not expressed, and it seems unnecessary and curious to inquire about it. Nevertheless, some commentators suppose him to be the patriarch Jacob, because shortly afterward his oracle or prophecy is recited. And so the author proceeds in a most orderly fashion to the description also of the Son of God, by whom the heavenly Father, as all Scripture affirms everywhere, governs all things. Hitherto he has described the one who sits on the throne, and before that, the Holy Spirit. Therefore, these are wholesome and most profitable doctrines for the church, by which the true faith is confirmed.
---
[ORIGINAL]
The comfort of this elder, and verily the heavenly and most Godly doctrine tends to this end, that we should understand, that all the complaints, weeping, grudging, and the sundry tourmoylinges of our mind, can not be quenched, appeased and quieted, unless we see and believe, that to Christ (as here is most plainly and manifestly set forth) is given of the father all power in heaven and in earth: and therefore to be constituted like as the only redemer, so also the head, Prince, and governor of all, which under the seal of faith and verity, should govern all things that are by God his providence ordained, and even now despises them, and reveals unto us so much of God his iudgmentes as do suffice us. This if we believe with a faithful and sincere mind, we shall have quiet consciences in all the works of God, eve such as are harde to endure, and seem to some men most unreasonable. For we know that he by whom all things are governed, is of our nature and kind, yea eve our own brother: and such verily as favours us with all his heart, hath suffered death for us, and loves nothing better in all the world than man: Moreover which hath overcome death, son, the Devil and Hell, and hath overcome them for us. Who will now than suspect his government, permission or operation? Thou haste a brother in the Princes Court, whom thou art assured to favour thee from the botome of his heart: thou hear say, howe he has given unto him of the Prince the government and judgment of the whole country, would thou stick or be loathe to submit thy self unto him? nay rather thou doest trust and hope to obtain any thing of thy brother.
[DRAFT]
The comfort of this elder, and truly the heavenly and most godly doctrine aims at this end, that we should understand that all the complaints, weeping, grudging, and the various distresses of our mind, cannot be quenched, appeased, and quieted, unless we see and believe that to Christ (as is most plainly and manifestly set forth here) has been given by the Father all power in heaven and on earth: and therefore to be constituted, not only as the only Redeemer, but also as the head, Prince, and governor of all, which, under the seal of faith and truth, should govern all things that are by God’s providence ordained, and even now despises them, and reveals to us so much of God’s judgments as suffice us. This if we believe with a faithful and sincere mind, we shall have quiet consciences in all the works of God, even such as are hard to endure and seem to some men most unreasonable. For we know that he by whom all things are governed, is of our nature and kind, yes even our own brother: and such verily as favors us with all his heart, has suffered death for us, and loves nothing better in all the world than man: Moreover, who has overcome death, the Devil, and Hell, and has overcome them for us. Who will now then suspect his government, permission, or operation? You have a brother in the Prince’s Court, whom you are assured will favor you from the bottom of his heart: you hear how he has given to him of the Prince the government and judgment of the whole country, would you stick or be loathe to submit yourself unto him? Nay rather, you do trust and hope to obtain anything of your brother.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "despises them" (original) is changed to "even now despises them" (draft). The original implies a current state of disdain, which the draft emphasizes, potentially altering the nuance of God's actions.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "eve such as are harde to endure" (original) is changed to "even such as are hard to endure" (draft). While technically a correction of a typo, the original phrasing, with "eve," retains a slightly archaic, almost poetic quality that is lost in the more standard "even."
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes detected.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No content added.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a significant error.
[FINAL]
The comfort of this elder, and truly the heavenly and most godly doctrine aims at this end, that we should understand that all the complaints, weeping, grudging, and the various distresses of our mind, cannot be quenched, appeased, and quieted, unless we see and believe that to Christ (as is most plainly and manifestly set forth here) has been given by the Father all power in heaven and on earth: and therefore to be constituted, not only as the only Redeemer, but also as the head, Prince, and governor of all, which, under the seal of faith and truth, should govern all things that are by God’s providence ordained, and even despises them, and reveals to us so much of God’s judgments as suffice us. This if we believe with a faithful and sincere mind, we shall have quiet consciences in all the works of God, eve such as are harde to endure and seem to some men most unreasonable. For we know that he by whom all things are governed, is of our nature and kind, yes even our own brother: and such verily as favors us with all his heart, has suffered death for us, and loves nothing better in all the world than man: Moreover, who has overcome death, the Devil, and Hell, and has overcome them for us. Who will now then suspect his government, permission, or operation? You have a brother in the Prince’s Court, whom you are assured will favor you from the bottom of his heart: you hear how he has given to him of the Prince the government and judgment of the whole country, would you stick or be loathe to submit yourself unto him? Nay rather, you do trust and hope to obtain anything of your brother.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Therefore let us remember,[note 27.2] howe the Scripture not here only, but every where doth teach, that Jesus Christ the Son of God, and in deed of the same substance with us after his humanity, in dying for us, to have deserved to have a name given him, which is aboue all names, and that althinges should be subject to his government, what so ever be in the world visible or invisible. For so S. John testifies in the.1. Chapter. And S. Paul also to the philipp. 2. Coloſſ. 1. and to the Hebrews the.1. Chapter. He is said at this present to have overcome or obtained to open the book, and louse the Seals thereof. Therefore by the knoweledge of him, and through faith in him, we obtain, that with a ioyefull mind we may look upon the book, the judgements and all the works of God, and quietly and patiently to bear the opening thereof, and government of all together. &c.
[DRAFT]
Let us remember how the Scripture does not teach this only, but everywhere, that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, and in truth of the same substance with us after his humanity, in dying for us, deserved to have a name given him that is above all names, and that all things should be subject to his rule, whatever may be in the world, visible or invisible. For so John testifies in the first chapter. And Paul also to the Philippians, Colossians 1, and to the Hebrews in the first chapter. He is said at this present to have overcome or obtained to open the book and loose the seals thereof. Therefore, through knowledge of him and faith in him, we obtain that with a joyful mind we may look upon the book, the judgments, and all the works of God, and quietly and patiently bear the opening thereof and the rule of all things together. &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "howe the Scripture not here only, but every where doth teach" changed to "how the Scripture does not teach this only, but everywhere." The original phrasing emphasizes the pervasive nature of the teaching; the draft's phrasing is slightly less emphatic.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "that is" before "above all names" in "a name given him that is above all names" adds a slight interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. LOST AMBIGUITY: No issues.
[FINAL]
Let us remember how the Scripture does not teach this only, but everywhere, that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, and in truth of the same substance with us after his humanity, in dying for us, deserved to have a name given him that is above all names, and that all things should be subject to his rule, whatever may be in the world, visible or invisible. For so John testifies in the first chapter. And Paul also to the Philippians, Colossians 1, and to the Hebrews in the first chapter. He is said at this present to have overcome or obtained to open the book and loose the seals thereof. Therefore, through knowledge of him and faith in him, we obtain that with a joyful mind we may look upon the book, the judgments, and all the works of God, and quietly and patiently bear the opening thereof and the rule of all things together. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 27.3]But to the intent we may judge more rightly of Christ governor of all, although he hath already described him right lively: yet now he proceeds to paincte him out in his, that is to say, most godly and goodly colours, that we should not be nothing afraid of his government, neither that we should not with quiet minds most willingly submit our selves wholly to his government.
[DRAFT]
But so that we may judge more rightly of Christ as ruler of all, even though he has already depicted him vividly, now he proceeds to portray him in his most godly and excellent qualities, that we should not be afraid of his rule, nor fail to submit ourselves wholly and willingly to it with peaceful minds.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Paincte him out in his, that is to say, most godly and goodly colours" is flattened to "portray him in his most godly and excellent qualities." The original phrasing, while archaic, evokes a deliberate artistic rendering intended to reassure. The draft loses some of that intentionality.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "even though he has already depicted him vividly" is an interpretation of "although he hath already described him right lively." It's not explicitly stated in the original that the author believes Christ has *already* been depicted.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "neither that we should not..." is a double negative, creating a slight ambiguity that the draft resolves into a more straightforward statement. While the meaning is likely clear in context, the draft eliminates a stylistic quirk of the original.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short excerpt.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.
[FINAL]
But so that we may judge more rightly of Christ governor of all, although he hath already described him right lively: yet now he proceeds to paint him out in his, that is to say, most godly and goodly colours, that we should not be nothing afraid of his government, neither that we should not with quiet minds most willingly submit ourselves wholly to his government.

[ORIGINAL]
First is said, that a Lion of the tribe of Iuda hath overcome:[note 27.4] to wit that same Christ of ores: to have overcome the Devil, sin, death, the world, hell, and all power of the adversary. And he overcame in dying, and so achieved the high dignity, and was made Lord of all. The Devil is also called a Lion of S. Peter. Solomon and the Prophets call tirannes Lions.[note 27.5] Our Author therefore calls Christ a lion, not of the common sort, but of the tribe of Iuda. For he alludeth to the prophecy of the patriarch Iacob, which is in the.49. of Genes. he prophecies there that Schilo shall come, with plenty and good luck, which like a lion that hath taken his pray, nether is there any man that can drive him from it, can defend those that be his, whom he hath caught out of the dragos claws, so that no hostile power dare ones hiss against him. Christ therefore is declared a victor or co querour greatest,[note 27.6] most mighty, and most invincible. Which belongs to him alone. Yet shall you find Kings, which are every hour overcome of wicked lusts, which will suffer them selves to be called, invincible. Briefly, this first note in the description of Christ, shows that Jesus Christ governor of all, is that very same, whom the patriarches and prophets have prophesied to come into the world, a prince most invincible.
[DRAFT]
First, it is said that a Lion of the tribe of Judah has overcome [note 27.4]—that is, the same Christ of old—to have overcome the Devil, sin, death, the world, hell, and all power of the adversary. And he overcame in dying, and thus achieved the highest dignity, and was made Lord of all. The Devil is also called a lion by Saint Peter. Solomon and the Prophets call tyrants lions [note 27.5]. Our author therefore calls Christ a lion, not of the common sort, but of the tribe of Judah. For he alludes to the prophecy of the patriarch Jacob, which is in Genesis 49. He prophesies there that Shiloh shall come, with plenty and good fortune, which, like a lion that has taken its prey, neither can be driven from it, can defend those that are his, whom he has caught out of the dragon’s claws, so that no hostile power dares once hiss against him. Christ therefore is declared a victor, a conqueror greatest [note 27.6], most mighty, and most invincible. This belongs to him alone. Yet you will find kings who are every hour overcome by wicked desires, who will allow themselves to be called invincible. Briefly, this first note in the description of Christ shows that Jesus Christ, governor of all, is that very same whom the patriarchs and prophets have prophesied to come into the world, a prince most invincible.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Christ of ores" is rendered as "Christ of old." While close, "ores" implies a prior existence, a crucial theological point regarding Christ's pre-incarnate state. "Old" is less precise.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "the highest dignity" is an interpretation not explicitly present in the original. The original states "the high dignity," which is more neutral.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "can defend those that be his, whom he hath caught out of the dragos claws" is slightly more evocative and less direct than "can defend those that are his, whom he has caught out of the dragon’s claws." The draft loses a touch of the original's poetic quality.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]
First, it is said that a Lion of the tribe of Judah has overcome [note 27.4]—that is, the same Christ of old—to have overcome the Devil, sin, death, the world, hell, and all power of the adversary. And he overcame in dying, and thus achieved the highest dignity, and was made Lord of all. The Devil is also called a lion by Saint Peter. Solomon and the Prophets call tyrants lions [note 27.5]. Our author therefore calls Christ a lion, not of the common sort, but of the tribe of Judah. For he alludes to the prophecy of the patriarch Jacob, which is in Genesis 49. He prophesies there that Shiloh shall come, with plenty and good fortune, which, like a lion that has taken its prey, neither can be driven from it, can defend those that are his, whom he has caught out of the dragon’s claws, so that no hostile power dares once hiss against him. Christ therefore is declared a victor, a conqueror greatest [note 27.6], most mighty, and most invincible. This belongs to him alone. Briefly, this first note in the description of Christ shows that Jesus Christ, governor of all, is that very same whom the patriarchs and prophets have prophesied to come into the world, a prince most invincible.

[ORIGINAL]
Secodly Christ is called the rote of David, wherein he appears to have alluded to that saying of Isaiah in the.11.[note 27.7] chap. Than shall a bud come forth of the stock of Ieſſe, and a flour shall ascend out of the roots thereof. Namely Marie the daughter of David, of whom that most sacred flower Christ sprang and came, was the stock of Ieſſe. And of the very rotes of David, or of the virgin, I mean of the most true humane nature, Jesus Christ was borne very man into the world. For he toke no where the Angles nature, but the ſede of Abraham. He is therefore our brother,[note 27.8] of the same substance with us, after his humanity. These things do comfort us exceedingly, and confute heretics most strongly: which fine that Christ hath not a very humane body. We have more hereof in the.1. of Matthew. and.1.2.3. of Luke. [note 27.9] After it is expressly spoken of the same our lord, that he is in the midst of the Throne, in the midst of the iiii. beasts, and in the midst of the xxiiii. Elders: and is therefore exepted out of the nobre of creatures, out of the number of Angles, and out of the number of Saints. For he is greater than these, to wit of the same substance with the father, in glory and power equal. For the father is in the midst of the Throne, from thence proceeds the holy ghost: even there is found also now the lamb Christ, not only very man, but also very God.[note 27.10] And is a distinct person. For the blessed Trinity knows not any cofuſio. The father is God, the son is God, the holy ghost is God: yet are all three but one God, the father in his subsistence, the son in his, and the holy ghost in his, not making three Gods, but three proprieties and persons in one indiuiſible and eternal essence. And where as Christ is mentioned to be in the midst of the beasts, and in the midst of Elders: he is doubtless signified after the divine nature to be every where, to be the life and preservation of all creatures, also in the midst of his chosen, and of his Church. Therefore like as we believe Jesus Christ to be very man, so let us also believe him to be very God, of the same substance with God the father. Therefore let Seruiet perish with Arrius and Mahomet, and as many as deny Christ to be the son of God, coequal with the father in all things. Furthermore he is now also called a lamb,[note 27.11] not that he is a sheep of nature, but for that by a lamb is prefigured the innocent redemer of the world, and the only wholesome sacrifice of all faithful. A lamb is a token of innocence, and from the beginning appointed for sacrifices. Abel offered up a Lamb, after the law was offered a daily sacrifice, in the morning a lamb, and at evening a lamb. For christ is the expiation of them, which were in the beginning of the world, and which in the end shall be. The Paſchall lamb in the.12. of Exodus. Whose blood prohibited the Angel diſtroier from the houses and tents, represented the figure of Christ, by whose precious blood we are reconciled to God. This expoſitio of the Paschal lamb S. Peter him self in the.1. Pet. 1. and S. Paull in the.1. to the Corinth. 5. have brought. Isaiah accords with them in the.53. chapter. And so expounded by the Apostle S. Philip in the 8. of the Acts. Finally S. John Baptist, which with the fingar stretched out and poinctyng to Christ, exclamed: behold the Lamb of God, which takes away the sins of the world. Let us therefore believe, that the same Jesus Christ, unto whom all power is give of the father, to be our deliverer, our expiation, reconciliation, innocence, sanctification, justification and everlasting salvation: as he whom we shall hear in the xiii. Chapter. to have be slain from the beginning of the world, for so much as his only death, and one oblation made from the beginning of the world, and continually to the worlds end, doth sanctify all those that are sanctified Which the Apostle also affirms in the.10. to the Hebrews.
[DRAFT]
Secondly, Christ is called the root of David, wherein he appears to have alluded to that saying of Isaiah in the 11th chapter. Then shall a bud come forth of the stock of Jesse, and a flower shall ascend out of the roots thereof. Namely, Mary, the daughter of David, of whom that most sacred flower, Christ, sprang and came, was the stock of Jesse. And of the very roots of David, or of the virgin, I mean of the most true human nature, Jesus Christ was born very man into the world. For he took not the angelic nature, but the seed of Abraham. He is therefore our brother, of the same substance with us, after his humanity. These things do comfort us exceedingly and confute heretics most strongly, who deny that Christ has a very human body. We have more hereof in Matthew 1 and Luke 1:2-3. After it is expressly spoken of the same our Lord, that he is in the midst of the throne, in the midst of the four beasts, and in the midst of the twenty-four Elders: and is therefore excepted out of the number of creatures, out of the number of angels, and out of the number of saints. For he is greater than these, to wit, of the same substance with the Father, in glory and power equal. For the Father is in the midst of the throne, from thence proceeds the Holy Ghost: even there is found also now the Lamb, Christ, not only very man, but also very God. And is a distinct person. For the blessed Trinity knows no confusion. The Father is God, the Son is God, the Holy Ghost is God: yet are all three but one God, the Father in his subsistence, the Son in his, and the Holy Ghost in his, not making three Gods, but three properties and persons in one indivisible and eternal essence. And whereas Christ is mentioned to be in the midst of the beasts and in the midst of the Elders, he is doubtless signified after the divine nature to be everywhere, to be the life and preservation of all creatures, also in the midst of his chosen and of his Church. Therefore, like as we believe Jesus Christ to be very man, so let us also believe him to be very God, of the same substance with God the Father. Therefore let Servetus perish with Arius and Mahomet, and as many as deny Christ to be the Son of God, coequal with the Father in all things. Furthermore, he is now also called a Lamb, not that he is a sheep of nature, but for that by a lamb is prefigured the innocent redeemer of the world and the only wholesome sacrifice of all the faithful. A lamb is a token of innocence, and from the beginning appointed for sacrifices. Abel offered up a lamb; after the law was offered a daily sacrifice, in the morning a lamb, and at evening a lamb. For Christ is the expiation of them which were in the beginning of the world, and which in the end shall be. The Paschal lamb in Exodus 12 whose blood prohibited the destroying angel from the houses and tents, represented the figure of Christ, by whose precious blood we are reconciled to God. This exposition of the Paschal lamb, Jerome, Peter, and Paul have brought in 1 Peter 1 and 1 Corinthians 5. Isaiah accords with them in the 53rd chapter. And so expounded by the Apostle Philip in Acts 8. Finally, John the Baptist, which with the finger stretched out and pointing to Christ, exclaimed: “Behold the Lamb of God, which takes away the sins of the world.” Let us therefore believe that the same Jesus Christ, unto whom all power is given of the Father, is our deliverer, our expiation, reconciliation, innocence, sanctification, justification, and everlasting salvation: as he whom we shall hear in the 13th Chapter to have been slain from the beginning of the world, for so much as his only death and one oblation made from the beginning of the world, and continually to the world’s end, doth sanctify all those that are sanctified. Which the Apostle also affirms in Hebrews 10.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "He toke no where the Angles nature, but the ſede of Abraham" is modernized to "For he took not the angelic nature, but the seed of Abraham." While technically accurate, the original phrasing ("no where") emphasizes a deliberate absence, a lack of angelic lineage. The modernized version is less emphatic.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "Jerome, Peter, and Paul have brought in 1 Peter 1 and 1 Corinthians 5" is an interpretive addition. The original simply states "S. Peter him self in the.1. Pet. 1. and S. Paull in the.1. to the Corinth. 5." The draft explicitly attributes the interpretation to those figures, which isn't present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "of the very rotes of David, or of the virgin" is ambiguous, potentially referring to both David's lineage and Mary's virginity as sources of Christ's humanity. The draft's "And of the very roots of David, or of the virgin" retains the ambiguity, but the modernization subtly shifts the emphasis, making the "virgin" feel more like a clarification of the "roots of David" rather than a separate, potentially equally valid interpretation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is present in the draft not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom→Jerome" is missing. The original "Hierom" should be modernized to "Jerome." Austen should be Augustine, and Ireney should be Irenaeus.
[FINAL]

Namely, Mary, the daughter of David, of whom that most sacred flower, Christ, sprang and came, was the stock of Jesse. [ORIGINAL]
How be it this lamb or saviour of the world is said to stand in the midst of the throne, verily for that now he executes thoffice of a catholic king, and priest and governor, being always ready and prepared to save. So S. Stephen also in the.7. of the Acts sees him standing. Or else in other places we read that Christ sits on the right hand of the father. To the which this place gain says not, considering that to sit, is both to rest and to reign.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, this Lamb or Savior of the world is said to stand in the midst of the throne, truly because now he executes the office of a universal king, priest, and governor, always ready and prepared to save. Thus, Stephen also in Acts 7 sees him standing. Or else in other places we read that Christ sits on the right hand of the Father. This passage does not contradict that, considering that to sit signifies both rest and reign.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "catholic king" altered to "universal king." While related, "catholic" in 1561 carried a theological weight signifying the universal Church, not simply a broad descriptor of rulership. This shifts the nuance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "This passage does not contradict that" is an interpretive addition not present in the original. The original simply states the observation.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "gain says not" is awkward but preserves a slight ambiguity about the relationship between the two statements. The draft's phrasing eliminates this ambiguity, presenting a more definitive (and potentially unwarranted) reconciliation.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, this Lamb or Savior of the world is said to stand in the midst of the throne, verily for that now he executes the office of a catholic king, priest and governor, being always ready and prepared to save. So S. Stephen also in the.7. of the Acts sees him standing. Or else in other places we read that Christ sits on the right hand of the Father. To the which this place gain says not, considering that to sit is both to rest and to reign.

[ORIGINAL]
Moreover this our Lamb appears in the Throne of the divine majesty,[note 27.12] as if he were killed: not for that he was not slain in deed, and dead (for that same is a little after exceſſed most exactely) but for that he remained not in death, but the third day rose again from the dead, to the end he might so declare him self to be the life, and resurrection of the faithful. Or verily, for that after his humanity he is red to be slain, after his deity to be immortal, and subject to no reproach. Wherefore in the old law the one of the goats in the 16. of Leuitic. is slain: but the other is not killed but by the work of a man hereunto appointed is led forth into the desert. Nevertheless there is of the expoſitours, which expound it thus: he is said as though he were slain, for as much as after S. Chriſoſtome and S. Austen he hath reserved as yet the ſeares of the wounds of his death in token of his victory. &c.
[DRAFT]
Moreover, our Lamb appears on the throne of divine majesty,[note 27.12] as if he were slain; not because he was not truly slain and dead—for that is described in detail shortly thereafter—but because he did not remain in death, but rose again on the third day, so that he might declare himself to be the life and resurrection of the faithful. Or, indeed, because after his humanity he appears to be slain, but after his deity, he is immortal and subject to no reproach. Therefore, in the Old Law, one goat in Leviticus 16 is slain, but the other is not killed, but is led into the wilderness by the work of a man appointed for this purpose. Nevertheless, there are interpreters who explain it in this way: he is said to be as if he were slain, because, following John Chrysostom and Augustine, he has reserved the scars of his death’s wounds as a token of his victory. &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "exceſſed most exactely" changed to "described in detail shortly thereafter" loses the original's emphasis on the precision and thoroughness of the prior description of Christ's death.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "truly" before "slain" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing regarding Christ's appearance as slain allows for a more nuanced understanding of symbolic representation. The draft's "appears to be slain" is less evocative.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Austen changed to Augustine is correct. Ireney is missing.
[FINAL]
Moreover, our Lamb appears on the throne of divine majesty,[note 27.12] as if he were slain; not because he was not truly slain and dead—for that is described in detail shortly thereafter—but because he did not remain in death, but rose again on the third day, so that he might declare himself to be the life and resurrection of the faithful. Or, indeed, because after his humanity he appears to be slain, but after his deity, he is immortal and subject to no reproach. Therefore, in the Old Law, one goat in Leviticus 16 is slain, but the other is not killed, but is led into the wilderness by the work of a man appointed for this purpose. Nevertheless, there are interpreters who explain it in this way: he is said to be as if he were slain, because, following John Chrysostom and Augustine, he has reserved the scars of his death’s wounds as a token of his victory. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
Furthermore this lamb Christ Jesus our lord hath vii.[note 27.13] horns, not that in deed he carries so many horns like a goat of Iude. An horne, as appears by Daniel, and by the song of Zacharie in the 1. of Luke, signifies power and kingdom. The vii. number is the number of fulness. It is therefore signified that Christ is endued with all kind of power, divine, humane, imperial, pontifical, royal, briefly most absolute. In the 13. Chapter. we shall hear that the beast hath taken to him two horns, as it were of the lamb, whereof I shall speak in his place. Daniel in the 7. Chapter. And rule, says he, was given him, and honour and kyngedome, that all nations and tounges might worship him, whose rule is an everlasting rule, which shall not perish nor decay at any time. Now hath he seven eyes also. These he expounds, and says,[note 27.14] which are the vii. spirits of God, sent into the whole world. I showed you before, that the vii. spirits are called a ſeuenfold spirit. Here therefore is signified the fulness of the spirit, which the lord powereth out upon all flesh. Here is signified the universal knowledge of the son, in whose sight are present, what things so ever are done in heaven and in earth, openly and privily. For the spirit of Christ, that unmeasurable force, incomprehensible and most divine, searches and pearſeth all things, nothing is hid from his eyes, which view the whole world.
[DRAFT]
Furthermore, this Lamb, Christ Jesus our Lord, has seven horns, not that in truth he carries so many horns like a goat of Judea. An horn, as appears by Daniel and by the song of Zacharias in the first chapter of Luke, signifies power and kingdom. The number seven is the number of fullness. It is therefore signified that Christ is endowed with every kind of power, divine, human, imperial, pontifical, royal, briefly, most absolute. In the thirteenth chapter, we shall hear that the beast has taken to him two horns, as it were of the lamb, about which I shall speak in its place. Daniel in the seventh chapter says, “And rule was given him, and honor and kingdom, that all nations and tongues might worship him, whose rule is an everlasting rule, which shall not perish nor decay at any time.” Now he has seven eyes also. He expounds these, and says [note 27.14], which are the seven spirits of God, sent into the whole world. I showed you before that the seven spirits are called a sevenfold spirit. Here therefore is signified the fullness of the Spirit, which the Lord pours out upon all flesh. Here is signified the universal knowledge of the Son, in whose sight are present whatever things are done in heaven and on earth, openly and privately. For the spirit of Christ, that unmeasurable force, incomprehensible and most divine, searches and pierces all things; nothing is hidden from his eyes, which view the whole world.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "kind of power" changed to "every kind of power." While seemingly minor, "kind" implies a variety, whereas "every" suggests totality, potentially altering the nuance of the original's description of Christ's power.
2. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." (Not present in this passage, but a general note for future review.)
3. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is present.
[FINAL]
Furthermore, this Lamb, Christ Jesus our Lord, has seven horns, not that in deed he carries so many horns like a goat of Judea. An horn, as appears by Daniel, and by the song of Zacharie in the 1. of Luke, signifies power and kingdom. The seven number is the number of fulness. It is therefore signified that Christ is endued with all kind of power, divine, humane, imperial, pontifical, royal, briefly most absolute. In the 13. Chapter. we shall hear that the beast hath taken to him two horns, as it were of the lamb, whereof I shall speak in his place. Daniel in the 7. Chapter. And rule, says he, was given him, and honour and kyngedome, that all nations and tounges might worship him, whose rule is an everlasting rule, which shall not perish nor decay at any time. Now hath he seven eyes also. These he expounds, and says [note 27.14], which are the vii. spirits of God, sent into the whole world. I showed you before, that the vii. spirits are called a ſeuenfold spirit. Here therefore is signified the fulness of the spirit, which the lord powereth out upon all flesh. Here is signified the universal knowledge of the son, in whose sight are present, what things so ever are done in heaven and in earth, openly and privily. For the spirit of Christ, that unmeasurable force, incomprehensible and most divine, searches and pearſeth all things, nothing is hid from his eyes, which view the whole world.
---
[ORIGINAL]
And such is Christ, as we have heard described hitherto,[note 27.15] whom the Patriarches have before said should come, a victor and triumphant coquerour alone verily invincible, very man of our own substance, and also our very brother, yet very God nevertheless, of the same substance with the father and the holy ghost, the reconciler, redemer, and the only salvation of the world: hath suffered for us, and the same risen again from the dead, and ascended into heave, having all power in heaven and in earth, which sees all things, co municateth his spirit unto men, and is the most faithful keeper and defender of all man kind: This Christ Jesus our lord, came and received, he conueyed not or stale it away, but toke that book of the providence divine, of the judgements of God, of the universal, government of all things, that he might open, and louse the Seals thereof: that is to say, that he might reveal to us that are redeemed with his blood the judgements of God, and might dispose and order all things in heaven and in earth. Therefore sins we know that the governor of all things, is given to us a redemer, King, Bishop, and our only salvation, who will not from hence forth willingly submit himself to his government? And saying we now understand certainly, how that under the seal of faith and verity all things are done by Christ, who dare hereafter more curiouſely inquire of his works and iudgmentes, unto whose credit and government we should now comitte althinges,[note 27.16] in case they were in our power? Notwithstanding we shall observe, that the son doth not so receive these things of the father, that the father is deprived thereof. For in the.5. Chapter. of S. Iohns Gospel, the Lord says: my father works unto this time, and I work, &c. Certes the son is called the word, mouth, and arm of the father, &c. or that after the humanity the son might seem less than the father. For very godly Arethas, where the lamb, says he, received the book of the right hand of him that sits on the Throne, it must be understand on the behalf of his humanity: As also that he was slain. For concerning his deity, none of all those things that may worthily be spoken or thought of God, is severally assigned to three persons, saving the manner of bringing forth, of him that begets and of him that is begotten, and of him that proceeds, &c.
[DRAFT]
And such is Christ, as we have heard described hitherto, whom the patriarchs have previously said should come, a victor and triumphant conqueror alone, truly invincible, very man of our own substance, and also our very brother, yet very God nevertheless, of the same substance with the Father and the Holy Ghost, the reconciler, redeemer, and the only salvation of the world: he has suffered for us, and the same has risen again from the dead, and ascended into heaven, having all power in heaven and on earth, who sees all things, communicates his spirit to men, and is the most faithful keeper and defender of all humankind. This Christ Jesus our Lord came and received; he did not convey or steal it away, but took that book of divine providence, of the judgments of God, of the universal government of all things, that he might open and loose the Seals thereof: that is to say, that he might reveal to us who are redeemed with his blood the judgments of God, and might dispose and order all things in heaven and on earth. Therefore, if we know that the governor of all things is given to us as redeemer, King, Bishop, and our only salvation, who will from this time forth willingly submit himself to his government? And saying we now understand certainly how that under the seal of faith and truth all things are done by Christ, who would dare hereafter more curiously inquire of his works and judgments, to whose credit and government we should now commit all things, in case they were in our power? Nevertheless, we shall observe that the Son does not so receive these things of the Father that the Father is deprived thereof. For in the fifth chapter of John’s Gospel, the Lord says: “My Father works until now, and I work,” and so forth. Certainly, the Son is called the Word, mouth, and arm of the Father, and so on, lest after his humanity the Son should seem less than the Father. For very godly Arethas says, where the Lamb received the book from the right hand of him that sits on the Throne, it must be understood on behalf of his humanity, as also that he was slain. For concerning his deity, none of all those things that may worthily be spoken or thought of God is severally assigned to three persons, saving the manner of bringing forth, of him that begets and of him that is begotten, and of him that proceeds, and so on.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "verily invincible" is softened to "truly invincible." While not a massive shift, "verily" adds a level of emphasis absent in the draft.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "and so forth" is added multiple times. While intended to indicate continuation, it introduces a modern editorializing element that wasn't present in the original's more formal phrasing.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "he conueyed not or stale it away" is rendered as "he did not convey or steal it away." The original phrasing, with "stale it away," suggests a more subtle form of misappropriation than "steal it away" implies. This reduces the nuance.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The addition of "and so on" multiple times constitutes hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues. Hierom→Jerome, Austen→Augustine, Ireney→Irenaeus are not present in this passage.
[FINAL]
And such is Christ, as we have heard described hitherto, whom the patriarchs have previously said should come, a victor and triumphant conqueror alone, truly invincible, very man of our own substance, and also our very brother, yet very God nevertheless, of the same substance with the Father and the Holy Ghost, the reconciler, redeemer, and the only salvation of the world: he has suffered for us, and the same has risen again from the dead, and ascended into heaven, having all power in heaven and on earth, who sees all things, communicates his spirit to men, and is the most faithful keeper and defender of all humankind. This Christ Jesus our Lord came and received; he did not convey or steal it away, but took that book of divine providence, of the judgments of God, of the universal government of all things, that he might open and loose the Seals thereof: that is to say, that he might reveal to us who are redeemed with his blood the judgments of God, and might dispose and order all things in heaven and on earth. Therefore, if we know that the governor of all things is given to us as redeemer, King, Bishop, and our only salvation, who will from this time forth willingly submit himself to his government? And saying we now understand certainly how that under the seal of faith and truth all things are done by Christ, who would dare hereafter more curiously inquire of his works and judgments, to whose credit and government we should now commit all things, in case they were in our power? Nevertheless, we shall observe that the Son does not so receive these things of the Father that the Father is deprived thereof. For in the fifth chapter of John’s Gospel, the Lord says: “My Father works until now, and I work,” and so forth. Certainly, the Son is called the Word, mouth, and arm of the Father, and so on, lest after his humanity the Son should seem less than the Father. For very godly Arethas says, where the Lamb received the book from the right hand of him that sits on the Throne, it must be understood on behalf of his humanity, as also that he was slain. For concerning his deity, none of all those things that may worthily be spoken or thought of God is severally assigned to three persons, saving the manner of bringing forth, of him that begets and of him that is begotten, and of him that proceeds, and so on.

[ORIGINAL]
This description of Christ is singular, most excellent, very evangelical, and full of consolation: and therefore is it chiefly to be laid up in the botome of our hearts. Where we find also that they were deceived in their judgment, which were not afraid to say, that in this book, besides the Apoſtolical manner, few things were taught of Christ, and of our redemption. Let us pray unto the Lord, that he would voucheſafe to illumine our minds. Amen.
[DRAFT]
This description of Christ is singular, most excellent, very evangelical, and full of consolation; and therefore it is chiefly to be laid up in the bottom of our hearts. There we find that those were deceived in their judgment who were not afraid to say that, besides the apostolic manner, few things were taught in this book of Christ and of our redemption. Let us pray to the Lord that he would deign to illuminate our minds. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]
NO ISSUES
[FINAL]
This description of Christ is singular, most excellent, very evangelical, and full of consolation; and therefore it is chiefly to be laid up in the bottom of our hearts. There we find that those were deceived in their judgment who were not afraid to say that, besides the apostolic manner, few things were taught in this book of Christ and of our redemption. Let us pray to the Lord that he would deign to illuminate our minds. Amen.

[ORIGINAL]
## ¶ Here is described adoration and praise geuyng or an Himne song unto Christ of the beasts and Elders. The.xxviij. Sermon.
[DRAFT]
(heading preserved)
[CRITIQUE]

[FINAL]
## ¶ Here is described adoration and praise geuyng or an Himne song unto Christ of the beasts and Elders. The.xxviij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd when he had taken the book, the four beasts, and xxiiii. Elders fell down before the lamb, having Harps and golden Vialles full of odours (which are the prayers of Saints) and they sang a new song, saying: Thou art worthy to take the book, and to open the Seals thereof: for thou waste killed, and haste redeemed us by thy blood, out of all kinredes and tongues, and people and natios, and haste made us unto our God, Kings and Priests, and we shall reign on the earth.
[DRAFT]
And when he had taken the book, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb, having harps and golden vials full of fragrant incense (which are the prayers of the saints), and they sang a new song, saying: “You are worthy to take the book, and to open the seals thereof, for you were slain, and you have redeemed us by your blood, from every tribe and tongue, and people and nation, and you have made us to our God, kings and priests, and we shall reign on the earth.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "odours" to "fragrant incense" subtly alters the sensory experience. While incense is a plausible interpretation, "odours" is broader and could encompass other aromatic offerings.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None.
[FINAL]
And when he had taken the book, the four living creatures and the twenty-four elders fell down before the Lamb, having harps and golden vials full of odours (which are the prayers of the saints), and they sang a new song, saying: “You are worthy to take the book, and to open the seals thereof, for you were slain, and you have redeemed us by your blood, from every tribe and tongue, and people and nation, and you have made us to our God, kings and priests, and we shall reign on the earth.”
---
[ORIGINAL]
We have heard,[note 28.1] that the lamb hath received the book of the hand of him that sits in the Throne, that he might open it, and louse the seals of the same: that is we have understand, that Christ is the only and eternal Saviour and Lord, unto whom all power is given in heaven and earth: that he than only and evermore saves, that he reveals to us the mysteries and judgements of God, that he finally governs and disposes all things in the world. It follows moreover how all the creatures of God behaved them selves towards this son of God, the monarch and governor of all things. This thing is set forth with a maruelouſe figurative and plentiful speech in the Tip of the iiii. beasts, and xxiiii. Elders, &c. Certainly that we might of their gestures, words, and works understand, what it is mete for us to do in the judgements of God.
[DRAFT]
We have heard that the Lamb has received the book from the hand of him who sits on the throne, so that he might open it and break the seals of it; that is, we understand that Christ is the only and eternal Savior and Lord, to whom all power is given in heaven and earth; that he alone and forever saves, that he reveals to us the mysteries and judgments of God, and that he finally governs and disposes all things in the world. It follows moreover how all the creatures of God behaved themselves toward this Son of God, the monarch and governor of all things. This is set forth with a marvelous figurative and plentiful speech in the vision of the four beasts, twenty-four elders, and so on. Certainly, that we might from their gestures, words, and works understand what is fitting for us to do in the judgments of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "louse the seals" changed to "break the seals." "Louse" in this context means to release or unfasten, not break. This alters the original's nuance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "so that" after "from the hand of him who sits on the throne" adds a causal element not explicitly present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "Tip of the iiii. beasts" changed to "vision of the four beasts." "Tip" can refer to a section or part, leaving room for interpretation of its nature. "Vision" is more definitive and removes that ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues with proper nouns.
[FINAL]
We have heard that the Lamb has received the book from the hand of him who sits on the throne, so that he might open it and break the seals of it; that is, we understand that Christ is the only and eternal Savior and Lord, to whom all power is given in heaven and earth; that he alone and forever saves, that he reveals to us the mysteries and judgments of God, and that he finally governs and disposes all things in the world. It follows moreover how all the creatures of God behaved themselves toward this Son of God, the monarch and governor of all things. This is set forth with a marvelous figurative and plentiful speech in the vision of the four beasts, twenty-four elders, and so on. Certainly, that we might from their gestures, words, and works understand what is fitting for us to do in the judgments of God.
---
[ORIGINAL]
For this example is verily manifold, and even of six parts, such as you shall hardly find propounded in any other matter.[note 28.2] And in this matter is of very great force. First in deed we have heard in the iiii. Chapter. that the four beasts cried out before the Throne of him that sate: holy, holy, holy, lord God omnipotent. Second we understand that the xxiiii. Elders fell down, worshipped, cast away their crowns, and sang an Himne. Now follows the third degree of this example. For as first the beasts and Elders did these things severally, so now jointly with one accorde the beasts and Elders fall down together before the labe. Let us therefore fall down also in all the judgements and works of God, before the lamb governor of all, and let us worship. For although it be not here added, and they worshipped: yet are they to be understand for this intent to have fallen down, that they might worship. For to fall down, is to worship. Which thing is also perceived by this that follows.[note 28.3] For they offer prayers to the lamb, that is to wit, singe an Himne, which is a part of godly worshipping. Morouer it follows immediately, that every creature sang an Himne to him that sets in the Throne, and to the labe, &c. And verily two things especially and diligently S. John treats in this example. For first he paints out gallantly the behaviour of the beasts and elders. After he annexeth the Himne, praise geuyng, or song. And so much as appertains their behaviour. Before all things they fall down before the lamb: as even now I said.
[DRAFT]
For this example is truly manifold, and even consists of six parts, such as you will hardly find presented in any other subject. And this matter is of very great force. First, we have heard in the fourth chapter that the four beasts cried out before the throne of him who sat: holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty. Second, we understand that the twenty-four elders fell down, worshipped, cast away their crowns, and sang a hymn. Now follows the third degree of this example. For as first the beasts and elders did these things separately, so now jointly with one accord, the beasts and elders fall down together before the Lamb. Let us therefore fall down also in all the judgments and works of God, before the Lamb, governor of all, and let us worship. For although it is not here added, “and they worshipped,” they are to be understood to have fallen down for that purpose, because to fall down is to worship. This is also evident from what follows. For they offer prayers to the Lamb, that is to say, sing a hymn, which is a part of godly worship. Moreover, it follows immediately that every creature sang a hymn to him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb, and so on. And truly, two things especially and diligently Jerome treats in this example. First, he depicts brilliantly the behavior of the beasts and elders. After that, he adds the hymn, praise-giving, or song. And so much as pertains to their behavior. Above all, they fall down before the Lamb, as I said just now.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "manifold, and even of six parts" changed to "manifold, and even consists of six parts." While seemingly minor, "consists of" imposes a structure not explicitly stated.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "because to fall down is to worship" is an interpretation, not a direct translation of the original's implication. The original leaves the connection more suggestive.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing regarding the falling and worship is more suggestive and less definitive. The draft’s explanation reduces this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "S. John" changed to "Jerome." This is a significant error; the original refers to John, the author of Revelation.
5. HALLUCINATION: "praise geuyng" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Hierom→Jerome" is incorrect. The original text uses "S. John," which should be maintained as "St. John" in a modernized version. Austen should be Augustine, Ireney should be Irenaeus, but these are not present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
For this example is verily manifold, and even of six parts, such as you shall hardly find propounded in any other matter.[note 28.2] And in this matter is of very great force. First in deed we have heard in the iiii. Chapter. that the four beasts cried out before the Throne of him that sate: holy, holy, holy, lord God omnipotent. Second we understand that the xxiiii. Elders fell down, worshipped, cast away their crowns, and sang an Himne. Now follows the third degree of this example. For as first the beasts and Elders did these things severally, so now jointly with one accorde the beasts and Elders fall down together before the labe. Let us therefore fall down also in all the judgements and works of God, before the lamb governor of all, and let us worship. For although it be not here added, and they worshipped: yet are they to be understand for this intent to have fallen down, that they might worship. For to fall down, is to worship. Which thing is also perceived by this that follows.[note 28.3] For they offer prayers to the lamb, that is to wit, singe an Himne, which is a part of godly worshipping. Morouer it follows immediately, that every creature sang an Himne to him that sets in the Throne, and to the labe, &c. And verily two things especially and diligently St. John treats in this example. For first he paints out gallantly the behaviour of the beasts and elders. After he annexeth the Himne, praise, or song. And so much as appertains their behaviour. Before all things they fall down before the lamb: as even now I said.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.4]And this place is of efficacitie enough to prove the deity of our Saviour Christ. For these things ought to be conferred with those which are written upon the same words in the iiii. Chapter. The xxiiii. Elders fell down before him that sits in the Throne, and worship him that lives for ever and ever: And now it is said, that the self same elders have fallen down before the lamb: whereupon it follows that he that lives for ever and the lamb be worshipped with like glory, culte, and honour: And that the son is coequal with the father, to be worshipped for ever. Whereby now is openly perceived the abominable and detestable error of Arius and Seruetus,[note 28.5] confuted at this present not only of the beasts, but also of the whole cogregation of Saints in heave. Idle men reason subtly, and peruerte and wraſte gods word after their wonted giantly boldenes, at their pleasure: we will rather follow the examples of all saints and creatures in the world, and will worship the lamb with him that sits in the throne blessed for evermore.
[DRAFT]
And this passage is sufficiently powerful to demonstrate the deity of our Savior Christ. For these things should be compared with what is written on the same words in chapter four. The twenty-four elders fell down before him who sits on the throne and worship him who lives forever and ever. And now it is said that the very same elders have fallen down before the Lamb; whereupon it follows that he who lives forever and the Lamb are worshipped with like glory, cult, and honor: and that the Son is coequal with the Father, to be worshipped forever. By this, now, the abominable and detestable error of Arius and Servetus is openly perceived, refuted at this time not only by the beasts but also by the entire congregation of saints in heaven. Idle men reason subtly, perverting and twisting God’s word according to their usual, brazen boldness, at their pleasure. We will rather follow the examples of all saints and creatures in the world and will worship the Lamb with him who sits on the throne, blessed forevermore.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "efficacitie enough to prove" changed to "sufficiently powerful to demonstrate." While similar, "efficacity" implies a specific, inherent power, whereas "sufficiently powerful" is more general and weakens the original's assertion of inherent divine power.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's phrasing "should be compared" replaces "ought to be conferred," subtly shifting from a suggestion of reasoned discussion to a prescriptive instruction.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "giantly boldenes" is rendered as "brazen boldness." "Giantly" suggests a boldness born of size or scale, a nuance lost in "brazen."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Servetus is correctly modernized from Seruetus.
[FINAL]
And this place is of efficacitie enough to prove the deity of our Saviour Christ. For these things ought to be conferred with those which are written upon the same words in the iiii. Chapter. The xxiiii. Elders fell down before him that sits in the Throne, and worship him that lives for ever and ever: And now it is said, that the self same elders have fallen down before the lamb: whereupon it follows that he that lives for ever and the lamb be worshipped with like glory, culte, and honour: And that the son is coequal with the father, to be worshipped for ever. Whereby now is openly perceived the abominable and detestable error of Arius and Seruetus,[note 28.5] confuted at this present not only of the beasts, but also of the whole cogregation of Saints in heave. Idle men reason subtly, and peruerte and wraſte gods word after their wonted giantly boldenes, at their pleasure: we will rather follow the examples of all saints and creatures in the world, and will worship the lamb with him that sits in the throne blessed for evermore.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Again there are object to us the Elders lying prostrate on the pavement, holding in their hades harps and vialles.[note 28.6] An harp in the Pſalmes and holy history is an instrument of music, coſecrate to praises divine. Of the vialle, of what shape or fashion the cup was, the writers of vessels treat much: I understand it to be simply a cup or a bolle, such as we read there were many in the tabernacle and temple, appointed both for drink offerings, and also for sweet odours and incense. Nevertheless these things in the holy heavenly dwellers be not to be taken corporally, but spiritually, after a ſuffiguration. For what the spirit of God understood, the revealer of ſecrettes, S. John him self adds which be the prayers of Saints. Therefore is signified, that Saints offer prayers to God: which are much more acceptable to him,[note 28.7] than the sweet melody of Musical instruments is to man, or pleasant savour of sweet gumes or of incense. Arethas the expoſitour, in that they have Harps, says he, it shows a concorde and agreement in geuynge God thanks. And hereof we learn again, what we should do in the contemplation and understanding of the judgements and works of God. The lord is to be praised and blessed, because he is good, and his mercy endures for ever. But if thanks must be given to God, if his works and iudgmentes are to be praised, why do certain men expostulate with God, blaming, or bringing in suspicion his iudgemetes? let us learn moreover, that Organes and those corporal incenses do no longer become the church of God.
[DRAFT]
Again, the elders are presented to us, lying prostrate on the pavement, holding harps and vials. [note 28.6] A harp in the Psalms and holy history is an instrument of music, consecrated to divine praises. Concerning the vial, writers about vessels discuss much about its shape and fashion; I understand it to be simply a cup or a bowl, such as we read there were many in the tabernacle and temple, appointed both for drink offerings and also for sweet odors and incense. Nevertheless, these things in the holy heavenly dwellers are not to be taken corporally, but spiritually, by figuration. For what the Spirit of God understood, the revealer of secrets, Saint John himself adds, which are the prayers of the saints. Therefore, it is signified that saints offer prayers to God, which are much more acceptable to him [note 28.7] than the sweet melody of musical instruments is to man, or the pleasant savor of sweet gums or of incense. Arethas, the expositor, says that because they have harps, it shows a concord and agreement in giving God thanks. And from this we learn again what we should do in the contemplation and understanding of the judgments and works of God. The Lord is to be praised and blessed because he is good, and his mercy endures forever. But if thanks must be given to God, if his works and judgments are to be praised, why do certain men expostulate with God, blaming or bringing into suspicion his judgments? Let us learn moreover that organs and those corporal incenses do no longer become the church of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "holding in their hades harps" becomes "holding harps and vials." "Hades" is a specific, albeit archaic, term with theological connotations (the underworld) that is lost in the simplification.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The original uses "ſuffiguration" which implies a symbolic representation. The draft changes this to "by figuration," which is less precise and loses the nuance of the original’s theological context.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "the holy heavenly dwellers" is unusual and slightly ambiguous, potentially referring to angels or a heavenly realm. The draft's "the holy heavenly dwellers" removes this ambiguity, flattening the language.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of significant theological terms are present.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** All proper nouns are modernized correctly.
[FINAL]
Again, the elders are presented to us, lying prostrate on the pavement, holding harps and vials. [note 28.6] A harp in the Psalms and holy history is an instrument of music, consecrated to divine praises. Of the vial, writers about vessels discuss much about its shape and fashion; I understand it to be simply a cup or a bowl, such as we read there were many in the tabernacle and temple, appointed both for drink offerings and also for sweet odors and incense. Nevertheless, these things in the holy heavenly dwellers are not to be taken corporally, but spiritually, after a ſuffiguration. For what the Spirit of God understood, the revealer of secrets, Saint John himself adds, which are the prayers of the saints. Therefore, it is signified that saints offer prayers to God, which are much more acceptable to him [note 28.7] than the sweet melody of musical instruments is to man, or the pleasant savor of sweet gums or of incense. Arethas, the expositor, says that because they have harps, it shows a concord and agreement in giving God thanks. And from this we learn again what we should do in the contemplation and understanding of the judgments and works of God. The Lord is to be praised and blessed because he is good, and his mercy endures forever. But if thanks must be given to God, if his works and judgments are to be praised, why do certain men expostulate with God, blaming or bringing into suspicion his judgments? Let us learn moreover that organs and those corporal incenses do no longer become the church of God.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Of this place S. Ireney in the 4. book against heresies,[note 28.8] in the.33. and.34. Chapter. shows, that the prayers and thanks geuyng of Saints be the same oblation which Malachie prophesied to be offered up through out the whole world. And shortly after Tertullian followed the same exposition agyynſt the Jews, and in the iiii. book against Marcion: whom other doctors of the Church have followed. But those pleasant sophistical triflers, I mean the popiſſhe divines, do as it were triumph in those things, yet lead they in the mean time a shadowing and a most vain triumph. For they apply these things to their sacrifice, wherein they fain them selves under the some of bread and wine to offer up to God the father the body and blood of Christ, a propiciatory sacrifice for the quick and the dead. But Ireney and Tertullian speak not of such a sacrifice, but of the oblation of prayers, which the maſſemonging Priest offers not up alone, but the whole cogregation of Christ sanctified in his blood, geuynge thanks in the lords supper to God the father for their free redemption. These holy fathers never knew the sale Masses of these Chananites.
[DRAFT]
Saint Irenaeus, in the fourth book against heresies, chapters 33 and 34, demonstrates that the prayers and thanksgiving of the saints are the same offering that Malachi prophesied would be offered throughout the world. Shortly afterward, Tertullian followed the same explanation against the Jews and in the fourth book against Marcion, and other church doctors have followed suit. But those pleasant, sophistical triflers—I mean the Roman Catholic theologians—seem to triumph in these matters, yet they lead a shadowy and utterly empty triumph. For they apply these things to their sacrifice, in which they claim to offer to God the Father the body and blood of Christ, a propitiatory sacrifice for the living and the dead, under the guise of bread and wine. But Irenaeus and Tertullian do not speak of such a sacrifice, but of the offering of prayers, which the Mass-priest does not offer alone, but the entire congregation of Christ, sanctified in his blood, giving thanks to God the Father for their free redemption in the Lord’s Supper. These holy fathers never knew the sale Masses of these Canaanites.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "Mass" is introduced as a descriptor. The original uses "maſſemonging Priest" which is a pejorative, but doesn't explicitly name "Mass" as a concept. The draft's capitalization and framing subtly shifts the meaning.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "followed suit" is added after Tertullian. This implies a direct continuation of thought that isn't present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "Chananites" is rendered as "Canaanites." While the original is likely a scribal error, changing it removes the ambiguity and potential for a deliberate, albeit flawed, reference.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "popiſſhe divines" is modernized to "Roman Catholic theologians." While technically accurate, it loses the specific, and somewhat derogatory, tone of the original.
5. **MEANING DRIFT:** The original’s phrasing "lead they in the mean time a shadowing and a most vain triumph" is softened to "lead a shadowy and utterly empty triumph." The original is more forceful and condemnatory.
[FINAL]
Saint Irenaeus, in the fourth book against heresies, chapters 33 and 34, demonstrates that the prayers and thanksgiving of the saints are the same offering that Malachi prophesied would be offered throughout the world. Shortly afterward, Tertullian followed the same exposition against the Jews and in the fourth book against Marcion: whom other doctors of the Church have followed. But those pleasant, sophistical triflers—I mean the popiſſhe divines—do as it were triumph in those things, yet lead they in the mean time a shadowing and a most vain triumph. For they apply these things to their sacrifice, wherein they fain them selves under the some of bread and wine to offer up to God the father the body and blood of Christ, a propiciatory sacrifice for the quick and the dead. But Ireney and Tertullian speak not of such a sacrifice, but of the oblation of prayers, which the maſſemonging Priest offers not up alone, but the whole cogregation of Christ sanctified in his blood, geuynge thanks in the lords supper to God the father for their free redemption. These holy fathers never knew the sale Masses of these Chananites.
