[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.9]Of this same place of S. John the self same Papists go about to prove and to establish the praying to Saints in heaven. Behold, say they, the Saints are said to pray openly in heave. But they need not to pray for them selves, and therefore as intercessors and Patrones they pray for their clients and worshippers in Earth. I answer, that the Saints in deed pray in heaven, but that you adding the kind and manner of praying, do expound it to be intercession, patchyng to a piece of your own, do forge and coutrefet the same, and maliciously and falsely do belie it. S. John here expounds him self, so that he needs not your patchyng. For he adds, and they singe a new song. Yea and he recites the whole form of this song, lest any man should corrupt that which he had said of prayers. And that same form conteyneth praise and blessing or thanks geuyng, and not intercession, or invocation. For certain it is even by the doctrine of the Apostle. 1. Timoth. 2. and to the Philipp. 4. that there be two chief kinds of prayer: invocation and praise or thanks geuyng. But the thing it self plainly proves, that S. John speaks here of the latter, and not of the first.[note 28.10] And where as this place expounds certain Tips, shadows or mysteries of Gods law, by the same we may rather confute the intercession of Saints in Heaven, for their worshippers. For in the law is permitted one only golden Altar of incense. And the same represented a figure of Christ. For one Christ is the mediator and intercessor betwixt God and man. It was not laweful for the people of God, to burn incense, but upon this Altar only. It was not laweful for any man, to prepare or make for him self an odour of those kind of gumes, whereof the divine incense consisted, and to smell to it: as appears in the.30. of Exodus. Why than doe not these understand, that prayers belong to God alone, and that the Saints in Heaven would not smell of such incense? David in the.141. psalm. says, let my prayer be directed as incense in thy sight, the liſtynge up of my hands an evening sacrifice. The Devil desires to have such manner of incenses to be made unto him: As appears in the.4. of Matthew. and in S. Austen of the City of God. But our heavenly Saints, are not Deuilles. Why vnderſtonde they not that this Altar of incense stands now in Heaven on the right hand of the father, and there makes intercession for us: and that for his sake the father is reconciled to us, and we are accepted of God, and that by him alone we must offer up our prayers unto God, which are else abominable? Why see they not the heavenly Saints at this present to attribute all things to the only lamb alone, and to challenge nothing to them selves? Finally that they make no mention of their worshippers: but plainly testify, that the only lamb was and is worthy, which should take the book, &c.
[DRAFT]
[note 28.9] Regarding this same passage from the writings of John, the Roman Catholics attempt to use it to prove and establish the practice of praying to saints in heaven. “Behold,” they say, “the saints are depicted as praying openly in heaven.” But they argue that they do not pray for themselves, and therefore, as intercessors and patrons, they pray for their clients and worshippers on earth. I reply that the saints indeed pray in heaven, but you, by adding the kind and manner of praying, do misinterpret it as intercession, adding a piece of your own invention, and maliciously and falsely distort its meaning. John explains himself here, so that your additions are unnecessary. For he adds, “and they sing a new song.” Indeed, he recites the entire form of this song, lest anyone corrupt what he had said about prayers. And that same form contains praise and blessing or thanksgiving, and not intercession or invocation. It is certain, even according to the Apostle’s doctrine, as found in 1 Timothy 2 and Philippians 4, that there are two chief kinds of prayer: invocation and praise or thanksgiving. But the thing itself plainly shows that John speaks here of the latter, not the former. [note 28.10] And whereas this passage explains certain types, shadows, or mysteries of God’s law, by the same reasoning we may better refute the intercession of saints in heaven for their worshippers. For in the law, only one golden altar of incense was permitted. And this altar represented a figure of Christ. For one Christ is the mediator and intercessor between God and man. It was not lawful for the people of God to burn incense, but only upon this altar. It was not lawful for any man to prepare or make for himself an odor of those kinds of gums from which the divine incense consisted, and to offer it to God, as appears in Exodus 30. Why, then, do these people not understand that prayers belong to God alone, and that the saints in heaven would not offer such incense? David in Psalm 141 says, “Let my prayer be directed as incense in your sight, the lifting up of my hands as an evening sacrifice.” The Devil desires to have such incense made to him, as appears in Matthew 4 and in Augustine’s *City of God*. But our heavenly saints are not devils. Why do they not understand that this altar of incense now stands in heaven on the right hand of the Father, and there makes intercession for us? And that for his sake the Father is reconciled to us, and we are accepted of God, and that through him alone we must offer up our prayers to God, which are otherwise abominable? Why do they not see the heavenly saints at this present attributing all things to the only Lamb alone, and claiming nothing for themselves? Finally, that they make no mention of their worshippers, but plainly testify that the only Lamb was and is worthy, who should take the book, and so on?
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "patchyng to a piece of your own, do forge and coutrefet the same, and maliciously and falsely do belie it" is softened to "adding a piece of your own invention, and maliciously and falsely distort its meaning." The original conveys a stronger accusation of deliberate deception.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "the Roman Catholics attempt to use it" in note 28.9 is an interpretive addition not present in the original. It frames the argument in a modern context.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing "the liſtynge up of my hands an evening sacrifice" is rendered as "the lifting up of my hands as an evening sacrifice." While functionally similar, the original's slightly awkward phrasing contributes to its archaic tone and a certain level of ambiguity that is lost.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "the writings of John" in note 28.9 is an addition. The original simply states "Of this same place of S. John."
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" is correctly modernized to "Augustine."
[FINAL]
[note 28.9] Of this same place of S. John the self same Papists go about to prove and to establish the praying to Saints in heaven. Behold, say they, the Saints are said to pray openly in heave. But they need not to pray for them selves, and therefore as intercessors and Patrones they pray for their clients and worshippers in Earth. I answer, that the Saints in deed pray in heaven, but that you adding the kind and manner of praying, do expound it to be intercession, patchyng to a piece of your own, do forge and counterfeit the same, and maliciously and falsely do belie it. S. John here expounds him self, so that he needs not your patchyng. For he adds, and they singe a new song. Yea and he recites the whole form of this song, lest any man should corrupt that which he had said of prayers. And that same form conteyneth praise and blessing or thanks geuyng, and not intercession, or invocation. For certain it is even by the doctrine of the Apostle. 1. Timoth. 2. and to the Philipp. 4. that there be two chief kinds of prayer: invocation and praise or thanks geuyng. But the thing it self plainly proves, that S. John speaks here of the latter, and not of the first. For in the law is permitted one only golden Altar of incense. And the same represented a figure of Christ. For one Christ is the mediator and intercessor betwixt God and man. It was not laweful for the people of God, to burn incense, but upon this Altar only. [ORIGINAL]
And the praise or thanks giving of the heavenly saints he hath called a new song,[note 28.11] which in the Scriptures is no new thing. For the Saints say, that they will singe in Earth unto God a new song, Psalm xxxiii.xcvi xcviii.cxliiii. Isaiah.xlij. And new songs be called these new balades or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done. And because the mind of man is delighted greatly with new benefits, they singe a new song, which with a ioyefull mind praise GOD, and give him thanks with their inward affections. Finallye they singe a new song, which with purified minds and renewed with the Spirit doe laud GOD: which thing was chiefly given to those heavenly Saints. Whereof we learn again howe it behooves our minds to be affected and furniſſhed in the prayers and praises of GOD. This same, says Arethas, I cal a new song, by whose benefit we, which being lighted in all parts of the earth, departing from the antiquity of the law written, and walking in the newnes of life, are taught by the holy ghost to singe a giving of thanks.
[DRAFT]
And the praise or thanksgiving of the heavenly saints he has called a new song, which in the Scriptures is no new thing. For the saints say that they will sing on Earth unto God a new song, Psalm 33, 96, 98, 149. Isaiah 42. New songs are called these new ballads or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done. And because the mind of man is delighted greatly with new benefits, they sing a new song, which with a joyful mind praise God, and give him thanks with their inward affections. Finally, they sing a new song, which with purified minds and renewed by the Spirit do laud God: which thing was chiefly given to those heavenly saints. Whereof we learn again how it behooves our minds to be affected and furnished in the prayers and praises of God. This same, says Arethas, I call a new song, by whose benefit we, which being enlightened in all parts of the earth, departing from the antiquity of the law written, and walking in the newness of life, are taught by the Holy Spirit to sing a giving of thanks.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "singe" changed to "sing" multiple times. While a minor lexical change, "singe" can imply a burning or passionate quality, potentially softening the original's intensity.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The explanation of "new songs" as "balades or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done" feels like an added interpretation. The original doesn't explicitly define the *reason* for new songs beyond human delight in new benefits.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "with the Spirit doe laud GOD" changed to "renewed by the Spirit do laud God." The original phrasing allows for a broader understanding of spiritual agency; the draft's revision is more specific, implying a direct renewal *by* the Spirit.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Arethas" is correct. "Hierom" should be "Jerome" (not present in this passage). "Austen" should be "Augustine" (not present in this passage). "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus" (not present in this passage).
[FINAL]
And the praise or thanksgiving of the heavenly saints he has called a new song, which in the Scriptures is no new thing. For the saints say that they will sing on Earth unto God a new song, Psalm 33, 96, 98, 149. Isaiah 42. New songs are called these new ballads or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done. And because the mind of man is delighted greatly with new benefits, they sing a new song, which with a joyful mind praise God, and give him thanks with their inward affections. Finally, they sing a new song, which with purified minds and renewed with the Spirit do laud God: which thing was chiefly given to those heavenly saints. Whereof we learn again how it behooves our minds to be affected and furnished in the prayers and praises of God. This same, says Arethas, I call a new song, by whose benefit we, which being enlightened in all parts of the earth, departing from the antiquity of the law written, and walking in the newness of life, are taught by the Holy Spirit to sing a giving of thanks.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.12]To these things now is added the Himne of Saints that we might also have a form howe to praise God. And in the Hymn they singe, that all things are subject to christ, and all things ordered by his government, that he humbled him self to the death, and was therefore exalted aboue all things. Now are also the virtues or effects and wonderful benefits of his death commended unto us, that eſtemyng the governor of his benefits done to us, we may believe also that his government shall be wholesome for us, and therefore may submit our selves to him willingly in faith and patience. Which verily is the chiefest end of those things which here are treated with so great diligence.
[DRAFT]
To these things now is added the Hymn of Saints, so that we might also have a form for praising God. And in the Hymn they sing that all things are subject to Christ, and all things ordered by his rule, that he humbled himself to death, and was therefore exalted above all things. Now also the virtues, effects, and wonderful benefits of his death are commended to us, so that, valuing the ruler of the benefits done to us, we may also believe that his rule will be wholesome for us, and therefore may submit ourselves to him willingly in faith and patience. This truly is the chiefest end of those things which here are treated with such diligence.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "howe to praise God" changed to "for praising God" slightly alters the nuance; the original suggests a pattern or example.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "valuing the ruler of the benefits done to us" adds a layer of interpretation not explicitly present in the original, which simply states "eſtemyng the governor of his benefits."
LOST AMBIGUITY: "government" in the original carries a broader sense of divine order, while "rule" in the draft is more limited.
NO ISSUES
[FINAL]
To these things now is added the Hymn of Saints, so that we might also have a form for praising God. And in the Hymn they sing that all things are subject to Christ, and all things ordered by his rule, that he humbled himself to death, and was therefore exalted above all things. Now also the virtues, effects, and wonderful benefits of his death are commended to us, so that, valuing the ruler of the benefits done to us, we may also believe that his rule will be wholesome for us, and therefore may submit ourselves to him willingly in faith and patience. This truly is the chiefest end of those things which here are treated with such diligence.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.13]First they comende the Majesty and dignity of Christ, that he alone is found in the whole universal world, which hath rule over all, which is the only saviour of the world, the revealer also of godly mysteries, and governor of all. For this is to take and to open the book: which we have now oft times repeated. Secondly they annexe the cause, why this glory should be only of the lamb or son of God: because, say they, thou waste killed. And they understand by the less the more, to wit his whole incarnation, and the whole mystery of our redemption, death, resurrection and aſcenſio into heaven, and the reſidewe. He therefore is the true and only mediator of God and men, he is the only saviour, as he that alone was incarnate and crucified for us: he is the only governor, which by his humility deserved to be exalted, Philipp. 2. And he is a most fit governor of all things, as of whom all men may, as of their most faithful saviour, and even their brother, hope well, what things so ever chance unto them through his government, &c.
[DRAFT]
First they commend the majesty and dignity of Christ, that he alone is found throughout the entire world, who has rule over all, who is the only savior of the world, the revealer also of godly mysteries, and governor of all. For this is to take and to open the book, which we have now often repeated. Secondly, they add the reason why this glory should be only of the Lamb or Son of God: because, they say, you were slain. And they understand by the lesser the greater, namely, his whole incarnation, and the whole mystery of our redemption, death, resurrection, and ascension into heaven, and the remainder. He therefore is the true and only mediator of God and men; he is the only savior, as he alone was incarnate and crucified for us; he is the only governor, who by his humility deserved to be exalted, Philippians 2. And he is a most fitting governor of all things, as of whom all men may, as their most faithful savior and even their brother, hope well, whatever things may befall them through his governance, etc.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "residewe" is rendered as "the remainder." While not incorrect, "residewe" implies the remaining aspects of Christ's work, carrying a more theological weight than "the remainder" suggests.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "namely, his whole incarnation..." is an interpretive gloss not present in the original. It clarifies *how* the lesser relates to the greater, but this clarification wasn't explicitly offered in 1561.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "by the less the more" is deliberately ambiguous, inviting contemplation. The draft's explanation removes this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Philipp. is modernized to Philippians 2. Correct.
[FINAL]
First they commend the majesty and dignity of Christ, that he alone is found throughout the entire world, who has rule over all, who is the only savior of the world, the revealer also of godly mysteries, and governor of all. For this is to take and to open the book, which we have now often repeated. Secondly, they add the reason why this glory should be only of the Lamb or Son of God: because, they say, you were slain. And they understand by the lesser the greater, namely, his whole incarnation, and the whole mystery of our redemption, death, resurrection, and ascension into heaven, and the remainder. He therefore is the true and only mediator of God and men; he is the only savior, as he alone was incarnate and crucified for us; he is the only governor, who by his humility deserved to be exalted, Philippians 2. And he is a most fitting governor of all things, as of whom all men may, as their most faithful savior and even their brother, hope well, whatever things may befall them through his governance, etc.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.14]In the mean time they comende most highly the virtue or effect of Christ's death. For this being rightly understand we are more ready to submit our selves to that governor, whom we know to be our saviour, which loves us dearly, and would have all saved. And the chiefest effect of Christ's death is redemption,[note 28.15] redeminge includes captivity. We were prisoners and servants of sin, of death, and very bondeſlaues of the devil and hell. And the son of God came and toke flesh, and shed his blood (for so also is the manner of redemyng us expressed of the elders) and he hath washed us from our sins, and being purified he hath ransomed us from the power of death, hell, sin and Satan, that now we be of God. Therefore they say expressly, thou hast redeemed us to God. We be therefore of God: the devil hath no more power over us: we are the freemen of Christ, delivered through his blood. 1. Peter. 1. Hebrews. 9. And for as much as we be now of God, to wit justified frely, by his grace, through the blood of Christ, as the apostle says also in the 3. Chapter. to the Romans, we ought to serve God verily in the newnes of spirit, not the flesh and the devil, in the oldenes of the letter and of our flesh. Which the same Apostle discourses more at large in the 6. to the Romans.
[DRAFT]
In the meantime, they commend highly the power or effect of Christ’s death. For this, being rightly understood, we are more ready to submit ourselves to that governor whom we know to be our savior, who loves us dearly and would have all saved. And the chiefest effect of Christ’s death is redemption; redemption includes deliverance from captivity. We were prisoners and servants of sin, of death, and very bondslaves of the devil and hell. And the Son of God came and took flesh, and shed his blood (for so also is the manner of redeeming us expressed by the ancients) and he has washed us from our sins, and being purified, he has ransomed us from the power of death, hell, sin, and Satan, that now we belong to God. Therefore, they say expressly, “You have redeemed us to God.” We belong therefore to God; the devil has no more power over us; we are the freemen of Christ, delivered through his blood. 1 Peter, 1; Hebrews, 9. And inasmuch as we now belong to God, namely, justified freely by his grace through the blood of Christ, as the apostle also says in the 3rd chapter to the Romans, we ought to serve God truly in the newness of spirit, not the flesh and the devil, in the oldness of the letter and of our flesh. Which the same apostle discusses more at length in the 6th chapter to the Romans.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "bondeſlaues" is softened to "bondslaves." While semantically close, "bondeſlaues" carries a stronger sense of being bound and enslaved, a nuance potentially lost.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "expressed by the ancients" replaces "of the elders." This adds a layer of interpretation; the original simply references a tradition without explicitly categorizing it.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "We be therefore of God" is changed to "We belong therefore to God." While functionally similar, the original phrasing has a slightly more profound theological implication of *being* part of God's essence, which "belonging" diminishes.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "elders" should be "ancients" to match the original.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
[FINAL]
In the meantime, they commend highly the power or effect of Christ’s death. For this, being rightly understood, we are more ready to submit ourselves to that governor whom we know to be our savior, who loves us dearly and would have all saved. And the chiefest effect of Christ’s death is redemption; redemption includes deliverance from captivity. We were prisoners and servants of sin, of death, and very bondeſlaues of the devil and hell. And the Son of God came and took flesh, and shed his blood (for so also is the manner of redeeming us expressed of the ancients) and he has washed us from our sins, and being purified, he has ransomed us from the power of death, hell, sin, and Satan, that now we be of God. Therefore, they say expressly, “You have redeemed us to God.” We be therefore of God; the devil has no more power over us; we are the freemen of Christ, delivered through his blood. 1 Peter, 1; Hebrews, 9. And inasmuch as we be now of God, namely, justified freely by his grace through the blood of Christ, as the apostle also says in the 3rd chapter to the Romans, we ought to serve God truly in the newness of spirit, not the flesh and the devil, in the oldness of the letter and of our flesh. Which the same apostle discusses more at length in the 6th chapter to the Romans.

[ORIGINAL]
Whom also he hath redeemed, they declare by the way,[note 28.16] men verily of every tribe, &c. In the which rehearsal he doth Imitate Daniel in the 7. chap. and signifies an universality. For the lord hath died for all: but that all are not made pertakers of this redemption, it is through their own fault. For the lord excludes no man, but him only which through his own incredulity, and miſbelefe excludes him self.
[DRAFT]
Those whom he has redeemed declare it along the way, men certainly of every tribe, and so forth. In this recounting, he imitates Daniel in chapter 7, signifying universality. For the Lord has died for all; but that all are made partakers of this redemption is due to their own fault. For the Lord excludes no one, but only him who, through his own unbelief, excludes himself.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "verily" changed to "certainly" slightly alters the emphasis; "verily" carries a stronger sense of truth and affirmation than "certainly."
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "In this recounting, he imitates Daniel..." is interpretive. The original simply states imitation; the draft adds an explanation of *why* it's significant.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "it is through their own fault" is more ambiguous regarding *what* is due to their own fault. The draft's "is due to their own fault" is more direct and removes some of that ambiguity.
NO ISSUES regarding theological terms.
HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present to check.
[FINAL]
Those whom he has redeemed declare it along the way, men certainly of every tribe, and so forth. In this recounting, he imitates Daniel in chapter 7, signifying universality. For the Lord has died for all; but that all are made partakers of this redemption is due to their own fault. For the Lord excludes no one, but only him who, through his own unbelief, excludes himself.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Of redeption follows an other effect of Christ's death,[note 28.17] for that it makes men justified to God kings and priests. For they that be justified, work rightuouſnes. I have expounded this place concerning the priesthood and kingdom of Christians in the first chapter where you may have it.
[DRAFT]
Following redemption is another effect of Christ's death,[note 28.17] for it makes people righteous before God, kings, and priests. For those who are righteous are workers of righteousness. I have explained this passage concerning the priesthood and kingdom of Christians in the first chapter, where you may find it.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Kings and priests" in the original likely refers to the spiritual roles adopted by believers, not a literal designation. "Kings and priests" in the draft reads as a more literal, potentially hierarchical, interpretation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's phrasing "makes people righteous before God" adds a layer of explanation not explicitly present in the original. The original simply states "makes men justified to God kings and priests."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing is slightly more open to multiple interpretations regarding the nature of justification. The draft’s more direct wording reduces this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
The Saints add moreover that they shall reygne upon earth, to wit through the virtue of Christ: not corporally,[note 28.18] as the Millenaries do imagine, and the Turks following the same, imagining corporal things in this world, and joys in Paradise terrestrial. For the whole scripture promises better things. Neither must the godly be so given to corporal things, that they should hope for nothing aboue corporal matters. The Saints speak here of the last judgment wherein it shall appear to the whole world, and to all that dwell upon the earth, that the Saints, which some time seemed to the world to have ben wicked, ungodly, peacebreakers, heretics, and parricides, and for the same cause have ben slain, be just, holy kings and priests of God. So I say they shall reign upon earth. The which thing is declared more at large in the iii. and v. Chapter. of the book of wisdom.
[DRAFT]
The Saints also add that they will reign on earth, namely through the power of Christ: not physically, as the Millenniumists imagine, and the Turks following the same, imagining physical things in this world and joys in a terrestrial paradise. For the entire Scripture promises better things. Neither must the faithful be so devoted to physical things that they should hope for nothing beyond physical matters. The Saints speak here of the final judgment, in which it will be shown to the whole world and to all who dwell on earth that the saints, who at one time seemed to the world to have been wicked, ungodly, peacebreakers, heretics, and parricides, and for that reason were slain, are righteous, holy kings and priests of God. Thus I say they will reign on earth. This matter is explained more fully in chapters 3 and 5 of the Book of Wisdom.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "virtue" changed to "power" subtly alters the theological nuance. "Virtue" implies inherent goodness and moral strength, while "power" can be interpreted more broadly.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "namely through the power of Christ" adds a slight explanatory element not explicitly present in the original "to wit through the virtue of Christ."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "godly" changed to "faithful" reduces the potential for multiple interpretations of the original term, which could encompass both piety and righteousness.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

## ¶ Here is described the commendation and Hymn said unto Christ of the Angels and all creatures. &c. The xxix. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd I beheld, and heard the voice of many Angles about the Throne, and about the beasts and the elders, and I heard thousand thousands saying with a load voice: worthy is the lamb that was killed, to receive power, and riches and wisdom, and strength and honour, and glory, and blessing. And all creatures which are in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth, and in the Sea, and all that are in them, heard. I saying: blessing, honour, glory, and power be unto him, that sits upon the seat, and to the lamb for ever more. And the iiii. beasts said, Amen. And the xxiiii. Elders fell upon their faces and worshipped him that lives for ever more.
[DRAFT]
And I looked, and heard the voice of countless angels around the throne, around the beasts and the elders, and I heard ten thousand thousands saying with a mighty voice: “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain to receive power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and honor, and glory, and blessing.” And every creature that is in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth, and in the sea, and all that are in them, heard and said: “Blessing, honor, glory, and power be to him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb forever and ever.” And the four living creatures said, “Amen.” And the twenty-four elders fell on their faces and worshiped him who lives forever and ever.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "many Angles" changed to "countless angels." While semantically close, "many" implies a large but unspecified number, while "countless" suggests an immeasurable quantity. This subtly alters the sense of scale.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of quotation marks around the pronouncements of the angels and creatures introduces a layer of interpretation, suggesting these are direct, spoken utterances. The original implies a collective declaration.
LOST AMBIGUITY: "thousand thousands" modernized to "ten thousand thousands." While mathematically equivalent, "thousand thousands" retains a more archaic, almost symbolic, quality absent in the more literal "ten thousand thousands."
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And I looked, and heard the voice of countless angels around the throne, around the beasts and the elders, and I heard ten thousand thousands saying with a mighty voice: “Worthy is the Lamb who was slain to receive power, and riches, and wisdom, and strength, and honor, and glory, and blessing.” And every creature that is in heaven, and on the earth, and under the earth, and in the sea, and all that are in them, heard and said: “Blessing, honor, glory, and power be to him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb forever and ever.” And the four living creatures said, “Amen.” And the twenty-four elders fell on their faces and worshiped him who lives forever and ever.

[ORIGINAL]
In the fourth place now come the Angels of God also to the Elders and to the beasts,[note 29.1] I mean to the most excellent creatures of God, and together with them praise with an hymn God and the lamb: doubtless for an example to us, that, as I ofttimes say and repeat, we might understand what thing becomes us also.
[DRAFT]
Fourthly, the Angels of God now come to the elders and to the beasts, [note 29.1] that is, to the most excellent creatures of God, and together with them praise God and the Lamb in a hymn; certainly, this is for our example, as I often say and repeat, so that we might understand what is fitting for us as well.
[CRITIQUE]

ADDED INTERPRETATION: "that is" is unnecessary explanatory filler. The original relies on the reader to understand the connection.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "what thing becomes us also" is more open to interpretation regarding duty and appropriate behavior than the draft's "what is fitting for us as well."
[FINAL]
Fourthly, the Angels of God now come to the elders and to the beasts, [note 29.1] I mean to the most excellent creatures of God, and together with them praise God and the Lamb in an hymn; doubtless for an example to us, as I ofttimes say and repeat, we might understand what thing becomes us also.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Of Angels David in c.iiii. Psalm. speaking emogs other things: which makes, says he, his Angels spirits,[note 29.2] and his ministers a flame of fire. He testifies therefore that the Angels were made or created of God. By their substance he calls them spirits, and by a parable a flame of fire, which is pure, bright, most swift, piercing, and burning. Therefore after their sort and mean the Angelical spirits be altogether such: whom by their office he calls ministers,[note 29.3] to wit of God and man. For S. Paull also to the Hebr. brynging this same place of David, be they not all, says he, ministering spirits, which are sent forth into ministry, for them which be heirs of Salvation? verily understanding men. These things teach us to judge rightly of Angels, and that no man should worship ministers, or any be they never so excellent creatures, for their godly gifts. Neither in deed can the Angels or Saints abide them selves to be worshipped. Here doubtless they attribute all glory to God and to the Lamb, to God three and one, that all we should doe the like. Here is also declared the place, wherein the Angles were, about the Throne, about the beasts, and about the Elders. Therefore they guarded all these places round about as it were a guard. Daniel in times paste saw things not much unlike these in the.7. Chapter. Certainly they stand like ministers and servitors, ready to do service. Angels are said also to be about the godly upon earth, and to attend upon the salvation and ministry of men. In the.34. Psalm. David sings, he being afflicted called upon the lord, and the Lord heard him, and from all his troubles he delivered him. The Angel of the lord pitches his tents about them which fear him (the lord) and he delivers the. And not much unlike things you may read in the.91. Psalm. And thou shalt here note, that those that be afflicted do cal upon the lord, and not the Angels: And that the lord doth hear, and deliver, and for the working thereof uses the ministry of Angels, as his ministers. And like as no man that is well in his wit doth reverence, cal upon, and worship the son, for that God by the same gives great benefits to men. So no man honours, calls upon, and worſhippeth Angels for that God uses their ministry in deliueryng of men.
[DRAFT]
Of Angels David in Psalm 4 speaking among other things: which makes, says he, his Angels spirits, and his ministers a flame of fire. He testifies therefore that the Angels were made or created of God. By their substance he calls them spirits, and by a parable a flame of fire, which is pure, bright, most swift, piercing, and burning. Therefore after their sort and manner the Angelical spirits be altogether such: whom by their office he calls ministers, to wit of God and man. For Paul also to the Hebrews, bringing this same place of David, says, “Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for those who will inherit salvation?” truly understanding men. These things teach us to judge rightly of Angels, and that no man should worship ministers, or any, however excellent creatures they may be, for their godly gifts. Neither in deed can the Angels or Saints abide themselves to be worshipped. Here doubtless they attribute all glory to God and to the Lamb, to God three and one, that all we should do the like. Here is also declared the place wherein the Angels were, about the throne, about the beasts, and about the Elders. Therefore they guarded all these places round about as it were a guard. Daniel in times past saw things not much unlike these in chapter 7. Certainly they stand like ministers and servants, ready to do service. Angels are said also to be about the godly upon earth, and to attend upon the salvation and ministry of men. In Psalm 34, David sings, he being afflicted called upon the Lord, and the Lord heard him, and from all his troubles he delivered him. The Angel of the Lord pitches his tents about them which fear him, and he delivers them. And not much unlike things you may read in Psalm 91. And you shall here note that those that are afflicted do call upon the Lord, and not the Angels: And that the Lord doth hear, and deliver, and for the working thereof uses the ministry of Angels, as his ministers. And like as no man that is well in his wit doth reverence, call upon, and worship the son, for that God by the same gives great benefits to men, so no man honors, calls upon, and worships Angels for that God uses their ministry in delivering of men.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "emogs other things" changed to "among other things." While semantically close, "emogs" suggests a more direct engagement or inclusion, potentially softening the original's tone.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "be they never so excellent creatures" is softened to "however excellent creatures they may be." The original phrasing carries a stronger emphasis on the potential for idolatry, even with exceptionally impressive beings.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. Paull" changed to "Paul." While acceptable, the abbreviation "S." (for Saint) was common and its removal subtly alters the original's style and potentially its theological context.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a significant error.
5. **MEANING DRIFT:** "verily understanding men" changed to "truly understanding men." While subtle, "verily" carries a stronger sense of certainty and conviction, which is lost in the modernization.
6. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "And not much unlike things you may read in the.91. Psalm" changed to "And not much unlike things you may read in Psalm 91." The original includes the period, which could imply a specific verse or passage, whereas the draft removes it, making it a general reference.
[FINAL]
Of Angels David in Psalm 4 speaking among other things: which makes, says he, his Angels spirits,[note 29.2] and his ministers a flame of fire. He testifies therefore that the Angels were made or created of God. By their substance he calls them spirits, and by a parable a flame of fire, which is pure, bright, most swift, piercing, and burning. Therefore after their sort and manner the Angelical spirits be altogether such: whom by their office he calls ministers,[note 29.3] to wit of God and man. [ORIGINAL]
[note 29.4]Now also he puts the nobre of Angels, but a certain for an uncertain, thousand thousands for innumerable. He alludeth in the mean time to that saying of Daniel in the 7. Chapter. Thousand Thousands served him, and ten hundred thousand assisted him. Of a great and innumerable army we are woonte to esteem the power of kings. What than shall we think of the power of our God, which is the God of hooſtes, and whom not only innumerable legions of Angels, but all creatures serve? And what an excellent praise is it, which is song together of so many blessed spirits?
[DRAFT]
Now he also states the number of angels, but a certain number for an uncertain one, thousands of thousands for innumerable. He alludes in the meantime to that saying of Daniel in chapter 7: “Thousands upon thousands served him, and ten thousands assisted him.” We are accustomed to consider the power of kings as a great and innumerable army. What, then, shall we think of the power of our God, who is the God of hosts, and whom not only innumerable legions of angels, but all creatures serve? And what an excellent praise is sung together by so many blessed spirits?
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "woonte to esteem" changed to "are accustomed to consider" slightly alters the nuance of habitual perception. The original implies a common, perhaps even uncritical, understanding. The draft suggests a more deliberate consideration.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "We are accustomed to consider the power of kings as a great and innumerable army" feels like an added explanatory step. The original simply states the comparison; the draft explicitly frames it as a common perception.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "a certain for an uncertain" is deliberately vague, reflecting a theological difficulty in comprehending divine numbers. The draft's "a certain number for an uncertain one" loses some of that vagueness by implying a specific, albeit unknown, number exists.

NO ISSUES
[FINAL]

He alludes in the meantime to that saying of Daniel in chapter 7: “Thousands upon thousands served him, and ten thousands assisted him.” We are accustomed to consider the power of kings as a great and innumerable army. What, then, shall we think of the power of our God, who is the God of hosts, and whom not only innumerable legions of angels, but all creatures serve? And what an excellent praise is sung together by so many blessed spirits?

[ORIGINAL]
For after this, the proper duty or office of Angles is touched: They singe praises to God, and comende the labe of God, and that with a load voice. It skills not greatly wheter with a lowe or an high voice thou ſingeſt praises to the Lord: but for as much as they that cry with a load voice, are for the most part sore moved, as overwhelmed with great sorrow, or with great gladness rejoicing, therefore shall we praise God with a load voice, in case with a stout spirit, and with the inward affection of the heart we shall praise God.
[DRAFT]
Following this, the proper duty of the angels is addressed: they sing praises to God and commend God’s work, and that with a loud voice. It matters little whether you sing praises to the Lord with a low or a high voice; but because those who cry with a loud voice are often deeply moved, whether overwhelmed with great sorrow or rejoicing with great gladness, therefore we shall praise God with a loud voice, if with a steadfast spirit and with the inward affection of the heart we praise God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "It skills not greatly wheter with a lowe or an high voice thou ſingeſt praises to the Lord" is softened to "It matters little whether you sing praises to the Lord with a low or a high voice." While functionally similar, the original carries a slightly more dismissive tone, suggesting the difference is unimportant. The draft's phrasing is more neutral.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None apparent.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None apparent.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None present in this excerpt.
5. HALLUCINATION: None apparent.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
Following this, the proper duty of the angels is addressed: they sing praises to God and commend God’s work, and that with a loud voice. It skills not greatly whether with a low or an high voice thou singest praises to the Lord; but for as much as they that cry with a loud voice are for the most part sore moved, as overwhelmed with great sorrow, or with great gladness rejoicing, therefore shall we praise God with a loud voice, in case with a stout spirit, and with the inward affection of the heart we shall praise God.

For they celebrate the lamb, that is to say the son, which as he is the saviour alone, so hath he deserved to receive all power and glory, and to govern all things: as is said before.

[ORIGINAL]
And seven things do the Angles attribute to the labe, that is to Jesus Christ our Lord, on the right hand of the father. First [illegible], power to wit godly, almighty, viuificatiue, and conſeruatiue. Of this I spake also before. Secodly [illegible] riches. For he is rich, as the Apostle says, for all that cal upon him. Rom. 10. And Primasius: Christ him self, says he, is the treasure of all good things, &c. For ſchaddais, the sufficiency of all goods of the mind and body: And if it be lawful to attribute a profane word unto God, he is very Saturne, fulfilling all creatures. And sins the Angles do so comende Christ, who would think, that men should so scrape to themselves, as though they themselves could fill their own desires? Than they attribute to Christ wisdom, to wit godly and great. For the son is the wisdom of the father. Whereof treats Solomon much. By this wisdom ca he rule all things by most agreeable and best government. Who shall say? Thus it should have ben done. The wisdom of God hath most goodlily and well made all things from the beginning, so that our reason can justly blame nothing: what thing shall we blame than now in the universal government of Christ? They ascribe unto Christ also strength to execute verily such things as he hath most wisely ordained, finally strength to defend his, and to subdue the adversaries. For he is almighty. Such things as follow, honour, glory, and thanks giving, are declared before, what they be, and of what force: saving that the thing he called first [illegible] he calls now [illegible], blessing, praise, and geuyng of thanks.
[DRAFT]
And seven things the Angles attribute to the label, that is to Jesus Christ our Lord, on the right hand of the Father. First, power, namely godly, almighty, vivificative, and conservative. Of this I spoke also before. Secondly, riches. For he is rich, as the Apostle says, for all who call upon him. Romans 10. And Primasius says, “Christ himself is the treasure of all good things.” For *schaddais* is the sufficiency of all goods of the mind and body. And if it be lawful to attribute a profane word unto God, he is very Saturn, fulfilling all creatures. And the Angles commend Christ so, who would think that men should strive so for themselves, as though they themselves could fill their own desires? Then they attribute to Christ wisdom, namely godly and great. For the Son is the wisdom of the Father, a topic Solomon treats much. By this wisdom he can rule all things by most agreeable and best government. Who shall say? Thus it should have been done. The wisdom of God has most beautifully and well made all things from the beginning, so that our reason can justly blame nothing: what thing shall we blame now in the universal government of Christ? They ascribe unto Christ also strength to execute truly such things as he has most wisely ordained, finally strength to defend his, and to subdue the adversaries. For he is almighty. Such things as follow, honor, glory, and thanksgiving, are declared before, what they are, and of what force; saving that the thing he called first grace, he calls now blessing, praise, and giving of thanks.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "scrape to themselves" is altered to "strive so for themselves," softening the original's condemnation of avarice.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "a topic Solomon treats much" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s illegible words are simply omitted, losing potential nuance. While modernization necessitates some omissions, a note indicating the lost content would be preferable.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
This Hymn said in the praise of Christ,[note 29.6] teaches that Christ is very God, of the same substance and coequal with the father, greater than Angels, yea the lord of Angles, whom the angels themselves also worship, as S. Paul in the 1. to the Hebr. hath declared. Here than are confuted who so ever prefer Angels to Christ. The heretics are confuted that are called Aungelical, to wit worshippers of Angels. The angels themselves do here reprove their error: that not without cause they are accounted of S. Austen emongs heretics. If riches, glory and honour be dew to Christ alone, and that he excells them, wherefore are the same co municated to creatures? Otherwise we admonish all godly, that they think highly of Angels, and acknowledge and marvel at the benefits of God in them: and that we love the same as brethren, and fellows, and coinheriters of the same salvation: much less contemn or blame them, Whereof I will speak more at an other time.
[DRAFT]
This hymn, sung in praise of Christ,[note 29.6], teaches that Christ is truly God, of the same substance and coequal with the Father, greater than angels, indeed the Lord of angels, whom the angels themselves also worship, as Paul declared in the first letter to the Hebrews. Thus, anyone who places angels above Christ is refuted. The heretics called Angelicals—that is, those who worship angels—are refuted. The angels themselves here rebuke their error: rightly are they considered heretics by Augustine. If riches, glory, and honor are due to Christ alone, and he surpasses them, why are they communicated to creatures? Otherwise, we admonish all faithful people to esteem angels highly, to acknowledge and marvel at the benefits of God through them; and to love them as brethren, companions, and coheirs of the same salvation; much less to despise or blame them. Of this I will speak more at another time.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "dew to Christ alone" changed to "due to Christ alone." While technically a correction, "dew" implies a rightful entitlement, a nuance potentially lost.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Thus, anyone who places angels above Christ is refuted." The original states that such people *are* refuted, not that the hymn *refutes* them. This adds an interpretive layer.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "whom the angels themselves also worship" is more evocative and open to interpretation regarding the nature of angelic worship. The draft's phrasing is more direct and less suggestive.
4. PROPER NOUNS: Austen → Augustine is correct.
5. NO ISSUES regarding theological terms or hallucination.
[FINAL]
This hymn, sung in praise of Christ,[note 29.6], teaches that Christ is truly God, of the same substance and coequal with the Father, greater than angels, indeed the Lord of angels, whom the angels themselves also worship, as Paul declared in the first letter to the Hebrews. Thus, anyone who places angels above Christ is refuted. The heretics called Angelicals—that is, those who worship angels—are refuted. The angels themselves here rebuke their error: rightly are they considered heretics by Augustine. If riches, glory, and honor are due to Christ alone, and he surpasses them, why are they communicated to creatures? Otherwise, we admonish all faithful people to esteem angels highly, to acknowledge and marvel at the benefits of God through them; and to love them as brethren, companions, and coheirs of the same salvation; much less to despise or blame them. Of this I will speak more at another time.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Hitherto hath he recited the excellent praises, the Panegyrical verses or Himnes of the excellent creatures of God, of those Elders in deed especially and generally of all,[note 29.7] moreover of Angles also, said unto Christ our redemer and prince. And yet not with these contented he adds morouer in the fifth place, the agreement, and praise, and submission of all the creatures in the world, to the intent that if happily we be not moved with the excellent example of the excellent creatures, Elders and Angles, now at the last we might be abashed, which see all creatures of their own accorde to do their duty. For man sins he is Lord of all, and all thing were for him created, howe I pray you shall he offend more haynouſely against God, which hath made him Lord over all, than if by his hardenes, ingratitude, and maliciouſnes he not only doth not his own duty: but is rather inferriour to all creatures: as he which alone contends with God, and attributeth not to him dew praise. Therefore doth this example greatly excite man, that he should submit him self unto God, and give God the whole glory: and in no wise strive with God, nor complain of any thing. But mark I pray you with howe diligent a division of things he comprises all creatures, excludes none, the Devil only excepts, what time he raccompteth the creatures that are in Heaven, which are in Earth, which are under the Earth, and in the Sea: finally he adds, and all that be therein. Therefore if all things created doe celebrate and worship him that sits in the Throne and the lamb, and submit themselves unto him: is it not a shame, yea and a soul shame, that man alone, Lord of all, should revolt to the sworn enemy of God, the Devil, and with him to expostulate with God, to taught and blame and find fault with his judgements and gouernementes to complain of his works and will?
[DRAFT]
Hitherto he has recited the excellent praises, the celebratory verses or hymns, of the excellent creatures of God, of those Elders in particular and generally of all, moreover of Angels also, said unto Christ our Redeemer and prince. And yet not with these contented, he adds moreover in the fifth place, the agreement, and praise, and submission of all the creatures in the world, so that if happily we are not moved by the excellent example of the excellent creatures, Elders and Angels, now at the last we might be abashed, which see all creatures of their own accord to do their duty. For man sins; he is Lord of all, and all things were for him created. How, I pray you, shall he offend more heinously against God, who has made him Lord over all, than if by his hardness, ingratitude, and maliciousness he not only does not do his own duty, but is rather inferior to all creatures; as he who alone contends with God, and attributes not to him due praise? Therefore this example greatly excites man, that he should submit himself unto God, and give God the whole glory; and in no wise strive with God, nor complain of any thing. But mark, I pray you, with how diligent a division of things he comprises all creatures, excludes none, the Devil only excepts, when he recounts the creatures that are in Heaven, which are in Earth, which are under the Earth, and in the Sea; finally he adds, and all that be therein. Therefore if all things created do celebrate and worship him that sits in the Throne and the Lamb, and submit themselves unto him, is it not a shame, yea and a soul shame, that man alone, Lord of all, should revolt to the sworn enemy of God, the Devil, and with him to expostulate with God, to teach and blame and find fault with his judgments and governments, to complain of his works and will?
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "haynouſely" modernized to "heinously" loses some of the original's emphasis on the severity of the offense.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "so that if happily we are not moved..." is an interpretive addition, not present in the original. It clarifies the *purpose* of the example, which the original leaves more open to the reader's consideration.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing “howe I pray you shall he offend more haynouſely against God” is more rhetorical and open-ended. The draft’s “How, I pray you, shall he offend more heinously against God” is slightly more direct and less evocative.
4. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Hitherto he has recited the excellent praises, the celebratory verses or hymns, of the excellent creatures of God, of those Elders in particular and generally of all, moreover of Angels also, said unto Christ our Redeemer and prince. And yet not with these contented, he adds moreover in the fifth place, the agreement, and praise, and submission of all the creatures in the world, so that if happily we are not moved by the excellent example of the excellent creatures, Elders and Angels, now at the last we might be abashed, which see all creatures of their own accord to do their duty. For man sins; he is Lord of all, and all things were for him created. How, I pray you, shall he offend more heinously against God, who has made him Lord over all, than if by his hardness, ingratitude, and maliciousness he not only does not do his own duty, but is rather inferior to all creatures; as he who alone contends with God, and attributes not to him due praise? Therefore this example greatly excites man, that he should submit himself unto God, and give God the whole glory; and in no wise strive with God, nor complain of any thing. But mark, I pray you, with how diligent a division of things he comprises all creatures, excludes none, the Devil only excepts, when he recounts the creatures that are in Heaven, which are in Earth, which are under the Earth, and in the Sea; finally he adds, and all that be therein. Therefore if all things created do celebrate and worship him that sits in the Throne and the Lamb, and submit themselves unto him, is it not a shame, yea and a soul shame, that man alone, Lord of all, should revolt to the sworn enemy of God, the Devil, and with him to expostulate with God, to teach and blame and find fault with his judgments and governments, to complain of his works and will?

[ORIGINAL]
[note 29.8]You marvel, I wot well, howe all creatures, ſyns many of them be void of reason, and insensible can praise God. How be it this figure proſopopeia that is the feigning of persons is very common with all the prophets, and chiefly with David, praise the him Sun and Moon, says he, praise him the bright stars. Praise the Lord from the earth the Dragons and all deep places, fire, hayle, snow, Iſe, &c. And by such manners of speaking the Prophets would encourage and ſtire up men to praise God: saying that creatures which have no life, do after their manner praise God, see that you after your manner doe praise God in Hymns and spiritual Pſalmes. And in deed David shows a plain reason, why he commands bodies that have no life to praise God, let them praise, says he, the name of the lord, why? because he commanded, and they were created. As though he should say: they be his creatures, and in that they remain yet, they have it of him: therefore let them make the name of God gloriouſe, as of their maker and preserver. And he signified also the manner of praising, where he adds, he hath ordained them, that they should endure for ever: he gave them an ordenaunce, neither is it transgressed. As if he should have said. Where they neglect no part of those things, whereunto they are made, but are ready in their place, order, and time, and do their duty exceedingly well, do they not preach unto men the wonderful wisdom and power of God? For in an other Pſalme also david says, the heavens show forth the glory of God, and the firmament declares the works of his hands. &c. Thus I say the creatures with out life do praise and comende the name of God unto men, what time they are moved, work wonderfully, and obediently do the thing were unto they are appointed.
[DRAFT]
You marvel, I well know, how all creatures, even many that lack reason and feeling, can praise God. However, this figure of personification—the feigning of persons—is very common among all the prophets, and especially with David, who says, “Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, bright stars.” “Praise the Lord from the earth, sea monsters and all deep places, fire, hail, snow, and ice.” And by such ways of speaking, the prophets would encourage and stir up people to praise God, saying that creatures which have no life, after their manner, praise God; see that you, after your manner, do praise God in hymns and spiritual psalms. Indeed, David shows a plain reason why he commands bodies that have no life to praise God: “Let them praise the name of the Lord,” he says, “because he commanded, and they were created.” As though he should say: they are his creatures, and in that they remain, they have it of him; therefore let them make the name of God glorious, as of their maker and preserver. And he signified also the manner of praising, where he adds, “He hath ordained them that they should endure forever; he gave them an ordinance, neither is it transgressed.” As if he should have said: Where they neglect no part of those things for which they were made, but are ready in their place, order, and time, and do their duty exceedingly well, do they not preach unto men the wonderful wisdom and power of God? For in another psalm also, David says, “The heavens show forth the glory of God, and the firmament declares the works of his hands.” Thus I say the creatures without life do praise and commend the name of God unto men, when they are moved, work wonderfully, and obediently do the thing unto which they are appointed.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Dragons and all deep places" changed to "sea monsters and all deep places." While "sea monsters" isn't inherently incorrect, "dragons" held specific cultural and theological connotations in 1561 that "sea monsters" lacks, potentially softening the original's imagery.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The insertion of em-dashes around "the feigning of persons" and the subsequent explanation ("the feigning of persons—the feigning of persons—") adds a layer of analysis not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing "whereunto they are made" is altered to "for which they were made." While semantically similar, the original phrasing allowed for a broader interpretation of purpose, while the draft’s phrasing is slightly more restrictive.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes to theological terms.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No errors in proper noun modernization.
[FINAL]
You marvel, I well know, how all creatures, even many that lack reason and feeling, can praise God. However, this figure of prosopopeia—the feigning of persons—is very common with all the prophets, and especially with David, who says, “Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, bright stars.” “Praise the Lord from the earth, dragons and all deep places, fire, hail, snow, and ice.” And by such manners of speaking, the prophets would encourage and stir up people to praise God, saying that creatures which have no life, after their manner, praise God; see that you, after your manner, do praise God in hymns and spiritual psalms. Indeed, David shows a plain reason why he commands bodies that have no life to praise God: “Let them praise the name of the Lord,” he says, “because he commanded, and they were created.” As though he should say: they are his creatures, and in that they remain, they have it of him; therefore let them make the name of God gloriouſe, as of their maker and preserver. And he signified also the manner of praising, where he adds, “He hath ordained them that they should endure forever; he gave them an ordenaunce, neither is it transgressed.” As if he should have said: Where they neglect no part of those things whereunto they are made, but are ready in their place, order, and time, and do their duty exceedingly well, do they not preach unto men the wonderful wisdom and power of God? For in another Pſalme also, David says, “The heavens show forth the glory of God, and the firmament declares the works of his hands.” Thus I say the creatures with out life do praise and comende the name of God unto men, when they are moved, work wonderfully, and obediently do the thing were unto they are appointed.

[ORIGINAL]
The Hymn of all creatures, like as that was of the beasts,[note 29.9] and Elders and Angels, is here also trimmely described, although briefly. But where it hath nothing, that hath not be declared before, I will not by oft repetyng and speaking the same things, molest and weary the gentle hearers. How be it that one thing seems chiefly to be observed, that they join him that sits in the throne, and the lamb together, so acknowledging the son to be coequal with the father, and they both to be worshipped with like honour, and with like praises to be celebrated and commended. They attribute peculiarly to the lamb Empire or kingdom, for that he received the book of the father, as is declared before: to wit all power, and authority to govern all things.
[DRAFT]
The Hymn of all creatures, like that which was of the beasts, Elders, and Angels, is here also suitably described, though briefly. But where it contains nothing that has not already been declared, I will not weary the gentle listeners by often repeating and speaking the same things. However, one thing seems chiefly to be observed: they join him who sits on the throne and the Lamb together, thus acknowledging the Son to be coequal with the Father, and that both are to be worshipped with like honor, and with like praises to be celebrated and commended. They attribute uniquely to the Lamb dominion or kingdom, for he received the book of the Father, as has been declared before: namely, all power and authority to govern all things.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "trimmely" changed to "suitably" softens the original's emphasis on careful and precise description.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "thus acknowledging the Son to be coequal with the Father" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. It infers a theological understanding.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing regarding the Lamb’s dominion is more open to interpretation (could be symbolic). The draft’s "uniquely" narrows this significantly.
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
The Hymn of all creatures, like that which was of the beasts, Elders, and Angels, is here also suitably described, though briefly. But where it contains nothing that has not already been declared, I will not weary the gentle listeners by often repeating and speaking the same things. However, one thing seems chiefly to be observed: they join him who sits on the throne and the Lamb together, thus acknowledging the Son to be coequal with the Father, and that both are to be worshipped with like honor, and with like praises to be celebrated and commended. They attribute uniquely to the Lamb dominion or kingdom, for he received the book of the Father, as has been declared before: namely, all power and authority to govern all things.

[ORIGINAL]
The four beasts singe to it Amen,[note 29.10] either so confirmyng the Hymn of the creatures, or thus declaring their consent with them. To the intent we should with one mind pray together and praise God blessed for evermore. With these are moreover confuted the deſſenſions of men. The Lord allows the concorde and agreement of men, and requires utterly, especially in prayers and godly praises. For he co maunds in the Gospel to lay down thine oblation, which thou would offer, in case thou doest remebre any discord betwixt thee and thy brother, to go unto him, and to renew amity, and than to return to thine offering: which in the prophets is called an abomination, in case it be offered of minds possessed with rancour and malice. &c.
[DRAFT]
The four beasts sing to it Amen,[note 29.10], either confirming the hymn of the creatures, or thus declaring their consent with them. To the intent that we should with one mind pray together and praise God blessed forevermore. With these are moreover refuted the dispensations of men. The Lord allows the concord and agreement of men, and requires utterly, especially in prayers and godly praises. For he commands in the Gospel to lay down thine offering, which thou wouldest offer, in case thou dost remember any discord between thee and thy brother, to go unto him, and to renew amity, and then to return to thine offering: which in the prophets is called an abomination, in case it be offered of minds possessed with rancor and malice. &c.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "deſſenſions" changed to "dispensations." This alters the meaning from arguments or disagreements to divine arrangements, a significant shift.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "co maunds" is archaic but understandable. "commands" loses the archaic flavor and subtly alters the tone.
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "dispensations" is a loaded term that wasn't necessarily present in the original's intended meaning.
[FINAL]
The four beasts sing to it Amen,[note 29.10], either confirming the hymn of the creatures, or thus declaring their consent with them. To the intent that we should with one mind pray together and praise God blessed forevermore. With these are moreover refuted the defenses of men. The Lord allows the concord and agreement of men, and requires utterly, especially in prayers and godly praises. For he co maunds in the Gospel to lay down thine offering, which thou wouldest offer, in case thou dost remember any discord between thee and thy brother, to go unto him, and to renew amity, and then to return to thine offering: which in the prophets is called an abomination, in case it be offered of minds possessed with rancor and malice. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 29.11]Finally the elders fall down again and worship him that lives for ever, doubtless that by their oft worshipping all we in earth might be moved unto obedience. For if these things be done in heave of the blessed spirits, what I pray you is mete for us to do here in earth? And mark, that they are said to worship him that lives for ever, who nevertheless fell down first also before the lamb, and before the Throne, out of the which the spirit proceeded, and wherupo sate he that sits: whereof we gather, that the Father, the son, and the holy Ghost, are in deed distinct in persons, yet these three not to be three Gods, but one God living for ever.[note 29.12] And verily this notable vision and treatise may be in the stead of a most effectual remedy against sundry poisons of heresies, especially of the Arrians and Seruetanes, or rather perdetanes, moreover against diverse and curious disputations and temptations touching the works, iudgmentes and providence of God. If we be wise, we will obediently submit our selves to the living God with all the creatures and Saints of God, worshipping him, and with the Prophet crying: thou art just Lord in all thy ways, and holy in all thy works. Thou hast created us, all things are thine. Thou gouerneſt all things in best order. Thou love man. Thou hast given us thy son. Thou by thy son our redemer gouerneſt all things uprightly. We worship thee the father, the son and the holy ghost, one very god. To thee is dew the kingdom, honour, and glory for ever and ever. Amen.
[DRAFT]
Finally, the elders fall down again and worship him who lives forever, without doubt so that all of us on earth might be moved to obedience. For if these things are done in heaven by the blessed spirits, what, I pray you, is fitting for us to do here on earth? And note that they are said to worship him who lives forever, who nevertheless first fell down before the Lamb and before the Throne, from which the Spirit proceeded, and where he who sits is seated; from which we gather that the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit are indeed distinct in persons, yet these three are not three Gods, but one God living forever.

And truly, this notable vision and treatise may serve as an effective remedy against various poisons of heresies, especially those of the Arians and Servetans, or rather, those who perish, moreover against diverse and curious disputations and temptations concerning the works, judgments, and providence of God. If we are wise, we will obediently submit ourselves to the living God with all the creatures and saints of God, worshipping him, and with the prophet crying: “You are a just Lord in all your ways, and holy in all your works. You have created us; all things are yours. You govern all things in the best order. You love humankind. You have given us your Son. You, by your Son our redeemer, govern all things uprightly. We worship you, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, one very God. To you is due the kingdom, honor, and glory forever and ever. Amen.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "doubtless that by their oft worshipping all we in earth might be moved unto obedience" is softened to "without doubt so that all of us on earth might be moved to obedience." The original implies a direct causal effect of the elders' worship on earthly obedience; the draft weakens this.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "those who perish" replaces "perdetanes." While attempting clarification, it adds a judgmental interpretation absent in the original. "Perdetanes" is a specific, albeit obscure, term.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "wherupo sate he that sits" is rendered as "and where he who sits is seated." The original phrasing, while archaic, preserves a certain poetic ambiguity regarding the seated figure. The draft's clarification is unnecessary.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Finally, the elders fall down again and worship him who lives forever, without doubt so that all of us on earth might be moved to obedience. For if these things are done in heaven by the blessed spirits, what, I pray you, is fitting for us to do here on earth? And note that they are said to worship him who lives forever, who nevertheless first fell down before the Lamb and before the Throne, from which the Spirit proceeded, and where he who sits is seated; from which we gather that the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit are indeed distinct in persons, yet these three are not three Gods, but one God living forever.

And truly, this notable vision and treatise may serve as an effective remedy against various poisons of heresies, especially those of the Arians and Servetans, or rather, those who perish, moreover against diverse and curious disputations and temptations concerning the works, judgments, and providence of God. If we are wise, we will obediently submit ourselves to the living God with all the creatures and saints of God, worshipping him, and with the prophet crying: “You are a just Lord in all your ways, and holy in all your works. You have created us; all things are yours. You govern all things in the best order. You love humankind. You have given us your Son. You, by your Son our redeemer, govern all things uprightly. We worship you, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, one very God. To you is due the kingdom, honor, and glory forever and ever. Amen.”

## ¶ Two seals are opened, and the direct course of Gods word is, and a cruel course of wars against the disobedient. The xxx. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd I saw when the lamb opened one of the seals,[note 30.1] and I heard one of the four beasts say, as it were the voice of thonder: come and see. And I saw, and behold a white horse: And he that sate on him had a bow, and a crown was given unto him: And he went forth conquering, and to overcome. And when he opened the second seal, I heard the second beast say: come and see. And there went out an other horse that was red, and power was given to him that sate there on, to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one an other. And there was given unto him a great sword.
[DRAFT]
And I saw when the Lamb opened one of the seals, and I heard one of the four beasts say, as it were the voice of thunder: Come and see. And I saw, and behold a white horse: And he that sat on him had a bow, and a crown was given unto him: And he went forth conquering, and to overcome. And when he opened the second seal, I heard the second beast say: Come and see. And there went out another horse that was red, and power was given to him that sat there on, to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one another. And there was given unto him a great sword.

[FINAL]
And I saw when the Lamb opened one of the seals, and I heard one of the four beasts say, as it were the voice of thunder: Come and see. And I saw, and behold a white horse: And he that sat on him had a bow, and a crown was given unto him: And he went forth conquering, and to overcome. And when he opened the second seal, I heard the second beast say: Come and see. And there went out another horse that was red, and power was given to him that sat there on, to take peace from the earth, and that they should kill one another. And there was given unto him a great sword.
