[ORIGINAL]
Hitherto hath the Apostle, prepared the auditors to hear with a quiet mind, the judgements of God and fatal destinies of the church, and patiently to bear all adversity, and that we should worship him in all things, and give glory to his name: consequently he expounds in a most goodly order the judgements of God, and destinies of the Church, showing how the son of God governs the ordenauces of God and his eternal providence. And this is as it were a Prognostication for all times and Ages unto the worlds end.[note 30.2] For we shall not think that here are rehearsed only the acts of one age or two, but of all. And first all things are generally described by parts, afterward particularly, when we come to the opening of the ſeueth seal. The some is, the lord sends forth the preaching of the truth into the world, which when men refuse and despise, they are destroyed with wars, and other calamities innumerable.
[DRAFT]
Hitherto, the Apostle has prepared the audience to hear with a quiet mind the judgments of God and the fatal destinies of the church, and to patiently bear all adversity, and that we should worship him in all things and give glory to his name. Consequently, he explains in a very orderly fashion the judgments of God and the destinies of the church, showing how the Son of God governs God’s ordinances and his eternal providence. And this is as it were a prophecy for all times and ages until the world’s end. [note 30.2] For we shall not think that here are rehearsed only the acts of one age or two, but of all. And first, all things are generally described by parts; afterward particularly, when we come to the opening of the seventh seal. The sum is, the Lord sends forth the preaching of the truth into the world, which when men refuse and despise, they are destroyed with wars and other innumerable calamities.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "goodly order" changed to "very orderly fashion" slightly alters the tone; "goodly" implies a pleasing or admirable order, while "very orderly fashion" is more neutral.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "ſeueth seal" is modernized to "seventh seal." While technically correct, the original phrasing carries a slightly archaic and heightened sense of importance, which is lessened by the more commonplace modern rendering.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
Hitherto, the Apostle has prepared the audience to hear with a quiet mind the judgments of God and the fatal destinies of the church, and to patiently bear all adversity, and that we should worship him in all things and give glory to his name. Consequently, he explains in a very orderly fashion the judgments of God and the destinies of the church, showing how the Son of God governs God’s ordinances and his eternal providence. And this is as it were a prophecy for all times and ages until the world’s end. [note 30.2] For we shall not think that here are rehearsed only the acts of one age or two, but of all. And first, all things are generally described by parts; afterward particularly, when we come to the opening of the seventh seal. The sum is, the Lord sends forth the preaching of the truth into the world, which when men refuse and despise, they are destroyed with wars and other innumerable calamities.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 30.3]But before all things S. John is excited (and in him all we) to be attentife. And one, that is to wit the first of the beasts doth excite him. One of the Sabbat is set for the first day in the weke, that same is verily the sunday. The voice of the beast is like unto thonder. Whereby is signified that here is treated of great and most weighty matters. For most great and terrible things follow, which shake the whole world. Therefore let us not play the ſlepy sluggards, let us not be blind and deaf. Doubtless the ſloughtfulnes of our time is such, that we little consider the works of God and what is done in our time. The storks, swallows and Turtels, and the rest of living things pass us, which full well observe their time. Therefore are we here well stirred up, that we should not be slothful, but should mark what things are declared and showed us of the Lord.
[DRAFT]
But above all, Saint John is stirred and eager (and in him, all of us should be) to be attentive. And one, that is to say, the first of the beasts, excites him. One of the Sabbath is set for the first day of the week, which is truly Sunday. The voice of the beast is like unto thunder, signifying that great and weighty matters are being treated. For most great and terrible things follow, which shake the whole world. Therefore, let us not play the sleepy sluggards, let us not be blind and deaf. Doubtless, the slothfulness of our time is such that we little consider the works of God and what is done in our time. The storks, swallows, and turtles, and the rest of living things pass us by, which carefully observe their time. Therefore, let us be stirred up here, that we should not be slothful, but should mark what things are declared and shown to us by the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "excited (and in him all we)" changed to "stirred and eager (and in him, all of us should be)" subtly shifts the tone from a sense of divine prompting to a more generalized exhortation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "signifying that great and weighty matters are being treated" is an interpretation not present in the original. The original simply states the voice is "whereby is signified," leaving the signification open.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "One, that is to wit the first of the beasts doth excite him" is somewhat enigmatic. The draft's "that is to say, the first of the beasts, excites him" clarifies it too directly, potentially losing the original's intended mystification.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
But above all, Saint John is excited (and in him all we) to be attentive. And one, that is to wit the first of the beasts doth excite him. One of the Sabbath is set for the first day in the week, that same is verily the sunday. The voice of the beast is like unto thunder, whereby is signified that here is treated of great and most weighty matters. For most great and terrible things follow, which shake the whole world. Therefore let us not play the sleepy sluggards, let us not be blind and deaf. Doubtless the slothfulnes of our time is such, that we little consider the works of God and what is done in our time. The storks, swallows and Turtels, and the rest of living things pass us, which full well observe their time. Therefore are we here well stirred up, that we should not be slothful, but should mark what things are declared and showed us of the Lord.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 30.4]And when S. John had diligently marked what was don, he sees the lamb, Christ I mean our redemer, open one seal, that is to say the first: And straight ways came forth a white horse, on whom he that sate, had a bow bent, and an Arrow in it. To him was given a crown, and he wet forth conqueryng, that he might overcome. This is the vision: the exposition whereof is easy. For the Lord says, that he will declare the destinies of the church.
[DRAFT]

Immediately a white horse came forth, on whom he who sat had a bow drawn, and an arrow in it. To him was given a crown, and he went forth conquering, that he might overcome. This is the vision: the explanation of which is easy. For the Lord says that he will declare the destinies of the church.

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "wet forth conqueryng" changed to "went forth conquering" loses the archaic, slightly more forceful and unusual phrasing of the original.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes.

[ORIGINAL]
Horses[note 30.5] of sundry colours are also brought forth of Zacharie in the.1. Chapter. And they signify the variable course and state of the people of Israel. The white colour is coſecrated to Innocence,[note 30.6] purity, victory, and felicity. Therefore by the white horse is signified the lucky utterance of Gods word, or prosperous preaching of the Gospel. For upon the horse sits a horseman, which guides the horse, and hath a bow. Certainly Christ doth prosper the course of the preaching of the Gospel. And the.45. Psalm. doth attribute to the same ſhaftes or Arrows.[note 30.7] For he strikes his enemies far of, and brings them into his subjection. Briefly with the word of his mouth he subdued to him self people and nations. Isaiah in the.49. bringing in Christ speaking, says: And he put my mouth as a sharpe sword, the shadow of his hand covered me, and he put me as a piked Arrow, he hid me in his queuer. Through Christ therefore proceeds the preaching of the word, he gives strength to the preaching, he shakes his bent bow. What force so ever the word hath, that same is whole dew to the Horseman.
[DRAFT]
Horses of sundry colors are also brought forth by Zechariah in the first chapter. They signify the variable course and state of the people of Israel. The white color is consecrated to innocence, purity, victory, and felicity. Therefore, the white horse signifies the fortunate proclamation of God’s word, or the prosperous preaching of the Gospel. For upon the horse sits a horseman, who guides the horse and has a bow. Certainly, Christ prospers the course of the preaching of the Gospel. And Psalm 45 attributes to the same shafts or arrows. For he strikes his enemies from afar and brings them into his subjection. Briefly, with the word of his mouth, he subdued to himself people and nations. Isaiah, in chapter 49, bringing in Christ speaking, says: “And he put my mouth as a sharp sword, the shadow of his hand covered me, and he put me as a sharpened arrow, he hid me in his quiver.” Through Christ, therefore, proceeds the preaching of the word; he gives strength to the preaching; he draws back his bent bow. Whatever force the word has, that is wholly due to the Horseman.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "coſecrated" changed to "consecrated." While similar, "coſecrated" carries a nuance of being set apart *again* or *in addition to* something else, potentially implying a secondary dedication. "Consecrated" is more general and weakens this subtle distinction.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "fortunate proclamation" replaces "lucky utterance." "Lucky" suggests a providential element, a sense of divine favor. "Fortunate" is more neutral, removing that implication.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "he shakes his bent bow" changed to "he draws back his bent bow." The original suggests action, a release of power. The draft implies a preparatory action, altering the dynamic.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
[FINAL]
Horses of sundry colors are also brought forth by Zechariah in the first chapter. They signify the variable course and state of the people of Israel. The white color is consecrated to innocence, purity, victory, and felicity. Therefore, the white horse signifies the fortunate proclamation of God’s word, or the prosperous preaching of the Gospel. For upon the horse sits a horseman, who guides the horse and has a bow. Certainly, Christ prospers the course of the preaching of the Gospel. And Psalm 45 attributes to the same shafts or arrows. For he strikes his enemies from afar and brings them into his subjection. Briefly, with the word of his mouth, he subdued to himself people and nations. Isaiah, in chapter 49, bringing in Christ speaking, says: “And he put my mouth as a sharp sword, the shadow of his hand covered me, and he put me as a sharpened arrow, he hid me in his quiver.” Through Christ, therefore, proceeds the preaching of the word; he gives strength to the preaching; he draws back his bent bow. Whatever force the word has, that is wholly due to the Horseman.

[ORIGINAL]
To the same is given a Crown,[note 30.8] to wit a kingdom and all power of ruling. For David propheciyng before said, the Lord shall send forth the rod of his power out of Zion, to rule among thine enemies. Moreover there is given him a crown, that he may crown such as serve him faithfully. And it is a phrase of speaking, and he went forth conqueryng, that he might overcome: for that which is, he that went forth is a conqueror, and to this end went forth, that he might overcome. For it signifies that Christ will advance the preaching of his word through out the world, no man being able to resist, and even in despite also of Hell gates. For the word of the Lord endures for ever.
[DRAFT]
To him is given a crown, namely a kingdom and all power of ruling. For David prophesied beforehand, saying, “The Lord will send forth the rod of his power out of Zion, to rule among your enemies.” Moreover, a crown is given to him, that he may crown those who serve him faithfully. And it is a figure of speech, and he went forth conquering, that he might overcome: for he who went forth is a conqueror, and for this purpose went forth, that he might overcome. For it signifies that Christ will advance the preaching of his word throughout the world, no one being able to resist, and even in defiance of the gates of Hell. For the word of the Lord endures forever.

[ORIGINAL]
And this place teaches,[note 30.9] that the Church shall be always in the world, and likewise always the truth preached, though the enemies bowels burst. But if we read over the story of the Church, we shall better understand all things, and shall perceive that this Prognostication hath always ben most certain. Christ was ones through the ministry of the word showed to the world by the Apostles, and the matter proceeded most luckily, howe much some ever the most mighty of this world resisted the same. The thing is wonderful, in case those five hundred years be considered, which immediately after the incarnation of our Lord are accounted. In them went forth the conqueror that he might overcome: And overcame in deed, the whole world receyuyng Christ, and worſhippyng him. Sins those years, as before also, certain ſedes of errors began to be sown abroad. The Bishops began to contend for the supremacy, and who should be the universal head of the Church in earth: They began to reason of the use of Images in the Church, and brought them into Churches in deed, as also they called the Biſſhoppe of Rome the supreme and general head of the Church in earth. And mighty Princes, and in a manner the whole state of learned men conspired in these opinions: but he hath vanquished, which went forth, that he might vanquish. He had in his church innumerable, which bowed not their knees before this Baal. A thousand years after the incarnation of Christ, the Biſhoppes began to profanely to pollute the lords supper: and other undefiled doctrines of faith: but what I pray you did they prevail by so many counsels, determinations, and endeavours most ernest? he that went forth to overcome, hath overcome. That white Horse hath stoutly invaded to the salvation of many. For how great battles in these last five hodreth years the godly and learned me have sustained against the Popes and Bishops, stories bear witness. At this day also appears through out the whole world, howe luckily yet that white horse goes forward, which hath pierced even until our time. The Gospel is believed, neither can that faith be extinguished with any waters or fires.
[DRAFT]
And this place teaches that the Church shall be always in the world, and likewise always the truth preached, though the enemies’ hearts burst. But if we read over the story of the Church, we shall better understand all things, and shall perceive that this prophecy has always been most certain. Christ was once through the ministry of the word showed to the world by the Apostles, and the matter proceeded most successfully, however much some of the most powerful in this world resisted it. The thing is wonderful, if those five hundred years are considered, which immediately after the incarnation of our Lord are accounted. In them went forth the conqueror that he might overcome: and overcame in deed, the whole world receiving Christ and worshipping him. Since those years, as before also, certain seeds of errors began to be sown abroad. The bishops began to contend for supremacy, and who should be the universal head of the Church on earth: they began to reason of the use of images in the Church, and brought them into churches in deed, as also they called the bishop of Rome the supreme and general head of the Church on earth. And mighty princes, and in a manner the whole state of learned men conspired in these opinions: but he has vanquished, which went forth that he might vanquish. He had in his church innumerable, who bowed not their knees before this Baal. A thousand years after the incarnation of Christ, the bishops began to profanely pollute the Lord’s Supper and other undefiled doctrines of faith: but what, I pray you, did they prevail by so many councils, determinations, and earnest endeavors? He that went forth to overcome has overcome. That white horse has stoutly advanced to the salvation of many. For how great battles the godly and learned have sustained against the Popes and bishops in these last five hundred years, stories bear witness. At this day also appears throughout the whole world, how successfully yet that white horse goes forward, which has pierced even until our time. The Gospel is believed, neither can that faith be extinguished with any waters or fires.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "bowels burst" changed to "hearts burst." While conveying a similar sentiment, "bowels burst" carries a more visceral, potentially hyperbolic, and scripturally resonant meaning absent in "hearts burst."
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "worſhippyng him" changed to "worshipping him." The original spelling, while archaic, retains a degree of ambiguity regarding the intensity or nature of the worship. The modernized spelling removes this nuance.
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "lords supper" changed to "Lord’s Supper." While capitalization is a modernization choice, it subtly shifts the perceived importance and formality of the sacrament.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Biſſhoppe" changed to "bishop." While a standard modernization, it loses the original's specific spelling which might have had regional or idiosyncratic significance.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
[FINAL]
And this place teaches[note 30.9] that the Church shall be always in the world, and likewise always the truth preached, though the enemies’ bowels burst. But if we read over the story of the Church, we shall better understand all things, and shall perceive that this prophecy has always been most certain. Christ was once through the ministry of the word showed to the world by the Apostles, and the matter proceeded most successfully, however much some of the most powerful in this world resisted it. The thing is wonderful, if those five hundred years are considered, which immediately after the incarnation of our Lord are accounted. In them went forth the conqueror that he might overcome: and overcame in deed, the whole world receiving Christ and worshipping him. Since those years, as before also, certain seeds of errors began to be sown abroad. The bishops began to contend for supremacy, and who should be the universal head of the Church on earth: they began to reason of the use of images in the Church, and brought them into churches in deed, as also they called the Biſſhoppe of Rome the supreme and general head of the Church on earth. And mighty princes, and in a manner the whole state of learned men conspired in these opinions: but he has vanquished, which went forth that he might vanquish. He had in his church innumerable, who bowed not their knees before this Baal. [ORIGINAL]
Thou make exception, that they were heretics which resisted the bishop and See of Rome in these.500. last years, as Bertram, John Scot ſurnamed Dunſe, Berengarius, Arnoldus Brixianus, Waldo Wicleffe, and Huſſe, Luther, and Zwinglius, and such other men of the same sort, moreover certain of these were overcome also, and put to death by the Pope. I answer, that as men they might err in many things: but in those things wherein with the Scripture they consent against the See of Rome, I affirm that they erred not, but said the truth. Whereupon it is certain that Christ overcame by them. What time Micheas, Helias, Zacharias, Amos, Ieremias and others preached by the word of God against Idols and worshippers of Idols, they were also condemned for ſeditioufe and heretics: yea and certain of them were taken out of the way: but was the verity vanquished? Antichrist is said that he should have good fortune, and that he should punish and afflict the strong, and the people of God: but men being ministers may be oppressed, the ministry never decays. S. Paull says that he is bounden for the Gospels sake, but the Gospel not to be bouden. Therefore hath he overcome hitherto, and shall overcome still, which went forth that he might conquer. They stumble upon this conqueror as at the stone of offence, who so ever, and what so ever they be, which seek to interrupt the plain course of the Gospel.
[DRAFT]
You make exception that they were heretics who resisted the bishop and See of Rome in these last 500 years, as Bertram, John Scot, surnamed Duns, Berengarius, Arnoldus Brixianus, Waldo, Wycliffe, and Hus, Luther, and Zwinglius, and such other men of the same sort; moreover, certain of these were also overcome and put to death by the Pope. I answer that, as men, they might err in many things; but in those things wherein with the Scripture they consent against the See of Rome, I affirm that they erred not, but said the truth. Whereupon it is certain that Christ overcame by them. What time Micah, Elijah, Zechariah, Amos, Jeremiah, and others preached by the word of God against idols and worshippers of idols, they also were condemned as seditious and heretics; yea, and certain of them were taken out of the way. But was the truth vanquished? Antichrist is said to have good fortune, and to punish and afflict the strong and the people of God; but men being ministers may be oppressed, the ministry never decays. Saint Paul says that he is bound for the Gospel’s sake, but the Gospel is not to be bound. Therefore he has overcome hitherto, and shall overcome still, which went forth that he might conquer. They stumble upon this conqueror as at the stone of offense, whoever and whatever they be, which seek to interrupt the plain course of the Gospel.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "bounden" changed to "bound." While functionally similar, "bounden" carries a sense of obligation and duty that "bound" lacks, subtly altering the theological weight of Paul's situation.
2. **Hallucination:** "Saint Paul" added. The original uses "S. Paull."
3. **Proper Nouns:** "Micheas" to "Micah," "Helias" to "Elijah," "Zacharias" to "Zechariah," "Ieremias" to "Jeremiah." These are all correct.
4. **Theological Terms:** No changes or removals detected.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
Moreover what time the second seal should be opened the second beast,[note 30.10] to wit the Ore or Calfe exhorts again S. John to attentiuenes, and that we should consider what is propouded unto us. And now comes forth the red horse, whose colour is somewhat like fire: there sits also on him a rider, to whom power is given to disturb peace in earth, and that men should kill one an other. For there is given him a great sword. The red horse signifies the state of wars,[note 30.11] full of fire and blood. He that sits on this horse is Mars, or rather the father of lies, I mean the Devil, which was a murderer from the beginning. He gathers to him the dregs of men to make civil commotions, for the wars, destruction, firyng, slaughter, and desolation. You see from whence the breaking of peace is, which God hates. And we hear howe it is given him: Mark given, by the just judgment of God to be permitted, that troublyng all peace, he should take it away, and set men together by the ears, that one may wound and kill an other. For so we read in the.1. of Iob, howe Satan had power given him of God against Iob. Unto bloody soldiers is given a great sword, great power to hurt, a wonderful force of fighting: As also Nahum expounds it. Neither is it a rare thing in the scriptures for Monarchs, tirannes, and mighty men of war to be called, a sword. For so Ezekiel called Nabuchodonoſor: and Isaiah called Sinacherib king of Aſſyrians a whetstone.
[DRAFT]
Moreover, concerning the time when the second seal should be opened, the second beast,[note 30.10] namely, the Ore or Calf, exhorts again Saint John to attentiveness, and that we should consider what is proposed to us. And now comes forth the red horse, whose color is somewhat like fire; there sits also on him a rider, to whom power is given to disturb peace on earth, and that men should kill one another. For there is given him a great sword. The red horse signifies the state of wars, full of fire and blood. He who sits on this horse is Mars, or rather the father of lies, that is, the Devil, who was a murderer from the beginning. He gathers to him the dregs of men to make civil commotions, for the wars, destruction, burning, slaughter, and desolation. You see from whence the breaking of peace is, which God hates. And we hear how it is given him: Mark given, by the just judgment of God to be permitted, that troubling all peace, he should take it away, and set men together by the ears, that one may wound and kill another. For so we read in the first chapter of Job, how Satan had power given him of God against Job. To bloody soldiers is given a great sword, great power to hurt, a wonderful force of fighting, as also Nahum explains it. Neither is it a rare thing in the scriptures for monarchs, tyrants, and mighty men of war to be called a sword. For so Ezekiel called Nebuchadnezzar; and Isaiah called Sennacherib, king of Assyria, a whetstone.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Ore or Calfe" is rendered as "the Ore or Calf" - the original phrasing suggests a question or uncertainty about the identity, which is lost in the definite article.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "that is, the Devil" is an unnecessary addition after "the father of lies." The original text's association is clear without this explanatory phrase.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "Mark given" remains unchanged, but the original's ambiguity regarding whether "Mark" is a person or a reference to the Gospel of Mark is preserved. The draft does nothing to clarify, which is appropriate, but the lack of change should be noted.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Nabuchodonoſor → Nebuchadnezzar is a correct modernization. Sinacherib → Sennacherib is a correct modernization. Hieronymus is missing from the original, so no correction needed. Austen, Ireney, etc. are also absent.
[FINAL]

For there is given him a great sword. The red horse signifies the state of wars,[note 30.11] full of fire and blood. He that sits on this horse is Mars, or rather the father of lies, I mean the Devil, which was a murderer from the beginning. [ORIGINAL]
And the chiefest rightuouſenes is,[note 30.12] to give every man his own. Therefore this place doth iuſtely ascribe that which is good unto God, and that which is evil to the Devil. But, thou say, if God permit, the same that he doth not prohibit he doth. He prohibiteth not war, for because justice will not suffer him so to doe: but he comaudeth him by war to punish the wicked, and to try the good: but in permittyng wars God offends nothing, saying that for most just causes he permits the same. For they would not embrace peace offered them by the preachers of the Gospel, therefore were they worthy to be entangled with wars. The Jews knew not the day of Christ's visitation, therefore were they worthily visited of the Romans and destroyed: And this thing is in the world perpetual, that they that will not obeye the Gospel, must obeye the Captain of the wars: they that will not hear christ, must hear Antichrist. Thou may not contend with God, which he doth this, and permits that. Worship God rather, as thou hast ben taught in the.4. and.5. Chapter.
[DRAFT]
And the greatest righteousness is to give every man his due. Therefore, this passage justly ascribes that which is good to God, and that which is evil to the Devil. But you say, if God permits, the same that he does not prohibit he does. He does not prohibit war, for because justice will not suffer him so to do; but he commands him by war to punish the wicked and to try the good. But in permitting wars, God offends nothing, saying that for most just causes he permits them. For they would not embrace peace offered them by the preachers of the Gospel, therefore were they worthy to be entangled with wars. The Jews did not know the day of Christ’s visitation, therefore they were justly visited by the Romans and destroyed. And this thing is perpetual in the world, that those who will not obey the Gospel must obey the Captain of wars; they that will not hear Christ must hear Antichrist. You may not contend with God, which he does this, and permits that. Worship God rather, as you have been taught in the fourth and fifth chapters.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "rightuouſenes" to "greatest righteousness" subtly shifts the emphasis. "Chiefest" implies primacy, while "greatest" suggests a superlative among many righteous acts.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "But you say" is an interpretive insertion. The original presents a statement without framing it as a direct response to an objection.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "obeye the Captain of the wars" loses the slightly more nuanced and potentially metaphorical sense of the original "obeye the Captain of the wars." It becomes overly literal.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: "But he commands him by war" - The original reads "but he commandeth *him* by war." The addition of "him" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And the greatest righteousness is to give every man his due. Therefore, this passage justly ascribes that which is good to God, and that which is evil to the Devil. But you say, if God permits, the same that he does not prohibit he does. He does not prohibit war, for because justice will not suffer him so to do; but he commands him by war to punish the wicked and to try the good. But in permitting wars, God offends nothing, saying that for most just causes he permits them. For they would not embrace peace offered them by the preachers of the Gospel, therefore were they worthy to be entangled with wars. The Jews did not know the day of Christ’s visitation, therefore they were justly visited by the Romans and destroyed. And this thing is perpetual in the world, that those who will not obey the Gospel must obey the Captain of wars; they that will not hear Christ must hear Antichrist. You may not contend with God, which he does this, and permits that. Worship God rather, as you have been taught in the fourth and fifth chapters.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Let us peruse over stories, and see and such wars be not found, wherein men have slain themselves with mutual wounds, and have killed one an other like beasts. If you will read Herodiane, Oroſius, and other good Hiſtoriographers, you may find that the Roman Emperors have ben troubled with most grievous wars, for none other cause, than that they refused peace offered to them by the gospel. For none other cause was Rome it self at the last taken by the Weſtgothians, of the Eaſtgothians burnt and destroyed. The lord had give them Christen Princes: but they loved more Idols. For Simmachus governor of the city was so bold to require a restitution of Idolatry. I speak nothing now of Athila, nothing of the Persian and Affricane wars. And what time there was a wonderful strife emogs the Bishops about the supremacy, the Saracenes sprang up and became mighty. After the Thousand year began the holy war, which as it was most bloody, so was it of longest continuance. Never any such war was made in all the world. Boniface the.8. instituted first the year of Iubiley, a most wicked man, who also did exhibit him self to be scene of the people both Pope and Emperor. But the same year of a thousand and three hundred, wherein he did these things, arroſe up in Asia the whip or scourge of God Ottoman, the original of themperours of Turks which reign at this day. For so when Solomon builded places of Idolatry, his enemies sprang up, which wonderfully vexed and afflicted the kingdom of Solomon. What wars are made now a days, and what be the causes of wars, all wise men do see. We will not receive the peaceable gospel: It is reason therefore, that the Turkiſſh Armies should invade us, that we may both feel Antichrist to be a stout warrior, and may all abhor and deteſte him.
[DRAFT]
Let us peruse stories and see if such wars can be found, in which men have slain themselves with mutual wounds, and have killed one another like beasts. If you will read Herodotus, Orosius, and other good historians, you may find that the Roman Emperors were troubled with most grievous wars, for no other cause than that they refused peace offered to them by the gospel. For no other cause was Rome itself at the last taken by the Visigoths, and burnt and destroyed by the Ostrogoths. The Lord had given them Christian princes, but they loved idols more. For Simmachus, governor of the city, was so bold as to require a restoration of idolatry. I speak nothing now of Attila, nothing of the Persian and African wars. And when there was a wonderful strife among the bishops about supremacy, the Saracens sprang up and became mighty. After the thousand years began the holy war, which was as bloody as it was long. Never was such a war made in all the world. Boniface VIII instituted first the year of Jubilee, a most wicked man, who also exhibited himself to be seen by the people, both as Pope and Emperor. But in the same year of one thousand three hundred, in Asia arose Ottoman, the scourge of God, the origin of the emperors of Turks who reign at this day. For so when Solomon built places of idolatry, his enemies sprang up, which wonderfully vexed and afflicted the kingdom of Solomon. What wars are made nowadays, and what are the causes of wars, all wise men see. We will not receive the peaceable gospel; it is reason therefore that Turkish armies should invade us, that we may both feel Antichrist to be a stout warrior, and may all abhor and detest him.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "Let us peruse over stories" changed to "Let us peruse stories." While seemingly minor, "over" implies a careful and thorough examination, a nuance lost in the draft.
2. **Added Interpretation:** "The lord had give them Christen Princes" changed to "The Lord had given them Christian princes." The original phrasing carries a slightly more active, providential tone, suggesting divine intervention. The draft's wording is more passive.
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** "Weſtgothians" changed to "Visigoths" and "Eaſtgothians" changed to "Ostrogoths." While the modern spellings are correct, the original’s slightly archaic spelling may have been intentional, reflecting a specific historical context or source. The draft removes this potential ambiguity.
4. **Hallucination:** No content is added.
5. **Proper Nouns:** "Oroſius" changed to "Orosius." Correct. "Athila" changed to "Attila." Correct. "Simmachus" changed to "Simmachus." Correct.
6. **Theological Terms:** "Antichrist" remains unchanged. "Apocalypse," "dispensation," "sacrament" are not present in the original and therefore not altered.
[FINAL]
Let us peruse stories and see if such wars can be found, in which men have slain themselves with mutual wounds, and have killed one another like beasts. For no other cause was Rome itself at the last taken by the Visigoths, of the Ostrogothians burnt and destroyed. The lord had give them Christen Princes, but they loved Idols more. For Simmachus, governor of the city, was so bold to require a restitution of Idolatry. I speak nothing now of Attila, nothing of the Persian and Affricane wars. And what time there was a wonderful strife emogs the Bishops about the supremacy, the Saracenes sprang up and became mighty. After the Thousand year began the holy war, which as it was most bloody, so was it of longest continuance. Never any such war was made in all the world. 2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "are is laid" is intentionally obscure and potentially metaphorical. "The groundwork is laid" clarifies it, removing the original's enigmatic quality.

## ¶ Here is opened the iii. and iiii. Seal, and is declared what the world shall suffer of honger and Pestilence. The xxxi. Sermon.

[ORIGINAL]
ANd when he opened the third Seal, I heard the third beast say: come and see. And I beheld and lo, a black horse: and he that sate on him, had a pair of Balaunces in his hand. And I heard a voice in the midst of the four beasts say: A measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny: and oil and wine see thou hurt not.
[DRAFT]
And when he opened the third seal, I heard the third voice say: “Come and see.” And I looked, and there was a black horse; and he who sat on him had a pair of scales in his hand. And I heard a voice in the midst of the four beasts saying, “A measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny; and do not damage the oil and the wine.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Balaunces" (balances) is replaced with "scales." While related, "balaunces" carries a more specific, potentially symbolic weight in the original context (often interpreted as representing a system of weights and measures, or even a form of judgment). "Scales" is more generic.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of quotation marks around "Come and see" imposes a sense of direct speech that isn't explicitly signaled in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing regarding oil and wine ("see thou hurt not") is deliberately vague and open to interpretation. The draft's "do not damage" is more prescriptive and loses some of that ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this excerpt, so no issues here.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
And when he opened the third seal, I heard the third voice say: “Come and see.” And I looked, and there was a black horse; and he who sat on him had a pair of scales in his hand. And I heard a voice in the midst of the four beasts saying, “A measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny; and do not damage the oil and the wine.”

And when he opened the fourth Seal, I heard the voice of the fourth beast say: come and see. And when he opened the fourth Seal, I heard the voice of the fourth living creature say, “Come and see.” And I looked, and behold a pale horse, and the name of the one who sat on him was Death, and Hades followed after him, and authority was given to them over the fourth part of the earth, to kill with sword, and with famine, and with death of the beasts of the earth.

[ORIGINAL]
Christ exalted aboue all things,[note 31.1] and Lord of all in heaven and in earth, opens the seals of the book divine, that is to wit disposes and governs with great rightuouſnes the ordenaunces and judgements of God: and first in deed he gives a proſperouſe course to the preaching of the gospel, sending always faythefull ministers preaching the Gospel of the kingdom of God, peace, and concorde. But for as much as evil men do contemn the Evangelical peace, they are certainly worthy to be molested with cruel wars. Therefore the lamb opens the second seal, and there rush out cruel wars, slaughters, ſeditions and robberies.
[DRAFT]
Christ, exalted above all things, and Lord of all in heaven and on earth, opens the seals of the divine book, that is to say, he disposes and governs with great righteousness the ordinances and judgments of God. First, indeed, he gives a prosperous course to the preaching of the gospel, sending always faithful ministers preaching the Gospel of the kingdom of God, peace, and concord. But because evil men despise the Evangelical peace, they are certainly worthy to be afflicted with cruel wars. Therefore, the Lamb opens the second seal, and there rush out cruel wars, slaughters, seditions, and robberies.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "molested" changed to "afflicted." While related, "molested" carries a sense of unwarranted interference or harassment, which might be subtly different from the more general "afflicted."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None present.

Christ, exalted above all things,[note 31.1] and Lord of all in heaven and on earth, opens the seals of the divine book, that is to wit he disposes and governs with great righteousness the ordinances and judgments of God: and first in deed he gives a prosperous course to the preaching of the gospel, sending always faithful ministers preaching the Gospel of the kingdom of God, peace, and concord. But for as much as evil men do contemn the Evangelical peace, they are certainly worthy to be molested with cruel wars. Therefore, the Lamb opens the second seal, and there rush out cruel wars, slaughters, seditions, and robberies.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 31.2]But before the third Seal is opened, the third beast resembling the countenance of man, exhorts us to take most diligent heed: that when we see these things come to pass which are here spoken of before, we should consider from whence they come, and for what causes they are sent, and that they may be turned away by dew repentance. Certain refer these things absolutely to chance and fortune, some again to the natural causes, of God, and the divine operation no respect had at all: Where we know that God uses natural causes after his good will and pleasure. Let us watch therefore, look and consider, and know that the righteous God works all things for the salvation of the chosen, and the overthrow of his enemies. That black horse with his rider, showing a balance in his hand, signifies the unfortunate or sorrowful time of ſcarſetie, famine and penury of all things.[note 31.3] For it is a worthy and a codigne punishment, that they that do nothing esteem the bread of life, nor have no consideration of the food of souls, but both reject it themselves, and by their tyrannical proclamations bring to pass that it is not received of others, finally which for the bread of life do spoil the godly of their goods, and most wickedly waste the same in all kind of riot, should be driven to by things necessary at excessive prices: yea and can not find things necessary, but should pine for honger. We know that the black colour is used in mourning and heuineſſe:[note 31.4] and that when the flesh and blood are consumed for want of meat, the skin grows black and evil favoured: and therefore this horse is black.
[DRAFT]
But before the third Seal is opened, the third beast, resembling the countenance of a man, exhorts us to take most diligent heed: that when we see these things come to pass which are here spoken of before, we should consider from whence they come, and for what causes they are sent, and that they may be turned away by true repentance. Some refer these things absolutely to chance and fortune, others to natural causes, of God and the divine operation having no respect at all: whereas we know that God uses natural causes after his good will and pleasure. Let us watch therefore, look and consider, and know that the righteous God works all things for the salvation of the chosen, and the overthrow of his enemies. That black horse with its rider, showing a balance in its hand, signifies the unfortunate or sorrowful time of scarcity, famine, and penury of all things.

For it is a worthy and fitting punishment that those who do not esteem the bread of life, nor have any consideration of the food of souls, but both reject it themselves, and by their tyrannical proclamations bring to pass that it is not received of others, and who for the bread of life do spoil the godly of their goods, and most wickedly waste the same in all kinds of riot, should be driven to by things necessary at excessive prices: yea, and cannot find things necessary, but should pine for hunger. We know that the black color is used in mourning and heaviness; and that when the flesh and blood are consumed for want of meat, the skin grows black and ill-favored: and therefore this horse is black.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "dew repentance" changed to "true repentance." "Dew" in this context carries a nuance of gentle, restorative repentance, which "true" lacks.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "of God and the divine operation having no respect at all" is a slight expansion of "of God, and the divine operation no respect had at all." It adds a subtle judgmental tone not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "driven to by things necessary" changed to "driven to by things necessary." The original phrasing is slightly ambiguous, potentially implying a forceful movement *towards* necessities, which the draft clarifies, losing a potential layer of meaning.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
But before the third Seal is opened, the third beast, resembling the countenance of a man, exhorts us to take most diligent heed: that when we see these things come to pass which are here spoken of before, we should consider from whence they come, and for what causes they are sent, and that they may be turned away by true repentance. Some refer these things absolutely to chance and fortune, others to natural causes, of God and the divine operation having no respect at all: whereas we know that God uses natural causes after his good will and pleasure. Let us watch therefore, look and consider, and know that the righteous God works all things for the salvation of the chosen, and the overthrow of his enemies. That black horse with its rider, showing a balance in its hand, signifies the unfortunate or sorrowful time of scarcity, famine, and penury of all things.

For it is a worthy and fitting punishment that those who do not esteem the bread of life, nor have any consideration of the food of souls, but both reject it themselves, and by their tyrannical proclamations bring to pass that it is not received of others, and who for the bread of life do spoil the godly of their goods, and most wickedly waste the same in all kinds of riot, should be driven to by things necessary at excessive prices: yea, and cannot find things necessary, but should pine for hunger. We know that the black color is used in mourning and heaviness; and that when the flesh and blood are consumed for want of meat, the skin grows black and ill-favored: and therefore this horse is black.

[ORIGINAL]
The rider of this horse holds in his hand a balance? [illegible],[note 31.5] with two scales hanging at either end of the beam, which we cal a pair of weightes. Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For thou hast sat, says David upon thy Throne which judge rightuouſnes: therefore is a balance the judgment of the just judgment of God. Arethas hath not alleged these things amiss, how be it we ought rather to prefer the exposition of S. John him self. For a voice is heard from the midst of the beasts, which expounds to us the balance. For it sounds, a measure[note 31.6] of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny. And this measured called Choinix, signifies a diet or daily meat: as Eraſmus hath in his proverb, sit not upon thy measure. The same in his annotations upon this place: Choinix, says he, is a measure of wheat, or other breadcorne, which is sufficient for one days meat. Budaeus thinks that it weighs iiii. pound, Pollux. iii. The word therefore signifies, that a very little meat shall cost a great price, and yet not to be gotten for money. Which chau ceth in the time of famine. What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price. Therefore two things are signified, ſcarſitie or derth of corn, and famine. Dearth reiſeth the price beyond reason. Famine hath nothing to by, though he hath never so much money lying by him: but hongreth, waunteth, pines, and at the last miserably, consumes to naught: wherein verily dearth and famine do differ. The Germanes discern them by several words calling dearth ſcarſetie, and famine, honger. Yet are they for the most part indiuiſible.
[DRAFT]
The rider of this horse holds in his hand a balance, with two scales hanging at either end of the beam, which we call a pair of weights. Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For you have sat upon your throne which judges righteousness, therefore a balance is the judgment of the just judgment of God. Arethas has not alleged these things amiss, but we ought rather to prefer the exposition of Saint John himself. For a voice is heard from the midst of the beasts, which expounds to us the balance. For it sounds, a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny. And this measure, called Choinix, signifies a diet or daily meat, as Erasmus has in his proverb, “Sit not upon your measure.” The same in his annotations upon this place: Choinix, he says, is a measure of wheat, or other breadcorn, which is sufficient for one day’s meat. Budaeus thinks that it weighs four pounds, Pollux three. The word therefore signifies that a very little meat shall cost a great price, and yet not be gotten for money, which happens in the time of famine. What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price. Therefore two things are signified, scarcity of corn, and famine. Scarcity raises the price beyond reason. Famine has nothing to buy, though he has never so much money lying by him, but hungers, wants, pines, and at the last miserably consumes to naught, wherein verily scarcity and famine do differ. The Germans discern them by several words, calling scarcity “scarsetie,” and famine, “honger.” Yet they are for the most part indivisible.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For thou hast sat, says David upon thy Throne which judge rightuouſnes" is changed to "Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For you have sat upon your throne which judges righteousness." The original phrasing, "thou hast sat, says David," is more evocative of a direct quotation and maintains the original's slightly archaic tone. The draft's phrasing is less specific and loses the direct attribution to David.
2. **Added Interpretation:** The phrase "we ought rather to prefer the exposition of S. John him self" is changed to "but we ought rather to prefer the exposition of Saint John himself." While seemingly minor, the addition of "but" introduces a subtle shift in argumentative flow that wasn't present in the original.
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original's "[illegible],[note 31.5]" is simply removed. This eliminates a potential source of information for a scholar, and the removal itself is an interpretive act.
4. **Hallucination:** The original includes "What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price." The draft retains this, but the original’s phrasing is slightly different.
5. **Proper Nouns:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is not present in the provided text, but is a common error.
[FINAL]
The rider of this horse holds in his hand a balance,[note 31.5] with two scales hanging at either end of the beam, which we call a pair of weights. Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For thou hast sat, says David upon thy Throne which judge rightuouſnes: therefore is a balance the judgment of the just judgment of God. Arethas hath not alleged these things amiss, how be it we ought rather to prefer the exposition of S. John him self. And this measured called Choinix, signifies a diet or daily meat: as Eraſmus hath in his proverb, sit not upon thy measure. [ORIGINAL]
And we read in the old story of the Bible,[note 31.7] that for the contempt of the preaching of Gods law, and the bringing in of a strange kind of worshipping God, the Iſraelites in the times of Helias and Heliſaeus were most greuouſely punished with honger and penury. These things be plentifully declared in the.3. book of Kings the.17. and.18. Chapter. Also in the.4. of Kings, the.6. and.7. Chapter. Moreover in the time of the Emperor Claudius, whilst the Apostles preached the Gospel faithfully, and the Jews and Gentiles stoutly repulsing it, famine most greuouſely afflicted the Roman Empire: which thing S. Luke rehearses in the Acts of the Apostles.11. Chapter. Which things were done in deed before this reuelatio was to S. John exhibited. Sins that time the Hiſtoriographers recite sundry and innumerable famines, dearthes and penuries, in diverse countries, sent of God for contempt of the truth. Nauclerus mentions of a famine in the year of our lord. D xxxix. wherein mothers also devoured their own children. What hath chanced in our memory in those wars of Millan and else where, it is no need to rehearse. They be yet freſſhe in memory, and written in the stories of Galeacius Capella. We felt some part hereof also in the year of our Lord. M D.XXIX. and the years following. The just Lord puniſſheth, and more will puniſſhe the great ingratitude, and contempt of his Godly word: as he did in the destruction of Jerusalem, would God it would please the world most blind, through repentance to convert unto God when he punishes and with free and willing minds embrace the word of verity: for so should there be more felicity and less misery.
[DRAFT]
And we read in the old story of the Bible,[note 31.7] that for the contempt of the preaching of God’s law, and the bringing in of a strange kind of worshiping God, the Israelites in the times of Elijah and Elisha were most grievously punished with hunger and poverty. These things are plentifully declared in the 3rd book of Kings, chapters 17 and 18. Also in the 4th of Kings, chapters 6 and 7. Moreover, in the time of Emperor Claudius, while the Apostles preached the Gospel faithfully, and the Jews and Gentiles stoutly repulsed it, famine most grievously afflicted the Roman Empire: which thing Luke rehearses in the Acts of the Apostles, chapter 11. Which things were done indeed before this revelation was exhibited to John. Since that time, historians recount sundry and innumerable famines, dearths, and penuries in diverse countries, sent of God for contempt of the truth. Nauclerus mentions a famine in the year of our Lord 1339, wherein mothers also devoured their own children. What has chanced in our memory in those wars of Milan and elsewhere, it is no need to rehearse. They are yet fresh in memory, and written in the histories of Galeazzo Capella. We felt some part hereof also in the year of our Lord 1529 and the years following. The just Lord punishes, and more will punish the great ingratitude and contempt of his godly word, as he did in the destruction of Jerusalem. Would God it would please the world, so blind, through repentance to convert unto God when he punishes, and with free and willing minds embrace the word of truth: for so should there be more felicity and less misery.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "penury" changed to "poverty." While related, "penury" implies a more extreme and desperate state than "poverty." This softens the original's severity.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "Galeacius Capella" changed to "Galeazzo Capella." This is an error; the original name is correct.
3. PROPER NOUNS: "D xxxix" changed to "1339." While the modernization is technically correct, it loses the original's Roman numeral style, which was common and deliberate in the 1561 text. A note explaining the conversion might be preferable.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
[FINAL]
And we read in the old story of the Bible,[note 31.7] that for the contempt of the preaching of God’s law, and the bringing in of a strange kind of worshipping God, the Israelites in the times of Elijah and Elisha were most grievously punished with hunger and penury. These things be plentifully declared in the 3rd book of Kings the.17. and.18. Chapter. Also in the.4. of Kings, the.6. and.7. Chapter. Moreover in the time of the Emperor Claudius, whilst the Apostles preached the Gospel faithfully, and the Jews and Gentiles stoutly repulsing it, famine most greuouſely afflicted the Roman Empire: which thing S. Luke rehearses in the Acts of the Apostles.11. Chapter. Which things were done in deed before this reuelatio was to S. John exhibited. Since that time the Hiſtoriographers recite sundry and innumerable famines, dearthes and penuries, in diverse countries, sent of God for contempt of the truth. Nauclerus mentions of a famine in the year of our lord. D xxxix [note: Roman numeral for 1339], wherein mothers also devoured their own children. What hath chanced in our memory in those wars of Millan and else where, it is no need to rehearse. They be yet freſſhe in memory, and written in the stories of Galeacius Capella. We felt some part hereof also in the year of our Lord. M D.XXIX. and the years following. The just Lord puniſſheth, and more will puniſſhe the great ingratitude, and contempt of his Godly word: as he did in the destruction of Jerusalem, would God it would please the world most blind, through repentance to convert unto God when he punishes and with free and willing minds embrace the word of verity: for so should there be more felicity and less misery.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 31.8]How be it for a comfort at the end of this Seal is added, and Oil and Wine see thou hurt not. He names the kinds most necessary for the use of man, and means, that God doth mercifully reserve some things, that be chiefly necessary for mans use, especially for the electes sake, that all should not perish and pine in general. Whereby we understand that the lord forgets not his mercy, even in the midst of afflictio and plagues that he sends. Thus in times paste minding to punish Aegipte and other nations with famine, he sent before Ioſeph, by whom he might preserve the house of Iacob, and other people innumerable. You see herein most clearly, that it is of God, that sometime the corn is blasted, and the vines and olives perish: and to be of him, that the corn increases, and wine also. So hath he also before protested in the law, Leuitic. 26. And Deuteron. 28.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, for comfort at the end of this seal, it is added, “See that you hurt not the oil and the wine.” He names the things most necessary for human use, and means that God mercifully reserves some things that are chiefly necessary for humanity, especially for the sake of the elect, so that all do not perish and suffer. By this, we understand that the Lord does not forget his mercy, even in the midst of affliction and plagues that he sends. Thus, in times past, intending to punish Egypt and other nations with famine, he sent Joseph beforehand, through whom he might preserve the house of Jacob and other people innumerable. You see clearly herein that it is from God that sometimes the grain is blighted, and the vines and olive trees perish; and that it is from him that the grain increases, and the wine also. So he has also previously protested in the law, Leviticus 26, and Deuteronomy 28.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "pine" is replaced with "suffer." "Pine" carries a connotation of wasting away, a more specific and evocative image of suffering than the general term "suffer."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "intending to punish Egypt" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original simply states "minding to punish."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "of God" is rendered as "from God." While similar, "from God" implies a more direct causation than the original's phrasing, which allows for a broader range of divine agency.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: All proper nouns are correctly modernized.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, for comfort at the end of this seal, it is added, “See that you hurt not the oil and the wine.” He names the things most necessary for human use, and means that God mercifully reserves some things that are chiefly necessary for humanity, especially for the sake of the elect, so that all do not perish and pine. By this, we understand that the Lord does not forget his mercy, even in the midst of affliction and plagues that he sends. Thus, in times past, minding to punish Egypt and other nations with famine, he sent Joseph beforehand, through whom he might preserve the house of Jacob and other people innumerable. You see clearly herein that it is of God that sometimes the grain is blighted, and the vines and olive trees perish; and that it is of him that the grain increases, and the wine also. So he has also previously protested in the law, Leviticus 26, and Deuteronomy 28.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 31.9]We are come now to the fourth Seal, at the opening whereof, and to behold the operation, we are excited of the Egle, the fourth beast. Of whom we have spoken before ones or twice. And the pale Horse[note 31.10] comes forth, in Greek [illegible], which colour resembles withered grass and Herbs. Solomon in the.12. Chapter. of Eccleſ. calls the colour apperyng in dead bodies, and their countenances, Golden liquor. All Poets call death Pale. And the rider in deed is expressly called Death. We understand the course of the plague and of all diseases, and even of death it self: whom Hell follows, that is to say a pit or a grave. For Scheol in Hebrewe signifies a pit or a grave. But if you will needs understand it of the place of them that be damned, doubtless they be carried headlong into Hell, so many as here with sickness consumed, die with out faith and repentance. Therefore hell follows death rightly. But if thou had rather by hell understand, a grave: it signifies that all shall be full of coarſes and sepulchres.
[DRAFT]
We have now come to the fourth Seal, at the opening of which, and to observe its effect, we are stirred up by the Eagle, the fourth beast. Of whom we have spoken before on one or two occasions. And the pale Horse comes forth, in Greek *chloros*, which color resembles withered grass and herbs. Solomon in chapter 12 of Ecclesiastes calls the color appearing in dead bodies and their faces a golden liquid. All poets call death pale. And the rider is indeed expressly called Death. We understand the course of plague and all diseases, and even death itself; and Hell follows, that is to say, a pit or a grave. For *Sheol* in Hebrew signifies a pit or a grave. But if you will necessarily understand it of the place of the damned, undoubtedly they are cast headlong into Hell, so many as here, consumed by sickness, die without faith and repentance. Therefore Hell follows death rightly. But if you would rather understand Hell as a grave, it signifies that it will be full of corpses and sepulchers.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "excited of the Egle" changed to "stirred up by the Eagle." While not drastically different, "excited" carries a sense of awe or prompting that "stirred up" lacks, potentially diminishing the original's tone.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "*chloros*" in brackets, while providing a Greek term, introduces an element of scholarly explanation not present in the original. It's a gloss, not a modernization.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "But if you will needs understand it of the place of them that be damned..." allows for a degree of interpretive wiggle room. The draft's "But if you will necessarily understand it of the place of the damned..." is more forceful and less open to alternative readings.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No errors in proper noun modernization.
[FINAL]

Chapter. of Eccleſ. calls the colour apperyng in dead bodies, and their countenances, Golden liquor. All Poets call death Pale. And the rider in deed is expressly called Death. [ORIGINAL]
And that in deed plagues and peſtileces most mortal have sore afflicted the Roman Empire,[note 31.11] Oroſius is wittenes in his seventh book in the Acts of L. Aurel. Verus and Decius Emperors, the most cruel perſecutours of our faith. Euagrius in the.29 Chapter, of the.4. Book of the Eccleſiaſt. story tells of a maruelouſe plague that lasted about.50. years. And all men know with what a pestilence and ſoden death Italy was wasted in the time of Maurice Emperor. And Gregory bishop of Rome. The time would fail me, in case I would recite out of Histories all the plagues and calamities of all times. What is done at this day, and hath ben done in our memory, you your selves know best. There are sprung up new diseases, whose names to our elders were never known. With these evils and calamities God wastes the world, and ever hath done to the intent that by plagues he might call us again to repentance. Thus verily we shall judge always of calamities. If any judge otherwise, they are not amended, therefore are they punished here, and after this shall burn in perpetual tourmentes.
[DRAFT]
And indeed plagues and pestilences most mortal have severely afflicted the Roman Empire, [note 31.11] as Orosius testifies in his seventh book concerning the Acts of Emperor L. Aurelius Verus and Decius, the most cruel persecutors of our faith. Evagrius in chapter 29 of book 4 of Ecclesiastical History tells of a marvelous plague that lasted about 50 years. And all men know with what pestilence and sudden death Italy was wasted in the time of Emperor Maurice and Bishop Gregory of Rome. The time would fail me if I were to recount from histories all the plagues and calamities of all times. What has been done at this day, and has been done in our memory, you yourselves know best. New diseases have arisen, whose names were never known to our elders. With these evils and calamities God wastes the world, and has always done so, intending by plagues to call us again to repentance. Thus we must always judge of calamities. If anyone judges otherwise, they are not reformed, therefore they are punished here and after this will burn in perpetual torments.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Thus verily we shall judge always of calamities" changed to "Thus we must always judge of calamities." The original implies a judgment based on faith and divine will; the draft shifts to a prescriptive statement.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "intending by plagues to call us again to repentance" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original says God "to the intent that by plagues he might call us again to repentance," which is more descriptive than the draft's phrasing.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "If any judge otherwise, they are not amended, therefore are they punished here, and after this shall burn in perpetual tourmentes" is softened to "If anyone judges otherwise, they are not reformed, therefore they are punished here and after this will burn in perpetual torments." The original’s “amended” carries a stronger theological connotation of spiritual correction, lost in “reformed.”
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

Aurelius Verus and Decius, the most cruel persecutors of our faith. Evagrius in chapter 29 of book 4 of Ecclesiastical History tells of a marvelous plague that lasted about 50 years. [ORIGINAL]
To these moreover is added an other thing also,[note 31.12] and power was given them, &c. For when men will not amend with simple Calamities, the evils or plagues of God are doubled. The same are raccompted in the like order and number with the Prophets, Ieremie the.15. Chapter. and Ezekiel in the.14. Chapter. For they be these, Sword, Famine, Death or Pestilence, and beasts: so are they recited in the Law also. With these as it were sent in from the iiii. parts of the world, God most righteous executes his judgements.
[DRAFT]

For they are these: sword, famine, death or pestilence, and wild beasts; so they are recited in the Law also. With these, as if sent from the four parts of the world, God most righteously executes his judgments.

[note 31.13]Certainly stories testify, how in desperate matters, when all things are brought to an extremity of mischief, God hath brought in sword, pestilence, famine, and beasts, which have plaged men. [ORIGINAL]
Therefore if we covet to be quit of so great evils, let us serve God in truth, annd make much of his word, which he hath sent to heal us. And reason it is that such as reject soud doctrine, should be vexed with sundry diseases of soul and body, &c.
[DRAFT]

[CRITIQUE]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 31.14]You will say, but these evils invade also the best that is. So they doe in deed. Why God permits this S. Austen shows at large in the first book of the city of God. Certainly to the godly all things turn to the best. The thieves suffered the same death of the cross, that Christ did, and he as they: but the consideration of them is far diverse. The apostles and innumerable Martyrs dye of the sword, likewise do soldiers in the wars, but with unlike lot. The Godly are made pertakers of the passion of the son of God. The ungodly are punished for their wickedness, and their suffering is without glory: yea rather this is the beginning, unless they acknowledge him that strikes them, of everlasting tourmentes, The Lord preserve us from evil.
[DRAFT]
You will say, but these evils invade even the best of things. Indeed, they do. Why God permits this, Augustine explains at length in the first book of *The City of God*. Certainly, for the godly, all things work for the best. The thieves suffered the same death on the cross that Christ did, and he as they: but the outcome is very different. The apostles and countless martyrs die by the sword, just as soldiers do in war, but their fates are unlike. The godly share in the suffering of God’s Son. The ungodly are punished for their wickedness, and their suffering is without glory; rather, it is the beginning, unless they acknowledge the one who strikes them, of everlasting torments. The Lord preserve us from evil.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "consideration of them is far diverse" changed to "outcome is very different" weakens the theological nuance of the original's emphasis on judgment and divine perspective.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "work for the best" instead of "turn to the best" adds a subtly different theological emphasis, implying a purposeful mechanism rather than a divinely ordained consequence.
3. PROPER NOUNS: Austen should be Augustine.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
[FINAL]
You will say, but these evils invade even the best of things. Indeed, they do. Why God permits this, Augustine explains at length in the first book of *The City of God*. Certainly, for the godly, all things work for the best. The thieves suffered the same death on the cross that Christ did, and he as they: but the outcome is very different. ## ¶ The fifth Seal is opened, and the persecution of the faithful set before our eyes, and also the state of Martyrs in an other world. The.xxxij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd when he had opened the fifth seal, I saw under the Altar, the souls of them that were killed for the word of God. And for the testimony which they had, and they cried with a load voice saying: howe long tarieſt thou, lord, which art holy and true, to judge, and to avenge our blood on them that dwell on the earth? and long white Garments were given unto every one of them: and it was said unto them, that they should rest for a little season, until the number of their fellows and brethren, and of the which should be killed as they were, were fulfilled.
[DRAFT]
And when he had opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the souls of those who had been killed for the word of God, and for the testimony they had borne. They cried out with a loud voice, saying, “How long, O Lord, holy and true, will you delay to judge and to avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth?” And white robes were given to each of them, and it was said to them that they should rest for a little while—until the number of their fellow servants and their brothers, who were to be killed as they had been, was completed.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "for the testimony which they had" changed to "for the testimony they had borne." The original implies possession of testimony; the draft implies they actively *performed* it. Subtle, but a shift.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "they had borne" in "for the testimony they had borne" introduces an interpretive element not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "their fellows and brethren" changed to "their fellow servants and their brothers." The original phrasing is more inclusive and potentially broader than the draft's more specific "fellow servants."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: "servants" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And when he had opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the souls of those who had been killed for the word of God, and for the testimony they had borne. They cried out with a loud voice, saying, “How long, O Lord, holy and true, will you delay to judge and to avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth?” And white robes were given to each of them, and it was said to them that they should rest for a little while—until the number of their fellow servants and their brothers, who were to be killed as they had been, was completed.

[ORIGINAL]
The fifte Seal being opened of the lamb, he exhibiteth to our eyes, or rather obiecteth to be scene the continual persecutions of the church: and shows unto us diligently, what is the state of them which die in persecutions. Verily the Lord Christ sends forth ministers and preachers for the salvation of men. And they unthankful, overwhelm with all kind of injuries the faythefull messengers of God, and at length most cruelly slay them. Of the which matter sins the talk of men among themselves is diverse, the very son of God at this present doth gallantly instruct his church, declaring what the godly shall suffer.
[DRAFT]

And they, unthankful, overwhelm the faithful messengers of God with all kinds of injuries, and at length most cruelly slay them. Concerning this matter, the talk of men among themselves is diverse; but the very Son of God at this present boldly instructs his church, declaring what the godly shall suffer.
[CRITIQUE]

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None apparent.

[FINAL]
The fifth seal being opened by the Lamb, he exhibiteth to our eyes, or rather objecteth to be seen, the continual persecutions of the church; and he shows us diligently what is the state of those who die in persecutions. Concerning this matter, the talk of men among themselves is diverse; but the very Son of God at this present doth gallantly instruct his church, declaring what the godly shall suffer.
