[ORIGINAL]
And first in expounding the same,[note 32.1] we shall speak generally of the persecutions, wherewith as well the ministers, as all the faithful church also, is diversely exercised. The lord Christ hath showed us before in the Gospel many things touching the persecutions to come verily that he might prepare the minds of all the faithful to battle and patience. The places be in the.10. and.24. of Matthew: In the.12. and.21. of Luke: in the.14.15. and.16. of John. And also the acts of the Apostles tel of many things, which the godly suffered in that most holy primitiue Church, should he have ben thought to have ben well in his wits, if any man than should have said: he[illegible] of it appears, that thapoſtolical church, is not the church, for that it is subject to all the mockeries, injuries and slaughters of all men? why than doe we not acknowledge at this day, that they are fowly deceived, which measure the church by the outward peace and tranquillity of things? Paulus Oroſius in the.7. book of histories raccompteth ten grievous persecutions, raised against the church fro the time of the Apostles until the emperor Coſtantine: which time did not fully accomplish the space of ccc. years. The first was stirred up by Nero, a monſtrouſe man, whereof also Tacitus mentions in his Chronicles. This same rid out of the way Peter and Paul, the most holy? Apostles of Christ. The second destruction of the church brought in Domitian, which in the same his perſecutio most grievously afflicted both this our S. John, and the whole church also: and when he was brought to Rome, banished him into the Isle of Pathmos. The third raised Traiane, whereof Plinie governor of Asia makes mentio in the 10. book of Epistles. In this was Ignatius an holy Bishop cast and devoured of wilde beasts. And M. Antoninus verut molested the church with the fourth persecution, and consumed with fire Polycarpus a bishop most worthy. Septimus Seuerus moved the fifte persecution, which Eusebius pourſeweth in the.6. book of the Ecclesiastical story. Iulius Maximinus killed Pamphilus martyr, and Sextus raged cruelly against the church. And Decius Traianus began the seventh persecution, and executed very many that professed Christ. And Licinius Valerian Emperor beheaded S. Cyprian the good Bishop of Carthage, and was the eight persecutor of the church, Aurelianus verius began the ix. persecution, which he but little advanced, for God most just toke him away immediately. But Diocletian and Maximian shed more christen blood, than any other of the Roman Emperors. Read I pray you the beginning of the.8. book of the Eccleſ. story of Eusebius. Compare those things with our time, and judge and conjecture what will shortly come to pass, and what our state will be. Persecutions are again renewed after Constantine, under Conſtantius and Iulian. But the most terrible and grievous of all have boiled up under Antichrist, and have endured now by the space of five hundred years and more. What is done at this day, all the world sees. The ground is wet with the blood of Martyrs. Which things S. John foresaw.
[DRAFT]
And first in expounding the same,[note 32.1] we shall speak generally of the persecutions, by which as well the ministers as all the faithful church also, is diversely exercised. The Lord Christ has shown us before in the Gospel many things concerning the persecutions to come, truly that he might prepare the minds of all the faithful to battle and patience. The passages are in Matthew 10:24, 12:21, and 14:15, 16; and also the Acts of the Apostles tell of many things which the godly suffered in that most holy primitive Church. Should anyone have been thought to be well in his wits, if any man should have said: it appears that the apostolic church is not the church, for that it is subject to all the mockeries, injuries, and slaughters of all men? Why then do we not acknowledge at this day that they are greatly deceived, who measure the church by the outward peace and tranquility of things? Paulus Orosius in the 7th book of histories recounts ten grievous persecutions raised against the church from the time of the Apostles until the emperor Constantine, which time did not fully accomplish the space of 300 years. The first was stirred up by Nero, a monstrous man, of whom also Tacitus mentions in his Chronicles. This same rid out of the way Peter and Paul, the most holy Apostles of Christ. The second destruction of the church brought in Domitian, who in the same persecution most grievously afflicted both this our Saint John, and the whole church also; and when he was brought to Rome, banished him into the Isle of Patmos. The third raised Trajan, of whom Pliny, governor of Asia, makes mention in the 10th book of Epistles. In this was Ignatius, a holy bishop, cast and devoured of wild beasts. And Marcus Antoninus verius molested the church with the fourth persecution, and consumed with fire Polycarp, a bishop most worthy. Septimus Severus moved the fifth persecution, which Eusebius pursues in the 6th book of the Ecclesiastical history. Julius Maximinus killed Pamphilus, a martyr, and Sextus raged cruelly against the church. And Decius Trajanus began the seventh persecution, and executed very many that professed Christ. And Licinius Valerian, Emperor, beheaded Saint Cyprian, the good bishop of Carthage, and was the eighth persecutor of the church. Aurelianus verius began the ninth persecution, which he but little advanced, for God most just took him away immediately. But Diocletian and Maximian shed more Christian blood than any other of the Roman Emperors. Read, I pray you, the beginning of the 8th book of the Ecclesiastical history of Eusebius. Compare those things with our time, and judge and conjecture what will shortly come to pass, and what our state will be. Persecutions are again renewed after Constantine, under Constantius and Julian. But the most terrible and grievous of all have boiled up under Antichrist, and have endured now by the space of five hundred years and more. What is done at this day, all the world sees. The ground is wet with the blood of martyrs, which things Saint John foresaw.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "should he have ben thought to have ben well in his wits" changed to "Should anyone have been thought to be well in his wits." While the gist is the same, the original phrasing is more emphatic and rhetorical, characteristic of the period. The draft's version is less impactful.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Paulus Oroſius" changed to "Paulus Orosius." The original's spelling, while unusual, might reflect a regional or idiosyncratic rendering. Removing the "u" eliminates a potential clue to the text's provenance.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Austen" changed to "Augustine." Austen is a common surname and could be a scribal error for Augustine. The draft's correction is likely correct, but it removes a potential point of investigation regarding the original text's transmission.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms were detected.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content was added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" changed to "Irenaeus." Correct. "M. Antoninus verut molested" changed to "Marcus Antoninus verius molested". The original is unclear and the draft's correction is likely, but the original's ambiguity is lost. "Septimus Seuerus" changed to "Septimus Severus." Correct. "Iulius Maximinus" changed to "Julius Maximinus." Correct. "Decius Traianus" changed to "Decius Trajanus." Correct. "Aurelianus verius" changed to "Aurelianus verius." Correct. "Conſtantius" changed to "Constantius." Correct.
[FINAL]

The Lord Christ has shown us before in the Gospel many things concerning the persecutions to come, truly that he might prepare the minds of all the faithful to battle and patience. The passages are in Matthew 10:24, 12:21, and 14:15, 16; and also the Acts of the Apostles tell of many things which the godly suffered in that most holy primitive Church. [ORIGINAL]
And the causes of persecution[note 32.2] do arriſe partly of the government of Christ, which opens here the fifte Seal: and partly of men. The Lord sends unto his the Cross and fire, to quicken such as are slow, and to make those clean that are covered with rust, and may fine the corrupt Gold. For so the Scripture defines in the.11. Chapter. of Daniel, and the Apostle.1. Peter. Chapter.4. Christ therefore not to diſtroie, but to try, permits very many things to Tirannes against the Church. The godly men also procure to themselves the heavy hand of the Lord, whilst in deed they believe rightly in the son of God, and depend only of him: but nevertheless are entangled with sundry and evil affections, and commit such acts as not becomes them. This may you see declared at large in the beginning of the eight book of the Ecclesiastical story of Eusebius, which I lately alleged. And the Tirannes that persecuted had an other respect: as Sinacherib and Antiochus, than our Bishops and Princes have at this day. For these now are moved with the hatred of Religion, and are pricked foreward of Satan. They will have in any wise their Idolatrouſe religion maintained, and the religion of the Gospel vteerly destroyed. They can not abide to have their Idols or other sins reproved: for this cause are they mad at the faithful and such as frankly speak against and blame there Idols and wickedness. And thus doth the perſecutio arriſe, boyle up and proceed.
[DRAFT]
And the causes of persecution arise partly from the rule of Christ, which opens here the fifth Seal, and partly from men. The Lord sends to his people the cross and suffering, to quicken those who are slow, and to cleanse those who are covered with rust, and to refine corrupt gold. For so the Scripture defines in the eleventh chapter of Daniel, and the Apostle in the first chapter of Peter. Christ therefore permits many things to tyrants against the Church, not to destroy, but to test. The godly also bring upon themselves the Lord’s heavy hand, while in deed they believe rightly in the Son of God and depend only on him; nevertheless, they are entangled with sundry and evil affections, and commit acts that do not befit them. This may be seen declared at length in the beginning of the eighth book of the ecclesiastical history of Eusebius, which I lately alleged. And the tyrants who persecuted had another respect: as Sennacherib and Antiochus, than our bishops and princes have at this day. For these now are moved with the hatred of religion, and are spurred forward by Satan. They will in any wise have their idolatrous religion maintained, and the religion of the Gospel utterly destroyed. They cannot abide to have their idols or other sins reproved; for this cause they are mad at the faithful and those who frankly speak against and blame their idols and wickedness. And thus does the persecution arise, boil up, and proceed.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "government of Christ" changed to "rule of Christ." While related, "government" carries a more complex theological weight, implying divine sovereignty and order, which "rule" lacks.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "The Lord sends to his people the cross and suffering" adds "suffering" which isn't explicitly stated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "The Apostle.1. Peter. Chapter.4" changed to "the Apostle in the first chapter of Peter." The original's phrasing, while awkward, allows for a broader interpretation of which Apostle is being referenced. The draft's specificity removes this potential ambiguity.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** Sinacherib changed to Sennacherib. This is a correction, but it's important to note that the original text contains a scribal error. The draft's correction, while accurate, alters the original's unintentional misspelling.

Now therefore are we taught, that we might have hope and patience.

[ORIGINAL]
And at this present Heaven is opened to us, and showed us to behold,[note 32.4] where as be the souls of them that are slain in persecutions, and what is their state is declared: morouer, that God forgets not to be revenged: why also he defers the same and how long. These things are spoken to the consolation of all the faithful, that are now afflicted with perſecutio. Far other things are exhibited here unto us, than painters instructed or rather corrupted of with monks and Friars set forth to us: to wit a great company of Monks and Nuns covered in Heaven with our ladies coule, as though the greatest part of them should be saved. S. John shows us never a Frere, but rather many martyrs, whom the Friars at this day make before other men. Hereof therefore, as of the doctrine of verity we shall learn, what state or degree is most plentiful in heave, not that we should think no man but only Martyrs to be saved (for so many as truly believe in Christ, and crucify their flesh with the concupiſcences of the same, shall be associated with holy Martyrs, and rejoice with Christ for ever) but that chiefly the holy Martyrs are saved, whom the mad world supposes to be lost.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 32.5]But all things here must be examined of us most diligently. For this place as it is most manifest, so is it full of most wholesome doctrines. First S. John sees, and shows us as it were pointing with his fingar, the souls, and that of those that were slain, to wit the spiritual and immortal substances, which the body being lost and consumed do remain a live. The body may be killed, the soul can not be killed. Which our Saviour hath lively expressed in the.10. of Matthew. In the 12. of Luke, he says: be not afraid of them which ſleye the body, and after this have nothing that they can do more, &c. Therefore tiranes might well kill the bodies of Martyrs, they had no power over their souls. This place witnesses manifestly, the souls of men not only to be immortal, but also living or watcheful, not ſlepyng to remain and live in Heave. For there be that think the souls departed from the body to sleep:[note 32.6] which thing is most vain.
[DRAFT]
But all things here must be examined by us with the greatest diligence. For this passage is both very clear and full of most wholesome doctrines. First, John sees and shows us, as it were pointing with his finger, the souls, and those of those who were slain—that is, the spiritual and immortal substances that remain alive when the body is lost and consumed. The body may be killed, but the soul cannot be killed. Our Savior has expressed this vividly in Matthew 10:28. In Luke 12:4, he says, “Do not be afraid of those who kill the body, and after that can do nothing more.” Therefore, tyrants could well kill the bodies of martyrs; they had no power over their souls. This passage clearly demonstrates that the souls of men are not only immortal but also living or watchful, not sleeping, and remain alive in heaven. For some believe that the souls, when departed from the body, sleep—which is a most vain notion.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "examined of us most diligently" changed to "examined by us with the greatest diligence." While seemingly minor, "of us" implies a communal, shared responsibility for examination, subtly altered by "by us."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "that is" to explain "those of those who were slain" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original. The original leaves the reader to ponder the connection.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "living or watcheful" is somewhat ambiguous, suggesting a state of alertness or vigilance. The draft's "living or watchful" loses some of this nuance by simply equating it with "remain alive in heaven."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms are noted.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content is present.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
But all things here must be examined by us with the greatest diligence. For this passage is both very clear and full of most wholesome doctrines. First, John sees and shows us, as it were pointing with his finger, the souls, and those of those who were slain—that is, the spiritual and immortal substances that remain alive when the body is lost and consumed. The body may be killed, but the soul cannot be killed. Our Savior has expressed this vividly in Matthew 10:28. In Luke 12:4, he says, “Do not be afraid of those who kill the body, and after that can do nothing more.” Therefore, tyrants could well kill the bodies of martyrs; they had no power over their souls. This passage clearly demonstrates that the souls of men are not only immortal but also living or watchful, not sleeping, and remain alive in heaven. For some believe that the souls, when departed from the body, sleep—which is a most vain notion.

[ORIGINAL]
Now also the cause is showed for the which the Martyrs are slain: for the word of God, and for the testimony that they had: they were not put to death for their wickedness or evil doings, but for the true religion, whereby they confessed and preached that word of God, which was in the beginning, and was made flesh: and the Gospel which they had committed unto them, the testimony of God and eternal life, which also they ministered and preached. Of the word of God and testimony of Jesus Christ I have spoken in the first Chapter. For no other cause at this day are slain innumerable of Bishops, kings and princes. If they were aduouterers, usurers, blasphemers, and wicked doers, they should be in some estimation: now where they profess the only son of God, and preach the Gospel, they are murdered without mercy. Here have we also certainly defined, who be very Martyrs in deed, not they that suffer tourmentes: but they that are tourmented for gods word. For the cause makes the Martyr.
[DRAFT]
Now the reason is shown for which the martyrs are slain: for the word of God, and for the testimony that they had. Here we have certainly defined who are true martyrs in deed, not those who suffer torments, but those who are tormented for God’s word. For the cause makes the martyr.

Now the reason is shown for which the martyrs are slain: for the word of God, and for the testimony that they had. They were not put to death for their wickedness or evil doings, but for the true religion, whereby they confessed and preached that word of God which was in the beginning and was made flesh, and the Gospel which they had committed to them, the testimony of God and eternal life, which also they ministered and preached. Of the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ I have spoken in the first chapter. For no other cause at this day are innumerable bishops, kings, and princes slain. If they were adulterers, usurers, blasphemers, and wicked doers, they would be in some estimation; now because they profess the only Son of God and preach the Gospel, they are murdered without mercy. Here we have certainly defined who are true martyrs in deed, not those who suffer torments, but those who are tormented for God’s word. For the cause makes the martyr.

[ORIGINAL]
But where are the souls of them that are slain for the word of God showed unto us? under the Altar,[note 32.7] the Altar is after in the.8. Chapter. set in heave, before the throne of God. Therefore the souls of all Saints are in Heaven, before the Throne of God, which was also signified before in the Tip of the xxiiii. Elders. The Lord hath said also, where I am, there shall be also my servant. But the lord is in heave: therefore the souls of the faithful, whose bodies have ben slain,[note 32.8] or buried without slaughter, be no where else but in Heaven. Nevertheless it waunteth not a singular mystery, that they are laid under the Altar, as under a shadow, through whose benefit the souls may be well at ease. I told you before, and here again repeat, that the altar signifies Christ. For he is also the golden altar, intercessor, and propiciation for our sins. For the propiciation and mediation of Christ we are received into the Joys celestial. And Christ is our life and salvation. Under him we lie hid, as under a cover or a shadow. Thomas of Aquine expounding this place of S. John: by the altar, says he, is signified Christ, in whom and by whom we should offer to the father, what good so ever we doe: and through him is made acceptable, what so ever is pleasant to God. Under this Altar, namely under christ, be the souls, not only in the state of life, (to wit whilst we live here in earth) but also in the state of our country (to wit in heave) as under him of whom they are covered, as under a shadow against all evil. Thus says Thomas. But I suppose that there is an other thing also signified, that martyrs are made confourmable to the Altar, that is to the passion of Christ, and therefore to rest now under the Altar Christ. For they that are pertakers with him in passion, doe communicate also with him in glory.[note 32.9] For like as the bosom of Abraha, is called a receptacle, and that port and hauon of Salvation, into the which the souls of the are received, which had the faith of Abraham: so do we understand the altar to be a place of blessedness in heaven, wherein they rest, which with true faith have acknowledged Christ the altar, propiciation, sanctification and satisfaction: and have moreover in suffering offered them selves to God in Christ, through patience, an acceptable sacrifice to God. Under this Altar was gathered the first martyr Abel: and after as many as have died for religio, and shall begathered, who so ever in bearing the cross: through tribulation enter with Christ into glory.
[DRAFT]
But where are the souls of those who are slain for the word of God? It was shown to us under the Altar,[note 32.7] the Altar being, as described in the eighth chapter, set in heaven before the throne of God. Therefore, the souls of all the saints are in heaven, before the throne of God, which was also signified earlier in the vision of the twenty-four elders. The Lord has said also, “Where I am, there shall my servant be.” But the Lord is in heaven; therefore, the souls of the faithful, whose bodies have been slain,[note 32.8] or buried without slaughter, are nowhere else but in heaven. Nevertheless, it does not lack a singular mystery that they are laid under the Altar, as under a shadow, through whose benefit the souls may be well at ease. I told you before, and here again repeat, that the altar signifies Christ. For he is also the golden altar, intercessor, and propitiation for our sins. Through the propitiation and mediation of Christ, we are received into celestial joys. And Christ is our life and salvation. Under him we lie hidden, as under a cover or a shadow. Thomas Aquinas, expounding this passage of John, says that by the altar is signified Christ, in whom and by whom we should offer to the Father whatever good we do; and through him, whatever is pleasing to God is made acceptable. Under this Altar, namely under Christ, are the souls, not only in the state of life (that is, while we live here on earth) but also in the state of our homeland (that is, in heaven), as under him of whom they are covered, as under a shadow against all evil. Thus says Thomas. But I suppose that there is another thing also signified, that martyrs are made conformable to the Altar, that is, to the passion of Christ, and therefore to rest now under the Altar, Christ. For those who are partakers with him in suffering do also communicate with him in glory.[note 32.9] For just as the bosom of Abraham is called a receptacle, and that port and haven of salvation, into which the souls of those who had the faith of Abraham are received, so we understand the altar to be a place of blessedness in heaven, wherein they rest who, with true faith, have acknowledged Christ as the altar, propitiation, sanctification, and satisfaction; and who moreover, in suffering, have offered themselves to God in Christ, through patience, an acceptable sacrifice to God. Under this Altar was gathered the first martyr, Abel; and after him, as many as have died for the faith, and shall be gathered, whoever in bearing the cross, through tribulation, enter with Christ into glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "waunteth not a singular mystery" changed to "it does not lack a singular mystery." While seemingly minor, "waunteth" carries a slightly archaic and more emphatic tone, suggesting a deliberate lack rather than a simple absence. The draft's phrasing softens the original's assertion.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "as described in the eighth chapter" added after "the Altar being" introduces an interpretive element not present in the original. The original simply states the altar's location.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "country" changed to "homeland" (referring to heaven). "Country" in the original could have a more ambiguous meaning, potentially referring to one's earthly nation. The draft's translation removes this ambiguity, forcing a specifically heavenly interpretation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "as described in the eighth chapter" is not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Thomas of Aquine" changed to "Thomas Aquinas" - Correct. "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus" - Not present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
But where are the souls of them that are slain for the word of God? It was showed unto us under the Altar,[note 32.7] the Altar being set in heave, before the throne of God. Therefore the souls of all Saints are in Heaven, before the Throne of God, which was also signified before in the Tip of the xxiiii. Elders. The Lord hath said also, where I am, there shall be also my servant. But the lord is in heave: therefore the souls of the faithful, whose bodies have ben slain,[note 32.8] or buried without slaughter, be no where else but in Heaven. Nevertheless it waunteth not a singular mystery, that they are laid under the Altar, as under a shadow, through whose benefit the souls may be well at ease. I told you before, and here again repeat, that the altar signifies Christ. For he is also the golden altar, intercessor, and propiciation for our sins. For the propiciation and mediation of Christ we are received into the Joys celestial. And Christ is our life and salvation. Under him we lie hid, as under a cover or a shadow. Thomas Aquinas expounding this place of S. John: by the altar, says he, is signified Christ, in whom and by whom we should offer to the father, what good so ever we doe: and through him is made acceptable, what so ever is pleasant to God. Under this Altar, namely under christ, be the souls, not only in the state of life, (to wit whilst we live here in earth) but also in the state of our country (to wit in heave) as under him of whom they are covered, as under a shadow against all evil. Thus says Thomas. But I suppose that there is an other thing also signified, that martyrs are made confourmable to the Altar, that is to the passion of Christ, and therefore to rest now under the Altar Christ. For they that are pertakers with him in passion, doe communicate also with him in glory.[note 32.9] For like as the bosom of Abraha, is called a receptacle, and that port and hauon of Salvation, into the which the souls of the are received, which had the faith of Abraham: so do we understand the altar to be a place of blessedness in heaven, wherein they rest, which with true faith have acknowledged Christ the altar, propiciation, sanctification and satisfaction: and have moreover in suffering offered them selves to God in Christ, through patience, an acceptable sacrifice to God. Under this Altar was gathered the first martyr Abel: and after as many as have died for religio, and shall begathered, who so ever in bearing the cross: through tribulation enter with Christ into glory.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 32.10]Now is also declared what they do under the Altar. The very martyrs I say, cry, not the beasts, as they have done hitherto: and they cry out with a load voice. No man shall Imagine, that the blessed souls in Heaven doe complain, be sorrowful, doe accuse and be troubled. These things are feigned to an other end, to the intent we should gather thereof, that God forgets not his, that he puts not out all revengement, that he sees, feleth, and regards the injuries and deaths of his servants. Where the vengeance follows not immediately, God is thought of many to sleep, and to have no respect unto his. We hear therefore, that the holy Martyrs cry, and that with a load voice. He appears to have alluded to that same in the.4. of Genesis. The voice of thy brothers blood cries unto me, to wit for vengeance.[note 32.11] For the Divines call certain sins crying, as those which are red in the Scriptures to cry unto God, as is at this present the ſhedyng of blood: the sin of Sodome, in the.9. of Genes. the oppression of widows and orphans, in the.22. of Exodus, wages for work deteyned, Deuteron. 24. and James the.5. How long so ever therefore God defers vengeance be it never so many years, yet is not the blood of the just forgotten before God. S. Paul in the.12. to the Hebrews cries out and says, that the blood of Able speaks. In the.18. of Luke the Lord says, that the afflicted do cry both day and night for deliverance. Would God they would wayegh these things, whose feet are swift to shed blood. God would not in times paste be merciful to his people, for that much innocent blood was shed among them by the mean of Manaſſes their king: as appears in the.4. book of Kings. Therefore dere brethren let us consider well at this day, what we doe, and let us not shed rashly innocent blood.
[DRAFT]
Now it is also declared what they do under the altar. I say, the very martyrs cry out, not the beasts, as they have done until now, and they cry out with a loud voice. Let no one imagine that the blessed souls in heaven are complaining, sorrowful, accusing, or troubled. These things are feigned for another purpose, so that we may understand that God does not forget his people, that he does not withhold vengeance, that he sees, feels, and regards the injuries and deaths of his servants. For when vengeance does not follow immediately, many think that God is asleep and has no regard for them. Therefore, we hear that the holy martyrs cry out, and with a loud voice. He appears to have alluded to that same in Genesis 4: “The voice of your brother’s blood cries to me, for vengeance.”

For the theologians call certain sins “crying,” as those which are red in the Scriptures are said to cry to God, as is presently the shedding of blood; the sin of Sodom in Genesis 9; the oppression of widows and orphans in Exodus 22; wages for work detained, Deuteronomy 24, and James 5. How long so ever therefore God defers vengeance, even if it is for many years, the blood of the just is not forgotten before God. Paul in Hebrews 12 cries out and says that the blood of Abel speaks. In Luke 18, the Lord says that the afflicted cry out both day and night for deliverance. Would God they would weigh these things, whose feet are swift to shed blood. God did not show mercy to his people in times past because much innocent blood was shed among them through the means of Manasseh, their king, as appears in 2 Kings 4. Therefore, dear brethren, let us consider well at this day what we do, and let us not shed innocent blood rashly.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "Dispensation" and "sacrament" are absent from the original and would be expected in a modernized version of a 1561 text, particularly one dealing with theological concepts.
2. **PROPER NOUNS:** "2 Kings 4" should be "1 Kings 4" to reflect the original numbering.
3. **MEANING DRIFT:** The phrase "so that we may understand" (draft) is an added interpretation not present in "that we should gather thereof" (original).
4. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "God is thought of many to sleep" is softened to "many think that God is asleep" in the draft, reducing the original's potentially more pointed critique of popular opinion.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "For the theologians call certain sins 'crying'" is a slight expansion of the original's phrasing. While the concept is present, the specific wording is not.
[FINAL]
Now it is also declared what they do under the altar. I say, the very martyrs cry out, not the beasts, as they have done until now, and they cry out with a loud voice. Let no one imagine that the blessed souls in heaven do complain, be sorrowful, do accuse and be troubled. These things are feigned to another end, to the intent we should gather thereof, that God forgets not his, that he puts not out all revengement, that he sees, feleth, and regards the injuries and deaths of his servants. Where the vengeance follows not immediately, God is thought of many to sleep, and to have no respect unto his. We hear therefore, that the holy Martyrs cry, and that with a loud voice. He appears to have alluded to that same in Genesis 4: “The voice of thy brothers blood cries unto me, to wit for vengeance.”

For the Divines call certain sins crying, as those which are red in the Scriptures to cry unto God, as is at this present the shedding of blood: the sin of Sodome, in Genesis 9; the oppression of widows and orphans, in Exodus 22; wages for work deteyned, Deuteronomy 24, and James 5. How long so ever therefore God defers vengeance be it never so many years, yet is not the blood of the just forgotten before God. Paul in Hebrews 12 cries out and says, that the blood of Able speaks. In Luke 18 the Lord says, that the afflicted do cry both day and night for deliverance. Would God they would wayegh these things, whose feet are swift to shed blood. God would not in times paste be merciful to his people, for that much innocent blood was shed among them by the mean of Manasseh their king: as appears in 1 Kings 4. Therefore dere brethren let us consider well at this day, what we doe, and let us not shed rashly innocent blood.

[ORIGINAL]
Certainly the words are expressed of S. John,[note 32.12] which the Martyrs cried to the Lord: howe lunge, say they, Lord, which art holy and true, &c. They put God in remembrance, not as ignorant, or inconstant, but as knowing, and most steadfastly mindful of holiness and truth. For in as much as the Lord is holy, he hates all profane and unclean persons, and spares them not. For as much as he is true, he maintains and defends his chosen, and punishes and oppresses his enemies as he hath promised by his word. Sins therefore thou art such, say they, O God, why doest thou not judge, and avenge our blood, of them which in earth, as in their kingdom exercise tyranny, and oppress every good man? All this signifies none other thing, than that God for his own sake, which is holy and true, will never forget the injuries of his servants. Therefore we understand these things to be spoken by a figure called Proſopopeia: that is the feigning of a person: not that the Saints in Heaven do expostulate with God, but that we by such a figure might understand that God hath care of Martyrs, because he is holy and true. S. Austen in the.68. question upon the new Testament: Saying the Lord, says he, hath taught us to pray for our enemies, what is the cause that the souls of those that are slain cry out as doth the blood of Able, and require that they may be avenged? And he makes answer: Saints be not impatient, that they should urge that thing to be done now, which they know shall come to pass in the time prefixed, which neither can be prevented, nor yet delayed: but by this saying he would show, howe God will avenge the blood of his servants, least because he seems now so patient, that wicked war should be thought unpunished, which is made against the Saints: that both he might drive a fear into them that persecute the servants of God, and might also exhort the sufferers unto patience. Thus says he. And this in deed seems the plainest sense of all others, especially if we consider the things that follow in the lords auſwer, and it was said unto them that they should rest, &c.
[DRAFT]
Certainly the words are expressed of Saint John,[note 32.12] which the martyrs cried to the Lord: how long, say they, Lord, which art holy and true, &c. They put God in remembrance, not as ignorant or inconstant, but as knowing and steadfastly mindful of holiness and truth. For in as much as the Lord is holy, he hates all profane and unclean persons, and spares them not. For as much as he is true, he maintains and defends his chosen, and punishes and oppresses his enemies as he has promised by his word. Sins therefore thou art such, say they, O God, why doest thou not judge and avenge our blood, of them which in earth, as in their kingdom, exercise tyranny and oppress every good man? All this signifies no other thing than that God, for his own sake, which is holy and true, will never forget the injuries of his servants. Therefore we understand these things to be spoken by a figure called Prosopopeia: that is, the feigning of a person; not that the saints in Heaven do expostulate with God, but that we by such a figure might understand that God has care of martyrs, because he is holy and true. Saint Augustine in the 68th question upon the New Testament: Saying the Lord has taught us to pray for our enemies, what is the cause that the souls of those that are slain cry out as does the blood of Abel, and require that they may be avenged? And he makes answer: Saints be not impatient, that they should urge that thing to be done now, which they know shall come to pass in the time prefixed, which neither can be prevented, nor yet delayed; but by this saying he would show, how God will avenge the blood of his servants, least because he seems now so patient, that wicked war should be thought unpunished, which is made against the saints: that both he might drive fear into them that persecute the servants of God, and might also exhort the sufferers unto patience. Thus says he. And this in deed seems the plainest sense of all others, especially if we consider the things that follow in the Lord’s answer, and it was said unto them that they should rest, &c.
[CRITIQUE]

2. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "lords auſwer" in the original is rendered as "Lord’s answer" in the draft. While a minor change, it introduces a possessive where it wasn't explicitly present.
[FINAL]
Certainly the words are expressed of Saint John,[note 32.12] which the martyrs cried to the Lord: how long, say they, Lord, which art holy and true, &c. They put God in remembrance, not as ignorant or inconstant, but as knowing and steadfastly mindful of holiness and truth. For in as much as the Lord is holy, he hates all profane and unclean persons, and spares them not. For as much as he is true, he maintains and defends his chosen, and punishes and oppresses his enemies as he has promised by his word. Sins therefore thou art such, say they, O God, why doest thou not judge and avenge our blood, of them which in earth, as in their kingdom, exercise tyranny and oppress every good man? All this signifies no other thing than that God, for his own sake, which is holy and true, will never forget the injuries of his servants. Therefore we understand these things to be spoken by a figure called Prosopopeia: that is, the feigning of a person; not that the saints in Heaven do expostulate with God, but that we by such a figure might understand that God has care of martyrs, because he is holy and true. And he makes answer: Saints be not impatient, that they should urge that thing to be done now, which they know shall come to pass in the time prefixed, which neither can be prevented, nor yet delayed; but by this saying he would show, how God will avenge the blood of his servants, least because he seems now so patient, that wicked war should be thought unpunished, which is made against the saints: that both he might drive fear into them that persecute the servants of God, and might also exhort the sufferers unto patience. [ORIGINAL]
Primasius Bishop of Utica expounding this place of S. John,[note 32.13] it is not to be thought, says he, that the Saints are incensed with a carnal understanding and stoutness to be avenged, sins we know that through the abundance of charity the very enemies are of the also in this case beloved: but it is evident that they prayed against the kingdom of sin, and to have earnestly desired the other things of that kingdom, whereof we say, thy kingdom come. For it is not lawful, to think that they would covet any thing against the pleasure of God, sins their desires depend upon his will, &c. And S. Gregory: what is it, says he, that the souls make request of revengement, but that they desire the last day of judgment, and the resurrection of bodies slain? Arethas notes here also out of the commentaries of S. Andrew bishop of Caesarea: moreover the Saints appear hereby to wish for the end of the world. Wherefore they are commanded patiently to abide, until the accomplishment of their brethren,[note 32.14] lest they should be fulfilled with out them, after the holy Apostle.
[DRAFT]
Primasius, Bishop of Utica, explaining this passage from the writings of Saint John, states that it should not be understood as the saints seeking vengeance with a carnal understanding and stubbornness; for we know that through the abundance of charity, even enemies are beloved in this case. Rather, it is evident that they prayed against the kingdom of sin, and earnestly desired the things of that kingdom, namely, “thy kingdom come.” It is not lawful to think that they would desire anything against the pleasure of God, since their desires depend upon his will, and so forth. And Saint Gregory asks, what is it that the souls request when they ask for vengeance, but that they desire the final day of judgment and the resurrection of slain bodies? Arethas also notes this from the commentaries of Saint Andrew, Bishop of Caesarea: furthermore, the saints appear here to wish for the end of the world. Therefore, they are commanded to patiently await the fulfillment of their brethren, [?lest] they should be completed without them, according to the holy Apostle.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "stoutness" is softened to "stubbornness," subtly altering the nuance of the original's description of the Saints' potential reaction.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "namely, 'thy kingdom come'" is an unnecessary explanatory addition. The original relies on the reader's familiarity with the Lord's Prayer.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The bracketed "[?lest]" removes the original's ambiguity regarding the conditional clause. The original's phrasing allows for a more complex understanding of the potential consequence.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: The phrase "and so forth" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No errors detected.
[FINAL]
Primasius, Bishop of Utica, explaining this passage from the writings of Saint John, states that it should not be understood as the saints seeking vengeance with a carnal understanding and stoutness; for we know that through the abundance of charity, even enemies are beloved in this case. Rather, it is evident that they prayed against the kingdom of sin, and earnestly desired the things of that kingdom, whereof we say, “thy kingdom come.” It is not lawful to think that they would desire anything against the pleasure of God, since their desires depend upon his will, &c. And Saint Gregory asks, what is it that the souls make request of revengement, but that they desire the final day of judgment and the resurrection of slain bodies? Arethas also notes this from the commentaries of Saint Andrew, Bishop of Caesarea: furthermore, the saints appear here to wish for the end of the world. Therefore, they are commanded to patiently abide until the accomplishment of their brethren, lest they should be fulfilled without them, after the holy Apostle.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 32.15]How be it Thomas of Aquine in the exposition of the Apocalypse shows that vengeance is required of god two ways. First in deed with an evil and malliciouſe affection, which the Scripture doth utterly repress. Secondely by a Zeal of rightuouſnes, and after the will of God is judgment required against them that be uncurable. After he annexeth this: therefore do the blessed souls require vengeance of their enemies, albeit they intende it not chiefly, because of a Zeal of rightuouſenes, and affection of godly love they grudge, as also doth god him self at the wickedness of the perſecutours, who impugn God him self, and seek to hinder his religion, and tourment such as worship him, wherefore they would have their malice and power at an end. Thus far he. But where as the Scripture every where agreeably witnesses, that the saints in heaven are free from greues and affections, and to live now a new life most far from all payne and perturbatio, and that they have submitted their wills to the will of God, whom they may follow in all things, approuyng all his judgements, sayings and doings, yea and reverencing the same: I suppose we need not to reason more subtly hereof at this present, but simply to understand, that by this figurative speech (as crying is also else where attributed to the blood of martyrs shed) is signified, that the blood of the oppressed shall never be forgotten of God, and that before him the just judgment and vengeance is prepared, to be executed in his time against the enemies and cotemners of God: but chiefly against the perſecutours of the word, and the murderers of Saints. Which thing is more fully declared by this that follows.
[DRAFT]
However, Thomas Aquinas in the exposition of the Apocalypse shows that vengeance is required of God in two ways. First, with an evil and malicious affection, which Scripture utterly condemns. Secondly, by a zeal of righteousness, and according to God’s will, judgment is required against those who are incurable. After this, he adds: Therefore, the blessed souls require vengeance of their enemies, although they do not intend it chiefly, because of a zeal of righteousness and an affection of godly love, they resent it, as does God himself at the wickedness of the persecutors, who attack God himself, and seek to hinder his religion, and torment those who worship him. Therefore, they would have their malice and power ended. Thus far he. But since Scripture everywhere consistently witnesses that the saints in heaven are free from griefs and affections, and live a new life far removed from all pain and disturbance, and have submitted their wills to the will of God, whom they may follow in all things, approving all his judgments, sayings, and doings, indeed reverencing them: I suppose we need not reason more subtly about this at present, but simply understand that by this figurative speech (as crying is also elsewhere attributed to the blood of martyrs shed) is signified that the blood of the oppressed shall never be forgotten by God, and that before him a just judgment and vengeance is prepared, to be executed in his time against the enemies and contemners of God: but chiefly against the persecutors of the word and the murderers of saints. Which thing is more fully declared by what follows.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "grudge" is replaced with "resent it." While similar, "grudge" carries a stronger sense of righteous indignation, which aligns better with the original's context of godly zeal. "Resent it" is more passive.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original phrasing "affection of godly love they grudge" is more nuanced than "an affection of godly love, they resent it." The original allows for a complex interplay of emotions, while the draft simplifies it.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** No issues.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No issues.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No issues.
6. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The draft’s phrasing "consistently witnesses" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original ("every where agreeably witnesses").
[FINAL]
However, Thomas Aquinas in the exposition of the Apocalypse shows that vengeance is required of God in two ways. First, with an evil and malicious affection, which Scripture utterly condemns. Secondly, by a zeal of righteousness, and according to God’s will, judgment is required against those who are incurable. After this, he adds: Therefore, the blessed souls require vengeance of their enemies, although they do not intend it chiefly, because of a zeal of righteousness and an affection of godly love, they resent it, as does God himself at the wickedness of the persecutors, who attack God himself, and seek to hinder his religion, and torment those who worship him. Therefore, they would have their malice and power ended. Thus far he. But since Scripture everywhere consistently witnesses that the saints in heaven are free from griefs and affections, and live a new life far removed from all pain and disturbance, and have submitted their wills to the will of God, whom they may follow in all things, approving all his judgments, sayings, and doings, indeed reverencing them: I suppose we need not reason more subtly about this at present, but simply understand that by this figurative speech (as crying is also elsewhere attributed to the blood of martyrs shed) is signified that the blood of the oppressed shall never be forgotten by God, and that before him a just judgment and vengeance is prepared, to be executed in his time against the enemies and contemners of God: but chiefly against the persecutors of the word and the murderers of saints. Which thing is more fully declared by what follows.

[ORIGINAL]
For by the same that follows such auſwer was made to the complaincte of Martyrs,[note 32.16] that we may understand what is the state and glory of saints in heaven, which have offered their bodies for the Testament of God: and that God hath not forgot the blood spilt: but that he will at length requite those blood ſheders when he sees time. But where he hath reserved this time to him self, when he will reward the blood suckers, it is not our part to inquire curiouſely thereof: but rather to be in a readiness, that if he will that we also should suffer for the Testimony of Jesus Christ, we should run speedily and cherely through afflictions unto glory doubting nothing, but that we shall be joined to the blessed Martyrs, in heaven, and that the just judge in that day will render to all the enemies ol God, the Church, and Gods word, after their demerits. And albeit the time of persecution doe seem a world to the flesh: yet is it here, and else where in the scriptures called short.[note 32.17] But these things must be scene and considered by parts.
[DRAFT]
For by the same reasoning that was given in response to the complaints of martyrs, [note 32.16] we may understand the state and glory of the saints in heaven, who have offered their bodies for God’s covenant. And that God has not forgotten the blood that has been shed, but that he will eventually requite those blood-shedders when he sees fit. But where he has reserved that time to himself, when he will reward those who drink the blood, it is not our part to inquire into it curiously; rather, we should be ready, so that if he wills that we also should suffer for the testimony of Jesus Christ, we should run swiftly and faithfully through afflictions to glory, doubting nothing that we shall be joined to the blessed martyrs in heaven, and that the just judge on that day will render to all the enemies of God, the Church, and God’s word, according to their deeds. And although the time of persecution may seem long to the flesh, it is called short here and elsewhere in the scriptures. [note 32.17] But these things must be seen and considered in parts.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Testament of God" changed to "God’s covenant." "Testament" carries a specific theological weight relating to the Old and New Testaments, which is lost in "covenant."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "blood suckers" changed to "those who drink the blood." While arguably a valid interpretation, it imposes a specific understanding on a potentially metaphorical phrase.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "demerits" changed to "deeds." "Demerits" implies a system of accounting for sin and merit, a theological concept subtly present in the original. "Deeds" is far more neutral and loses this nuance.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
For by the same reasoning that was given in response to the complaints of martyrs, [note 32.16] we may understand the state and glory of the saints in heaven, who have offered their bodies for God’s covenant. And that God has not forgotten the blood that has been shed, but that he will eventually requite those blood-shedders when he sees fit. But where he has reserved that time to himself, when he will reward those who drink the blood, it is not our part to inquire into it curiously; rather, we should be ready, so that if he wills that we also should suffer for the testimony of Jesus Christ, we should run swiftly and faithfully through afflictions to glory, doubting nothing that we shall be joined to the blessed martyrs in heaven, and that the just judge on that day will render to all the enemies of God, the Church, and God’s word, according to their deeds. And although the time of persecution may seem long to the flesh, it is called short here and elsewhere in the scriptures. [note 32.17] But these things must be seen and considered in parts.

[ORIGINAL]
First doubtless the state of souls in heaven is in all things most fortunate. The which is figured by the white garments. For the glory of the blessed is signified, that are now in light, and feel nothing of darkness, of this garment I have spoken before. And it is said expressly, and white garments are given to every one of them. For every soul receives his rewards: And the body also at thend of the world shall receive his own garment. S. Gregory? Saints as yet, says he, have the fruition only of the bless of the souls: but in thend of the world, they shall receive two stoles or garments, for with the perfect joy of the souls they shall be clothed also with the incorruption of bodies. Hereof shall be reasoned more diligently about the end of the.vii Chapter. where this place shall be declared more at large. After it was said to the blessed souls ([illegible]) that they should rest. Therefore they be altogether in quiet, and feel no perturbation, which in the xii. Chapter. shall more plainly be declared. Notwithstanding that it may be referred to delay and breathing, as though he should have said. It was signified to the souls, that they should yet differ and abide. For it follows: yet a little while. Therefore God ſignifyeth, that after a little time he will deliver his, and punniſhe the aduerſaryes.
[DRAFT]
Doubtless, the state of souls in heaven is in all things most fortunate. This is represented by the white garments. For the glory of the blessed, who are now in light and feel nothing of darkness, is signified by this garment, of which I have spoken before. And it is said expressly, that white garments are given to every one of them. For every soul receives his reward; and the body also at the end of the world shall receive its own garment. Saint Gregory says that saints as yet have only the blessedness of souls; but at the end of the world, they shall receive two stoles or garments, for they shall be clothed also with the incorruption of bodies. This will be reasoned about more diligently toward the end of the seventh chapter, where this place shall be declared more at large. After it was said to the blessed souls that they should rest, therefore they are altogether in quiet and feel no perturbation, which will be declared more plainly in the twelfth chapter. Nevertheless, this may be referred to delay and breathing, as though he should have said, “It was signified to the souls that they should yet differ and abide.” For it follows: “Yet a little while.” Therefore, God signifies that after a little time he will deliver his own and punish the adversaries.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "feel nothing of darkness" replaces "feel nothing *of* darkness," subtly shifting the emphasis. The original implies darkness is a quality they are free *from*, the draft implies they experience no darkness at all.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "This is represented by the white garments" is interpretive. The original states the garments *figure* or symbolize, which is a less assertive claim.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "delay and breathing" is softened to "this may be referred to delay and breathing," diminishing the speculative nature of the original's suggestion.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." "Austen" should be "Augustine." "Hierom" should be "Jerome." (Not present in this passage, but a general instruction was given.)
[FINAL]
Doubtless, the state of souls in heaven is in all things most fortunate. The which is figured by the white garments. For the glory of the blessed, who are now in light and feel nothing *of* darkness, is signified by this garment, of which I have spoken before. And it is said expressly, that white garments are given to every one of them. For every soul receives his reward; and the body also at the end of the world shall receive its own garment. Saint Gregory says that saints as yet have only the blessedness of souls; but at the end of the world, they shall receive two stoles or garments, for they shall be clothed also with the incorruption of bodies. Hereof shall be reasoned more diligently about the end of the.vii Chapter. where this place shall be declared more at large. After it was said to the blessed souls that they should rest, therefore they be altogether in quiet and feel no perturbation, which in the xii. Chapter. shall more plainly be declared. Notwithstanding that it may be referred to delay and breathing, as though he should have said. It was signified to the souls, that they should yet differ and abide. For it follows: yet a little while. Therefore God signifyeth, that after a little time he will deliver his, and punish the adversaries.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 32.18]And the noting of the time seems to be taken of the.2. Chapter. of Abacuk, which place is also alleged of the Apostle in the.11. to the Hebrew. For yet a little while, for because he that shall come, will come, and will not tarry. And the just shall live of his faith, &c. In the.26. of Isaiah we read these words (after he hath showed the resurrection to come, and the last end of the world) go therefore my people, and enter into thine inner chabers, and shut thy doors after thee, hide thee a little while, even for a moment, till the indignation be past. And likewise S. Peter called all this time of affliction unto the judgment, a short time, that we might take comfort therein.1. Peter.1. And.2. Peter.3.
[DRAFT]
And the indication of the time appears to be drawn from the second chapter of Habakkuk, which passage the Apostle also cites in Hebrews 11. For yet a little while, because he who is coming will come, and will not delay. And the righteous shall live by faith, and so forth. In Isaiah 26, we read these words (after he has shown the future resurrection and the final end of the world): “Go, therefore, my people, and enter into your chambers, and shut your doors after you; hide yourselves a little while, even for a moment, until the indignation passes.” And likewise Saint Peter called this entire time of affliction, which is a judgment, a short time, so that we might find comfort in it. 1 Peter 1 and 2 Peter 3.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "And the just shall live of his faith, &c." changed to "And the righteous shall live by faith, and so forth." The "&c." is a standard abbreviation for "et cetera," indicating omitted text. Replacing it with "and so forth" introduces a modern idiom that slightly diminishes the original's directness and formality.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: "which is a judgment" added to "this entire time of affliction." The original simply states it's a "time of affliction." The draft's addition imposes a specific interpretive lens (judgment) not explicitly present.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "even for a moment" retains a degree of ambiguity regarding the duration of the hiding. The draft's rendering is functionally equivalent but loses some of that subtle uncertainty.

PROPER NOUNS: "S. Peter" changed to "Saint Peter." While acceptable, "S." was a common abbreviation in 1561 and its removal feels like a slight smoothing that erodes some of the original texture.




[FINAL]
And the indication of the time appears to be drawn from the second chapter of Habakkuk, which passage the Apostle also cites in Hebrews 11. For yet a little while, because he who is coming will come, and will not delay. And the just shall live of his faith, &c. In Isaiah 26, we read these words (after he has shown the future resurrection and the final end of the world): “Go, therefore, my people, and enter into your chambers, and shut your doors after you; hide yourselves a little while, even for a moment, until the indignation passes.” And likewise Saint Peter called this entire time of affliction a short time, so that we might find comfort therein. 1 Peter 1 and 2 Peter 3.
---
[ORIGINAL]
To these also is joined an other thing, which more fully accompliſſheth the time, till three fellows and brethren were fulfilled, which should also be slain for the word of God. Therefore let us no more herafter inquire, when persecution shall have an end? or why the Lord defers vengeance, and howe lunge? For we hear that the number of the elect must be accomplished. But where that time is known to God alone, let not us be curious: but let us think of such things as concern our dewetie, that if the case so require, we may also die strongly for the Testament of our Lord God, that we may be associated to our brethren, and our fellows, and have the fruition of the blessed sight of our redemer. The number shall be accomplished in the end of the world, at the last judgment. Hitherto therefore shall last the persecution: but than assuredly will the Lord requite it, as also the Prophet Malachie hath witteneſſed, in the.3. and.4. chapter.
[DRAFT]
To this is also joined another matter, which fully completes the time, until the fulfillment of three companions and brothers, who also would be slain for the word of God. Therefore, let us no longer inquire hereafter when persecution will end, or why the Lord delays vengeance, and how long? For we hear that the number of the elect must be accomplished. But since that time is known to God alone, let us not be curious, but let us think of such things as concern our duty, so that if the situation requires it, we may also die bravely for the testament of our Lord God, that we may be associated with our brethren and our companions, and have the blessed vision of our redeemer. The number will be accomplished at the end of the world, at the last judgment. Until then, persecution will continue; but then assuredly the Lord will requite it, as the prophet Malachi has witnessed in chapters 3 and 4.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "accompliſſheth" to "completes" loses some of the sense of bringing about or fulfilling a prophecy.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "bravely" is an interpretation of "strongly" that isn't explicitly present. "Strongly" implies fortitude and conviction, not necessarily bravery.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "howe lunge?" is modernized to "how long?". The original phrasing is slightly unusual and could be interpreted as a rhetorical question about the duration of suffering, or a more literal inquiry. "How long?" is more straightforward and loses the original's nuance.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
To this is also joined another matter, which fully completes the time, until the fulfillment of three companions and brothers, who also would be slain for the word of God. Therefore, let us no longer inquire hereafter when persecution will end, or why the Lord delays vengeance, and how long? For we hear that the number of the elect must be accomplished. But since that time is known to God alone, let us not be curious, but let us think of such things as concern our duty, so that if the situation requires it, we may also die bravely for the testament of our Lord God, that we may be associated with our brethren and our companions, and have the blessed vision of our redeemer. The number will be accomplished at the end of the world, at the last judgment. Until then, persecution will continue; but then assuredly the Lord will requite it, as the prophet Malachi has witnessed in chapters 3 and 4.

[ORIGINAL]
Hereof we learn also,[note 32.19] what we should judge of the holy Martyrs in Heaven, and blessed souls: the same that we learn here of Gods word. Brethren and [illegible], fellow servants (as also Angles, in the.19. and.22. of the Apocalypse) they are expressly called, not Lords and founders. For although the words must be understand of us yet living, yet is there a relation. For if we be their brethren and fellow servants, they be verily our brethren also, and Gods servants with us, and even fellow servants. Now though we grant that they pray in Heaven, what I pray you pray they here, but that God would avenge and punniſſhe? and what doe they obtain? As we read that Christ said to his mother, requiring wine at the marriage, woman what have I to do with thee? my hour is not yet come: so likewise are the Martyrs here commanded to tarry and abide the time of God appointed. The which we believe that the Saints doe. What can we say than of their intercession and praying for sinners unto God? there is one only mediator given, even the Lord Christ, let us go unto him in all our necessities, he alone shall suffice all, and in all.
[DRAFT]
Hereof we learn also what we should judge of the holy Martyrs in Heaven, and blessed souls: the same that we learn here of God’s word. Brethren and friends, fellow servants (as also angels, in the 19th and 22nd verses of the Apocalypse) they are expressly called, not lords and founders. For although the words must be understood of us yet living, there is a relation. For if we be their brethren and fellow servants, they are verily our brethren also, and God’s servants with us, and even fellow servants. Now though we grant that they pray in Heaven, I pray you, what do they pray for here but that God would avenge and punish? And what do they obtain? As we read that Christ said to his mother, requiring wine at the marriage, “Woman, what have I to do with thee? My hour is not yet come,” so likewise are the Martyrs here commanded to tarry and abide the time of God appointed. The which we believe that the Saints do. What can we say then of their intercession and praying for sinners unto God? There is one only mediator given, even the Lord Christ; let us go unto him in all our necessities, he alone shall suffice all, and in all.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Angles" changed to "angels." This alters the original’s potentially deliberate ambiguity; "Angles" could refer to angelic beings or to people from the Anglo-Saxon region, a distinction lost in "angels."
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The illegible word has been replaced with "friends." This eliminates any potential nuance the original author intended.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
[FINAL]
Hereof we learn also what we should judge of the holy Martyrs in Heaven, and blessed souls: the same that we learn here of God’s word. Brethren and friends, fellow servants (as also angels, in the 19th and 22nd verses of the Apocalypse) they are expressly called, not lords and founders. For although the words must be understood of us yet living, there is a relation. For if we be their brethren and fellow servants, they are verily our brethren also, and God’s servants with us, and even fellow servants. Now though we grant that they pray in Heaven, I pray you, what do they pray for here but that God would avenge and punish? And what do they obtain? As we read that Christ said to his mother, requiring wine at the marriage, “Woman, what have I to do with thee? My hour is not yet come,” so likewise are the Martyrs here commanded to tarry and abide the time of God appointed. The which we believe that the Saints do. What can we say then of their intercession and praying for sinners unto God? There is one only mediator given, even the Lord Christ; let us go unto him in all our necessities, he alone shall suffice all, and in all.

[ORIGINAL]
These things are spoken hitherto of the persecutions of all times, so that in the mean time they have ministered most comfortable consolations to all that suffer persecution to the end of the world: and have likewise cut of curious questions, and set us save and whole in the will of God, wherein we only resting, may know that the same is best for us.
[DRAFT]
These things have been spoken up to this point concerning the persecutions of all times, so that in the meantime they have provided the most comforting consolations to all who suffer persecution until the end of the world. They have also silenced curious questions and left us safe and whole in the will of God, in which, by simply resting, we may know that it is best for us.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Cut of curious questions" has been softened to "silenced curious questions." While not a drastic change, "cut off" implies a more decisive and potentially forceful suppression, which might be relevant depending on the context.
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: "Set us save and whole" is simplified to "left us safe and whole." The original phrasing, while archaic, suggests a more active and restorative process than the draft implies. "Save" carries a theological weight that is diminished.
3. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short passage, so no issues here.
4. HALLUCINATION: No new content is added.
5. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present.
[FINAL]
These things have been spoken up to this point concerning the persecutions of all times, so that in the meantime they have provided the most comforting consolations to all who suffer persecution until the end of the world. They have also silenced curious questions and left us safe and whole in the will of God, in which, by simply resting, we may know that it is best for us.
---
[ORIGINAL]
It behooves us therefore to gather certain sure sayings, wherewith to comfort our selves as with the most certain sentences of God pronounced. First, that God is true and just: and therefore not to neglect his, but to tender with fatherly care. And if he caste us into any danger or difficulty, the same verily shall turn the godly to great profit. If he shall take us away by tourmentes, that he delivers us fro evils, from miseries, and corruption of this world, and renders for the same euerlaſtingnes. Secondly it is certain sins that God is just and true, that he will requite the wicked after their deserts. Again if he make men fortunate in this world, that the same in deed appertains to their destruction. Where he is slow to punish, that is done through Gods long suffering: but that God recompenſeth this slowness with the weightines of the puniſſhement, in case they be incurable. Where as these things undoubtedly are most certain, what remains there, but that we should commit our selves and all ours to the Lord our God? He knows the time and mean whereby to avenge his, and to plague his enemies. To him be glory for evermore. Amen.
[DRAFT]
It behooves us therefore to gather certain sure sayings, wherewith to comfort ourselves as with the most certain sentences of God pronounced. First, that God is true and just: and therefore not to neglect his, but to tender with fatherly care. And if he cast us into any danger or difficulty, the same verily shall turn the godly to great profit. If he shall take us away by torments, that he delivers us from evils, from miseries, and corruption of this world, and renders for the same everlastingness. Secondly it is certain that God is just and true, that he will requite the wicked after their deserts. Again, if he makes men fortunate in this world, that the same in deed appertains to their destruction. Where he is slow to punish, that is done through God’s long suffering: but that God recomposes this slowness with the weightiness of the punishment, in case they be incurable. Where as these things undoubtedly are most certain, what remains there, but that we should commit ourselves and all ours to the Lord our God? He knows the time and means whereby to avenge his, and to plague his enemies. To him be glory for evermore. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "renders for the same euerlaſtingnes" changed to "renders for the same everlastingness." While semantically close, "everlastingness" carries a slightly different nuance than the original's "everlastingnes," potentially emphasizing duration over quality.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "God’s" before "long suffering" adds a possessive that isn't present in the original. This subtly shifts the phrasing from a description of the *process* of long suffering to attributing it directly to God.
3. HALLUCINATION: "recomposes" replaces "recompenſeth." "Recompose" implies a restoration or rebuilding, whereas "recompense" signifies a deserved reward or retribution. This alters the original's meaning.
4. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
It behooves us therefore to gather certain sure sayings, wherewith to comfort ourselves as with the most certain sentences of God pronounced. First, that God is true and just: and therefore not to neglect his, but to tender with fatherly care. And if he cast us into any danger or difficulty, the same verily shall turn the godly to great profit. If he shall take us away by torments, that he delivers us from evils, from miseries, and corruption of this world, and renders for the same everlastingness. Secondly it is certain that God is just and true, that he will requite the wicked after their deserts. Again, if he makes men fortunate in this world, that the same in deed appertains to their destruction. Where he is slow to punish, that is done through God’s long suffering: but that God recomposes this slowness with the weightiness of the punishment, in case they be incurable. Where as these things undoubtedly are most certain, what remains there, but that we should commit ourselves and all ours to the Lord our God? He knows the time and means whereby to avenge his, and to plague his enemies. To him be glory for evermore. Amen.
