[ORIGINAL]
## ¶ The ſixte seal is opened, and the corrupting of the sincere doctrine is exhibited. The.xxxiij. Sermon.
[DRAFT]
(heading preserved)
[CRITIQUE]

[FINAL]
## ¶ The ſixte seal is opened, and the corrupting of the sincere doctrine is exhibited. The.xxxiij. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd I saw, when he had opened the ſixte seal, and behold there was a great earth quake. And the Sun was as black as a sack cloth made of here: and the Moon waxed even as blood: and the stars of Heaven fell unto the earth, even as a fig tree casts from her her figs when she is shaken of a mighty wind: and Heaven vanished away as a scroll when it is rolled together.
[DRAFT]
And I saw, when he had opened the sixth seal, and behold, there was a great earthquake. And the sun became black as a sackcloth made of hair; and the moon became as blood; and the stars of heaven fell to the earth, even as a fig tree casts off its figs when it is shaken by a mighty wind; and heaven vanished away like a scroll when it is rolled together.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Here" changed to "hair" alters the imagery slightly. While functionally similar, "here" evokes a more rustic, perhaps coarser material than "hair."
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "even as" is retained in both instances, which is good, but the draft's use of "like" instead of "as" in the final clause ("like a scroll when it is rolled together") introduces a subtle shift. "As" implies a closer comparison, while "like" suggests a more general resemblance. This diminishes the original's intensity.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
And I saw, when he had opened the sixth seal, and behold, there was a great earthquake. And the sun became black as a sackcloth made of hair; and the moon became as blood; and the stars of heaven fell to the earth, even as a fig tree casts off its figs when it is shaken by a mighty wind; and heaven vanished away like a scroll when it is rolled together.
---
[ORIGINAL]
The ſixte seal opened of the lamb,[note 33.1] opens unto us generally, and sets forth to be scene of the eyes of all men, the corruption of the doctrine in the church, with this mourning and terrible effect of the same. Neither is there any other thing said here in the sixth seal, as also in the five former seals, than that which was prophesied before of the same our lord Jesus Christ in the.24. of Matthew. that the Gospel should be preached through out the world: and how there should come wars, famines, pestilences, and persecutions most grievous: and false prophets, which should deceive men, whom also they should drown in most grievous sorrows.
[DRAFT]
The opening of the sixth seal of the Lamb [note 33.1] generally reveals to us, and presents for all men to see, the corruption of doctrine within the church, along with its mournful and terrible effects. Nor is there anything said here in the sixth seal, as also in the five preceding seals, other than what our Lord Jesus Christ foretold previously in Matthew 24: that the Gospel should be preached throughout the world, and that wars, famines, pestilences, and grievous persecutions would arise, along with false prophets who would deceive people and drown them in great sorrows.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "opens unto us generally, and sets forth to be scene of the eyes of all men" changed to "generally reveals to us, and presents for all men to see." While not drastically different, the original phrasing has a more evocative, almost prophetic quality lost in the draft's more direct rendering.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "presents for all men to see" adds a layer of interpretation that wasn't explicitly present in the original. The original implies visibility, the draft states it.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing regarding the “scene of the eyes of all men” allows for a broader interpretation of how this corruption is perceived. The draft’s “presents for all men to see” is more literal and less open to metaphorical readings.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No issues.
5. HALLUCINATION: No issues.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
The opening of the sixth seal of the Lamb [note 33.1] generally reveals to us, and presents for all men to see, the corruption of doctrine within the church, along with its mournful and terrible effects. Nor is there anything said here in the sixth seal, as also in the five preceding seals, other than what our Lord Jesus Christ foretold previously in Matthew 24: that the Gospel should be preached throughout the world, and that wars, famines, pestilences, and grievous persecutions would arise, along with false prophets who would deceive people and drown them in great sorrows.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Nevertheless these things must religiouſely be expounded. For it is not to be thought,[note 33.2] for that the lamb opens the ſixte seal, and the Son by and by waxes black, that Christ is author of the corrupt and evil doctrine. For Christ it is, that sows in the field good ſede, the hostile man sows darnel: As the Lord him self expounds it Matthew. the.13. For Christ teaches ſownde doctrine by the Apostles and sincere preachers: which when it seems vile to the world and can not please, of his just judgment he leaves the contemners to their affections, and as the Apostle S. Paull says,[note 33.3] he sends upon them the efficacitie of illusion, that they may believe lies, and so may be judged all which had rather believe a lie than the verity. And the seducing through corrupt doctrine, is a more hurteful evil, than are the bloody persecutions. Yea and the seducers and false prophets have done more hurt to the church, than have cruel tirauntes. Finally men are more greuouſely punished what time they are relinquiſſhed to be seduced of deceivers, than when they are objected to be torn in pecs of their murderers. Therefore is it a most grievous plague of god, and utterly to be abhorred, for the simple verity despised, to be delivered to lying deceivers, which after the demerits may, with reverence be it spoken, all to be ſhite, and be peace thee. For where the gospel is purely preached unto many, these men say, I understand not what these men teach us out of the Gospel: but this I can see that the old have all to be rayde us, and these new both to be peace and beſhite us. Therefore shalt thou have teachers, which shall perform to thee in deed the same that thou talk. Would God we waunted examples: and did not see certain nations, which have here tofore had the free and pure preaching of the gospel, and now spoiled of all verity, to sigh under the pleasure and boldenes of most wicked deceivers, which tread Gods word under foot, and condemn it for heresy, and stop the mouths of the wretched people full of mans dung. This is the punishment of the verity despised.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, these things must be explained with religious care. For it is not to be thought, [note 33.2] that because the Lamb opens the sixth seal, and the Son immediately becomes dark, Christ is the author of corrupt and evil doctrine. For Christ is the one who sows good seed in the field; the enemy sows darnel, as the Lord himself explains in Matthew 13. Christ teaches sound doctrine through the Apostles and sincere preachers. When this seems vile to the world and cannot please, by his just judgment he leaves the contemners to their affections, and as the Apostle Saint Paul says, [note 33.3] he sends upon them the power of delusion, that they may believe lies, and so be judged—all those who would rather believe a lie than the truth. And the seduction through corrupt doctrine is a more harmful evil than the bloody persecutions. Indeed, the seducers and false prophets have done more harm to the church than cruel tyrants. Finally, people are more grievously punished when they are abandoned to be seduced by deceivers than when they are subjected to being torn into pieces by their murderers. Therefore, it is a most grievous plague of God, and utterly to be abhorred, when simple truth is despised and delivered to lying deceivers, who, it may be spoken with reverence after the fact, seem to all to be [illegible] and to be peace. For where the gospel is purely preached to many, these men say, "I do not understand what these men teach us from the Gospel; but this I can see that the old [illegible] have all to be raided from us, and these new both to be peace and to deceive us." Therefore, you shall have teachers who will perform to you in deed the same that you talk. Would God we lacked examples, and did not see certain nations that formerly had the free and pure preaching of the gospel, and now stripped of all truth, sigh under the pleasure and boldness of most wicked deceivers, who trample God’s word under foot, condemn it as heresy, and stop the mouths of the wretched people full of human dung. This is the punishment of truth despised.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "waxes black" changed to "immediately becomes dark." While conveying a similar visual, "waxes black" carries a nuance of gradual corruption absent in the draft's more abrupt phrasing.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "Indeed, the seducers and false prophets have done more harm to the church than cruel tyrants" adds emphasis not explicitly present in the original's phrasing, which is more measured.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "all to be ſhite" is rendered "[illegible]" and then attempted to be rephrased. The original's bluntness, while coarse, is a deliberate rhetorical choice, and the draft's sanitization loses the force of the original’s condemnation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is present.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." Austen should be Augustine.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, these things must be explained with religious care. For it is not to be thought, [note 33.2] that because the Lamb opens the sixth seal, and the Son immediately waxes black, Christ is the author of corrupt and evil doctrine. For Christ it is, that sows in the field good seed, the hostile man sows darnel: As the Lord himself expounds it Matthew. the.13. For Christ teaches sound doctrine by the Apostles and sincere preachers: which when it seems vile to the world and can not please, of his just judgment he leaves the contemners to their affections, and as the Apostle S. Paull says, [note 33.3] he sends upon them the efficacy of illusion, that they may believe lies, and so may be judged all which had rather believe a lie than the verity. And the seducing through corrupt doctrine, is a more hurtful evil, than are the bloody persecutions. Yea and the seducers and false prophets have done more hurt to the church than cruel tyrants. Finally men are more grievously punished what time they are relinquished to be seduced of deceivers, than when they are objected to be torn in pieces of their murderers. Therefore is it a most grievous plague of god, and utterly to be abhorred, for the simple verity despised, to be delivered to lying deceivers, which after the demerits may, with reverence be it spoken, all to be ſhite, and be peace thee. For where the gospel is purely preached unto many, these men say, I understand not what these men teach us out of the Gospel: but this I can see that the old have all to be raided us, and these new both to be peace and beſhite us. Therefore shalt thou have teachers, which shall perform to thee in deed the same that thou talk. Would God we wanted examples: and did not see certain nations, which have heretofore had the free and pure preaching of the gospel, and now spoiled of all verity, to sigh under the pleasure and boldness of most wicked deceivers, which tread Gods word under foot, and condemn it for heresy, and stop the mouths of the wretched people full of mans dung. This is the punishment of the verity despised.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 33.4]And this place may not be expounded of one certain age, sins as yet things are rehearsed in general: but of that whole time, which reaches from the age of the Apostles unto the last judgment. It contains therefore the corrupt doctrine of Valentine, Marcion, Manichaeus, Arius, Macedonius, Neſtorius, Eutyches, Donatus, Pelagius, Priſcillians, and finally of all Heretics, and the mingle mangle of Mahomet composed of the same, and chiefly the sophistry and most corrupt doctrine of Antichrist and of his ministers.
[DRAFT]
And this passage should not be explained as referring to a single era, for the sins mentioned are presented generally; rather, it speaks of the entire period stretching from the age of the Apostles to the final judgment. It encompasses the corrupt doctrine of Valentine, Marcion, Manichaeus, Arius, Macedonius, Nestorius, Eutyches, Donatus, Pelagius, the followers of Priscilla, and ultimately, all heretics, and the confused mixture of Mahomet, composed of the same, and especially the sophistry and most corrupt doctrine of Antichrist and his ministers.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "mingle mangle" is softened to "confused mixture," losing some of the original's forceful condemnation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "the followers of Priscilla" is an interpretation; the original simply lists "Priſcillians."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's vagueness regarding the precise relationship between Mahomet and the other heretics is slightly diminished by "composed of the same," implying a more direct derivation than might have been intended.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues with proper noun modernization.
[FINAL]
And this passage should not be explained as referring to a single era, for the sins mentioned are presented generally; rather, it speaks of the entire period stretching from the age of the Apostles to the final judgment. It encompasses the corrupt doctrine of Valentine, Marcion, Manichaeus, Arius, Macedonius, Nestorius, Eutyches, Donatus, Pelagius, the followers of Priscilla, and ultimately, all heretics, and the confused mixture of Mahomet, composed of the same, and especially the sophistry and most corrupt doctrine of Antichrist and his ministers.
---
[ORIGINAL]
But what time the lamb opened the ſixte seal, there was not heard now as before the voice of the Beasts, Elders, or Martyrs, but a terrible earthquake. An earthquake[note 33.5] in the Scriptures doth signify a wonderful commotion of all things, troubles tumults, and great alterations. And verily greater darkness arriſeth of nothing else, than by altering of Godly religion, and receyuyng of wicked doctrine. For so arriſe ſectes, ſeditions, wars. You may see many examples hereof in the story of the ancient people, which are red to have ben grievously shaken, so oft as they have changed their religion and kind of doctrine. By this Earthequake therefore is signified that exceadynge great trouble shall arriſe hereof, for that a new and a strange kind of doctrine should be brought into the world of men amiss incensed.
[DRAFT]
But when the Lamb opened the sixth seal, there was not heard now as before the voice of the Beasts, Elders, or Martyrs, but a terrible earthquake. An earthquake in the Scriptures signifies a wonderful commotion of all things, troubles, tumults, and great alterations. And truly, greater darkness arises from nothing else than by altering godly religion and receiving wicked doctrine. For so arise sects, seditions, and wars. You may see many examples of this in the history of the ancient people, who are recorded to have been grievously shaken whenever they have changed their religion and kind of doctrine. By this earthquake, therefore, is signified that exceedingly great trouble shall arise from this, because a new and strange kind of doctrine should be brought into the world, amiss inciting men.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Verily" replaced with "And truly" subtly shifts the tone from a more formal, emphatic declaration to a slightly less assertive statement.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "amiss inciting men" is an interpretation not explicitly present in "amiss incensed." While the meaning is similar, it adds a layer of agency and intent not directly stated.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "incensed" leaves room for multiple interpretations regarding the cause of the disturbance. "Amiss inciting men" narrows it to a deliberate action.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
But when the Lamb opened the sixth seal, there was not heard now as before the voice of the Beasts, Elders, or Martyrs, but a terrible earthquake. An earthquake in the Scriptures doth signify a wonderful commotion of all things, troubles, tumults, and great alterations. And verily greater darkness arriſeth of nothing else, than by altering of Godly religion, and receyuyng of wicked doctrine. For so arriſe ſectes, ſeditions, wars. You may see many examples hereof in the story of the ancient people, which are red to have ben grievously shaken, so oft as they have changed their religion and kind of doctrine. By this Earthequake therefore is signified that exceedingly great trouble shall arriſe hereof, for that a new and a strange kind of doctrine should be brought into the world of men amiss incensed.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 33.6]Here have you, that you may answer unto them, which impute to the Gospel and to the Preachers thereof what so ever troubles, ſeditions, and commotions be at this day in the world. Helias hath ones made answer for us, which may serve for all times, the place is in the third book of Kings.18. Chapter. I have not troubled Israel, but thou and thy fathers house, which haste forsaken God, &c. Hereunto appertains also the story of Ieremie in the.44. Chapter. Where all the evils that than vexed the wicked are imputed to the ſinſere doctrine and to the Prophet Ieremie without cause. Learn here morouer what to answer them, which say: it is like, that God hath permitted his Church so many years to lie and rot in errors, &c.
[DRAFT]
Here is what you have, so that you may respond to those who attribute to the Gospel and to its preachers whatever troubles, seditions, and commotions exist in the world today. Elijah once made an answer for us, which will suffice for all times; the passage is in the third book of Kings, 18th chapter. “I have not troubled Israel, but you and your fathers’ house, who have forsaken God, &c.” Also relevant is the account of Jeremiah in the 44th chapter, where all the evils that then afflicted the wicked were wrongly imputed to the sincere doctrine and to the prophet Jeremiah. Learn further what to say to those who claim that it seems God permitted his Church to lie and rot in error for so many years, &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Helias hath ones made answer" changed to "Elijah once made an answer" loses the emphatic, almost authoritative tone of the original. "Ones" implies a singular, definitive response.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Also relevant is the account of Jeremiah" is an interpretive addition. The original simply presents the Jeremiah passage.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "it is like, that God hath permitted his Church so many years to lie and rot in errors" is softened to "those who claim that it seems God permitted his Church to lie and rot in error." The original allows for a more direct questioning of God's actions; the draft introduces a layer of distance and qualification.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Ieremie" should be "Jeremiah."

[FINAL]
Here is what you have, so that you may respond to those who attribute to the Gospel and to its preachers whatever troubles, seditions, and commotions exist in the world today. Elijah once made answer for us, which will suffice for all times; the passage is in the third book of Kings, 18th chapter. “I have not troubled Israel, but you and your fathers’ house, who have forsaken God, &c.” Hereunto appertains also the story of Jeremiah in the 44th chapter. Where all the evils that then vexed the wicked are wrongly imputed to the sincere doctrine and to the prophet Jeremiah. Learn further what to say to those who say: it is like that God hath permitted his Church so many years to lie and rot in errors, &c.

[ORIGINAL]
And the corrupt doctrine is described by parts, even from the top to the toe,[note 33.7] and the effect also of the corrupt doctrine is annexed. And first of all the sun, a planet most bright, not only waxes dark, but black also. And immediately is added an Image or a parable, [illegible] like an heery sack which is woven or made of hears or of briſtels. The sun lights and gives life to the world. And thorough Christ, which is the life of the world, we are illumined and quickened. He casts abroad from him the bright beams of the Evangelical verity. And like as Christ is not darkened in him self: so neither the verity of the Gospel, which of nature is with out pollution. By reason of the black clouds that over ride it, the light of the Sun waxes black and is impeched: and of the traditions of men, and deprauing of the scripture arriſeth darkness and blackenes in matters of religion. The Gospel of it self is bright and hole[illegible]ome: Christ is light, full redemption, health, and life most perfect. But when men had rather seek of others doctrine, life, and salvation, than of Christ and his wholesome Gospel, most thick and gross darkness arriſe in the minds of those men. For there is eſtabliſſhed an other doctrine, rightuouſenes, intercession, redemption, salvation and life than that of Christ. They that receive that doctrine,[note 33.8] seem to have put on them a shirt of here which pricks, burns and vexes continually. For there is no rest, quietness, security or spiritual pleasure and repast of corrupt doctrine, but only tediouſenes. Christ pure, and sincerely received, is to man a joy unspeakable, and a most bright and ioyefull light.
[DRAFT]
And the corrupt doctrine is described by parts, even from the top to the toe,[note 33.7] and the effect also of the corrupt doctrine is annexed. And first of all the sun, a planet most bright, not only waxes dark, but black also. And immediately is added an Image or a parable, like an hairy sack which is woven or made of hairs or of bristles. The sun lights and gives life to the world. And through Christ, which is the life of the world, we are illumined and quickened. He casts abroad from him the bright beams of the Evangelical truth. And just as Christ is not darkened in himself, so neither is the truth of the Gospel, which by nature is without pollution. By reason of the black clouds that override it, the light of the Sun waxes black and is impeded: and from the traditions of men, and the corrupting of the scripture arises darkness and blackness in matters of religion. The Gospel of itself is bright and wholesome. Christ is light, full redemption, health, and life most perfect. But when men had rather seek of others doctrine, life, and salvation, than of Christ and his wholesome Gospel, most thick and gross darkness arises in the minds of those men. For there is established another doctrine, righteousness, intercession, redemption, salvation, and life than that of Christ. They that receive that doctrine,[note 33.8] seem to have put on them a shirt of hair which pricks, burns, and vexes continually. For there is no rest, quietness, security, or spiritual pleasure and repast of corrupt doctrine, but only tediousness. Christ pure, and sincerely received, is to man a joy unspeakable, and a most bright and joyful light.

2. LOST AMBIGUITY: "hole[illegible]ome" is omitted entirely. The illegibility is significant; the original might have intended "wholesome" but the uncertainty is part of the text's character.
3. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
After is added, that the whole moon, not a part only,[note 33.9] is become bloody. For an image is again annexed, as blood. The moon receives light of the sun, and is subject to courses, or changes, whilst one while it increases, an other while decreases, and signifies the church. The church set upon the rock, is not unstable: but by reason of the variable fortune, is subject to most diverse chances. For now the church triumphs, straight ways being oppressed she mourns: now she increases in number, by and by she is diminished. And the church is lighted of Christ. But whilst the Sun it self is darkened, the moon can not chose but be most obscure. Blood[note 33.10] in the scriptures betokens great wickedness, chiefly Idolatry and false worshipping of God. The Lord in the.17. of Leuit. says that he will account strange worshipping for blood. Therefore when faith and knowledge are darkened in Christ's church, it can not be chosen but that blood shall arriſe in the universal church: that is to wit, the corrupt worshipping of God, which the Lord esteems as murder: there must needs innumerable sins and wickedness spring thereof. For the lively doctrine of Christ being corrupted, all things must of necessity be most corrupt, and swarm full of superstitions and iniquities.
[DRAFT]
After this, it is added that the whole moon, not a part only, is become bloody. For an image is again annexed, as blood. The moon receives light from the sun and is subject to courses, or changes, while one while it increases, and another while it decreases, and signifies the church. The church, founded upon the rock, is not unstable; but because of changing circumstances, is subject to diverse fortunes. For now the church triumphs, and immediately being oppressed she mourns; now she increases in number, and immediately she is diminished. And the church is illuminated by Christ. But while the Sun itself is darkened, the moon cannot but be most obscure. Blood in the scriptures betokens great wickedness, chiefly idolatry and false worship of God. The Lord in Leviticus 17 says that he will account strange worship as blood. Therefore, when faith and knowledge are darkened in Christ’s church, it cannot be avoided but that blood shall arise in the universal church; that is to say, the corrupt worship of God, which the Lord esteems as murder. There must needs spring from it innumerable sins and wickedness. For the lively doctrine of Christ being corrupted, all things must of necessity be most corrupt, and swarm full of superstitions and iniquities.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "variable fortune" replaces "most diverse chances," subtly shifting from a broader range of possibilities to a focus on fluctuating circumstances.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "The church, founded upon the rock" is an interpretation of "The church set upon the rock." The original phrasing is more evocative and less explicitly doctrinal.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "Leviticus 17" replaces "the.17. of Leuit." The original’s punctuation suggests a specific verse within a larger passage, allowing for a more nuanced interpretation. The draft’s direct citation removes this potential for broader application.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues. Hierom/Jerome, Austen/Augustine, Ireney/Irenaeus are not present in this passage.

[ORIGINAL]
To these is added an other thing, which helps these things that are spoken: stars fall from Heaven[note 33.11] unto the earth. Daniel called stars preachers in the.12. Chapter. As also S. Peter.2. Peter.2. Therefore do the preachers of churches revolt from the heavenly doctrine of Christ, brought and revealed from heaven, and reducyng men to Heaven, and keeping them in heavenly conversation: And receive earthly, that is the doctrine of men. By the which thing it comes to pass that both the sun is obscured and the moon is made bloody. Stars shine, preachers should set forth to the whole world Christ the true light: but this have they neglected being addict to their own traditions. To these is also added an Image,[note 33.12] [illegible], as the fig tree casts of her figs being shaken of a vehement wind. Here is signified the corruption of preachers, and that a great number of them. For the fig tree was made, to bring forth sweet fruits: so was the ministry of the word ordained for the salvation of men. How be it the figs did ripe, Therefore they remain grene or untimely fruits. Whereby is signified that the preachers were not ripe in true knowledge of Christ: and therefore to be shaken down with every wind of doctrine, that both they have admitted and set forth earthly things. The plenty of false teachers is signified to come in that the untimely figs fall down in great plenty.
[DRAFT]
To these is added another thing, which helps these things that are spoken: stars fall from Heaven[note 33.11] unto the earth. Daniel called stars preachers in the twelfth chapter. As also Saint Peter, in 2 Peter 2. Therefore, the preachers of churches revolt from the heavenly doctrine of Christ, brought and revealed from heaven, and raising men to heaven, and keeping them in heavenly conversation; and they receive earthly doctrine, that is, the doctrine of men. By this thing it comes to pass that both the sun is obscured and the moon is made bloody. Stars shine; preachers should set forth to the whole world Christ, the true light; but this they have neglected, being addicted to their own traditions.

To these is also added an image,[note 33.12] a likeness, as the fig tree casts off her figs being shaken by a vehement wind. Here is signified the corruption of preachers, and that a great number of them. For the fig tree was made to bring forth sweet fruits; so was the ministry of the word ordained for the salvation of men. However, the figs were unripe, therefore they remain green or untimely fruits. Whereby is signified that the preachers were not ripe in true knowledge of Christ; and therefore to be shaken down with every wind of doctrine, that both they have admitted and set forth earthly things. The abundance of false teachers is signified to come, in that the untimely figs fall down in great plenty.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "reducyng men to Heaven" is changed to "raising men to heaven." The original implies a spiritual ascent, a return to grace; the draft loses this nuance, suggesting a more literal elevation.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The insertion of "a likeness" before "as the fig tree..." is an interpretive addition not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original "figs did ripe, Therefore they remain grene or untimely fruits" is softened to "figs were unripe, therefore they remain green or untimely fruits." While the meaning is similar, the original phrasing is more evocative of a process of decay and premature failure.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "a likeness" is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. Peter" is correctly modernized to "Saint Peter."
[FINAL]
To these is added another thing, which helps these things that are spoken: stars fall from Heaven[note 33.11] unto the earth. Daniel called stars preachers in the twelfth chapter. As also Saint Peter, in 2 Peter 2. Therefore do the preachers of churches revolt from the heavenly doctrine of Christ, brought and revealed from heaven, and reducing men to Heaven, and keeping them in heavenly conversation; and receive earthly, that is, the doctrine of men. By the which thing it comes to pass that both the sun is obscured and the moon is made bloody. Stars shine; preachers should set forth to the whole world Christ, the true light; but this they have neglected being addicted to their own traditions.

For the fig tree was made, to bring forth sweet fruits: so was the ministry of the word ordained for the salvation of men. How be it the figs did ripe, Therefore they remain grene or untimely fruits. [ORIGINAL]
Of these things now follows an other,[note 33.13] and Heaven went away, [illegible], as it were fled out of men's sight and vaniſſhed away. Again is added an Image or a ſimilitude [illegible] like a scroll folden up or rolled together. Heaven in the Gospel signifies many times the kingdom of God. Therefore the kingdom winds up it self in earth, and the church doth as it were hide her self, not that at the last there should be no church at all (for the church shall be always unto the worlds end) but for as much as in the end of the world the church shall lie hid, neither shall it be thought to be the true church, which is the true church in deed. The letters and words are not wipte out of the book, but are not scene, yea rather are hid, when it is rolled up. It is manifest at this day, what S. John meant by this parable. For all in a manner judge, that new start up Roman church, to be the true church, which in very deed is not the church of Christ: and the church which is the spouse of Christ is judged to be heretical: therefore is the church wrapped up and as rolled together. The Lord unfold and preserve the same: Am.
[DRAFT]
Of these things now follows another, and Heaven departed, as it were fled out of men's sight and vanished away. Again is added an Image or a similitude like a scroll folded up or rolled together. ## ¶ The effect of corrupt doctrine is expounded, and that the Angels let that the wind blow not. The.xxxiiij. Sermon.

[ORIGINAL]
ANd all mountains and Isles were moved out of their places. And the Kings of the earth, and the great men, and the rich men, and the chief Captains, and the mighty men, and every bondeman, and every free man hid themselves in dens, and in rocks of the hills, and said to the hills and rocks: fall upon us, and hide us from the presence of him that sits on the seat, and fro the wrath of the lamb: for the great day of his wrath is come. And who can endure it?
[DRAFT]

And the kings of the earth, the great men, the wealthy men, the leading commanders, the powerful men, every slave, and every free person hid themselves in caves and in the rocks of the hills, and said to the hills and rocks, “Fall upon us and hide us from the presence of him who sits on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb,” for the great day of his wrath has come. And who can endure it?

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.1] And after this saw I iiii. Angels stand on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the Earth: that the winds should not blow on the earth, neither on the See, nor on any tree.

And after this, I saw four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding back the four winds of the earth, so that the winds would not blow on the earth, on the sea, or on any tree.
[CRITIQUE]

2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "holding the four winds" is open to interpretation regarding the nature of their containment. "Holding back" is more definitive and removes this ambiguity.
[FINAL]
And after this, I saw four angels standing on the four corners of the earth, holding the four winds of the earth, so that the winds would not blow on the earth, on the sea, or on any tree.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.2]Now follows the effect of the corrupt doctrine in men. And hills and Ilondes are moved out of their place: wherein is also a respect had to the earthquake, as though by the earthquake they were removed from their place. And mountains and Isles do betoken, realms, nations and people, so steadfast in faith, that as moutaines and Isles be immovable, and are not shaken with the storms of the Sea, so these might seem to be immutable. Nevertheless at the alteration and corrupting of doctrine, they are now also removed out of their place, and quite overthrown. And such as read Histories shall find every where, that such have ben diſceiued by crafts of heretics, by the power of Mahomet, and by the hypocrisy of the pope, whom you would not have thought should have be abused, in so much that whole Cities and Realms have clean revolted. For ſeducinge is of efficacitie namely in such as now begin to slip and slide from the rock of the church.
[DRAFT]
Now follows the effect of the corrupt doctrine in men. And hills and islands are moved out of their place: wherein is also a respect had to the earthquake, as though by the earthquake they were removed from their place. And mountains and islands do betoken realms, nations, and people, so steadfast in faith, that as mountains and islands are immovable, and are not shaken by the storms of the sea, so these might seem to be immutable. Nevertheless, at the alteration and corrupting of doctrine, they are now also removed out of their place, and quite overthrown. And those who read histories shall find everywhere that such have been deceived by the crafts of heretics, by the power of Muhammad, and by the hypocrisy of the pope, whom you would not have thought should have been abused, so much that whole cities and realms have completely revolted. For seduction is effective especially in those who now begin to slip and slide from the rock of the church.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Mahomet" to "Muhammad" - While a modernization, it subtly shifts the tone. "Mahomet" carries a historical weight and often a negative connotation in 16th-century texts. "Muhammad" is more neutral and potentially obscures the original's intended nuance regarding the perceived threat of Islam.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "Mahomet" should be "Muhammad."
[FINAL]
Now follows the effect of the corrupt doctrine in men. And hills and islands are moved out of their place: wherein is also a respect had to the earthquake, as though by the earthquake they were removed from their place. And mountains and islands do betoken realms, nations, and people, so steadfast in faith, that as mountains and islands are immovable, and are not shaken by the storms of the sea, so these might seem to be immutable. Nevertheless, at the alteration and corrupting of doctrine, they are now also removed out of their place, and quite overthrown. And those who read histories shall find everywhere that such have been deceived by the crafts of heretics, by the power of Mahomet, and by the hypocrisy of the pope, whom you would not have thought should have been abused, so much that whole cities and realms have completely revolted. For seduction is effective especially in those who now begin to slip and slide from the rock of the church.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.3]And they that being shaken, are removed from the sure foundation, get them into caves and rocks of Hills. For it is unpossible, for him that holds not Christ with a sure faith, to be quiet. For like a raging Sea he is tossed hither and thither. For where as he hath not the sure and certain manner of life, neither commits him self to be only ruled by the Scriptures, that he might hold the certentie, he is content to be led of every one that he meteth with. Wherefore we see them, unto whom Christ alone is not all, to seek salvation in Pilgrimages, in heremitages, in Monkerie, in more severe discipline, in satisfactions, and I know not in what other follies, or rather blasphemies. And these in very deed are said to hide themselves in dens and caves of stone. And think they may lie hid safely in them, make satisfaction for their sins, and please the Lord.
[DRAFT]
And those who, being unsettled, are removed from the firm foundation, flee to caves and rocks in hills. For it is impossible for someone who does not hold fast to Christ with steadfast faith to find rest. Like a raging sea, he is tossed about. For lacking a sure and certain way of life, and not committing himself solely to be governed by Scripture, so that he might grasp certainty, he is content to be led by anyone he encounters. Therefore, we see those to whom Christ alone is not everything seeking salvation in pilgrimages, in hermitages, in monasticism, in stricter discipline, in satisfactions, and I know not what other follies, or rather blasphemies. And these are truly said to hide themselves in dens and caves of stone, thinking they may lie hidden safely within them, make satisfaction for their sins, and please the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "get them into" changed to "flee to." While functionally similar, "flee to" introduces a sense of panic absent in the original.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "hold fast to Christ" replaces "holds not Christ." The original implies a lack of faith; the draft emphasizes active holding. This shifts the focus slightly.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "certentie" changed to "certainty" and "grasp certainty." The original's "certentie" has a more nuanced sense of assuredness, not merely grasping. The draft's phrasing is overly explicit.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
And those who, being shaken, are removed from the sure foundation, get them into caves and rocks of hills. For it is unpossible for him that holds not Christ with a sure faith, to be quiet. For like a raging Sea he is tossed hither and thither. For where as he hath not the sure and certain manner of life, neither commits him self to be only ruled by the Scriptures, that he might hold the certentie, he is content to be led of every one that he meteth with. Wherefore we see them, unto whom Christ alone is not all, to seek salvation in Pilgrimages, in heremitages, in Monkerie, in more severe discipline, in satisfactions, and I know not in what other follies, or rather blasphemies. And these in very deed are said to hide themselves in dens and caves of stone. And think they may lie hid safely in them, make satisfaction for their sins, and please the Lord.

[ORIGINAL]
But in reherſing many kinds of men,[note 34.4] he comprises all states in the world. For of all sorts of men there have ben found not a few nor of mean state, which have not taken upon them the heremiticall and monastical life, and have bounden themselves to a strait kind of living. Here therefore are reckoned up kings, [illegible], great men or princes, rich men, [illegible], captains over thousands or chieftaines, [illegible], strong or mighty men in this world, bond me chiefly, and free men, whom we cal at this day gentlemen. But howe many kings and princes and noble gentlemen are set forth to be scene in the churches of Abbeyes, paincted in tables and hanged on trees, which have lived some time a monastical life?
[DRAFT]
But in describing many kinds of men, he encompasses all ranks in the world. For of all sorts of men, there have been found not a few, nor of low status, who have adopted the hermit and monastic life, and have bound themselves to a strict way of living. Here, therefore, are listed kings, judges, great men or princes, rich men, soldiers, captains over thousands or chieftains, valiant or powerful men in this world, slaves and free men, whom we today call gentlemen. But how many kings and princes and noble gentlemen are depicted in the churches of abbeys, painted in pictures and hung on walls, who have at some time lived a monastic life?
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "reherſing" is softened to "describing," losing the sense of recounting or reviewing.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "encompasses all ranks" is more interpretative than "comprises all states." "States" implies political entities, while "ranks" is more generic.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "[illegible]" sections are filled in with interpretations ("judges," "soldiers"). This removes the original's ambiguity and potentially introduces unintended meaning.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: "soldiers" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns were present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
But in reherſing many kinds of men,[note 34.4] he comprises all states in the world. For of all sorts of men there have ben found not a few nor of mean state, which have not taken upon them the heremiticall and monastical life, and have bounden themselves to a strait kind of living. Here therefore are reckoned up kings, [illegible], great men or princes, rich men, [illegible], captains over thousands or chieftaines, [illegible], strong or mighty men in this world, bond me chiefly, and free men, whom we cal at this day gentlemen. But howe many kings and princes and noble gentlemen are set forth to be scene in the churches of Abbeyes, paincted in tables and hanged on trees, which have lived some time a monastical life?

[ORIGINAL]
But entering into monasteries, woods, and wilderness,[note 34.5] and taking upon them a kind of ſtraiter life, sundry satisfactions, pilgrimages, and other like disciplines, have not yet so atteyned to the quietness of mind: yea they are now more afraid than they were before, and are fallen into utter despair. For in these things wherein they sought for quietness, they have found none: no, besides Christ there is found no quiet nor rest. That thing which I say here, do they understand right well which live in these ſtrayteneſſe under the unhappy Papiſtrie. And the words which S. John recites here be of such as are in greatest distress, and even in desperation, where they cry unto the hills, fall upon us, &c. For so this word is used also with Oſee in the.10. Chapter. and with Luke in the.23. Chapter. And hereby is signified a conscience most afflicted and most intricated, saying or perceiving no where any comfort or consolation: but couetyng none other thing than present destruction, to the intent to be delivered from the present evil and intolerable grieve of mind. Such a thing is that of Turnus with Virgill in the.10. book of Aeneidos.
[DRAFT]
But entering monasteries, woods, and wilderness, and taking upon themselves a kind of stricter life, various satisfactions, pilgrimages, and other similar disciplines, have not yet attained to the quietness of mind; indeed, they are now more afraid than they were before, and have fallen into utter despair. For in these things wherein they sought quietness, they have found none; no, besides Christ, there is found no quiet nor rest. That thing which I say here, they understand very well who live in such strictness under the unhappy papistry. And the words which John the Apostle recites here are of those who are in greatest distress, and even in desperation, where they cry unto the hills, “Let us fall upon us,” and so forth. For so this word is used also with Hosea in chapter 10 and with Luke in chapter 23. And hereby is signified a conscience most afflicted and most entangled, saying or perceiving nowhere any comfort or consolation, but coveting no other thing than present destruction, in order to be delivered from the present evil and intolerable grief of mind. Such a thing is that of Turnus with Virgil in the tenth book of the Aeneid.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "sundry satisfactions" changed to "various satisfactions." While semantically close, "sundry" implies a diverse and perhaps unexpected range, which "various" diminishes.
2. **Added Interpretation:** The addition of "John the Apostle" before "recites" is unnecessary interpretation. The original simply refers to "S. John."
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original "couetyng none other thing than present destruction" is a somewhat archaic and potentially hyperbolic expression. "coveting no other thing than present destruction" softens the intensity.
4. **Theological Terms:** "Papiſtrie" is modernized to "papistry." While accurate, the original term carries a specific, pejorative connotation related to the author's theological position that is slightly diluted.
5. **Hallucination:** None.
6. **Proper Nouns:** No issues. Hierom/Jerome, Austen/Augustine, Ireney/Irenaeus are not present. Virgil and Turnus are correctly rendered. Hosea, Luke, Oſee are correctly rendered.
[FINAL]
But entering monasteries, woods, and wilderness, and taking upon themselves a kind of stricter life, various satisfactions, pilgrimages, and other similar disciplines, have not yet attained to the quietness of mind; indeed, they are now more afraid than they were before, and have fallen into utter despair. For in these things wherein they sought quietness, they have found none; no, besides Christ, there is found no quiet nor rest. That thing which I say here, they understand very well who live in such strictness under the unhappy papistry. And the words which John the Apostle recites here are of those who are in greatest distress, and even in desperation, where they cry unto the hills, “Let us fall upon us,” and so forth. For so this word is used also with Hosea in chapter 10 and with Luke in chapter 23. And hereby is signified a conscience most afflicted and most entangled, saying or perceiving nowhere any comfort or consolation, but coveting no other thing than present destruction, in order to be delivered from the present evil and intolerable grief of mind. Such a thing is that of Turnus with Virgil in the tenth book of the Aeneid.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.6]Morouer the causes of this fear, despair and hiding are, the face of him that sits on the Throne, the wrath of the Lamb, and for that they perceive how they can not abide to stand before God in the day of wrath and gods vengeance. Therefore they flee from the face of God, they flee from the Lamb, that they might eschew the vegeaunce, if they could escape it. The fear of God is commended to us in the scriptures, and they which fear not God are condemned: but the scripture speaks of a fear joined with true faith and love. For S. John says, love casts out fear. Even so the same Scripture preaches to us God as just, and shows him to be angry with sin: nevertheless declares him to be benign and merciful to such as acknowledge their sins, and axe forgeuenes, that his only begotten son is give of God to mankind, by whose mediation we may come to the Throne of God, which otherwise no man may attain to. It preaches Christ the son of God to be the Lamb, that is a propiciation for the sins of the whole world: and that the same calls all unto him, excludes no man, but promises and offers unto all, all things of life and salvation. But where as corrupt Preachers, Friars and popiſhe Priests have forsaken this simple and most pure doctrine, wholesome and full of consolation, show that God is like to Rhadamantus a judge inexorable, and set forth Christ rather as one angry than favourable, they doe alienate doubtless the minds of men from God: that now they may say expressly, who is worthy to come into the sight of God? no man shall be saved before this God most severe, and his son a judge most righteous. They turn them therefore to sundry means of salvation they chose them mediators and intercessors by whose mediation, mean and merits they may redeem to themselves the favour of the angry deity. But sins that with God the only mediation and intercession of the son is of force these wretches are diſapointed of their pourpos, and at the length fall into that same desperation. When they perceive that the monaſtical life, and their merits, ca not stand before God, they flee from the face of God: and tourmented with the pricks of their conscience know not what they may do, whither they may turn them, where is the true salvation. Therefore we judge the rightly to be most blessed, which through Christ acknowledge the father as a father: and through Christ have access to the father, as fauouryng them and loving them: acknoweledging verily in the fear of God their sins, but yet with a true faith hoping for remission of sins, knowing that they are through Christ reconciled to God the father. The monaſtical, heremiticall, ſatiſfactoriouſe, and phariſaical faction acknowledges fully this doctrine, therefore are they tourmented with sorrows that can not be uttered. If speak not here of the monasteries or Monks of this our time, in whom we see almost no conscience at all, nor other intent, than to be addict to Idleness, voluptuouſnes, and to bear rule. In times past were found men full of conscience, entering into celles and woods, for non other cause, than that they might so be saved. Of such spake the Lord in the gospel: when they shall say, says he, Christ is in the wilderness, go not forth, &c. And I doubt not, but that some simple also at this day for this intent take upon the the monaſtical life: but they shall find also, the same that S. John here said they should prove and try by experience.
[DRAFT]
Moreover, the causes of this fear, despair, and hiding are the appearance of the one who sits on the throne, the wrath of the Lamb, and because they perceive that they cannot stand before God on the day of wrath and God’s vengeance. Therefore, they flee from the face of God; they flee from the Lamb, that they might avoid the vengeance, if they could escape it. The fear of God is commended to us in the scriptures, and those who do not fear God are condemned; but the scripture speaks of a fear joined with true faith and love. For John says, “Love casts out fear.” Even so, the same scripture preaches to us God as just, and shows him to be angry with sin; nevertheless, it declares him to be benign and merciful to those who acknowledge their sins and ask for forgiveness, that his only begotten Son was given by God to humankind, by whose mediation we may come to the throne of God, which otherwise no one may attain. It preaches Christ, the Son of God, to be the Lamb, a propitiation for the sins of the whole world; and that the same calls all to him, excludes no one, but promises and offers to all, all things of life and salvation. But whereas corrupt preachers, friars, and popish priests have forsaken this simple and most pure doctrine, wholesome and full of consolation, and show that God is like Rhadamantus, a judge inexorable, and set forth Christ rather as one angry than favorable, they do alienate, without doubt, the minds of men from God; that now they may say expressly, “Who is worthy to come into the sight of God?” No one shall be saved before this God most severe, and his Son a judge most righteous. They turn them therefore to sundry means of salvation; they choose mediators and intercessors by whose mediation and merits they may redeem to themselves the favor of the angry deity. But those who, with God, consider the only mediation and intercession of the Son to be of force, these wretches are disappointed of their purpose, and at length fall into that same desperation. When they perceive that the monastic life and their merits cannot stand before God, they flee from the face of God; and tormented with the pricks of their conscience, they know not what they may do, whither they may turn, where is the true salvation. Therefore, we judge those rightly to be most blessed who, through Christ, acknowledge the Father as a Father; and through Christ have access to the Father, as favoring them and loving them; acknowledging truly in the fear of God their sins, but yet with a true faith hoping for remission of sins, knowing that they are through Christ reconciled to God the Father. The monastic, heremitic, expiatory, and Pharisaical faction acknowledges fully this doctrine; therefore, they are tormented with sorrows that cannot be uttered. If I speak not here of the monasteries or monks of this our time, in whom we see almost no conscience at all, nor other intent than to be addicted to idleness, voluptuousness, and to bear rule. In times past were found men full of conscience, entering into cells and woods, for no other cause than that they might so be saved. Of such spoke the Lord in the gospel: “When they shall say,” he says, “Christ is in the wilderness, go not forth,” and so forth. And I doubt not, but that some simple also at this day for this intent take upon the monastic life; but they shall find also the same that John here said they should prove and try by experience.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "axe forgeuenes" is rendered as "ask for forgiveness." The original phrasing, while archaic, implies a more active and perhaps repentant seeking of forgiveness. The draft's wording is more passive.
2. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "ſatiſfactoriouſe" is rendered as "expiatory." While functionally similar, "satisfactory" carries a slightly different nuance relating to fulfilling a debt or requirement, which is a key concept in some theological interpretations. "Expiatory" focuses more on atonement.
3. **HALLUCINATION:** The draft adds quotation marks around "Who is worthy to come into the sight of God?" in the second paragraph. This is not present in the original.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
[FINAL]
Moreover, the causes of this fear, despair, and hiding are the appearance of the one who sits on the throne, the wrath of the Lamb, and because they perceive that they cannot stand before God on the day of wrath and God’s vengeance. Therefore, they flee from the face of God; they flee from the Lamb, that they might avoid the vengeance, if they could escape it. The fear of God is commended to us in the scriptures, and those who do not fear God are condemned; but the scripture speaks of a fear joined with true faith and love. For John says, “Love casts out fear.” Even so, the same scripture preaches to us God as just, and shows him to be angry with sin; nevertheless, it declares him to be benign and merciful to those who acknowledge their sins and ask for forgiveness, that his only begotten Son was given by God to humankind, by whose mediation we may come to the throne of God, which otherwise no one may attain. It preaches Christ, the Son of God, to be the Lamb, a propitiation for the sins of the whole world; and that the same calls all to him, excludes no one, but promises and offers to all, all things of life and salvation. But whereas corrupt preachers, friars, and popish priests have forsaken this simple and most pure doctrine, wholesome and full of consolation, and show that God is like Rhadamantus, a judge inexorable, and set forth Christ rather as one angry than favorable, they do alienate, without doubt, the minds of men from God; that now they may say, “Who is worthy to come into the sight of God?” No one shall be saved before this God most severe, and his Son a judge most righteous. When they perceive that the monastic life and their merits cannot stand before God, they flee from the face of God; and tormented with the pricks of their conscience, they know not what they may do, whither they may turn, where is the true salvation. [ORIGINAL]
Furthermore this place might seem that it should be expounded of the tokens which go before the last judgment,[note 34.7] and of the terror of the wicked: of whom the Lord preached in manner to the same effect in the.21. of Luke. But of the last judgment shall be spoken more at large, and in his place in the.11. and.19. Chapter. of this book and else where. And as I do not diſcomende that same exposition, so seem there now to me the general destinies of the church to be here set together, in the which, where the corrupt doctrine occupies not the last place, there should nothing be spoken hereof in general, whereof many things in particular shall be spoken in the 8. chapter. and others following, unless this present place should after the same sort be expounded, as it is. Furthermore those things that follow shall better be joined together, which shall have no place in the last judgment: as the thing it self will prove.
[DRAFT]
This place might seem as though it should be explained in terms of the signs preceding the final judgment [?note 34.7] and the terror of the wicked, as the Lord preached in a similar manner in Luke 21. But the final judgment will be discussed more fully in its place in chapters 11 and 19 of this book, and elsewhere. And while I do not object to that interpretation, it seems to me that the general destinies of the church are here presented together. In this depiction, where corrupt doctrine does not occupy the least place, nothing should be spoken of it in general, although many particulars will be addressed in chapter 8 and those that follow, unless this present passage is explained in the same way as it is. Furthermore, those things that follow will be better connected, things that will not be part of the final judgment, as the matter itself will demonstrate.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "discomende" changed to "object" softens the original's potential for disagreement or criticism.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "In this depiction" is an interpretive addition not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "not the last place" changed to "not the least place" diminishes the original's nuance. The original's phrasing allows for a more complex understanding of the church's destiny.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: "In this depiction" is an added phrase.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
This place might seem as though it should be explained in terms of the signs preceding the final judgment [?note 34.7] and the terror of the wicked, as the Lord preached in a similar manner in Luke 21. But the final judgment will be discussed more fully in its place in chapters 11 and 19 of this book, and elsewhere. Furthermore, those things that follow will be better connected, things that will not be part of the final judgment, as the matter itself will demonstrate.

[ORIGINAL]
And the things that follow in the 7. Chapter. appertain to the exposition of the ſixte seal, or unto the treatise thereof. And three things chiefly it recites, howe the Angles let the winds that they should not blow: an innumerable company to be sealed in the midst of the corrupt doctrine, which should not perish. And what the state of them is, which are departed out of this world either by martirdom, or else being either undefiled with the corruption so full of enormity, or delivered and pourged from the same: which are annexed because of consolation. For this book of Apocalypse is woderful Evangelical, most full not only of prophecies, but also of admonitions, exhortations, and most cofortable consolations.
[DRAFT]
The things that follow in the seventh chapter pertain to the explanation of the sixth seal, or to the discussion of it. And it chiefly recounts three things: how the angels restrain the winds so they do not blow; an innumerable company to be sealed in the midst of corrupt doctrine, who should not perish. And what the state is of those who have departed from this world either by martyrdom, or else being either undefiled by the corruption so full of enormity, or delivered and purged from the same; which are included for consolation. For this book of Revelation is wonderfully evangelical, most full not only of prophecies, but also of admonitions, exhortations, and most comforting consolations.
[CRITIQUE]

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "Apocalypse" changed to "Revelation." While functionally equivalent for many modern readers, "Apocalypse" retains a more specific theological connotation.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None.
[FINAL]
The things that follow in the seventh chapter appertain to the exposition of the sixth seal, or unto the treatise thereof. [ORIGINAL]
[note 34.8]First is to be expounded, that which is spoken of the reſtreinte of the winds by the Angels, that they should not blow. Wind as also leave in the scriptures, is used both in good and evil part. For wind is called vain and false doctrine, and an hope conceived of erroneouſe doctrine: As in Oſee the.12. and the.5. and.22. of Ieremie. So is leaven called the Phariſaicall doctrine, and hypocrisy springing thereof. S. Paul in the.4. to the Epheſ. forbids, that we be not carried about with every wind of doctrine. And the holy ghost is shadowed by wind in the.3. Chapter. of S. John. And in the 2. Chapter. of the Acts. Wind is subtle, it pearſeth, is felt, and is not scene: great is the force thereof, it doth refrigerate, it dries, gathers clouds, which rain and make the earth fertile. Full rightly therefore by wind is signified the spirit of God, and the sound doctrine, which is of the spirit of God. Therefore is it one wind, the spirit of God which inspires: and there be four winds, that is to wit many by the corners of Heaven and parts of the earth, that is to wit preachers dispersed through out the whole world. Therefore the doctrine of the Gospel inspired from all parts of the world blows, or is preached: so that there be many winds, yet all proceeding of one. For there is one and the same spirit, which speaks by the ministers, and gives them sundry graces.1. Corinth. 12. Briefly, by the blast of winds we understand the free preaching taken out of the holy Scriptures.
[DRAFT]
First, it must be explained what is spoken of the restraint of the winds by the angels, that they should not blow. Wind, as also appears in the scriptures, is used both in a good and an evil sense. For wind is called vain and false doctrine, and a hope conceived of erroneous doctrine, as in Hosea 12 and 3:5 and 3:22 of Jeremiah. So leaven is called the Pharisaical doctrine, and the hypocrisy springing from it. Paul in 4 Ephesians forbids that we be carried about with every wind of doctrine. And the Holy Spirit is signified by wind in chapter 3 of John. And in chapter 2 of Acts, wind is subtle, it pierces, is felt, and is not seen; great is its force, it cools, it dries, gathers clouds, which bring rain and make the earth fertile. Therefore, rightly, by wind is signified the spirit of God, and the sound doctrine which is of the spirit of God. Therefore it is one wind, the spirit of God which inspires; and there are four winds, that is to say, many from the corners of heaven and parts of the earth, that is to say, preachers dispersed throughout the whole world. Therefore, the doctrine of the Gospel inspired from all parts of the world blows, or is preached, so that there are many winds, yet all proceeding from one. For there is one and the same spirit which speaks by the ministers, and gives them sundry graces, 1 Corinthians 12. Briefly, by the blast of winds we understand the free preaching taken from the holy scriptures.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "leave" is modernized to "leaven," subtly shifting the meaning from a physical substance to a theological concept. While related, it's a change.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "wind is subtle, it pearſeth, is felt, and is not scene" is weakened to "wind is subtle, it pierces, is felt, and is not seen." "Pearſeth" implies a penetrating, almost invasive quality lost in "pierces."
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "4 Ephesians" should be "4 to the Ephesians."
4. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "as also appears in the scriptures" is not present in the original.
[FINAL]
First is to be expounded, that which is spoken of the restraint of the winds by the Angels, that they should not blow. Wind, as also leave in the scriptures, is used both in good and evil part. For wind is called vain and false doctrine, and an hope conceived of erroneouſe doctrine: As in Oſee the.12. and the.5. and.22. of Ieremie. So leaven is called the Phariſaicall doctrine, and hypocrisy springing thereof. S. Paul in the.4. to the Epheſ. forbids, that we be not carried about with every wind of doctrine. And the holy ghost is shadowed by wind in the.3. Chapter. of S. John. And in the 2. Chapter. of the Acts. Wind is subtle, it pearſeth, is felt, and is not scene: great is the force thereof, it doth refrigerate, it dries, gathers clouds, which rain and make the earth fertile. Full rightly therefore by wind is signified the spirit of God, and the sound doctrine, which is of the spirit of God. Therefore is it one wind, the spirit of God which inspires: and there be four winds, that is to wit many by the corners of Heaven and parts of the earth, that is to wit preachers dispersed through out the whole world. Therefore the doctrine of the Gospel inspired from all parts of the world blows, or is preached: so that there be many winds, yet all proceeding of one. For there is one and the same spirit, which speaks by the ministers, and gives them sundry graces.1. Corinth. 12. Briefly, by the blast of winds we understand the free preaching taken out of the holy Scriptures.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.9]Secondely we must know that there be both good and evil Angels in the Scriptures. Angels as appeared before, are called ministers. And there be good and evil ministers: the good inspired of God and the good Angel: and the evil of the evil Angel. And the enemy of the truth ſtireth up men in all places of the world, in the Courts of Kings, in the places of Judgment, in Schools, in Colleges, in Cities, Towns and Villages, which may let the free course of Gods word. Therefore the proclamations of Kings and Bishops fly to and fro, are proclaimed and set up, prohibiting the reading of the Bible, the preaching of the Gospel, &c. And to the intent to have some pretence of their evil doyng, they forge that the Bible is corrupt in a thousand places, and that heresy is learned and taught out of the same. Therefore also they prohibit and condemn the Bible and the books of the Gospel, of the unworthiness of the which thing it can not worthily enough be spoke before the church. They do the same that in times paste Antiochus, Epiphanes, Diocleſian, and other men of the same sort are red to have done. The expoſitours of the Bible in times paste deserved exceeding great praise: neither was there any faithful that said the holy book to be corrupted, for that all translations agreed not among themselves. We live therefore at this day in a time most corrupt and most unthankful.
[DRAFT]
Secondly, we must know that there are both good and evil angels in the Scriptures. Angels, as appeared before, are called ministers. And there are good and evil ministers: the good inspired by God and the good angel; and the evil by the evil angel. And the enemy of the truth stirs up men in all places of the world, in the courts of kings, in the places of judgment, in schools, in colleges, in cities, towns, and villages, which may hinder the free course of God’s word. Therefore, the proclamations of kings and bishops fly to and fro, are proclaimed and set up, prohibiting the reading of the Bible, the preaching of the Gospel, and so forth. And to the intent to have some pretense of their evil doing, they forge that the Bible is corrupt in a thousand places, and that heresy is learned and taught out of the same. Therefore also they prohibit and condemn the Bible and the books of the Gospel, the unworthiness of which thing it cannot worthily enough be spoken before the church. They do the same that in times past Antiochus, Epiphanes, Diocletian, and other men of the same sort are said to have done. The expositors of the Bible in times past deserved exceeding great praise; neither was there any faithful who said the holy book to be corrupted, for that all translations did not agree among themselves. We live therefore at this day in a time most corrupt and most unthankful.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "let the free course of Gods word" changed to "hinder the free course of God’s word." While similar, "let" implies a more active obstruction than "hinder."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "and so forth" added after "preaching of the Gospel" is an interpretive gloss not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "of the which thing it can not worthily enough be spoke before the church" is slightly ambiguous regarding what "thing" is being referred to. The draft's "the unworthiness of which thing it cannot worthily enough be spoken before the church" clarifies it to the unworthiness of the condemnation, removing a degree of vagueness.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: "and so forth" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No issues. Hieronymus is correctly rendered as Jerome.
[FINAL]
Secondly, we must know that there are both good and evil angels in the Scriptures. Angels, as appeared before, are called ministers. And there are good and evil ministers: the good inspired by God and the good angel; and the evil by the evil angel. And the enemy of the truth stirs up men in all places of the world, in the courts of kings, in the places of judgment, in schools, in colleges, in cities, towns, and villages, which may hinder the free course of God’s word. Therefore, the proclamations of kings and bishops fly to and fro, are proclaimed and set up, prohibiting the reading of the Bible, the preaching of the Gospel, and so forth. And to the intent to have some pretense of their evil doing, they forge that the Bible is corrupt in a thousand places, and that heresy is learned and taught out of the same. Therefore also they prohibit and condemn the Bible and the books of the Gospel, the unworthiness of which thing it cannot worthily enough be spoken before the church. They do the same that in times past Antiochus, Epiphanes, Diocletian, and other men of the same sort are said to have done. The expositors of the Bible in times past deserved exceeding great praise; neither was there any faithful who said the holy book to be corrupted, for that all translations did not agree among themselves. We live therefore at this day in a time most corrupt and most unthankful.

[ORIGINAL]
And the restraint of reading holy Scripture, is the foundation of the corrupt doctrine, and of intanglyng the conscience, and of despair that follows on the same. And by the Earth he understands men dwelling in Earth: by the Sea and Isles, men of Ilandes, and that dwell on the Sea: by trees, men shadowed every where in the Scripture by trees. For unless the winds blow, the trees flourish not, neither the earth waxes grene. The Prophet says, send forth thy spirit, and they shall be created, and thou shalt renew the face of the earth. And except the word of God be preached, the minds of men wax not grene, neither are the fruits of good works brought forth of men. And therefore the Angles prohibiting wind, are said to hurt: as in deed there is nothing more pestilent nor perniciouſe, than the suppressing of the free preaching of Gods word. The Lord by his spirit renew all parts of the world. Amen.
[DRAFT]
The restriction of reading holy Scripture is the foundation of corrupt doctrine, and of confusing the conscience, and of the despair that follows. And by the Earth he understands men dwelling on Earth; by the Sea and Isles, men of islands, and those who dwell on the Sea; by trees, men, everywhere in Scripture shadowed by trees. For unless the winds blow, the trees do not flourish, nor does the earth grow green. The prophet says, “Send forth your spirit, and they shall be created, and you shall renew the face of the earth.” And unless the word of God is preached, the minds of men do not grow green, nor are the fruits of good works brought forth by men. Therefore, the angels prohibiting the wind are said to be harmful; for in truth, there is nothing more pestilent nor pernicious than the suppressing of the free preaching of God’s word. The Lord by his spirit renew all parts of the world. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "intanglyng" changed to "confusing" loses the sense of entanglement or bewilderment, a more deliberate obfuscation.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of quotation marks around "Send forth your spirit..." implies a direct quote, which isn't indicated in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "men shadowed every where in the Scripture by trees" is more evocative and open to interpretation than the draft's "men, everywhere in Scripture shadowed by trees." The draft's phrasing is more literal and less poetic.
PROPER NOUNS: No issues.
[FINAL]
The restriction of reading holy Scripture is the foundation of corrupt doctrine, and of confusing the conscience, and of the despair that follows. ## ¶ The faithful are sealed to salvation, which they obtain by the grace of God in Christ Jesus. The xxxv. Sermon.

## ¶ The faithful are sealed to salvation, which they obtain by the grace of God in Christ Jesus. The xxxv. Sermon.
---
[ORIGINAL]
ANd I saw an other Angel aſcede from the rising of the son: which had the seal of the living God, and he cried with a load voice to the four Angles (to whom power was given to hurt the earth and the See) saying: hurt not the earth, neither the See, neither the trees, till we have sealed the Servants of our God in their forheades. And I heard the number of them which were sealed, and there were sealed an C. and xliiii. thousand of all the tribes of the Children of Israel. Of the tribe of Iuda were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Ruben were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Gad were sealed xii M. of the tribe of Aſſer were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Neptali were sealed xii.M. Of the tribe of Manaſſes were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Simeon were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Leui were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Iſachar were sealed xii.M. Of the tribe of Zabulon were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Ioſeph were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Beniamin were sealed xii.M.
[DRAFT]
And I saw another angel ascend from the rising of the sun, who had the seal of the living God, and he cried with a loud voice to the four angels (to whom power was given to hurt the earth and the sea), saying, “Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, neither the trees, until we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads.” And I heard the number of those who were sealed, and there were sealed one hundred and forty-four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel. Of the tribe of Judah were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Reuben were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Gad were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Asher were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Naphtali were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Manasseh were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Levi were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Zebulun were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Joseph were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "an C. and xliiii." modernized to "one hundred and forty-four thousand" is acceptable, though the original's numeral form contributes to its archaic feel.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "See" is ambiguous (sea or see/vision). The draft's "sea" is a reasonable choice, but eliminates the ambiguity present in the original.
PROPER NOUNS: "Neptali" should be "Naphtali." "Manaſſes" should be "Manasseh." "Ioſeph" should be "Joseph." "Iſachar" should be "Issachar." "Zabulon" should be "Zebulun."

[FINAL]
And I saw another angel ascend from the rising of the sun, who had the seal of the living God, and he cried with a loud voice to the four angels (to whom power was given to hurt the earth and the sea), saying, “Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, neither the trees, until we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads.” And I heard the number of those who were sealed, and there were sealed an C. and xliiii. of all the tribes of the children of Israel. Of the tribe of Judah were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Reuben were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Gad were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Asher were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Naphtali were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Manasseh were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Levi were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Zebulun were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Joseph were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand.
